item.title: [a16z] a16z Goes Global: Why American Tech Must Lead the World
item.dc:creator: a16z
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、隐藏的悖论：a16z 正在做的，是"用资本主义的语言重写北约"

表面上这是一档 VC 的国际化发布会，但真正在发生的事，是私营资本正在填补一个战后国际秩序留下的空缺——一个连接盟友的"技术条约体系"。

Anj 的那句话点破了这层："deterrence is no longer just the size of a military. It's the pace of innovation." 这意味着，冷战时期由国务院、五角大楼签署的《共同防御条约》，正在被 API、模型权重、autonomous ship 的采购合同悄悄替代。a16z 把 global affairs、LP relations、go-to-market 三条线并成一体的组织架构，其实镜像了一个微型国务院 + 商务部 + USAID 的复合体——只不过 KPI 变成了 IRR。

一个让人意外的观察：当 Ben 说"America has to be a leader technologically"时，他实际上在描述一种"技术版的门罗主义"——但这版门罗主义与 19 世纪那版不同，它不是"美洲是美国的势力范围"，而是"运行在西方模型上的世界是自由世界的势力范围"。价值观通过 token 概率分布来投射，比通过舰炮投射更廉价、更隐蔽、也更彻底。

二、跨领域连接：Televisa × 11 Labs × 门罗主义 = 一种新的"文化贸易顺差"

墨西哥 Televisa 用 11 Labs 把西班牙语内容配音到全球市场，然后拿到 Netflix 的大单——这个故事被讲成"AI 让本地内容全球化"。但如果你把它放在语言学 + 地缘政治的坐标系里看，会发现一件更深层的事：

这是"美国技术堆栈的殖民化"与"本地文化的去殖民化"同时发生的罕见时刻。 阿根廷口音、墨西哥口音、哥伦比亚口音的差异，在 AI 之前是本地文化保留的"关税壁垒"；11 Labs 把这个壁垒拆掉后，理论上文化多样性应该被稀释——但实际发生的是反过来的：技术使得"没被翻译过的文化"第一次拥有了全球化的规模经济。

这里藏着一个可以类比的历史时刻：古腾堡印刷术让拉丁语失去了垄断地位，路德的德语圣经反而催生了民族语言的黄金时代。11 Labs 之于西班牙内容，可能就是印刷机之于路德——技术让"本地"变得可规模化，而不是让"全球"变得更同质化。

Anj 顺便提了一句几乎被主持人忽略的话，却是全场最重的一句："they knew it would be true to what was said." 当中国开源模型翻译时可能悄悄绕过天安门话题，美国模型的"不做审查"就从一个道德立场变成了一个可以在合同里定价的技术规格。这是历史上第一次，"言论自由"变成了一个可以写进 SLA 的可测量指标。

三、真正让我意外的模式：a16z 在承认"Silicon Valley 不可复制"，但同时在出售它

Ben 直接反驳了主持人："I don't think that's true. I think that's a happy talk." Silicon Valley 无法通过"分布式互联网"复制——这本是 VC 生意的死敌，因为它意味着 a16z 无法把自己的 playbook 简单批发出去。

但如果你换一个角度看：a16z 恰恰是在把这种"不可复制性"本身商品化。

  • 沙特买不来 Silicon Valley 的三个要素（大学、政策、文化崇拜创业者），但可以买到 a16z 的中介。
  • Ben 列的三要素（人才/政策/文化）里，第三条——"社会地位奖励创业者"——几乎无法通过政策制造。它是路径依赖，是几代人反复看到有人成功、有人破产、社会没有惩罚失败的累积结果。

这里有一个跨领域的隐喻：a16z 的国际化，做的其实是"文化保税区"生意。就像新加坡是法律和金融的保税区，迪拜是转口贸易的保税区，a16z 正在为其他国家提供一个"接入 Silicon Valley 文化算符"的临时通道——你不需要在本地重建文化，你只需要通过 a16z 的关系网络，让你的公司暂时进入那个文化的引力场。

而最反直觉的一个信号是 Ben 那句自嘲："I wish we could bring some of our own policy makers on those trips." 这句话里藏着 a16z 的真实战略焦虑——他们不怕中国，他们怕的是美国政策制定者亲手拆掉"文化崇拜创业者"这个最难复制的资产。所以这场国际化在某种程度上是双向对冲：一方面把 Silicon Valley 输出到盟友，一方面为万一 Silicon Valley 在美国本土被削弱时，在全球留下多个"备份节点"。

这就像流亡的拜占庭学者把希腊手稿带到佛罗伦萨——不是征服，是为一种可能被本土政治扼杀的文明形态寻找异地备份。

📝 转录文本

开场：三个论点

When AI becomes the interface for everything, every tool we have, every technology we use is going to be built on these models. And the models are not objective. They have opinions. They have strong opinions on things like history, on culture, on values. And those are very different depending where you are.

Deterrence is no longer just the size of a military. It's the pace of innovation in reacting to cheap software-built tech that adversaries may be using — whether they're a country, criminal, or a terrorist group. And those technologies today are not being built by governments. They're being built by the private sector.

Products and technology travel faster than companies can. So what happens is for startup companies, usually olden days, you didn't care about going international until you had a few hundred million in revenue. Now these companies have to go sooner and sooner internationally.

The number one question we often get when we're visiting different countries is how do you recreate Silicon Valley locally?

I don't think that's true, by the way. I think that's a happy talk.

一、a16z 为什么走向全球

We are here to talk international today. Um, lots of things going on at the firm — for a firm that used to historically be all centered in Menlo Park and we didn't even want to open up a San Francisco office. This is a big change for us.

So Ben, why don't you take us through this announcement around our global ambition and how all the pieces — between global affairs, international go-to-market, LP relations on the global partnership level — kind of fit in together?

Yeah. Well, it really kind of starts with America, interestingly. It's kind of been our belief, our strong belief, that if you drop a person into any country in the world and they don't have the culture and they don't have money and capital resources yet, and they just have an idea of how to make the world a better place, their best shot is in America. And that's just a very, very special thing.

So a big mission of our firm is kind of trying to preserve that special thing that is America. And that means America has to be a leader. It has to be a leader economically. It has to be a leader culturally. It has to be a leader militarily. And to do that we have to be a leader technologically. We have to be a tech leader. And that's really our role in the ecosystem and in the world: to help America be a leader.

从美国使命，到盟友合作

What we realized as we've gotten further along with this mission is that we need to bring in our partners, our allies, into that mission. It kind of started with the work that you're doing, Jen — with people from around the world wanting to participate in the US technological opportunity. That meant we started getting investors from around the world.

As we went out to see those investors and said, "how can we be a better partner?" we found some interesting things. One is: every country wants to not only economically benefit from the technology, but they'd like to actually directly benefit from the technology. Can you bring it to us? Can we get a chance to use it?

At a higher level, at the government policy level, people wanted to know: how can we be a great country for adopting technology, for innovating like you guys are, and for helping push our country into the future?

三大支柱：Global Affairs、Investment、Go-to-Market

As we looked at how we step up and step into that opportunity and be a good partner, we realized with our country partners we needed relationships at the government level, at the investor level, and at the adoption level. And that's what these three components become.

  • Global Affairs — led by Anj, builds our relationships with governments ranging from Saudi Arabia to Japan to Mexico.
  • Investment — sometimes those are the government, sometimes they're various companies in the government, sometimes companies that handle government money in the form of pensions.
  • Go-to-Market — where Raghu's effort comes in, because both governments and businesses want to adopt the technology.

二、Anj：技术如何成为国家安全的中心舞台

I'm going to get you involved here — how much of your decision to join a16z was motivated by this mission, and the sense of duty in helping other countries think about AI adoption and not wanting to get left behind?

Very much so. What I saw happen in the years in the intelligence committee and then the White House is technology move from being a tool of diplomacy, of national power, to the arena of it.

  • The supply chain for chips was not a technology issue as much as a foundational economic and national security issue.
  • The supply chain for the components that go into drugs, when used by one country to constrain or have impact on another country, becomes a tool of national power.
  • A software vulnerability is now not an IT issue. It can be a source of leverage, particularly if that vulnerability is in key parts of your power systems or your water systems.

So I saw that shift happen from tech being either an IT issue or just a tool, to the central arena of both economic growth and national power.

威慑的新定义

Quite frankly, from a deterrence perspective — which is the language we talk about between countries in terms of deterring an invasion of a country, maintaining an international system of stability — deterrence is no longer just the size of a military.

As we see in the Strait of Hormuz, or as we saw in the Red Sea, it's the pace of innovation in reacting to cheap software-built tech that adversaries may be using — whether they're a country, criminal, or a terrorist group.

And those technologies today are not being built by governments. They're being built by the private sector. Whether that's AI systems, autonomous systems, cyber defense systems — they are being built in the private sector.

So certainly from a US perspective, we need to ensure we have the relationships between government and private sector to leverage that for our own economic growth and as a force for good on the world stage — but also that our allies as well. We build together, we fight together. And to the extent that our tech also represents our values, it's important that the world run on America and on Western tech.

三、Ben：AI 作为一切事物的界面，价值观必然被投射

Actually that's a great point on the values point — because Ben, you've said before no country wants to get colonized in cyberspace, particularly because AI is now just getting built in the six-block radius in San Francisco. Say a little bit more about that.

When AI becomes the interface for everything — every tool we have, every technology we use, every system we have, from your car to your refrigerator to your education system — is going to be built on these models.

And the models are not objective. They have opinions. They have strong opinions on things like history, culture, ethics, values. And by the way, those are very different depending where you are. The history of wars, the history of a country and laws, whether that country was a good actor or a bad actor — are all very different depending on where you're getting the information from.

If you believe, as I do, that for all our flaws, our fundamental values and ideas about everything — from the way our economic system works to the rule of law — have resulted in basically the best opportunities for humans in the world, then you want those values to be projected onto the world more so than to be the recipients of some other values.

Yeah. Giving people a chance — that was the premise of the original announcement we did for our fund announcement earlier this year, and the importance of that. And then particularly extending that chance to the allies of America as well.

四、Raghu：产品跑得比公司快

Portfolio companies are increasingly going international way sooner. They're multi-country, multi-channel, multi-product way sooner in the life cycle. How much of this is also accelerated by what's happening in AI, and just the ability to push out software updates?

The reality is that products and technology travel faster than companies can. If you've got a great product, everybody learns about it very quickly, instantaneously. You can access it through APIs wherever in the world you are. You can build on top of those things.

So what happens is for startup companies — usually in the olden days you didn't care about going international until you had a few hundred million dollars in revenue, etc. Now these companies have to go sooner and sooner internationally.

While the products are being discovered internationally sooner, also to Ben's earlier point, it's easier to adapt these products for local markets much more easily thanks to AI — as voice becomes the interface, to localize and internationalize these products, it's so much easier.

但做生意的方式没变那么快

In order to capitalize on the opportunity at scale, things don't change. You still have to be in region. You still have to understand the market structure of the region you want to operate in. You have to understand partnerships. You have to build relationships. Lots of other countries have very relationship-based economies, not transaction-based economies.

So it's this interesting blend where the technology moves really fast, but the way you do business hasn't changed that fast.

案例：Televisa × 11 Labs

A really great example of what Raghu was talking about — when we were in Mexico recently, we were meeting with the CEO of Televisa-Univision, Alfonso de Angoitia, a great friend of the firm, who's one of our global partners. We were asking, "what are your struggles?"

One of his challenges is he faces heavy competition from some of the global streamers like Netflix or Disney, who have this US technology that's very powerful. But he has this unbelievable Spanish-speaking content — but it was hard for him to get to play on a global basis because it's in Spanish, and it's a Mexican Spanish accent, which is different than an Argentinian Spanish or Colombian Spanish accent.

He's like, "how should we solve this?" And we're like, well, we've got this great company, 11 Labs. They can help you with it. We put them together, and in a couple of months he was in France and Poland and all over the world with this incredible technology that had his actors basically acting exactly as they normally do — with their right kind of intonations, their right laugh, everything — but in different languages. He recently based on that success got a big deal with Netflix.

Here we have something where we can bring our partners who have a great product that's culturally local but really interesting, and make it a global product by not competing with their allies but partnering with their allies. I think that's a lot of what the program is about.

与中国开源模型的价值观对比

I want to tie what Ben just said back to the values point, because people often ask us: Chinese open source models, how do you compare and contrast?

In that case, they didn't have to worry that when the content was dubbed into different languages, if the speaker said something sensitive about Tiananmen Square or sensitive regarding the Chinese government, that using an American model that content would be translated and changed along the way. They knew it would be true to what was said.

That's what we mean when we talk about American values of not censoring, not injecting political will into how these models operate — in the way that one would contrast. Now, open source is something we deeply value, because at the end of the day, there's all different kinds of models and all different kinds of price points. But that's a good example of what we mean when we say American and Western values reflected in the models that we deploy around our infrastructure.

For sure — the telenovelas are spicy enough. I don't know how much the introduction of Tiananmen Square needs to be introduced necessarily in the storyline. But you never know, the border crossings, etc. Good point, they keep their audience well enough.

五、a16z 作为"Power Broker"：为创始人打开国际市场的第一扇门

This actually is a core part — when Packy McCormick wrote the article about us earlier this year, he called us a "power broker," and power being part of an offering that we enable our founders to tap into.

For a lot of companies, starting a Mexico office for example is probably not one, two, three, maybe even top 10 on their list. But if they have a deal that we're able to walk in and effectively enable them a shot at winning way earlier in the lifecycle, they might prioritize that. Raghu, Ben, how has that changed for founders?

A lot of markets are very top-heavy. You take any mature business and ask how much of your revenue comes from — even companies, I mean countries like UK — a significant chunk is going to come from the very top companies in UK.

The reality is whichever country you look at, it's five or 10 companies that matter, and of course the government is a big influencer and buyer. So the example you gave in Mexico is great — there are a few global companies in Mexico like the one Ben talked about.

It is not worthwhile setting up a whole office and serving all of the countries. But if you could get into the top five companies in Mexico, that probably is worth a lot more than setting up a full operation in some other country. So it is the top-heavy nature and the relationship-heavy nature that helps our portfolio of companies.

打开一扇门 = 提前解锁国际化窗口

Mexico probably isn't a top 10 thing that most companies go into first — and by the way, that's a terrible shame, because Mexico is our neighbor. When we think about it from a national security standpoint, how powerful would it be to have a Western Hemisphere supply chain ecosystem that was really well connected? I think it's kind of bad for the country that it is number 10.

Secondly, to open a country as a company is a very large expense. You have to set up an entity, hire a team, localize the product. Depending on the country and how committed you are, it could easily be five or 10 million just to get to your first deal. That's going to delay in your lifecycle when you can go international.

But what if, before you ever put up an entity or got started in that country, you had a big opportunity — a $5 million opportunity, a $10 million opportunity — that makes that investment much easier to do earlier? We can really enable both our companies and our allies by paving the way for that.

案例：Flow × 沙特

A great example is — nobody's going to Saudi Arabia first. However, Saudi Arabia is booming. There are great opportunities there, and they really need our help in developing the country. They're modernizing, which is incredibly important to the security of the world.

Saudi Arabia is going from this tribal, different kind of society, I would just say, to a modern, highly civilized player in the modern world and full ally of the United States. That transition is really important that it succeeds.

So we're able to take a company like Flow — Adam Neumann's company — bring them to Saudi Arabia, introduce them to the right people in the government, right people in business, right investors. It's the optimal place to live if you're an expat moving to Riyadh, help develop the economy. It's something that just isn't really possible without an effort like this.

六、优先地理：日本、韩国、中东、墨西哥、加拿大

Maybe let's talk about geos we're prioritizing. Are there themes as we think about different countries that make more sense versus others?

This is timely — Raghu and I have been debating this. From our standpoint, it's very powerful when they're a strong ally of the US, we've got a relationship with the people in government, with the people in business, with the investors. Those things are not separate — they all interact with each other. If we have that, then we can really accelerate the opportunities for them and for us.

Anj：从威慑视角看盟友

What's changed so much is that on the global stage — whether it's in the context of AI, or countries pressuring other countries via supply chains — there are parts of the world where we have close allies who are now in increasingly tenuous positions.

If we look at the Indo-Pacific region: China has vastly increased its military, vastly increased the number of nuclear capabilities it has, and its shipbuilding capability are orders of magnitude more than the United States. Our allies in the region have started responding. Japan has changed its really sacrosanct policy from the post-World War II period to say it must have a capable military.

It wants to build the military to fight today's wars, not yesterday's wars. It doesn't necessarily have the military-industrial base. That cuts both ways. From the halting steps the United States military has taken — the number of defense reform policies written in the last 20 years — they all say the same thing: fewer small numbers of exquisite platforms, more cheaper, larger numbers of platforms, in addition to very exquisite platforms for certain specific use cases.

So as Japan thinks about building a capable military to deter aggression in the region, it is looking to partner with new firms building autonomous ships, building AI for an integrated battle space. They want to build with us — they don't want to just buy — because they have their own capabilities they want in their own country, their own supply chains to protect.

中东与拉美

The Middle East has been an area of American interest for a long time. - Israel is an incredibly capable ally with capable military and intelligence community. - The UAE is as well. - Saudi Arabia is modernizing and thinking about AI infra, how it builds an entrepreneurial innovation base in order to compete globally.

When we think about Latin America — when we think about fentanyl coming into the United States, it comes in via Mexico. So public safety, national security, how autonomous ships can help a vast Mexican coastline are not only important to our ally and to the stability of Latin America, it's important to us as well.

That's at least the national security lens we bring to some of our partnerships in American Dynamism or in the AI infra space.

Raghu：四条选择标准

The reason Ben and I have been debating is there are so many sides to this that you've got to bring it all together.

  1. America's interest — it has to make a lot of sense from America's interest point of view. That is abundantly existing in all these countries.
  2. AI-forward governments — these governments are very AI-forward and putting in policies that are modernization-centric, whether it's in defense or across the economy.
  3. Top-heavy economies — you go hit the top companies, you are actually having global influence. For example, all those North Asian countries — I think 15–20% of the Global 2000 are across Japan, Korea, Taiwan.
  4. Different way of doing business — the way business is done in these countries is very different than let's say UK or some English-speaking country. So the value-add we can provide to our startups, and the value-add we can provide to these countries, is remarkably higher than in other places.

So when you put it all up and score it out, that's why we go into these countries.

And some of these countries are also leapfrogging — they didn't have to deal with the prior technology. Like Saudi: you go through the visa entry and it takes 30 seconds. Way that snap wasn't good — 30 seconds to go through the visa entry, which is unbelievable. Here you get stuck behind, unfortunately, a very long line increasingly for a number of different other reasons. It's a different approach to how you think about incorporating technology in everyday life — but it's not evenly distributed.

七、a16z 的国际化 vs. 传统 VC 的国际化

It's very different when we think about international prioritization compared to maybe other VC firms. The typical VC firm's international approach has always been investment-driven versus actually thinking about the portfolio companies and what they would like to do internationally. Speak a little more about that contrast — and quite frankly, why for the first 10 years of the firm, it was very rare we even did an international deal outside of not just the US but even outside of Silicon Valley.

We've always looked at investing through the lens of: what do we bring to the table? If you think about what we try to do for a company, we really try to help them network to the right talent, the right customers, the right expertise. And that is very hard to do in multi-geographies — even like San Francisco / Menlo Park in the early days. We were like: let's just have a really good product for this, and then we'll expand from there.

Other VCs think of the product much more as the money. So if the product is the money, then the international strategy is: okay, we'll find a team of investors in say the UK, we'll help them raise money, and then we'll take a percentage of their winnings for helping get them their product. That makes sense in that model.

It doesn't make sense for us, because our whole thing is: how do we help you build a better company? How do we accelerate your opportunity or growth? So we needed to get to a certain level of size and stature for that to make sense in more regions. Now that we are, it starts to make a lot of sense.

我们扮演的角色：填补生态缺口，而不是抢占早期机会

In our role in investing, we don't look at it as how do we snatch up the opportunities in Sweden or Japan or Saudi or UAE. We look at it as: what is your ecosystem missing in terms of capital and expertise to go global, and is there an opportunity for us to plug in?

We don't want to displace their A-round and seed investors necessarily. We want to say: if you've got companies that are emerging as real global companies, then that's where we can really step in and help. And if we're already in the country, already doing a lot of business, already understanding them and their entrepreneurs, then we're in great position to do that. But we would do that directly. We're not raising money for somebody who's not part of our firm, our culture, our mission and having them go run a play. That's just not us.

本地公司缺的往往是 growth capital

So much of the time when you think about these entrepreneurial ecosystems, what they're often missing is growth capital. They actually have a great talent base on the early stage side, but they're missing that component to actually go global, and they become a localized success versus actually thinking about global ambitions.

Raghu, drawing from your experience at VMware — how has the ambition for local companies to become global changed over time?

That's dramatically increased. Previously, as I said earlier, you would not even dream of going global until you're really well advanced. Now, because your product can be there, there is such a universal appeal for you. If you've got a great product, whether it's B2B or B2C, it behooves you to go earlier.

What is missing is the supporting ecosystem — and finding a way to plug into a new country. That's where our new offering really focuses on: helping them get the fast start in these regions.

For that you need to be in the region, to have the relationships, to build the trust, to build the credibility. All of those things don't happen overnight, and that's where we can provide that.

By spending time locally as well, we get to know the local ecosystem and then see the great entrepreneurs from those regions — and to the extent it makes sense, actually fund those and help them go international leveraging our network as well. So businesses in these countries are so much a collective ecosystem, rather than an individual company going in and dominating something there.

八、AI 时代的安全：开源 vs 封闭、进攻 vs 防守

Speak a little more about the prior tech cycles of adoption — particularly with AI. Anj, there are so many nuances around security, and even the debate between open versus closed. How have you been thinking about that and advising companies on that delicate balance?

It's a hot topic right now, and it's a hot topic around the world, because fundamentally — as many technologies are — AI is dual-use.

Let's use cyber as an example. The first step, whether you are an attacker or a defender, is finding vulnerabilities in systems. If you're a defender, you patch them. If you're an attacker, you exploit them.

Models make the step of finding vulnerabilities in code far easier. And quite frankly, it makes the next steps — exploit or defend — easier as well.

网络防御的经济学

The economics of cyber defense were broken well before the latest AI models came out. I saw that in the White House, where my nightmare was the ransomware attacks against rural hospitals in America. There wasn't another hospital two blocks down. So actual Americans' opportunity to access healthcare was disrupted by, in some cases, state-harbored criminal groups halfway around the world.

Infrastructure is very fragile, and the reality is that the models help both. I personally believe — focused on cybersecurity for a moment — that they help far more in defense, because defense is far harder. You have to be defending a broad expanse; an attacker has to find one way in.

So I actually think we are in a difficult space at this moment because there's such a broad attack surface, so many systems that are vulnerable. But we will see the opportunity for much more secure space.

政府与模型：如何在推广和管控之间平衡

When I talk with governments around the world, or companies around the world, all are grappling with that question. On the government side: most countries have two missions — defending the country as well as maintaining its interest overseas. Given both, how do we ensure these powerful capabilities don't fall into our adversaries' hands, don't fall into criminals' hands?

In the traditional cyber tools arena, we always grappled with this. When a capability is on a thumb drive, export controls are a joke. You can't actually control it in that way.

And there is real power in the models that the world runs on being run on trusted models. When people are using an American model, they don't have to worry there's an intentional backdoor. They don't have to worry that a chatbot's answers will be framed by censoring to fit a particular political story of an authoritarian government.

So the question for now is: how do we build in the degree of safety in models so they can be used in environments that need that safety? Because the main models are American, we're in a particular position to think through and build that out.

This is not done by government, not done by the private sector alone. What it really needs is people who understand the models best from the private sector at the table with folks on the government side who are thinking about how to both promote innovation and control for these risks — and building out the right set of government policies to enable both the use as well as the understanding of how to protect against the risks that come with the models as well.

As our partner Marc often says, technology is the dog that caught the bus. We've never seen before that countries are feverishly demanding the latest versions of the model, because they recognize how competitive it is to their economy, to their people, to their interests. This is unlocking all sorts of new paradigms as we think about globalization, in a way that feels categorically different than just selling product internationally. Hopefully this is an opportunity for many of our founders as well.

双刃剑：不放开就没人能防守，放开了坏人也能攻

No doubt. Although it is complicated. From a government standpoint, you'd like to say: okay, don't release your model with cyber hacking capabilities. But if you do, and you want to find the vulnerabilities in your own code and patch them, you can't do that without also enabling cyber attacks — because as soon as it can find the vulnerabilities, guess what?

You're in a situation where if you lock that capability up, then the only people who will be able to understand vulnerabilities are the bad actors who jailbreak the system.

So we're in a very — I would say — interesting world on that.

你要成为第一个 hack 自己系统的人

To that point — we'll focus on the cyber aspect because that's gotten so much attention with Anthropic's models, etc. — what AI models essentially do, to Ben's point, is: you want to be the first to hack your systems.

We often use the expression of "digital doorknobs." It now allows attackers to jiggle every doorknob continuously and at scale. So your hope for your network is using those models first to find where you're vulnerable — and frankly then be able to fix it. That wasn't a tractable problem before.

The models help you particularly for codebases that were public, particularly if you're relying on third-party code or open source code — and that's some of the biggest areas of risk, where there are dependencies on code that nobody was really maintaining.

We're now seeing companies coming together, taking responsibility for that, because the cost of not addressing insecure code has gone up, and the cost of addressing it has gone down. Which is a good shift from where we were before.

We may finally build secure systems. Exactly. Please God. And more importantly, the process for building new code can incorporate these as well.

网络攻击的演化史：破坏 → 盗窃 → 战争

It's very tricky from an industry standpoint. Originally cyber hacking was vandalism — okay, I'm going to mess up your PC. Then, with the internet, it became theft. And now it's war.

That evolution kind of snuck up on people. When it went from vandalism to theft, the vandalism company — Microsoft — is the one who created all the theft opportunities by putting Windows on the internet when it wasn't ready, because it wasn't built for that. With ActiveX and all these technologies that let people come into the internet and get right to your machine.

I think we're in a period like that where we've got all this technology that was built in the pre-AI era that's not nearly secure enough for the AI world. And we're going to have to make that transition.

There's a 20-year legacy of code that has to be tackled. AI is increasingly — many opportunities. Yes. Many opportunities.

九、结语：Silicon Valley 能被复制吗？

Ben, the number one question we often get when we're visiting different countries is: how do you recreate Silicon Valley locally? There are a few components to success that have enabled Silicon Valley, but there's also the proposition — what if the internet is actually Silicon Valley, or the actual Silicon Valley just distributed?

I don't think that's true, by the way. I think that's a happy talk.

One thing you learn when you go to different countries is: they're very different. The laws are different, the policies are different, the governance is different. That's why America has continued to be special. We've had the internet now for quite a long time, and that's absolutely not the case.

So what is going on? By the way, I wish we could bring some of our own policy makers on those trips so they could hear people say we want Silicon Valley — before they keep trying to wreck us. Many of our policy makers are outstanding, but some really don't like the fact that we have a technology industry.

三大成分

As we've traveled around, there's a combination of ingredients that make Silicon Valley special, and then America itself special.

1. Talent. You do need the talent. Talent is most easily seen in: is there a great technical university that graduates people who know how to build things? That's an essential thing. And by the way, many countries have that, that don't have a Silicon Valley.

2. Laws and policies that facilitate entrepreneurship. Do you have a set of laws and policies that are essentially good for business? Can you create a company easily? Can you hire people easily? Can you fire people easily? Is there a tax incentive structure that's not onerous? Can you own an illiquid asset that has value — or will it get seized?

Norway introduced an unrealized capital gains tax, and that effectively ended the tech ecosystem there. Those kinds of policies are super important. If they're not right, nobody is going to take the extreme risk — both personal and financial — of starting a company if it's not an environment conducive to that.

3. Culture that celebrates entrepreneurs. The third one, which is a combination of everything in a way: is the culture such that young people — the most capable young people willing to make the biggest contribution — do they get status, social status reward? Is it cool to either start or join a new company?

Very few countries have that. It's really really good in the US. It's clearly good in China, by the way — although they've done a few things to mess it up. But in most countries, success is frowned upon.

政治修辞与嫉妒的经济学

You hear a lot of this in the political rhetoric: "Oh, the only way you could make a billion dollars is if you're a thief." Which is completely absurd. You just look at the people — almost none of them are thieves. You can't steal a billion dollars is a real truth. But that's a rhetoric that works, because if you're like: how did he get all that money? Oh, he's a thief. Okay, I'm not a thief, so I feel much better about myself now.

But that's the thing that's so hard to replicate, and so hard to build, and so easy to destroy. It's something that I think we have to just be aware of — how special that is and unique in the world. We can try and help other people do it, but we also have to try equally hard not to wreck it ourselves.

结尾：美国式的野心

It's World Cup season, and there was a great tweet where someone was like, "I think America thinks they could potentially win the World Cup." And it's like — maybe they can, maybe they can't, but the fact that America thinks they can is a feature, not a bug.

There's a reason ambition is fantastic. There's a reason we all read our kids — what is it? The Little Engine That Could. "I think I can, I think I can, I think I can." That's in America. You can believe you can.

That's right. Amazing. That's a good note to end on. Thank you all.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、隐藏的悖论：a16z 正在做的，是"用资本主义的语言重写北约"
表面上这是一档 VC 的国际化发布会，但真正在发生的事，是**私营资本正在填补一个战后国际秩序留下的空缺**——一个连接盟友的"技术条约体系"。
Anj 的那句话点破了这层：**"deterrence is no longer just the size of a military. It's the pace of innovation."** 这意味着，冷战时期由国务院、五角大楼签署的《共同防御条约》，正在被 API、模型权重、autonomous ship 的采购合同悄悄替代。a16z 把 global affairs、LP relations、go-to-market 三条线并成一体的组织架构，其实**镜像了一个微型国务院 + 商务部 + USAID 的复合体**——只不过 KPI 变成了 IRR。
一个让人意外的观察：**当 Ben 说"America has to be a leader technologically"时，他实际上在描述一种"技术版的门罗主义"**——但这版门罗主义与 19 世

item.title: [Lex Clips] The biggest naming lie in history: The Byzantine Empire - historian explains | Anthony Kaldellis
item.dc:creator: Lex Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、"拜占庭"这个词，是一场持续了五百年的品牌重塑事件

最令人意外的观察是："拜占庭帝国"从来没有作为一个自称存在过。它是历史学家在这个国家灭亡几百年后，坐在西欧的书房里，追认给它的一个标签。这不是学术上的疏漏，而是一次逆向的历史命名权抢夺——就像你死后被人改了名字，还被从家谱里挪走。

Kaldellis 用了一个极其精准的词——认知失调（cognitive dissonance）。西欧文明明知道东罗马自称罗马、法律上是罗马、语言上是罗马公民，却选择性失明地把"罗马"的遗产据为己有。这里藏着一个跨领域的对照：这套操作和现代品牌收购中的"商标漂白"极其相似——当一家新公司想继承某个老品牌的声誉，它必须先在叙事上让原公司"死掉"。西罗马的"覆灭"于是被夸大成整个罗马的死亡，因为只有罗马死了，法兰克、神圣罗马、乃至后来的西方，才能名正言顺地"继承"它。

二、"举证责任在谁"——一个被翻转的历史学默认设置

Kaldellis 抛出的关键句是："举证责任在那些声称它不是罗马的人身上。" 这句话表面平淡，实际上是在把整个史学界过去 500 年的默认假设翻过来放。

这里有一个跨领域的深层类比——它和法律中的"无罪推定"结构一模一样。过去的西方史学采用的其实是"有罪推定"：默认东罗马是"另一个东西"，然后让持异议者去证明它是罗马。而 Kaldellis 做的事，是把默认值改回来：源头证据（一手史料）压倒性地说它是罗马，所以任何"它不是罗马"的说法才是需要证据的一方。这是一次史学的举证责任反转，比任何具体论点都更具颠覆性——因为它改变的不是结论，而是游戏规则。

三、隐藏的系统性真相：长度本身是一种反常

大多数人没意识到的是——从公元前 8 世纪的王政到 1453 年君士坦丁堡陷落，罗马作为一个连续的政治实体存在了 约 2200 年。这个长度在人类政治史上近乎奇迹级的反常。

一个跨领域的震撼对照：2200 年大约是从孔子到今天的距离，也是从佛陀到今天的距离。想象一个国家从孔子在世时开始运作，一路穿越汉唐宋元明清直到今天，中间从未换过法律主体的身份——这就是罗马做到的事。

而 Kaldellis 揭示的更深层模式是：这个国家之所以在西方叙事里被"切成两段"，恰恰是因为它太长了。一个连续 2200 年的政治实体会打破所有西方史学的分期框架（古代/中世纪/近代），会让"文艺复兴"变得不再是"复兴"（因为古典从未真正死去），会让"黑暗时代"这个概念直接坍塌。所以西方史学的分期学，本质上是为了让自己的故事讲得通，而必须让罗马提前死掉。

啊哈时刻：拜占庭这个名字，不是无知造成的，而是结构性必要——一个文明为了完成自我叙事，必须先杀掉它的祖父。

📝 转录文本

一、开场：一个被误命名的帝国

You've described that the Byzantine Empire is the Roman Empire. That calling it the Byzantine Empire is an invention of historians writing long after the empire has collapsed. That the people of that time considered themselves Romans, and that the so-called Byzantine Empire never actually existed. That it was, in every legal and cultural sense, the Roman Empire continuing unbroken.

So let's get our terms right. In the grand historical context, what is the Byzantine Empire, and why should we just call it the Roman Empire?

二、举证责任的反转

Kaldellis: So, Lex, the burden of proof is on those who would assert that what we've been calling the Byzantine Empire is something other than the Roman Empire — because all of our sources are very clear about this. And we've known about this. We've always known about it.

It's almost a form of cognitive dissonance, right? It's like when you know something is the case, but you carry on as if it's not.

三、东罗马：未被承认的延续

So, the Eastern Roman Empire — this is the direct continuation of the ancient Roman Empire in the East, right? Everybody knows the Western Empire fell in the 5th century. And for many conceptions of Western history, that was sufficient. Like, that's when we just called it "the Roman Empire fell in the 5th century." And we kind of — yeah, we kind of know that the Eastern half survived, but we don't want to include that in our cultural genealogy. And so we kind of pretend that it became something else.

But in fact, it called itself the Roman Empire. Its subjects were Roman citizens. They called themselves Romans all the way down through to the end and beyond.

四、"拜占庭"这个词的政治起源

So there are a number of reasons why Western Europeans wanted to think that the Eastern Empire is something different. And those reasons have created these models where they called it "Empire of the Greeks" for a thousand years, and they switched to "Byzantine Empire" for very political reasons.

Now, that's collapsing. So we're now moving into a phase where we have "the long Roman Empire." We recognize that the history of the Roman state, the Roman polity, is something that lasts an extraordinarily long time.

Lex: Mhm.

Kaldellis: They're unique almost in history. From antiquity — like the archaic period — down to the 15th century.

五、罗马国家的宏观时间线

Lex: I think it would be really nice to actually look, just for a brief moment, at the grand scale of the full Roman state as it lasted for over 2,200 years.

Kaldellis: Yeah.

Lex: So, its founding, some 753 BC, to let's say the fall of Constantinople in 1453 AD. So can we try to give a big-picture overview of everything that happened?

1. 王政时期

Kaldellis: Okay, so first we have the period of the kings. This is almost legendary — from like the 8th century BC down to around 510 BC. So this is when Rome is ruled by a series of local kings.

2. 共和国

The kings are expelled and the republic — or what we call the republic — is instituted. And this is a regime that's governed by mostly an aristocracy, but with cooperation from the people of Rome, in various ways that the arrangements change over the centuries. So that lasts for about five centuries.

3. 帝国的诞生

And then we have the end of the republic in the era of Julius Caesar and Augustus Caesar, when the civil wars lead to the creation of an imperial monarchy — right — under Augustus. So that's, we date that roughly either — yeah, 27 BC, something like that.

4. 一千五百年的帝制

And thereafter, it is an imperial monarchy for the next millennium and a half. Very broadly, we can divide that into two phases: one where the center of power is in Rome, and one where it's in Constantinople.

Lex: Mhm.

Kaldellis: And, you know, we can debate the dates of when that transition takes place. These big transitions usually take place over long periods of time — they're not like very sudden.

六、三段式总览

So that's the big picture.

Lex: Mhm.

Kaldellis: Kings, republic, imperial monarchy.

观看原视频 →

item.title: [Business of Sport] AC Milan’s Multi Billion Gamble, The Power of the Tour de France, & Tennis Player’s Limp ‘Boycott’
item.dc:creator: Business of Sport
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、AC米兰的"救赎剧本"其实是一场庞氏结构的加速器

表面上看，红鸟资本让AC米兰"三年盈利、还清埃利奥特债务、启动圣西罗新建"是一个教科书式的复兴故事。但如果把数字摞在一起看，你会发现一个诡异的现象：红鸟正在用一笔更贵的债（10.5% PIK notes）去替换一笔更便宜的债（8% 常规利息），并把利息滚进本金里。

这不是"再融资"，这是债务延迟爆炸装置——2031年到期时，650m会滚到950m，加上新球场约500m的股权投入，总敞口逼近15亿欧元。而米兰要能被卖到20亿欧元（红鸟的最低体面退出线），需要"非球员交易"营收做到8亿欧元——全世界只有皇马和巴萨达到过这个量级。

出人意料的观察是：红鸟其实不是在经营一家球队，而是在经营一个"9年到期的看涨期权"。它赌的不是米兰会重新伟大，而是2031年之前一定会出现某个中东主权基金或美国科技新贵，愿意付出"梦想溢价"接盘。而伊布被任命为负责人的真正原因，可能不是他懂足球——而是他本人就是那份Pitch Deck上最贵的一张封面。

二、环法、大师赛、AC米兰——三个"家族式垄断"的隐藏共通结构

这一集把三个看起来毫无关系的话题串在一起时，浮现出一个跨领域的共同模式：由私人家族守护、拒绝完全商业化的"精神性IP"，正在成为体育世界最稀缺的估值锚。

  • 环法（Amaury家族）：1200万观众全部免费，年利润只有3000-4000万欧元，主动放弃门票收入。
  • 美国大师赛（Augusta绿夹克委员会）：门票稀缺、转播克制、赞助商极少。
  • 温布尔登（AELTC）：允许排队买票、坚持白色球衣、抵抗夜场化。

这些"低效率"表面上是钱没赚够，实际上是在用商业克制换取文化不可替代性。而正是这种不可替代性，让它们变成了体育世界的"瑞士银行式资产"——不增长，但永远抗贬值。

跨领域的映射令人惊讶：这与奢侈品行业的"爱马仕悖论"完全同构。爱马仕不追求扩产、不追求最大化销售，用"配货制"人为压制供给——恰恰因为它拒绝饱和，它成了LVMH永远收购不到的对象。Amaury家族对环法的态度，与Dumas家族对爱马仕的态度，是同一种资本哲学。

真正吃亏的，其实是那些去挑战这类"精神性IP"的挑战者：LIV Golf烧了几十亿撬不动大师赛，One Cycling注定同样撞墙。因为它们试图用"更商业化"的说辞去打败故意反商业化的稀缺性——这在博弈论上是死局。

三、英格兰、网球球员、AC米兰新管理层——一个反直觉的"专家诅咒"

三个看似不相关的失败案例，其实揭示了同一种系统性病灶：当决策者过度依赖"我比别人看得更多"的信念时，往往会做出最偏离常识的判断。

  • 图赫尔看了20个月的球员数据，得出的结论是不带阿诺德、帕默、吉布斯-怀特——却带上了明显不到国家队水平的球员。
  • 网球球员看了自己的收入表和大满贯营收增长，得出的结论是"我们应该分更多"——却忘了他们从来不是营收增长的贡献者，而是Federer/Nadal/Djokovic/Williams时代的尾部受益者。
  • 卡多纳利看了整个AC米兰的资产负债表，得出的结论是解雇马尔蒂尼、法拉尼、阿莱格里，把整个球队交给伊布——一个三年前还在踢球的人。

这三个案例的共同点是：决策者都拥有"内部信息优势"，但这个优势反而让他们无视了外部人一眼就能看出的常识。

意外的连接：这与金融市场里的"分析师悖论"惊人相似——覆盖某只股票最深入的卖方分析师，往往是最晚下调评级的人。因为他们的"深度"变成了心理沉没成本，让他们必须为自己长期建立的模型辩护。

真正健康的系统性思考者，应该定期让自己"回到局外人的视角"——如果一个只看5分钟的陌生人都能看出问题，那你花了20个月却看不出来，问题可能不在数据里，而在你已经陷入了一个只有你自己能看懂的叙事。

四、彩蛋：为什么小威廉姆斯的复出与Serena W的"限媒抗议"其实是同一个心理机制

一个可能没被注意到的观察：Serena Williams的复出，和现役球员"用限制媒体采访来抗议奖金分配"，本质上是同一种对聚光灯的成瘾结构，只是方向相反。

  • 小威用"回归"来重获关注。
  • 现役球员用"退场"来重获关注。

两者都不是理性的策略选择，而是运动员这个身份对"存在感"的深度依赖——真正的商业罢工需要放弃收入、承担长期不确定性，但他们选择了成本最低、伤害最小、却也最没用的"象征性抗议"。这与工会经济学里的"信号性罢工"（signaling strike）是同一种病理：当你不敢承担真正的代价时，你选择的抗议形式暴露了你的真实议价能力——那就是没有。

建队理论 vs 现实差距

话题三：网球奖金争议与"象征性罢工"

But of course, if you strike, you're going to ensure that you don't take any of the potential 3.5 or so million that you'll get for winning it. The 80,000 that you even get just for turning up if you lose in the first round, which went up from 66,000 last year, should we just say.

大满贯的商业模式与利润流向

So, for anyone that hasn't been following it, basically this is a conversation that has been rumbling on about players wanting a higher percentage of revenues of the Grand Slams because the revenues of the Grand Slams themselves have been increasing. Um, but you know, as we've discussed, Charlie, I think there's a confusing element to this a few times that needs to be taken into account.

Should players be well paid? Yes, we're not saying they shouldn't be well paid. They are part of the attraction and they're a star part of the attraction, but business models of Grand Slams have diversified.

The Australian Open added a third week and that really was built, as Craig Tiley, the chief executive of Australian Open has said, as an entertainment product, basically an extra week of entertainment festival. It wasn't built around the development of the tennis product. So, Australian Open's record revenues that they had were not directly linked to a better pull or performance of the players. It was the Australian Open being a better business.

Then there's the other side which is it still has been going up but what we're really talking about — and the players seem to continually ask for — is a percentage of revenue. And my challenge has always been: well, should it be revenue or should it be part of the profit? Because the profit that the Grand Slam shakes off is the money that goes back into the system.

So for example, Wimbledon makes let's say about 60 million in profit I think and about 50-55 million goes to the LTA and tennis in this country to build better courts, better coaching, better facilities, etc. So, if you do end up taking more money, it has to come from somewhere. And the high likelihood is that that money is going to come from the places that need it the most. It's not going to come from a better ability to extract better deals with their vendors.

So, it's a challenging argument. It's a bit of the players pushing maybe a little bit too far from where they can actually achieve. But also, the most important thing is I just think it's a load of positioning that doesn't really do anything right now. Because if you're going to ban or boycott media or not even boycott it, restrict your media to 15 minutes at a showing because 15% is the currentish percentage that you get of Grand Slam revenues, it's a bit feeble.

大满贯营收增长其实是谁的功劳？

Yeah. The whole thing is what we might call in football "handbags." Um now, I've been very sort of keen to encourage the Grand Slam organizers to get their heads together and to get their narrative out there together and to hold a line on this together because otherwise it's a very slippery slope where this goes.

Fundamentally, if you track the increase in Grand Slam revenues, the increase in Grand Slam revenue comes from an era which was a fantastic era for men's and women's tennis. You had at the same time the Williams sisters plus a variety of other players who competed against them, but fundamentally the Williams sisters who were huge huge world stars on the women's side. And you had Federer, Nadal, and Djokovic on the men's side.

And those five players were global stars that transcended tennis and took tennis into areas where tennis had never been before. And that was over a long period of time. I mean, we're talking about a whole decade when you had both the Williams sisters on the women's side and the big three on the men's side. That's before we even get into the Andy Murrays of this world.

So effectively the Grand Slams have benefited from a decade of transcendental players — transcendent players who have been just hugely powerful for tennis. And off the back of that, audiences rose, commercial attention rose, and the Grand Slams themselves, as you've just said, got better at monetizing their own tournaments as well during the same period.

现役球员的"搭便车"逻辑

Now what you're seeing is a whole bunch of players coming along who are already benefiting massively from that entire period of growth. And what they're saying is: we deserve to get paid much more than Federer, Nadal, Djokovic, Serena Williams, Venus Williams and Co because because revenues are now up here.

Revenues being up here has nothing to do with you. Nothing. Zero. You're, you know, in the big scheme of things, you're a nobody. You're nothing like Serena Williams. No one's tuning in to see you. You are so fortunate to have come along at the tail end of an era when tennis went boom like that.

So for them to say that they now want to get even more having already massively benefited from all this, I think it's just absolute cods and I wouldn't touch a large bowl.

And also to you know the slams have increased. I mean, Wimbledon, they've been 19-20% increases on some of the pay packets. So, again, you know, I said if you lose in the first round last year in 2025, you got £66,000. If you lose this year in the first round, you got £80,000.

网球球员的现实收入水平

We went through when we last discussed this, which was about six weeks ago, Charlie, we went through what pay is like now in professional tennis. And broadly speaking, the world number one gets around 20 million a year and the world number 100 gets about a million a year, right?

So I don't think that there should be any crocodile tears being shed anywhere for anything that the tennis players are earning right now.

Now if they want to go full tube driver and they want to decide well yeah we might be really well paid but we we think we should be even better paid. Why? Because we're greedy. Right. If they want to go down that route, then as you say, what they have to do as the tube drivers do is they have to take the step of being willing to forego pay in the short term to put enough pressure on to then get even bigger pay in the medium term. But that will mean foregoing short-term money with no certainty that it will lead to longer-term reward.

And it would have to be the full collective. It would have to be the full collective. And realistically, Joel, as you said, there are players in there in the top 20 that would suddenly go, I have a chance to win a Grand Slam if I play this because all the key ones aren't playing. I have the chance to win three and a half million quid if I play this because all the top ones aren't playing. Why am I striking? I'm going to make more money out of potentially winning this than I am going and…

网球生涯太短，罢工代价太大

Taking it to a short career at the top, right? So, in other words, you know, it's one thing if you're a tube driver and you're like, well, I'm going to be a tube driver for the next 20-25 years. So if I can manage to get the pay hiked up higher, I'm going to benefit from that for a long long time to come.

Yeah.

Whereas in the tennis players case, in many of Well, in five years from now, I might be have an Achilles injury and not be playing at all. And yet I've got the opportunity now. Right here, right now, I'm in the top five. I'm in the top 10.

I noticed they couldn't even all get together to actually do their little media strike. There are various — No, no, they're not all doing it. I I'll come back next week with the ones who have refused to do it. And of course their reasons are well it's all very well but I've got commercial agreements with various sponsors and part of their stipulations are that I do a certain level of media appearances otherwise I'm not — so the whole thing is a bit of a farce.

网球治理的碎片化困境

If I was the Grand Slam the one little asterisk I would put is that I'd be fascinated to have heard Craig Tiley's view on this is why don't they speak with one voice?

Well you'll find out. I without any spoilers Craig Tiley is on the Business of Sport show next week. So he and we talk about that. And governance has always been the biggest problem in tennis. You have seven independent governing bodies. You have the four slams and then the ITF which is now World Tennis I think and then you have the ATP and the WTA.

And Charlie of course you know I ask that that is I think the biggest challenge that the sport faces and it is clearly his biggest challenge and frustration from a decision-making point of view. But the problem is that getting some form of collective thinking and collective approach when everyone has their own agendas and their own markets which they're really focused on, they've never been able to do so.

It's a fault on the executive side as well for their lack of ability to collectively operate under single governance and it's a fault on the players side to really understand when they've got it quite good and where they have fights that maybe they should enter and can win versus the ones that make them look a little bit silly.

I don't think it endears them to anyone and if there's a talk around developing the pyramid and the systems which is where the cash is really going from a lot of the Grand Slams then I think that's a hugely important part of the future of the sport anyway.

From a striking perspective, I think as a great union leader that you are I take that very seriously.

Yeah, if you're going to go on strike you got to do it properly. You've got to deliver that threat with absolute certainty, with absolute clarity, and you've got to be willing to take some pain. And you got to explain that to everyone. Look, we're going to get poorer in the short run to get richer in the medium run.

Whereas at the moment, it looks like they're having an element of what Boris Johnson used to call "cakeism" — which is I like having my cake and eating it. So, I want to carry on earning the massive money I already do earn. But then without having to sacrifice any of that, I want to put some pressure on them to get me to pay me even more. So, anyway, there we are.
