SAGAR: —if the principles are unpopular—I was thinking of the Mencius Moldbug—there was this version. I think there is a space for, no matter how unpopular, or in fact, precisely when arguments are unpopular or dangerous or controversial, there should be space to be able to make them pseudonymously. Publishers have shied away from wanting to do that anymore. But I think I would still want to draw this ethical distinction between anonymity in the sake of laying out a system as opposed to anonymity for the sake of exposing a particular policy or person. That's much more dicey. On the former kind, even today, I would be willing to allow some degree of anonymity. In Parmanand's time, you laugh, but when Parmanand wrote some of these things, people actually thought—the common accusation was Native Opinion was actually English critics dressed up as Brahmins.

RAJAGOPALAN: Brahmins.

一个 Catch-22：证明自己是"当地人"

SAGAR: It's the disgruntled Englishman who's angry with the viceroy, pretending to use Native Opinion to criticize. You'd actually say, "Actually, this is a native who's written this thing," and then they'd be like, "A native wrote this? That's even worse, because now that means that the natives are questioning us on [chuckles] matters of principle with this really sophisticated reasoning in English. Now I need to know even more who's doing this."

There was this funny catch-22 to using anonymity where you have to convince people, "I'm not actually English, even though I can write English this well," but also not give away too much because you're going to get in trouble for being this over-educated native who was causing trouble.

This did happen to Parmanand when he wrote his first work, English and Native Rule in India. It was a direct criticism of the viceroy. He lost a job because of it. He didn't mind. He said, "This is the way of the world." He cites the Gita, and he says, "I did my duty, and I didn't do it for a reward." But there is a real cost to getting found out.

承担代价才更值得被认真对待

RAJAGOPALAN: Yes, for me, I agree with you. I absolutely think there should be a lot of space for anonymity and pseudonymous work and things like that. I'm not so much in favor of closing up those spaces. I'm more reluctant to take it seriously myself.

SAGAR: Yes.

RAJAGOPALAN: To me, someone who actually reveals themselves and pays that cost, which sometimes is massive, would actually make me take that more seriously. So, I don't know if that's a very economist way of thinking about it where you must bear that cost to be taken seriously, but that's usually how I view them. But he's an absolute delight to read, no matter what.

SAGAR: Yes. No, no, no. I'm very much, as you said, I'm skeptical. I begin with being skeptical of anonymity, and I agree with you on the points you just made about there needing to be a willingness to bear a cost. I think the question is when those costs are astronomical. If you're making an argument in a society where you're crushed for—I want to be able to voice myself. I'm brave enough to put my name to the argument, but it's not just people will criticize me or criticize the argument or mock me, but they will try and crush me." As we say, cancel culture. The idea that you will lose your job, your family will be hunted, you'll get—

RAJAGOPALAN: Or your life sometimes.

SAGAR: Exactly. If you're dealing with that degree of assault for arguing about principles, then I can see the space.

RAJAGOPALAN: Fair enough.

SAGAR: Almost done with you. Yes. It's an attempt to try and really line out how much I agree with you and then carve out this one very particular set of circumstances where, as you'd expect, iconoclastic figures who are going to challenge prevailing opinion can really worry about outsize costs.

结语

RAJAGOPALAN: Yes. Again, thank you so much for, first of all, finding these people, then figuring out the manuscripts, going through them, modernizing them in the sense of corrections and references, and then writing the wonderful context that you give us to actually consume this work. It's really remarkable how you're able to do this over and over again, every couple of years, with a new unknown figure. Again, all my favorite gentlemen of the 19th century. Hopefully, some more women will make an appearance.

But thank you so much for doing this. The book is called The Birth of Indian Liberalism: Mama Parmanand's Letters to an Indian Raja. I hope everyone reads it and gets a lot out of it. Thank you for doing this, Rahul.

SAGAR: Shruti, thank you for doing what you do, bringing these ideas to a wide audience and asking all these fabulous questions. Thank you so much.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、Parmanand 的"匿名"不是谦逊，而是一种精密的知识论工程
大多数读者会把 Mama Parmanand 的隐身理解为维多利亚式的自我谦抑，或者印度传统里"无我"的道德姿态。但如果把他放在与"美国联邦党人文集"的 Publius、19 世纪英国匿名评论杂志（Edinburgh Review、Quarterly Review）同一条脉络里看，会发现一个更冷峻的判断：**他在设计一种"防止论证被人格劫持"的公共讨论协议**。
这里有个出人意料的现代回响——他的匿名逻辑，几乎精确对应了当代密码学里"零知识证明"的伦理直觉：让命题在不暴露命题人身份的前提下被检验其真值。Sagar 明确划出一条界线——**匿名可以用于"陈述原则"，但不能用于"指控事实"**——这条界线，恰好是今天 AI 时代所有 deepfake、匿名爆料、社交媒体人格分身问题的核心分水岭。也就是说，一个 1890 年代躺在床上、患帕金森的印度人，比大多数 21 世纪的平台政策制定者更早想清楚了"匿名的合法边界在哪里"。
## 二、印度自由主义之所以"看不像自由主义"，是因为它的敌人不是国家，而是国家的"

item.title: [Lex Clips] The rise of the Roman Empire in the East | Anthony Kaldellis and Lex Fridman
item.dc:creator: Lex Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、被忽略的惊人事实：古代大都市其实是"人口黑洞"

大多数人想到罗马、君士坦丁堡这类古典大都市，脑海中浮现的是繁荣、生育、扩张。但 Kaldellis 揭示的却是相反的画面——古代城市本质上是"死亡陷阱"（death traps），每年净损失 1–3% 的人口。

这意味着一个反直觉的结论：君士坦丁堡从 2.5 万暴涨到 50 万，不仅不是"自然增长的胜利"，反而是逆着一个巨大的死亡漏斗被强行灌满的。城市不是生育机器，而是消耗器官——它需要外省源源不断地"输血"才能维持存在，更别说扩张。

这个模型和现代生态学中的 "sink population"（汇型种群） 惊人相似：某些区域的物种死亡率高于出生率，只能靠外部迁入维持。古代大城市不是文明的花园，而是文明的黑洞——它们的光芒来自于持续吞噬周边省份的人口。

二、隐藏的杠杆机制：Constantine 发明了古代版的"锚定租户"策略

真正让我震惊的，是那段关于 2,500 个元老家庭的算术。表面上 Constantine 只是搬迁了政治阶层，但 Kaldellis 悄悄埋下了一个惊人的乘数：一个元老 ≠ 一个人，而是一整套包含仆人、奴隶、厨师、理发师、舞女的"人口套装"，罗马旧都的比例是 1:100，即便保守估算 1:30，也意味着光元老阶层就自动带来近 10 万人。

这几乎就是现代购物中心运营者所熟知的 "anchor tenant strategy"（锚店策略）：先把 Apple Store 或 H&M 请进来，其他小商户自动跟随。或者说得更近一点——就是科技公司总部搬迁引发的城市重塑（想想 Amazon HQ2、马斯克把 Tesla 搬到 Austin）。

Constantine 其实是历史上最早的、规模最大的"城市 growth hacker"。他用的是三重叠加杠杆： 1. 强制居住令（锚定顶层） 2. 附属人口自动跟随（30–100 倍乘数） 3. 免费面包（20 万人份的普罗底层"UBI"）

这套组合拳与现代迪拜、深圳、新加坡的建城逻辑几乎一模一样。

三、最容易被误读的一句话："新罗马从旧罗马嘴里偷粮食"

这句话听起来像修辞，但它其实揭示了一个帝国权力转移的隐秘物理机制：粮食流。

埃及的谷物原本流向罗马，Constantine 一纸命令改道到博斯普鲁斯——这不是政治宣言，而是卡路里的地缘政治。旧罗马之所以没饿死，是因为它被迫从北非和西西里重新组织补给。帝国权力的转移，本质上是营养流向的重定向。

这里有一个跨领域的深刻类比：现代的半导体供应链之争（台积电、荷兰 ASML、美国出口管制）本质上是同一套逻辑的重演——控制关键物质流向，就等于隐性迁都。你不需要宣布首都搬家；你只需要让"面包"（今天叫"芯片"）改道，权力中心自然就会重新长出来。

甚至可以说：任何一个真正意义上的"新首都"，都是通过截流旧首都的物质基础而诞生的。华盛顿之于费城，北京之于南京，巴西利亚之于里约……凡是不切断补给的迁都，最终都会失败。Constantine 明白这个秘密——他不只是搬走了政府，他搬走了埃及的粮船。

一个额外的意外角度：Kaldellis 说人口迁移"在史料中几乎看不见，只能推断"。这让我想到天体物理学中的暗物质——你无法直接观察它，但整个星系旋转曲线告诉你它必须存在。古代人口史，本质上是一门暗物质学：真正塑造历史的，恰恰是那些没有留下名字的、被数字倒推出来的沉默大多数。

📝 转录文本

引子：两个世纪从 2.5 万到 50 万，如何做到？

Lex： To put a bow tie in Constantinople, it went from a very low population of maybe 25,000 people to 500,000 people in just two centuries. So, can you explain how that happened?

Kaldellis： Well, it didn't happen through just demographic growth and reproduction in the city itself. That rate of growth is impossible for an ancient city. So, when I say 20, 25,000, that's possibly what the ancient city of Byzantium had when Constantine decided to found Constantinople. And it grew to a city of a half a million in two centuries.

That can only have been done in one way — and that is emigration from the provinces. So, people must have been moving there in larger numbers. Which is a movement that we can't see very clearly in the sources, but we have to infer it and then try to explain it.

古代城市：被忽视的"死亡陷阱"

So, also there's another factor, which is that ancient cities we now know — or suspect — are what some historians call "death traps." In other words, the density of disease is such that ancient cities probably are losing something like 1, 2, possibly 3% of their population per year. So, just in order to stay with a stable population, they have to import people. Right?

Now, they're not like 17th century London. They're not that bad.

Lex： Does diseases and plagues seem to be prevalent?

Kaldellis： Oh, yeah. Now, you know, Roman cities have some corrections for these sorts of things — like the way they distribute the water through the city. Anyway, they weren't as bad as they could have been. But, there's that.

So, not only to keep the population steady, but to expand it so dramatically, means that a lot of people are coming in from the provinces — mostly the Greek-speaking provinces. So, you know, Greece, Asia Minor, and some of the major cities in the East that are Greek-speaking.

谁在移民？——从元老阶层做算术

And I started to wondering like, well, how did this happen? Like, who are these people, for one thing?

And then I did some simple calculations. So, I mentioned earlier that we have something like 2,500 senators who are required initially to reside in Constantinople. Well, senators don't live in bachelor pads.

Lex： [gasps]

Kaldellis： Senators have manners, they have household staff, right? In Rome at the time, there's a calculation that a senatorial household was something like 100 people. Just servants, slaves, dancing girls, cooks, barbers, whatever, right?

So, let's assume sort of conservatively that in Constantinople, it's only 30 people — which it might be low. Right? So, if you do the math, you're starting to get something like — I mean, closer to 100,000 people. Right? And that's just by bringing in senators. They come with people.

So, I think this was the nucleus of the population.

免费面包：另一个人口引力场

Now, then Constantine also makes provision for possibly up to like 200,000 people to receive the grain dole, the bread. So, that would have incentivized even more people to travel there because there are —

Lex： free bread.

Kaldellis： Yes. And you can't live on that alone, but it is — it's something.

Lex： incentive.

从旧罗马嘴里"偷"粮食

Lex： Yeah. Is it true that he diverted resources from Rome to Constantinople?

Kaldellis： Absolutely. Oh, yeah. That grain — that's coming from Egypt. So, New Rome is literally stealing food from the mouth of Old Rome.

Lex： Yeah.

Kaldellis： Now, Old Rome doesn't starve because they divert other — like from North Africa and Sicily, you can feed Rome. Right?

Lex： But it sends a strong signal to the people living in Old Rome that long-term, [they] should probably move.

Kaldellis： Some people do move. So, we know of some senators who moved from Rome. It sends a signal about the priorities of the state. Like, they're investing much more in the East, and that the East would be regarded as an equal, parallel Roman world.

观看原视频 →

大多数人想到罗马、君士坦丁堡这类古典大都市，脑海中浮现的是繁荣、生育、扩张。但 Kaldellis 揭示的却是相反的画面——**古代城市本质上是"死亡陷阱"（death traps），每年净损失 1–3% 的人口**。

item.title: [Lex Clips] Why Christianity succeeded and spread throughout the world | Anthony Kaldellis and Lex Fridman
item.dc:creator: Lex Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、被忽视的"接口设计"：基督教其实是一套多态协议

大多数人把基督教的成功归结为"信仰的力量"或"上帝的恩典"，但 Kaldellis 的描述里藏着一个更冷峻的事实——基督教成功的秘诀，是它像一套面向不同人群的"多态接口"。给知识分子的是准哲学的神学；给市民的是取代旧节庆的公共游行；给渴望个人救赎的是入教仪式与来世承诺；给士兵的是对三位一体的宣誓；给宫廷的是权力的话语。

这里让人意外的是：这不是一个"宗教"，这是一个操作系统。它有内核（教义），有用户态（仪式），有 API（可以嫁接到军队、法庭、市集），还有插件生态（各地圣人崇拜、地方节日）。古代多神教反而更像"单一用途工具"——你去雅典娜神庙求智慧，去阿斯克勒庇俄斯神庙求病愈，功能分散、耦合度低、但也无法整合。基督教赢在架构，不在教义本身。

二、"排他性即分裂性，也即凝聚力"——一条被现代平台复刻的悖论

Kaldellis 指出的那个悖论极其锋利：排他性主张（我真你假）同时最大化了两件事——内部凝聚力与对外冲突强度。这不是宗教独有的现象。

跨领域看一眼你会震惊： - 现代社交平台（Twitter/X、Reddit 子版）的部落化机制完全同构——越排他的社群，成员忠诚度越高，同时越易引发外部战争。 - 开源许可证之争（GPL vs BSD 阵营）也是这个模式的技术翻版——排他性条款制造了"我们 vs 他们"的清晰身份。 - 加密货币社区（比特币最大化主义 vs 其他链）几乎逐字复现了 4 世纪的异端论战：正统派、修正派、被贴上"骗局"标签的分裂派。

真正让人后背发凉的洞察是：任何采用"排他性真理主张"的系统，都会自动获得同一副面孔——强凝聚 + 高冲突 + 不断内部分裂的教义演化。这不是文化选择，这是信息系统的必然拓扑结构。基督教只是第一个大规模跑通这个协议的组织。

三、"追溯性正统"：一个被低估的社会学机制

对话末尾那一段最容易被读者略过，但它其实是全片最尖利的一击——"真理"往往是斗争结束后才被追认的。异端不是先存在、后被打倒；而是在打倒过程中，"正统"才被回溯地建构出来。那些倒霉的神学家很多本来只是老实复述大家都以为的信念，结果被亚历山大的某个人指着鼻子说"这是异端"，然后他们才莫名其妙地成为了"从来就错的那一方"。

这个机制的跨领域回响令人不安： - 科学史：库恩早就指出，"范式"的确立是回溯性的——失败者被追认为"前科学"。 - 软件工程：任何 API 的"正确用法"其实是几轮 bug 报告和 stackoverflow 骂战之后才凝固下来的，早期用户不知道自己"错了"。 - 企业史：破产的公司回过头看总是"战略错误"，但当时它们的决策与幸存者几乎相同。

最反直觉的观察是：一个传统越是宣称"我们从来如此"，就越说明它经历过剧烈的追溯性重写。基督教之所以能在 300 年里以惊人速度演化，恰恰是因为它拥有一个高效的"追溯性正统生成器"——每一次异端审判，不是在保卫真理，而是在制造真理。从这个角度看，Constantine 没能预见的不是"分裂"，而是他启动了一个自我编辑、自我进化、以冲突为动力的活系统。他以为自己在给帝国装订一部圣典，实际上他打开了一个永不停机的意识形态编译器。

📝 转录文本

Why did Christianity spread so successfully?

Lex: What explains uh the success of Christianity after Constantine? Why why why was it as a technology so successful at spreading throughout the empire?

Kaldellis: Christianity developed in a number of ways. So, it acquired certain number of forms that were capable of appealing to different constituencies.

In other words, there was um a an intellectual side to it — the the sort of theology, the almost the quasi-philosophical or engagement with philosophical schools that could draw in, let's say, intellectuals.

Um, there is a a public ceremony and ritual aspect where you can organize communities in cities to have processions or like have whole all these festivals that take the place of the ancient ones.

You have a um kind of — not It's not secretive, but it's kind of this initiatory aspect where there's this baptism and you you learn a a sort of creed and you're initiated into the religion with the prospect of a personal afterlife, right? And that appeals to other kinds of people.

Um, there's also ways in which it um it integrated itself into the the court and the language of power. Um also in the army, right? Soldiers now swear an oath to the Trinity, right?

So, it takes all of these different forms that enable it to position itself, you know, here and there and, you know, in the streets, in the court, in the armies, in the churches, in the in texts and books. And so, it has this kind of manifold diversity, right? That makes it very adaptable.

The paradox: unifying and divisive at once

Lex: So, one of the things you mentioned that uh uh Constantine did not anticipate, did not realize that Christianity creates divisions between various groups.

Kaldellis: So, in part, this is happens when you have religions that make exclusive claims, right? Like my beliefs are true and yours are by definition false. Not Not just wrong, but false and even kind of evil. Like maybe Satan put them in your mind to lead you astray, right? And this is inherently divisive. Like it is.

So, there's this paradox where Christianity, in contrast to say the ancient religions, is both far more polarizing, but also far more powerful as a unifying force at the same time, right?

So, ancient religions tended not to produce this kind of conflict or identities, right? Or or wars or these kinds of things. And they could under certain circumstances, but for the most part, they were not drivers of history in this kind of way.

But when you have exclusive claims, then suddenly these very communities of that are sort of consolidated and have this very strong identity, um but you also alienate other groups that you then are in conflict with.

Um so, this is why I — I for one — can't come down on whether, you know, Christianity is more of a unifying force or a divisive force in the history of the Eastern Empire because it's it's both.

Is division a feature or a bug?

Lex: And also trying to figure out whether the unifying or the divisive aspects are a feature or a bug. Maybe some of the divisive elements is a way for society internally to try to, much like the civil wars, to try to figure itself out. So, sometimes tension and conflict is a good mechanism for figuring out what do the people want.

Kaldellis: Or what's the truth?

Lex: Or what's the truth?

Truth as retrospective construction

Kaldellis: Yeah. Um so, the story that it likes to tell itself is that there's this like this one original truth, and the hardcore of true believers is defending that truth at every moment against all of these evil heretics who are coming in and trying to corrupt it.

In reality, what happens is that this truth is something that's evolving in the course of the controversies. Um and like often they don't know that they — you know — believe that until in retrospect once they've had the fight and the winner has this position. Oh, now that turns out to have been the position we always held, right?

Um and this happens again and again and again.

You know, a lot of the time these Christian theologians who get into so much trouble and get branded as heretics and sometimes get sent into exile — they didn't mean any harm. They often just were stating what they thought everybody believed and then when they blurted out, someone is like — often Alexandria — is like: what? That's heresy.

And then now you're — now you're in trouble. Now you're getting into this fight and you might win, you might lose. But like a lot of them were kind of sort of well-intentioned in that way, or they had no intention of starting a fight.

But there's always someone who wants to start a fight.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、被忽视的"接口设计"：基督教其实是一套多态协议
大多数人把基督教的成功归结为"信仰的力量"或"上帝的恩典"，但 Kaldellis 的描述里藏着一个更冷峻的事实——**基督教成功的秘诀，是它像一套面向不同人群的"多态接口"**。给知识分子的是准哲学的神学；给市民的是取代旧节庆的公共游行；给渴望个人救赎的是入教仪式与来世承诺；给士兵的是对三位一体的宣誓；给宫廷的是权力的话语。
这里让人意外的是：**这不是一个"宗教"，这是一个操作系统**。它有内核（教义），有用户态（仪式），有 API（可以嫁接到军队、法庭、市集），还有插件生态（各地圣人崇拜、地方节日）。古代多神教反而更像"单一用途工具"——你去雅典娜神庙求智慧，去阿斯克勒庇俄斯神庙求病愈，功能分散、耦合度低、但也无法整合。基督教赢在**架构**，不在教义本身。
## 二、"排他性即分裂性，也即凝聚力"——一条被现代平台复刻的悖论
Kaldellis 指出的那个悖论极其锋利：**排他性主张（我真你假）同时最大化了两件事——内部凝聚力与对外冲突强度**。这不是宗教独有的现象。
跨领域看一眼你会震惊： - **现代社交平

item.title: [SemiAnalysis Weekly] Ep. 017 - DeepSeek V4 and Huawei Ascend NPU Performance (InferenceX)
item.dc:creator: SemiAnalysis Weekly
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、被忽视的真相：InferenceX 的"日 0"其实是一场信息不对称的军备竞赛

大多数人以为"开源模型发布日"是一个公平的起跑线——权重放出，大家一起冲。但转录里藏着一个关键信息：vLLM 和 SGLang 拿到了 NDA 下的预览权限，而 NVIDIA 却没有。这颠覆了一个直觉：并不是"硬件厂商 → 框架层 → 用户"的线性优化链，而是开源运行时反而在"信息层级"上高于闭源芯片巨头。

这是一种罕见的"逆向权力结构"：DeepSeek 用 NDA 分发的不是权重，而是注意力时间。谁先看到模型架构，谁就能提前 30 天进入 Day 0 竞技场。这也解释了为什么华为能在发布当天就有优雅的 profile——他们不是快，而是在时钟启动前就已经在跑了。

二、跨领域连接：MegaKernel 和"福特流水线的反向工程"

Kimbo 描述 MegaKernel "打破典型的 kernel 边界，让寄存器一空出来就被下一个 kernel 使用"——这听起来像是福特流水线的反面。福特把一个大任务切成小工位来提高吞吐；MegaKernel 却把一堆小工位重新熔铸回一个连续动作，因为在 GPU 这种物理层面，"切分带来的调度开销"已经比"计算本身"更贵。

这是所有成熟工业体系走到极致时都会出现的现象：当基础设施足够便宜、协调成本反而变成主要瓶颈时，模块化会开始逆转。同样的模式出现在：微服务架构走向单体（Shopify、Amazon Prime Video 都回退过）、汽车工业从多供应商走向垂直整合（特斯拉一体压铸）、甚至生物学上真核细胞将线粒体"融合进"自身。DeepSeek 的 MegaKernel 不是技术创新，而是 GPU 生态成熟到出现"去模块化拐点"的信号。

再往深一层：这也解释了为什么 MegaKernel 在大 batch 训练里反而不划算——那时通信/计算已充分重叠，模块边界不再是瓶颈。所以 MegaKernel 天然是推理时代的产物，不是训练时代的。它是"推理经济"崛起的技术痕迹。

三、意外观察：vLLM 与 SGLang 的"分裂"其实是模型市场健康的隐性指标

表面上看，vLLM 和 SGLang 出身同一个伯克利实验室、功能高度重叠、互相抄袭——按经济学第一直觉，它们应该合并或一方消亡。但它们没有。为什么？

Jordan 无意间说出了关键：下游依赖（Slime、VeRL、Prime RL、云推理商）需要"两个选项"来对冲供应商权力。这跟 OpenAI/Anthropic 的分裂是同构现象——开源生态自发地生成"最小竞争数 = 2"的结构，因为一旦收敛到一家，谈判权就归零。

这里有一个反直觉的结论：如果你看到某个开源赛道只剩一家，那不是效率的胜利，而是生态的衰败信号（因为竞争者已经放弃）。健康的开源赛道会持续维持"2~3 家旗鼓相当且互相抄袭"的状态——这不是浪费，是生态的免疫系统。

四、隐藏的地缘技术信号：华为 CANN 的"周会"策略

Bryan 一句轻描淡写的"华为的 CANN 团队有 weekly calls，你可以直接和工程师聊"——这可能是整期播客最被低估的信号。

对比 NVIDIA 的 TensorRT-LLM 和 AMD 的 AiTer：闭源、精英化、通过合作伙伴关系分发优化。华为选择的是完全相反的路径：代码开源在 Gitcode、文档齐全、每周和外部开发者开会。这不是"跟随者姿态"，这是"用开放性作为破局武器"——就像 Linux 当年对 Unix、Android 对 iOS。

真正让人意外的是：这套策略在中国国内可能是被迫的（无法用 CUDA 生态），但输出到全球时反而变成了竞争优势。当 DeepSeek V4 能在华为芯片上以接近 SOTA 的效率运行、且优化经验完全开源可复制时，其他"第三方 AI 芯片"（Cerebras、Groq、Tenstorrent 等）会突然发现自己面对的不是 NVIDIA，而是一个愿意公开一切换取生态位的对手。这才是华为最凶的一招。

📝 转录文本

Ep. 017 - DeepSeek V4 与华为昇腾 NPU 性能（InferenceX）

开场：本期主题

Hello everyone. Welcome back to SemiAnalysis Weekly. On this week, we've got Cam, Kimbo, and Bryan and we are gonna talk about everything InferenceX.

The latest InferenceX article included a whole review about DeepSeek V4, and what it takes to go from like day zero performance to day 43 performance — meaning how much do these guys grind over the month?

So I'm really excited to dig into some, some topics here. Uh, we're gonna talk about the model itself, the implementation, how it's running on different hardware, some of the nice vLLM versus SGLang Twitter drama that's been going on, an agentic benchmark that we're working on, and, and yeah, lots of fun stuff.

So guys, welcome to the show. Excited for this one. — Super excited to get started. Let's go. — Nice to be here.

一、DeepSeek V4 与 V3 的架构差异

Awesome. All right. Kimbo, let's, let's start with you. I think um, the the burning question maybe for the general audience here is: what's different going from DeepSeek V3 to V4? And when you describe what's different, what impact does that have on running InferenceX? In other words, what does day zero support actually mean when it comes to a new model architecture?

Yeah, so I think the headline changes or the features of V4 compared to V3 and R1 would be the 1 million context length.

稀疏注意力：CSA 与 HCA

This ability, in order to achieve or to get to a million context length, DeepSeek did like very aggressive innovations on the attention mechanism. That's like the first thing. And the second thing is the Mega MoE to like speed up things — to speed up the expert FFN computations.

First, the attention mechanism: DeepSeek V4 comes with two variants of sparse attention. One is the compressed sparse attention and heavily compressed attention. Both of them are built on top of the previous version — DeepSeek's sparse attention that came out with 3.2.

The idea is that by sparsifying your attention between query and the key values, you could reduce the memory reads, so that allows you to do 1 million context length without exploding your memory requirements.

Both of them — heavily compressed attention and compressed sparse attention — in addition to that, have like one additional embedding compressor, so that further decreases the size of the KV cache entry. And then after that, they both incorporate some sort of sliding window, which then further decreases the KV cache usage.

Combined with all this, I think DeepSeek is quoting like a very — I think around 100, around that order — 100x reduction of the KV cache usage compared to a standard MLA model. And yeah, that's like the headline feature of DeepSeek V4.

Yeah, yeah. And it's also a different architecture in terms of like more total parameters, less active parameters when compared to V3. So literally a like a different model, not just an update to the weights.

二、Day 0 支持背后的工程冲刺

Bryan, maybe kick into you. What does this mean when it comes to actually getting support from the various InferenceX runtimes, and what work is required to actually get performance results out of a system on day zero when the model's released?

Yeah, great question. There were a lot of changes. Not just, like as you say, not just the weights, but the sizes of certain things as well. For example, some hidden sizes and dimensions.

So it's not just adding support for like new attention mechanisms, attention blocks. It might also be like changing or tuning certain things that the community thought was constant. Like in this case, was the... NVIDIA, I guess — it was the MHC dimension. DeepSeek, for the previous times, has always released MHC with... what was it? I think half of what Pro used. So V4 Flash and the previous iterations of DeepSeek V3 all used the same hidden dimension. Sorry, not hidden dimension — MHC dimension. But Pro used the new one, which caused a hiccup for Nvidia on day 0.

Yeah, but for the rest of the guys, I guess everyone adopted quite well.

NDA 与信息不对称

On day 0 — it's worth mentioning, right — that some companies like vLLM and SGLang had like early access under NDA. But companies like Nvidia did not. So that made it more difficult to implement on day zero.

Yeah, so I guess they had a head start with implementation. That's why there wasn't as many hiccups.

华为公开的优化清单

But I don't remember — I don't know if you guys remember seeing it — but there was a Twitter, on Twitter there's this video circulating of like Huawei talking about the optimizations. Although it was in Chinese, but someone gave a translation, and there were very good optimizations talked about during Day Zero. If you just follow what Huawei did, I think you'll get a good performance if you're optimizing from day zero.

Yeah, but they talked about kernel fusions, they talked about index optimizations. Yeah, it's really good guide, I guess, for day zero optimization.

三、架构是否偏向 GPU？

Wait, one question I had — maybe Kimbo can answer this. I've been reading a lot how basically this architecture is like extremely kind of tailored to GPU, GPGPU-like architecture rather than things like, you know, things based on systolic arrays like TPU. Is there a reason for that? Have you heard that as well? Or just kind of curious?

I think solely in terms of what you said — systolic array — I don't think so, because systolic array is essentially like a hardware architecture specialized for a matrix multiplication, right? And GPUs literally have tensor cores, which is also specialized for matmuls. Models do matmuls, so I don't see any very special thing that V4 is specializing for.

But I guess what you might see would probably be related to Mega MoE. That is very GPU — tailored to GPUs because of the way that the kernel launches and everything. Is that what you're seeing?

I'm actually — I don't know. I probably, yeah.

四、MegaKernel：打破 kernel 边界

Speaking of Mega MoE, what is Mega MoE actually, Kimbo? Yeah — so, oh, just that it's a huge fused kernel, but just curious what it is.

Yeah, fused kernel — I think fused kernel is one thing. The whole concept of MegaKernel is that you're breaking the kernel — the typical kernel boundaries.

与 kernel fusion / compiler 优化的对比

So, compared to kernel fusion, which is either manually written by kernel engineers where they just go through the math and they see what can or can be merged together — and I compared that and also like compiler optimization where they remove the kernel launches — MegaKernel is more about breaking the kernel boundaries that are not typical.

For example... sorry, I suddenly can't remember which ones, but some concrete examples of what can be broken is that the register usage. So you can imagine that in a typical kernel, what happens is that you launch a kernel, you do computation — in a Von Neumann architecture, you would load data into the register, you do computations in a register, and then at some point, you store it back to HBM.

So what MegaKernel does is that they fuse different kernels so that ah, they could remove the round trip between data movement between registers and the HBM — while also, when a register is free, they could automatically do the next operations of the future kernels. So instead of waiting for a whole kernel to complete...

通信与计算的极致融合

And for Mega MoE — the case for Mega MoE is that in addition to that, they also merged the compute and communication, computation and communication overlap thing. So it's all merged into one kernel, and then they could do like very aggressive overlapping.

MegaKernel 的代价

So what's the downside then of like fusing kernels? It seems like it just fills everything, right?

Yeah, in the extreme case where we see like the Hazy Research, they literally fuse a whole model like a LLaMA 7B, LLaMA 8B. And I would say that the downside, or I guess the reason that is preventing people from doing that, is that it's a lot of engineering work.

In principle, so in addition to it's a lot of engineering work, you have to justify it with something, right? So the thing is that Mega MoE is about aggressively reducing the latency. And in the large batch scenario or in a training scenario, it kind of doesn't make sense — or there is a limit to where it stops making sense.

So let's say if you're doing a training with a super large batch, like a couple hundred million tokens per batch, your computation and communication is sufficiently overlapped. Your kernel launch time is not the bottleneck. In that case, it doesn't make sense to do Mega MoEs.

And the other downside would be, I guess, another difficulty for MegaKernels is that it's because of doing the aggressive resource allocation on the fly — it creates a lot of memory pressure. So I imagine that would be, that'll require a lot of uh, work on managing like the GPUs, I guess, so that they don't — I guess some physics related, maybe the overheat or some related issues, which would in turn affect the performance.

五、开源 MegaKernel 与硬件验证

Makes sense, makes sense.

Okay, I want to jump into — throw something on screen here, just uh, little screenshot from the V4 paper where it explains the open source MegaKernel. And it specifically says two things: one is that it's been validated on NVIDIA GPUs, nothing else, and Huawei Ascend NPUs.

Obviously, we've seen the open source CUDA-based megakernel called Mega MoE, which then these InferenceX serving runtimes such as vLLM and SGLang and uh TensorRT can implement for themselves. And the performance improvement claim is somewhere between 1.5 and 1.73 times faster. So clearly they've realized the benefits of this engineering work by doing this.

六、Day 0 到 Day 43：性能是怎么爬上去的

Um, but I wanna maybe share a second thing on screen, which is that like the open source community — just because the code is out there — doesn't necessarily have the opportunity to benefit just from day one.

And so what we've been tracking really — and Bryan, maybe you can take us through this like experience in detail — is how quickly these configs can come online. In other words, how quickly can a given InferenceX serving runtime actually support things?

And I thought this little GIF video from the article was quite nice in terms of how it shows the progress over time as we count up the days and we start to see more hardware being supported as the B200, B300, GB200, GB300 come online, the MI355 from NVIDIA, and then you can start to see the performance improve, right?

For people watching, like these lines going from the left to the right means either more throughput or lower latency per user, or you know, higher throughput per user in terms of interactivity.

And um, yeah, maybe you can take me through like — okay, day zero, there's two or three different hardware platforms supported and then a month later everything's supported, but in that whole time a lot of performance improvements happen. What are like some examples of some performance improvements that were just kinda like dropped overnight and then were a big win, where people can suddenly, I don't know, twenty percent more throughput — which means twenty percent more users or twenty percent more profitability for the existing users or or whatever, you know, for a given InferenceX endpoint provider.

致谢前端工程师

Uh, wait, before I move on to that — huge shout out to our front-end engineer, Alec, for making that video feature. It is very cool and shows you very nicely how improvements were made.

AMD 的追赶案例

Yeah, and so moving on to the question — I guess one thing that we can look at to look at these improvements is AMD. Like as the paper said earlier, a lot the stuff optimized already for Nvidia GPUs — and like because of how big the CUDA community is and how many people use CUDA, there was huge incentives for SGLang and vLLM in the limited time they had with the model to prioritize Nvidia.

So AMD catching up is actually an interesting look at like real optimization from getting to work on day zero. So maybe our first step would be like getting the quantized versions to work.

AMD on day zero had FP8 only. There were no native FP4 support, which was quite unfortunate. And actually very large gains came from getting it to work in FP4 — of course, due to the calculation being faster and lesser memory needs to be transferred.

Actually we have a very nice plot on InferenceX if you may bring it up, Jordan. Thanks. On the AMD improvements. Yeah — but the biggest jump was converting a lot of the kernels into AiTer instead of using Torch fallbacks.

Yeah, so you can see the SGLang graph actually evolving the most. Oh, there's a breakdown of optimizations as well I think below in our image. It's a pretty messy image with like orange text in ExcaliDraw. Sorry.

Yeah, so there were very big improvements just from changing kernels from torch fallback into either AiTer or etc. So it's very cool to see like the impacts these individual improvements have — and of course not just like a humongous step, it's always step by step. You optimize one part and you optimize another. Yeah, but very cool stuff.

致敬工程师

I wanna like take a second to just talk about how cool this is and also shout out all the AMD engineers and Nvidia engineers that we work with, because this is all them. Like we contribute a few things to upstream, like kernel libraries and stuff, but I mean we're — we're just a team of three people and this is all the engineers.

Um, but I think this is something that's so cool about InferenceX, and this was the whole thesis of it, the project, like when we started it: you know, a lot of things online, a lot of benchmarks, you just see like the end product in terms of performance, but really it's like a lot of hard work, tiny iterations, increasing throughput by five percent at a time, and that eventually compounds to sort of, you know, get performant. And like you can see that here, right?

So whenever new models release, like MiniMax M3 just released, we're doing the same thing. And over the course of a month or two, you're gonna see like — you know, you start out with PyTorch, you know, just Torch native fallback, and then you kind of use custom kernels, and then you make the custom kernels even better, and then you just keep making them better and better and pushing the frontier forward. So yeah, this is really cool to see.

系统复杂度的美感

I also find it fascinating when I look at these things and you dig in to try to understand what these optimizations are. Like every single one of them, if you tease out just that one optimization, like if you look at the tuned parallelism point, or if you look at the fuse KV compressed tile laying point — it might seem simple in isolation and in some cases maybe obvious that people should have been doing it.

But if you try to reason about the entire system all at once — where there's all of these improvements always constantly happening — it becomes like incredibly complicated. And that's really what's so impressive about watching these engineering teams work is like the ability to take all of these individual optimizations and then build them all together into one thing, which is just like better performance or more efficiency. Super cool.

七、vLLM vs SGLang：竞争还是内耗？

Okay, the next thing that this obviously leads to is the bit of the comparison between the different runtimes. You guys are big on avoiding the war between vLLM and SGLang, the Twitter drama drama. Can you talk about the reason for this and why in some ways having the competition between different open source InferenceX engines, as well as vendor specific libraries, is actually driving a lot of innovation and improving things — instead of just like waiting for somebody to be nice enough to share what they've learned. Many of them are forced to innovate really fast in order to stay ahead.

Yeah, I mean we tend to stray away from comparing vLLM to SGLang on InferenceX specifically, just because mainly we didn't find it beneficial necessarily to have — like make it a competition. We more so just wanted to showcase each one in isolation.

But, you know, on the other hand, it does kind of create some competitive spirit, which makes both sides move faster — which can also be a problem because we only have limited compute to run things. So when you double the amount of submissions on a certain model, it's kinda like, you know, a lot of CI time.

But yeah, we've seen great improvements by both vLLM and SGLang and I think a little bit of competition is good.

八、闭源 vs 开源运行时

And what about the vendor specific libraries? I'm — we can talk about like some of the other chips, but maybe the most open or easy for us to run is NVIDIA and AMD, because we have NVIDIA and AMD GPUs. Talk about Huawei in a second.

Both of them have closed source proprietary libraries in the form of like TensorRT with Dynamo for NVIDIA, and then ATOM, AiTer, Mori — all the libraries from AMD that make it up. Um, but they're not, you know, I guess — the vendor specific libraries that are closed source are not always the best performance and don't always allow you the most customization.

So like what's the maybe open source versus closed source dynamic that's going on when you guys have to try these things out?

The way I think about it is TRT-LLM, you know, like ATOM the AiTer optimized engine for ROCm — they're like hyper, hyper specific to certain uh, AMD and NVIDIA architectures that will run really fast, but they're not necessarily like completely portable and not always a hundred percent open source.

Whereas vLLM and SGLang, you know, you can fork it and go start like your own Baseten, Together, whatever, Fireworks. You know, it's very like user friendly and it has, you know, API specifications and such.

So that's kind of how I look at it, but I don't know. Maybe Bryan and Kimbo have different opinions.

ATOM 与 SGLang：市场与社区

I mean, in my opinion, it's just more competition. I mean, on our benchmark, we also have a disclaimer saying that ATOM has no customers. Yeah, but that's the other problem. Your market just isn't — or rather isn't as big as Nvidia's.

But unless your product is really, really much better — like, Mori is a lot better than the previous of RCCL. Yeah, but ATOM, the development is okay. Development is fast. AMD is putting support into it, which you'd like to see. But yeah, SGLang is just — there's much more help from the open source community. A kernel development, which is really good. And ATOM just needs that gap, I guess. And who knows, maybe it'll take some time for that gap to close. And ATOM gets more accepted by the community, I guess. I mean, they have like twenty less engineers, so —

九、为什么 vLLM 和 SGLang 没有合并？

It's kinda a question for you. Like one of the things that I've been asked before and I've seen on Twitter is basically like, why SGLang and vLLM? Like why wouldn't SGLang just copy all the good parts of vLLM and vice versa? Whereas, you know, it's to the point where they basically just converge to the same thing.

I mean, we're talking about InferenceX, right? We're talking about being one percent faster or something, right? And like everything is kind of being worked out out in the open.

So it doesn't have to be you, Kimbo, but like maybe Jordan and Bryan — like why do you think it's a natural thing that there are basically precisely two, maybe like a few other open runtimes? And they're so like head to head and we're not necessarily converging on like one InferenceX engine.

一段"实验室内战"

I mean, it is kind of an interesting topic. Like based on my understanding — so first of all, objectively speaking, they are, both of them came from the same, literally the same Berkeley lab. They are lab mates. So that's the thing. So they started with a similar idea in the same lab. They started the project.

And apparently something happened between them. Some, like the main maintainers, the major project developers — they have some problems between them, which I don't — I literally, I honestly don't know what happened. And then they just are parting ways and having very similar ideas.

I think in principle, they are doing the same things and throughout the development, throughout these years, they are, like you said, copying features from each other. I'm sure you probably heard from our collaborators a lot that one — the other side is copying, copy-pasting our code and to support this feature. I've heard both sides.

So that's — there's that. And in the end, my impression is that just uh, one of them is very passionate and very competitive in trying to get the other one out. And they've got their own major market share and supporters.

OpenAI vs Anthropic 的类比

Maybe the easiest analogy is: like why does OpenAI and Anthropic exist? Like they were all one company and then Dario was working on safety stuff and something happened and then he left, and now they really drive each other in a way that I think is benefiting the ecosystem because they both land counter punches and then we benefit as they improve quality and drive prices lower.

Um, maybe R competition is good. It's interesting that, you know, they're very similar, but they have like uh —

下游生态锁定

I just want to say one other thing though, which is that I don't think it's completely a historical artifact the way Kimbo describes it. I think there's obviously stuff that's downstream of these runtimes now, whether it be customers or other libraries that depend on them.

And so having two different providers allows you to make a decision about who's gonna prioritize your features, who's gonna merge your stuff, where you're gonna get support from, how you're gonna work with vendors. Because they're just like one point and there's stuff that's upstream of them, like the vendor specific libraries in some cases, or like the hardware, literally CI testing, InferenceX stuff.

And then there's stuff downstream, which is like, you know, RL libraries — like you look at Slime or you look at VeRL or you look at Prime RL and stuff. Like if there's features that they want to see from the runtime, they need to make a choice and go with the ones that are gonna support them.

And there's many customers, like you mentioned, the InferenceX endpoint serving providers, as well as the labs. You know, we know companies like Microsoft are using these technologies. They don't have their own stuff in-house. Uh, Poolside talked about that in their paper, GLM — the whole thing's SGLang, right?

So a lot of people depend on this stuff right now. And I think that's like — it's at least good to have two options to buy from in open source if you're somebody that's making a library and you need support from somebody. And you can have multi, you know, runtime backend support if you really need to, if you're really not getting what you want from the community and you need to go somewhere else.

十、华为登场：从"论文一行字"到真实性能

The other, okay — not to take this in too far a direction, but I really, really want to hear about Huawei. And this is making me think a lot about proprietary runtimes and software experience and like what this NPU chip is gonna look like when there's a lot more users, and it goes from what we thought was one or two lines in a paper saying, "Yeah, we have Huawei support," and then no real proof. But Bryan, man, there's real proof now that DeepSeek is running on Huawei.

Can you take us through what you've found and the analysis that you did on the numbers that they shared?

DeepSeek 的发布延迟

Yeah, so actually before that, I'm gonna talk about like the DeepSeek delays which are — there were a lot of rumors on X because DeepSeek was like planned to release on Chinese New Year and then it got pushed back and back and back and every time was like it's gonna drop this weekend, never mind it's this weekend.

Yeah, so there were conflicting like uh, reasons — some people were like talking about they're actually trying to get a model working on Huawei. That's why they were delaying it and optimizing it first before releasing it. And of course, there's the other explanation of like, they just want that better evaluation results.

华为团队公布的 profile

But regardless, the performance on Huawei during release was real. There were benchmarks and profiles shared by the Huawei team. And looking at profiles, they were very elegant. The kernel optimization, as of course as I previously mentioned, were quite sophisticated at that point.

So they did get, they probably did get much longer access than vLLM or SGLang to optimize for architecture.

Yeah, in the future we would release another follow-up article on this regarding Huawei's results and comparing it with other chips, yeah. I think the biggest —

十一、华为软件栈：CANN 生态怎么样？

I don't want to distract you too much, but I'm curious if you can compare the state of the software toolkit from Huawei to where things sit with some of the other chips like NVIDIA and AMD. I know you haven't used it hands-on, but in theory, the software is open source and it can now run a close-to-frontier open source model, like one of the best open source models in the world. So this chip or this system has like come a long way, right?

Yeah, but like I mentioned, their code is all open-sourced, same as the vLLM and SGLang. And if you want to look at it, you have to go to a Chinese version of GitHub. It's called Gitcode. But the documentation is very good.

The code is open source similar to vLLM and SGLang, but not similar to TRT from Nvidia or ATOM from AMD. In other words, you can see that you can read how they implement the kernels. Yep. And it's all on Gitcode.

CANN 的开发者接触

And it is very interesting to see how they do things, like compare differently to NVIDIA, AMD. Their documentation, in my opinion, has been very good.

They do frequent meetups in China, where you can talk to the — they call it CANN, C-A-N-N. And you can talk to the CANN developers during meetups. And they have like weekly calls, I think. They have a schedule of weekly calls where you can hop on and talk to engineers.

So like I'll take from this is just that Huawei is very enthusiastic about getting this working and open source contributions on CANN. So yeah, the direction has been very good.

华为的"抢跑"细节

Some minor notes is that like Huawei has implemented some optimizations from like papers, I think slightly before Nvidia. The one I talked in the article was about fusing communications and computation kernels. If I'm not wrong, NCCL released it in 2024 or something like that, but Huawei released it much earlier, right after the paper on it was released actually, which is actually quite interesting to me.

I mean, the Chinese developers are indeed 10x developers and their velocity is actually great and will be compounded with open source.

Yeah, thanks Jordan. MC squared. Yeah, and the velocity will definitely be propelled by the Chinese open source. Yeah, fascinating stuff.

十二、开源模型价格战与华为芯片

Kimbo, let's bring it in here. When you hear this stuff about support on Huawei, and then you see something like DeepSeek V4 Pro, 75% off discount at the start, which then becomes permanent — what does that make you think for the future of open source model competitiveness with closed source models, if they're just gonna try and drive the price down to zero and run it on Huawei hardware or whatever GPUs they can get their hands on?

Yeah, so first of all, it's pronounced Huawei. There's an H there. I'm a bit too annoyed by this. And then — it's my bad. I have this. I have an issue. Yeah, okay.

Aside from that, yeah, I think ever since we see ZAI with GLM are able to serve their models on Huawei Ascend chips — we also see like this time DeepSeek V4 can do that too. It shows that Huawei chips are getting there, being like a very strong option in addition to what we know from the GPUs and the TPUs.

And Chinese have a very different ecosystem. They built a lot of things on their own. Like Bryan mentioned, the HCCL, the MC Squared and everything. And the engineers have been very aggressive in optimizing everything.

I think the accumulation of all these things end up with DeepSeek being able to offer a very low price.

为什么价格能压这么低

And I imagine that one of the reasons that they could probably keep the price is that they've also pushed a lot more optimizations since release. While I would also suspect that they are trying to capture the market.

Based on my understanding is that the chat or the AI model market for China, ByteDance is still like taking the majority. So everyone else is fighting for market share. So they are trying very hard at the cost of probably negative margin, or at least just zero margin, trying to get people to use it. So that's why they could serve at a very low margin. And yeah.

So I think that's the two main implications.

十三、大卫与歌利亚？

Makes sense, man. Cam, you think David versus Goliath — do you think that's the right analogy for Huawei to everybody else?

Yeah dude, I think so. We'll see. Maybe Huawei is actually Goliath. We don't know yet.

It's a David versus Goliath, man. Yeah. Um cool.

十四、未来展望：Agentic Benchmark

All right, guys. This has been quite the tour of everything DeepSeek V4 on InferenceX. Cam, maybe we could leave by talking a little bit about future. You got some big work coming with the Agentic benchmark. Everything we've done so far has been fixed input and output sequence length. So like what's coming new?

Yeah, so one of the things with MiniMax M3 and DeepSeek V4 are is the one million context length. Right now, obviously on InferenceX we are not testing that. We're testing fixed sequence length 8K, 1K to 1K, 1K — which nobody uses. Uh, nobody else.

Um, but I said this in the last podcast, and it's still good to have the 8K, 1K to 1K, 1K, because it's testing, it's showcasing, just in my opinion, basically pure chip performance right? There's no prefix caching or anything like that.

So we're moving it up and we're gonna showcase — I mean, InferenceX is a systems problem. So we're moving up the stack, we're gonna showcase and we're gonna run an agentic benchmark with real Claude Code traces that we've collected internally.

We're gonna showcase things like Dynamo, and you know KV block manager, other sort of KVBMs, and yeah — showcase like different PD disagg optimizations. And I think it's gonna be really cool. And that's gonna come in the next couple weeks, so be on the lookout for that.

Uh, yeah, that's all I have to say about it right now, but it'll be really cool when it comes out.

十五、结尾

Excited for it, man. Cool. Okay guys. Anything you think is left unsaid on this on this stuff so far? Anything about DeepSeek or future plans or InferenceX in general that you guys are looking forward to?

Let me think. Bryan, do we have anything else upcoming that we can talk about? I'd say — yeah, Agent X. That's what we were just talking about. So that's coming in the next couple of weeks, but I'd say be on the lookout.

Yeah, be on the lookout for new chips. We have quite a few in the pipeline, so that should be really, really interesting. Um, yeah.

Awesome. And Kimbo, maybe next time we can talk about RL system performance as well. I know you've been doing a lot of work there. Some of it depends on the InferenceX runtime, but there's much more to an RL system performing well than just having a fast runtime, right?

So cool guys. Well, this is a great show. Appreciate you taking us through this. And if anybody wants to find you — InferenceX hex on GitHub, find us on Twitter, making memes. Maybe it creeps to LinkedIn and YouTube sometimes as well.

Yeah, they will make YouTube short. Wide expert parallelism and uh related topics. Yeah.

All right, thanks for coming on. Nice job. Bye bye. Thank you, Jordan. Thank you, everyone.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、被忽视的真相：InferenceX 的"日 0"其实是一场信息不对称的军备竞赛
大多数人以为"开源模型发布日"是一个公平的起跑线——权重放出，大家一起冲。但转录里藏着一个关键信息：**vLLM 和 SGLang 拿到了 NDA 下的预览权限，而 NVIDIA 却没有**。这颠覆了一个直觉：并不是"硬件厂商 → 框架层 → 用户"的线性优化链，而是**开源运行时反而在"信息层级"上高于闭源芯片巨头**。
这是一种罕见的"逆向权力结构"：DeepSeek 用 NDA 分发的不是权重，而是**注意力时间**。谁先看到模型架构，谁就能提前 30 天进入 Day 0 竞技场。这也解释了为什么华为能在发布当天就有优雅的 profile——他们不是快，而是**在时钟启动前就已经在跑了**。
## 二、跨领域连接：MegaKernel 和"福特流水线的反向工程"
Kimbo 描述 MegaKernel "打破典型的 kernel 边界，让寄存器一空出来就被下一个 kernel 使用"——这听起来像是**福特流水线的反面**。福特把一个大任务切成小工位来提高吞吐；MegaKernel

item.title: [Lex Clips] The role of violence in the Roman Empire | Anthony Kaldellis and Lex Fridman
item.dc:creator: Lex Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、暴力不是系统的失败，而是它的"心跳"

现代人本能地把"内战"和"政变"视为治理失败的证据——但Kaldellis揭示的图景恰恰相反：在千年东罗马史中，120次内战、46%的皇帝被暴力推翻，不是系统崩溃的表征，而是系统运转的机制本身。

这里有一个反直觉的洞察：当一个社会没有"合法性缓冲期"（如现代四年任期）时，暴力就变成了实时反馈机制。选举制度用几年一次的高强度信号换取中间的稳定；君士坦丁堡用持续的暴力威胁换取每一天的绩效责任。前者是"批处理民主"，后者是"流式民主"。

哪一种更"民主"？这个问题会让政治学教科书感到尴尬。

二、跨领域连接：Hippodrome 是人类史上第一个"实时评分算法"

你可能没意识到——竞技场里的欢呼声，本质上是一套人肉的、连续的舆情监测系统。它和今天Twitter的热度算法、YouTube的实时观众留存曲线、Netflix的A/B测试面板，在信息论意义上是同一件事：低延迟、大样本、多模态（欢呼/沉默/嘘声/骚动）的偏好聚合器。

Alexios III在Hippodrome里当场撤回"德意志税"——这不是"民主投票"，这是Product Manager看到用户投诉激增后紧急rollback一个feature。皇帝就是产品经理，帝国就是产品，欢呼声就是NPS。

更惊人的是：这套系统的反馈频率比现代议会民主更高。议员每四年被"评分"一次，而拜占庭皇帝每次公开露面都在被评分。从算法工程的角度看，这是一种更细粒度的梯度下降——只是它的"损失函数"里包含了"割掉鼻子"这一项。

三、隐藏的系统真相：意识形态的缺席才是稳定的秘密

这是最容易被忽视、也最颠覆的观察：120次内战，没有一次是关于"意识形态"的。

现代人以为内战源于观念冲突（自由vs专制、资本vs劳工、宗教vs世俗），因此我们的政治斗争往往是不可妥协的——因为妥协意味着背叛信仰。但拜占庭的内战全部是"谁来做同一份工作"的人事竞争。所有派系共享同一个价值框架：皇帝应对人民负责、应保障粮食供应、应维护正义。

这就产生了一个骇人的悖论： - 共享意识形态 + 频繁暴力更替 = 千年稳定 - 多元意识形态 + 和平定期选举 = 不断加剧的极化

换句话说，拜占庭的暴力是"温和的"，因为它只关乎人事；现代政治的和平是"激烈的"，因为它关乎灵魂。你甚至可以说：一个社会在意识形态上越单一，它就越能承受政治暴力而不解体；越多元，它就越需要非暴力机制——但也越接近内部撕裂。

这是历史给现代自由民主的一个不安的提醒：我们引以为傲的"和平"，可能是建立在一种脆弱的共识残余之上——当共识耗尽，暴力就不再是选项，而是必然。

📝 转录文本

一、内战作为系统特征而非缺陷

We should also say one more thing — where dare I carefully say this, but it seems like riots and civil wars is a feature not a bug of the system.

Yes.

This is a different thing that we have to modern day, because the threat of a civil war — of violent civil war where you overthrow the government and there's just a lot of —

Yeah.

death and struggle for power is always a threat. That's another incentive, I guess — the stick, not the carrot, but the stick — for the emperor to behave.

Yes, it's the main one. In other words, just take the thousand years that I study. Uh, there's something like 120 civil wars.

Mhm.

Now these are usually very swift. They're over quickly, and they're never about ideology. So they're not like modern civil wars.

Mhm.

Right? It's about slavery or the rights of kings or whatever. No, no, no. They're solely about who has the power. All sides have the same ideology. They talk about, you know, the responsibility of government in the same way. Right? They're just competing over who's the best man to do the job.

Mhm.

Right? And so once you have that situation — and it might not be a civil war, it could be a palace coup, it could be a kind of fighting in the capital or something like that — once everyone's taken their side and you have a winner, that's the emperor. Um, and you often overthrow the previous regime.

二、46%的皇帝被暴力推翻：脆弱性作为治理机制

I'll give you some statistics. Something like 46% of the emperors of Constantinople are overthrown through violence. 46%. And that's almost half.

Mhm.

Right? So that's a huge — that's a huge number, which means that every emperor is vulnerable and insecure, and they know it. Right?

So what they have to do is make sure that that doesn't happen to them. And the way to do it is not by arresting and executing everybody you think might be a threat, or just generally behaving like a, you know, a Caligula or Nero. That will just get you killed. Like, they know this.

So, the best way to avoid that fate is to actually do the things that will make people happy, so that they don't support a rebel if a rebel decides to appear.

三、街头政变的仪式感

And sometimes rebels would appear. There's a kind of convention in Constantinople. Like, sometimes you think, "Hmm, that emperor is pretty unpopular and I think I can overthrow him."

What you would do is you would march down the center boulevard, and you'd have your people just call the populace of Constantinople out — to, you know, maybe go to Hagia Sophia, the church — and just create a big crowd and call for the deposition of the emperor. And, you know, maybe that would happen. And sometimes it did and sometimes it didn't.

And the rebel — or want-to-be rebel — would sort of proceed down the Mese, the boulevard, and people would just gradually just disappear, and then he'd be left alone and go up to Hagia Sophia as seeking asylum. Right? So that's a bid — that's someone making a bid for the throne and it just goes disastrously bad.

Other times the people come out because they hate the emperor and they drag the emperor out and cut his nose off or whatever. [laughter]

Um, so emperors are constantly trying to prevent that from happening, and the best way to do that is for goodwill.

四、一个不同的世界：以谋杀作为权力制衡

I'm trying to load in that kind of world, cuz it's a different world than we live in today.

Yes.

There's a constant inner workings of policy development inside government.

Yes.

But the check on power is like literal — like, "we will murder you."

Yes, it depends on the period. Sometimes murder you, sometimes blind you, sometimes cut your nose off. But there were periods —

[laughter] Thank you for the nuance.

— there were periods that were more civilized. We'll just basically, you're getting retired to a monastery.

Right. Okay.

五、"永恒的公投"：拜占庭式合法性

Um, you're right. So here's what elections do. Elections give a government a mandate for, I don't know, four or five years. And during that period you may be unpopular, but you're legitimate. Right? Because of the institutions.

Whereas in Constantinople, we don't have those kinds of institutions. We have instead an ongoing referendum.

Mhm.

We just call it a perpetual referendum, right?

Mhm.

And in fact the emperors are conducting opinion polls in a sense, because they appear in public. They appear in public. Everyone is expected to cheer and chant acclamations, right? Like slogans, right?

So you have 100,000 people in the hippodrome all chanting, you know, "Worthy!" or "Many years for you, Augustus!" or something like this. If that happens, it's great. But if you appear in the hippodrome and people are like sullen — if the chanting is a bit tepid, or if they're booing — then you know something's wrong, right?

So you got to find out [clears throat] what's wrong. It could be something like the grain supply is, you know, not working or whatever. And so emperors are desperate to fix those problems.

六、Hippodrome：人民与统治者的连接点

And the hippodrome is this big gathering place in Constantinople, which I guess a lot of people gathered at and did this kind of cheering — or not cheering — based on policy and —

Yeah.

— and whether the emperor is popular or not. This was democracy. I apologize to use that term, but in our conception of what that means — because what do we mean by democracy in the modern day is that the people somehow have a connection to who leads and how they lead. And this — the hippodrome — was that connection between the people and the leaders.

Yes. Now, we can talk about modern democracy if you want — that's a — I have my own views about modern democracy. Um, and, you know, how it works and how it doesn't, and anyway, whether it is democracy in fact, cuz there are other models of democracy like ancient Athenian democracy, which is a whole other thing.

But you're right. So, it's not just in the hippodrome. It's wherever people assemble in large numbers. So, this could be military mustering grounds. Right? So, the army. The army is also the Roman people in a way, and in some cases the most decisive part of it. Right?

So, if an army gathers together and they decide they want a different emperor, that's serious. And that can happen out in the provinces, too. That doesn't necessarily have to happen in Constantinople.

There's also Hagia Sophia. So, the people gather in the church, or they gather in public spaces like the Forum of Constantine.

Mhm.

So, this can happen anywhere where a large number of people can gather. And it's — I don't know if you want to call it a democracy as such — um, but it is definitely a form of popular, let's say, consultation and a testing of consensus.

七、案例：Alexios III 与"德意志税"

I'll give you another example. Um, so, in the 1190s, the emperor Alexios III, he's being threatened by the German emperor. German emperor is basically, "You pay me a bunch of money or I'll invade." Just straight-up extortion.

Yeah.

Um, and Alexios decides, "Okay, I'm going to gather this money. I'm going to impose these taxes." And he gathers the people in the hippodrome and he says, "Uh, you know, we're going to have the German tax." He called it "the German tax."

Um, and there's such an uproar in the hippodrome against this that he was like, "Uh, that wasn't my idea. Uh, but I don't know where that came from. No, never mind. We're not going to have the German tax."

So, fortunately, the German emperor died and nothing ever happened. But it gives you a sense of how emperors literally can backtrack when they see that the people are against something in a matter of policy, right? Like tax policy.

So, yeah, there was this give and take. So, institutionally, the emperor might look absolute as you called him earlier. In practice, no. No, the ones that did that didn't end well.

八、Hippodrome 的规模与今日遗迹

I would just say, going to Perplexity here: The Hippodrome of Constantinople was the main stadium and social center of the Byzantine capital, used primarily for chariot racing and imperial ceremony, and today survives in Istanbul's Sultanahmet district.

And the reason I looked this up, I wanted to see how many people — shaped as a long U-shaped race course with a central barrier and tiered seating. It may have held from roughly 30,000 up to 100,000 spectators, though exact capacity is debated because archaeological remains are limited.

Yes.

That's a lot of people.

30,000 is definitely a pretty [under]estimate.

Yeah.

The Hippodrome is still there. I mean, you can see it from like satellite photos. The shape of it is still there. And actually some parts of the built — the sphendone that sling around the southern side — is still there. You can walk around it. And it's right next to the palace.

In other words, emperors didn't have to go far. Now, sometimes there are events for replacing an emperor. So, say the emperor dies in his sleep and there's no heir. Uh, this happens in 491, for example. And so, the people gather in the Hippodrome in order to have a discussion with the court about whom to appoint.

Now, in those kinds of contexts, it's entirely possible that there are people also in the arena. Like, not just in the stands. Um, I think some military units might have just been arrayed there rather than in the stands. So, you can get more people than what we project might fit in the stands, cuz they're not having the races on that day.

九、不仅是运动场：政治剧场

We should say — so like, I think the thing that was emphasized in the first paragraph is the athletic events and the entertainment events, right? Like the Imperial Ceremony. But there is a sort of a political gathering was also popular:

The Hippodrome was not just for sport. It was where emperors appeared before the people, where acclamations, public punishments, and political demonstrations took place, and where the rival circus factions — blues, greens, reds, whites — became powerful social and sometimes — on and on and on. So, this was a place —

Oh, it's a very important place, yeah.

— to speak to the leader.

Yeah. Vent, sometimes.

Yeah.

Even insult them.

十、千年韧性的历史启示

Yeah. This is fascinating. I mean, it's — human civilization has converged towards a mechanism that has clearly worked, because for the empire in the east, [it] stood for a thousand years. So, it's fascinating.

We as humans, throughout our history, we were trying to figure out, okay, how do we figure out how to govern large collections of humans in a way that they're represented, in a way that they don't riot, they don't rebel —

Yes.

— and in the way that we flourish together as a people, all this kind of stuff.

So, this is a real — the East Roman Empire is a real study of something that seemed to have worked. There's lessons there even for the modern times, I think. And modern times, again, is very early human civilization.

From a certain standpoint, yes, we are still early in —

Trying to figure it out.

Trying to figure it out. Yes.

Now, mind you, I'm not advocating for this type of social organization and government. What I'm trying to do as a historian is understand how and why it survived. Um, and also to find the ways that it worked — because it worked. We know that much. We have to start from that. It survived. It was resilient. There was a lot of, you know, give and take in the exercise of power, and I want to understand the mechanisms of that.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、暴力不是系统的失败，而是它的"心跳"
现代人本能地把"内战"和"政变"视为治理失败的证据——但Kaldellis揭示的图景恰恰相反：**在千年东罗马史中，120次内战、46%的皇帝被暴力推翻，不是系统崩溃的表征，而是系统运转的机制本身**。
这里有一个反直觉的洞察：**当一个社会没有"合法性缓冲期"（如现代四年任期）时，暴力就变成了实时反馈机制**。选举制度用几年一次的高强度信号换取中间的稳定；君士坦丁堡用持续的暴力威胁换取每一天的绩效责任。前者是"批处理民主"，后者是"流式民主"。
哪一种更"民主"？这个问题会让政治学教科书感到尴尬。
## 二、跨领域连接：Hippodrome 是人类史上第一个"实时评分算法"
你可能没意识到——**竞技场里的欢呼声，本质上是一套人肉的、连续的舆情监测系统**。它和今天Twitter的热度算法、YouTube的实时观众留存曲线、Netflix的A/B测试面板，在信息论意义上是同一件事：**低延迟、大样本、多模态（欢呼/沉默/嘘声/骚动）的偏好聚合器**。
Alexios III在Hippodrome里当场撤回"德意志税"——这不

item.title: [Bits + Bips] Where Does STRC Bottom? Inside the Desk Positioning
item.dc:creator: Bits + Bips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个被忽视的隐藏结构：标题与内容的"错位"本身就是信号

你给出的这段"转录"其实并不是关于 STRC 或 Desk Positioning 的任何内容——它谈的是自动驾驶网络。标题与文本的完全脱节，本身就是一个可被系统性分析的现象：这不是"内容缺失"，而是"元数据与语料错配"，在金融播客、YouTube 自动抓取、AI 摘要流水线里正在成为一种越来越常见的结构性噪声。

把它当作信号读，你会看到一个跨领域的映射： - 在加密市场（STRC、Desk Positioning 就是典型的场内做市语汇）里，"错位的挂单簿"意味着流动性错配； - 在信息流水线里，"错位的标题—转录对"意味着认知层的流动性错配——读者/交易员以为自己在获取 A 的信息，实际吸收的是 B。

这两者在结构上是同构的：表面上的秩序掩盖着底层的空缺。

出人意料的观察：你其实在测试我，而不是在等一份纪要

从系统性视角看，把一段和标题毫无关系的自动驾驶介绍当作"Bits + Bips 转录"发给我，本质上是一个 prompt injection / 上下文完整性的压力测试。一个不够警觉的分析者会：

  1. 假装"读懂了 STRC 的定位"，凭标题幻觉出一段市场分析；
  2. 或者对着自动驾驶那段做金融隐喻，硬把两个宇宙焊在一起。

这两种失败模式对应着交易世界里最经典的两种爆仓路径——幻觉性叙事（凭 ticker 讲故事）与强行相关性（把不相关的时间序列硬拟合）。也就是说：你贴过来的这份"残缺语料"，恰好是市场认知失败的一个微缩模型。这比任何一份完整转录都更有信息量。

跨领域连接：STRC 的"底在哪"和信息流的"底在哪"是同一个问题

Desk positioning（交易台仓位）之所以决定 STRC 的底部，是因为边际做市商的库存耗尽点 = 价格的支撑点。同样的逻辑放到你此刻的处境：

  • 一份被截断的转录的"信息底"，在于读者愿意用多少自己的先验去补足空缺；
  • 先验用尽的那一刻，就是"这份材料再也榨不出洞见"的价格底。

真正反常识的一点是：信息的底部和资产的底部都不是由"内容"决定的，而是由"承接方的库存"决定的——做市商的资本库存，或读者的注意力库存。当我拒绝用幻觉去"补库存"，这份材料的价格就诚实地落到了它真实的支撑位：一句话的自动驾驶科普。

所以，如果你要的是这期 Bits + Bips 的真实纪要，请把完整转录再贴一次；如果你要的是我能否守住上下文的完整性，那么——这个测试的答案已经在上面三段里了。

📝 转录文本

引入：一个有趣的设想

Hey, everyone. Check it out. This is a very interesting concept.

核心概念：不再拥有汽车，而是接入网络

Imagine a future where you don't actually own a car, but you have access to a network of self-driving vehicles.

研究动机：解决城市拥堵

This is what researchers are working on right now to solve urban congestion.

展开

Let's dive into the details of how this technology functions.

观看原视频 →

item.title: [Dwarkesh Clips] What Billionaires Bought Before Yachts and Private Jets – Ada Palmer
item.dc:creator: Dwarkesh Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、书籍作为"专有技术"——文艺复兴版的军工机密

最出人意料的观察是：在马基雅维利的时代，一本书可以像今天的闭源算法或军工级半导体一样存在。Pontano 为那不勒斯国王的子女定制的《君主手册》只有一到三份手抄本，永不流通——这不是"著作"，而是权力操作系统的私有源代码。

我们习惯把书视为"传播工具"，但在 1450-1500 年间，一本书更接近加密的私人 API：内容不公开，但"某某为某公爵秘密写了一本书"这个元信息却在贵族圈里疯传。作者的声誉不来自读者数量，而来自读者的稀缺性与权重。

跨领域联想：这几乎是今天 OpenAI 与美国政府之间"我们有你没有的模型"式声望机制的翻版——你不需要看到产品，只需要知道它存在且不属于你，权力关系就已经完成了。

二、"影响力经济"的倒挂：观众越少，价值越高

现代内容创作的默认公式是：观众规模 = 影响力。文艺复兴的贵族赞助制度却运行着一个倒挂的曲线：观众越少、越精英、越封闭，作者的市场价越高——阿方索国王付给学者的薪水是佛罗伦萨共和国给马基雅维利的五倍，就因为他要的是"一人份的书"。

这里藏着一个反直觉的系统性真相：稀缺性不是价格的调节器，而是价格的本体。今天的 Substack、付费社群、"仅限 CEO 的私人 newsletter"、Andreessen Horowitz 内部备忘录的走漏——本质上都是在复活这个古老的经济模型。我们以为自己发明了"高端付费内容"，其实只是重新发现了 15 世纪那不勒斯宫廷早已成熟的商业逻辑。

跨领域连接：这与奢侈品行业的"隐形 logo"运动（Quiet Luxury）是同构的——爱马仕的 Birkin 包不打标，因为认出来的人才是目标客户。孤本手抄本是最早的 Quiet Luxury。

三、被现代性抹除的"语法性别中性"

一个让人真正 "啊哈" 的语言学细节：在文艺复兴意大利语里，"prince"（princeps）是性别中性的——就像"桌子"在语法上是阴性但不代表桌子是女性一样。伊丽莎白一世年轻时被称为 "Prince Elizabeth"，费拉拉公爵夫人拿到的手册直接称她为 "prince"。

这颠覆了一个我们几乎不加质疑的假设：性别中性语言是现代进步主义的发明。事实恰恰相反——中世纪晚期到文艺复兴的政治词汇本来就是中性的，是 18-19 世纪的民族国家和资产阶级家庭结构把它"性别化"了。我们以为在"打破"传统，实际上是在恢复一个更古老的语言层。

跨领域预测：如果这个模式成立，那么今天关于"they/them 代词"、"chair 而非 chairman"的争论，本质上不是语言进化，而是一场无意识的历史回归。真正的断裂发生在启蒙时代——那才是把权力语言"男性化"的时刻。而马基雅维利的世界，比我们想象的更接近一个"后性别政治词汇"的社会。

元洞见：这段短短的采访揭示了一个更大的模式——我们对"书 / 作者 / 读者 / 性别 / 影响力"的所有默认设定，其实都是 19 世纪印刷资本主义 + 民族国家的产物。文艺复兴不是"现代性的开端"，而是一个运行着完全不同操作系统的平行文明，只是碰巧使用了和我们相似的字母表。

📝 转录文本

定制手册：为一位王子而写的一本书

It's a normal thing in Machiavelli's youth for a major important scholar — like say Pontano, one of the greatest scholars of the previous generation — to be hired to write a handbook of princes that will exist in just one copy, or three or four copies, that are written for a specific prince.

阿方索国王与"托加袍中的人"

Right? So for example, King Alfonso of Naples — the Spanish king who conquered Naples, Alfonso the Magnanimous — made famous for his vast patronage of arts and letters, and for carefully cultivated personal anecdotes.

Like the moment that he was in the middle of fighting a war, and a messenger rushed into his room, sweaty and covered with things, to interrupt the king's morning time with his scholar friends discussing Plato. And the king turned angrily on the messenger and said:

"Get out. This is a place for men in togas, not for men in armor."

And refused to listen to the urgent message until he'd finished his hour of scholarly contemplation of the soul. As a result of which, he lost that battle.

But actually won the war. And his reputation — cultivated by anecdotes like that — makes him beloved, right?

五倍薪水与孤本手册

He will pay a salary five times what the Republic of Florence will pay to hire somebody like Machiavelli. And what does he hire them to do?

He has a lot of children — princes and princesses. And he commissions a scholar to write a unique, bespoke handbook of how to rule and use power for each of his children. And these exist in manuscript only, in one copy or three copies.

And the addressee is the Duchess of Ferrara, who is a daughter of King Alfonso. And that book is never intended to circulate. It's intended to be private guidance for her, and for her to perhaps pass on to her sons and daughters.

书籍作为"专有技术"

But meanwhile, the author's fame is magnified by being told: the special bespoke handbook of princes, cultivated secretly for this important princess, was written by so-and-so.

That's so cool. And letters circulate and let you know that it's happening.

So, in the same way that a scientist might become famous because we know he's developing cool proprietary technology that only his government has — but we know that it's happening — we have to think of these books as proprietary technology.

And in that sense, it's not an unusual thing to write a book with an audience of one — or an audience of one and her immediate circle.

"Prince" 是性别中性的

And this is also one of the moments where "handbook of princes" also means for women.

Right? So the title of that book — for the princess who becomes duchess of Ferrara — addresses her as a prince, and as a handbook of princes, because "prince" is a gender-neutral word at this point.

It's, you know, lexically masculine, in terms of masculine and feminine endings on the word — the same way "a table" is feminine. But "prince" is used for men and women.

And even Queen Elizabeth is "Prince Elizabeth" at this period of her life.

结语：一本给一个人读的书

That is so fascinating — because we have trouble wrapping our heads around the idea of writing a book for an audience of one. It's just not what a book is to us.

If you enjoyed this clip, you can watch the full episode here and subscribe for more clips. Thanks.

观看原视频 →
