With photography, at the beginning of this AI era we got a lot of pushback from photographers: "I just want to capture. I don't edit." Now they're slowly getting to "oh, it's fine to remove some distracting object in that image, it doesn't matter to me, and that's still my creativity." People are transitioning as well. Technology needs to stay a little bit ahead of that spectrum.

Q： That's such a great point. It is a chicken-and-egg problem. You can't implement 100% of what users ask. A seasoned Photoshop user may ask you to reinvent a lasso tool. The question is: do you want to do that? Is that the mental model you want your model to have? Maybe the next abstraction is there's no lasso tool—we're not implementing the world in the old Photoshop way.

五、用户超乎预期的用法

Q： When you work closely with creators, what is the surprising thing about their workflow? Anything that you pushed out there but wasn't designed for, that had a lot of adoption?

Matt： One of the first things that comes to mind is that you tend to think an artist or creative has an end goal in mind—like they know exactly what they want. But in reality, that's not true. Part of art is the iteration, so making sure the tools are able to handle that level of iteration ends up being extremely important. This also makes it very hard to benchmark these things, because their optimum isn't grounded in their head.

Zach： For us, it's a bit specific to photos. We do a lot on identity preservation, identity consistency. We set out to do image editing of real photos that people want to preserve the identity in. What surprised me is that people are actually very willing to generate a new image—put themselves in different environments, generate AI headshots, generate AI video of themselves using Photo's technology. That surprised me a little bit. And people are getting very creative with that.

Q： The video use case is actually a surprising one. Can you explain it further?

Zach： Right now our technology mostly works on images. But people are getting creative. They say: "Oh, the video models are so bad at identity preservation. What I can do is use Photo to generate frames of those videos, and then use a video model to take those frames into a video, so that identity is good." That's something we didn't think of at the beginning—but they figured out, creators.

Matt： I've seen it multiple times now where people find very interesting ways to use tools—not just our tools, but our tools in addition to other tools that you never expect to work. For every reason it shouldn't work, but people find crazy ways to get these things to work.

六、工具抽象：给人用 vs. 给 agent 用

Q： When it comes to tool abstraction—I almost see the end user as a type of agent who needs to use tools. There are tools for humans and tools for agents. How do you see those differ?

Matt： It's interesting to look at how creative tools have existed traditionally. You look at Photoshop, Illustrator, Blender—whatever modality you're interested in—and if you hop into those programs, they're extremely complex. You can literally have a whole career learning the ins and outs. Where agents come along, and the ability to have agents work with tools, is then the ability to tune the tool to the user. Some users care about relighting an image or removing a background. Some want extreme levels of control. So the question is: how do you interact with an agent that can choose that level of abstraction that makes sense for you?

Zach： Creativity is about directing something to achieve your creative mind. This directing thing is so important. That's where the agent comes into place. It's not about mastering those tools—it's about directing an agent who can use those tools to achieve your creativity.

Another lesson we learned in photography: a lot of people edit photos, but a lot of people just don't want to edit photos at all. They have creativity in mind, they just don't want to do the work, or they're too busy. But they still have a creative mind. So if you have some kind of agent that understands your creativity and can express it without much effort from you—these passive agents that run in the background—I feel like that's even better.

七、下一代界面：人与 agent 共用

Q： First we had simplistic tools where users prompt and get an image—one shot. Later we got crazy nodes and concrete workflows. Then canvases. So how do you think about the next interface for both agents and humans?

Matt： I think it's false to assume every user is going to want the same thing. You look at directors of movies—some go super deep into the weeds, making sure every glass is in the right place. Others don't care about any of that; all they care about is "is the actor doing the line in the right way?" I think all that is true in these creative tools too. So you have to create an agent that can work with these different directors—one that has the ability to manipulate things at very fine-grained levels, and one that's more on the literary side and just wants to talk and interact and have fun with the tool.

Zach： Personalization is absolutely key here. Different people have different levels of acceptance of complexity, and different preferences and tastes. The agent needs to understand the user really well and be flexible. It also needs really good memory—if I tell you once, I shouldn't need to tell you again in the future.

Q： If you travel down the abstraction layers far enough, you can build anything with C and a compiler. But it would be weird to assume agents are going to rebuild creative tools every time. Somewhere you have to draw a line in the sand. Where is that layer?

Matt： I don't know if I have a concrete answer because it's constantly changing. There are arguments for different underlying representations. Is it a 3D world you're trying to model? Images? Are you trying to break up images into layers? I'm not sure we've really hit on, as an industry, what the right representation is.

Zach： There's the abstraction of what's a model and what's a tool. A tool can be a little more than a model. A model can do a lot of things that a lot of traditional tools used to do. I personally think models are replacing a lot of the old tools for not-so-precise control. But at the same time, people are putting more precise control back into the model. So it's a back-and-forth process.

八、如何评估"好"：从指标到品味

Q： How do you think about what "good" looks like when it comes to generation?

Matt： Being a researcher, there's always a focus on how to convert "good" into a metric you can measure, so agents and research teams can optimize against it. However, there's no way that will ever tell the full story. You have to involve humans in the loop during development—bring in creatives and people whose taste you're trying to mimic. But that also can't be the full story, because one person creating one video is going to have a different vision than someone else. This is where personalization really becomes key.

Q： Can you measure taste?

Matt： I think you can measure taste given some constraint. Given a population, you can measure the taste of that population. You can also measure the taste of an individual user. Depending on how coarse you get, it becomes more muddled.

Zach： For us, there's evaluation before you ship and evaluation after you ship. With identity preservation, we really care about each individual user saying: are they happy with the result? It's very hard for us to come up with a universal metric, especially for subjective things like taste, and even for very personal things like identity. We always ask the person themselves, or someone close to them, to evaluate identity.

After shipping, especially for subjective things, that's so important for personalization. Have we made the user happy? Can you collect that feedback in an ambient, non-intrusive way? That's just so important.

Q： I was talking to someone earlier about their photo result. His view was: happiness and truth-seeking are two different things. When he saw the result he said "this is exactly me, the likeness is 100%—but I look too fat." So he's not happy. How are you guys solving that?

Zach： At the end of the day, the user is the one in control. If they want to look a certain way in the photo, that's their choice and we need to empower them. Beautification has been a long-time debate. Everyone has their own taste. Personalization is key.

九、长尾用例与隐藏需求

Q： When you launch a tool, there's a distribution of use cases. Are there specific use cases you wanted to move from the fat tail to the head?

Matt： One interesting one was seeing how brands would bring in brand guidelines. It's not something we considered very much, but once they brought them in, you realize this was an extremely useful source of information for getting to what a user is interested in.

Zach： Our current API only supports humans and pets for identity. There were some users trying to hack our system to use it for personalization for product photography. They were like: "We want this." So now that's on our roadmap.

Q： How do you see the difference between human likeness and product likeness?

Zach： There are similarities. Everybody says, "Oh, the general foundation model is already very good at identity"—until you try to generate an image of yourself. Same thing with product photography: it's pretty good until you're the product owner trying to generate a photo of that particular product. It just stops working.

The difference: identity is more subjective and dynamic—it's really hard to put into words. With products, you need to get the text right, the shape right. Text rendering is so important in product photography, not so much in identity preservation.

十、控制力（Controllability）

Q： Phase one of AI: we generate things and keep reprompting—almost like a lottery. Phase two: we put more control either at the model level or the system level. What does it mean for an AI creative tool to have better controllability?

Matt： It's an open question, but the short answer is you definitely need to add control. That was the number one complaint from anyone trying to use these tools professionally: you don't want to operate with text. There has to be something more than text. Most recently we've done a lot of work in video-to-video because that's a great way to start incorporating pretty precise controls—in time, in space, scribble, point to certain regions. We're adding more of these controls on top of the model, both for users and so agents can be instructed to do these edits.

Zach： It's also about helping the user say what they want. Often the model doesn't do exactly what you want because the user's prompt is ambiguous. The under-exploited research direction is: if the model can tell you what it needs from you—ask you for inputs before it does something. Today it's almost a one-way street: the user tells the model what to do.

When you work with a creative professional as an amateur, they ask you questions. You tell them your opinions, preferences, and they achieve those for you.

Matt： That's a really good point. If you go to a studio and say "make me a 10-second video about a dog jumping in the grass," they're never going to take that deal. They're going to want more specific. You have to figure out how to apply that in AI.

十一、模型与应用的协同设计

Q： Does model-app co-design have benefits when it comes to introducing more controllability?

Matt： I think so. Being able to have users interact in your app and work through their workflow, you learn which things a user actually cares about—which steps they have to redo multiple times, why they chose what they chose. That can help a lot with model design.

Zach： The other direction is also important: your product can help educate the user on the best way to use the model. We're seeing that with coding—people used to do everything in VS Code, and now Claude Code in the terminal can be the best way to use a model because you cut through a lot of other things. App and model co-design has that advantage too.

十二、无头工具与视觉表征

Q： When we think about AI creative tools, the first thing that comes to mind is they're very visual—we're visual animals. Now we're used to working with agents. Is there a world where creative tools become primarily headless?

Matt： Going back to working with a studio: you're talking to an agent, you're talking to someone who's working with you. But they're not talking back to you in text. They're showing you examples. "Do you like these ideas?" I think that's the direction AI creative tools will go.

Zach： When I try to brainstorm something—if you write code to generate an HTML page showing the graphics, I just want a sketch. An image is more than a thousand words. On the other hand, people haven't figured out the best way to feed visual information to a model. Models today are a little bit blind. They're getting better at high-level semantic understanding, but visual is very redundant information—pixels are redundant, unlike language. How do you give the model that information so it's not blind anymore? That's so important, and I don't think we've figured it out yet.

Q： This reminds me of the importance of the right representation. Matt, you worked on Nerf, and before that people were on meshes, now Gaussian Splat. How do you think about representations?

Matt： The representation question ultimately comes down to the control question too. What do you want to be able to modify in the future? If you're designing a poster and you'll be changing the text, you probably want a representation that's not pixel level. If you're just modifying pictures, then pixels make sense. If you're navigating 3D scenes for movies, you may want a 3D representation. There's no one answer—it's whatever makes sense with the given level of control.

Zach： Right now a lot of people focus on what's the best representation for the model. But the consumer of those representations is not just the model—it's also the human. Can you represent it in a way the human can understand and control?

十三、作为艺术家做研究：直觉的来源

Q： How do you develop the intuition for building for creators as former researchers? Matt, I know you're also an artist.

Matt： It gives you some insight on the control aspect. One of the first things drilled in during our classes when I was younger was the blank canvas problem. There's a tendency of getting stuck by that, then starting very meticulously, doing one section at a time. But in reality, it's better to just go at it, try things out, mess with it until you get to the thing you're ultimately happy with. AI tools are no different. The iteration cycle is fast, and you really want to iterate—it's not just put in a single prompt and get a final answer.

Another aspect is figuring out what language to use to describe what works or doesn't work in the tool—because this can be very important for evaluation. Throughout art history, people have built ways to describe composition, lighting, etc. Having that knowledge and incorporating it into the design of the models can be very helpful.

Zach： Talking to users is really important. When you ask the user how they want to improve a photo, they don't know. When you make some edit, now they have opinions on whether they like your edits or not. They know what they don't like. So talking to users is really important. And having a very good understanding of the technology so you can stay a little ahead of users is also important.

十四、过程 vs. 结果

Q： In art, if a human is creating, a lot goes into the process. The final result is when you decide the process has come to a point of your satisfaction. How do you compare that with how agents create art?

Matt： In many ways it can be quite similar, and even when agents create art, there's still the human involved—because that process is the part that's fun for artists. Look at VFX breakdowns: you see how many different steps and layers went into the final pixels. People who do that work find it very exciting. The way things break down in the future with AI tools will look very different, but it'll still exist—a back-and-forth between agents doing some parts of the work and humans tying it all together.

Zach： There's always a debate: is regular photography going to die? I don't think so. There are a lot of people doing photography not just because they enjoy the result—they enjoy the process so much. Even though digital cameras have become so good, many people still take film. Film has this unique thing: you have to capture that moment, otherwise it's gone, and you don't see the result right away—you have to develop the film. People still enjoy those processes.

At the same time, there are people who just want the results. How do you empower them with AI creative tools to unlock their creativity? That's where I'm excited about AI tooling for creativity.

十五、潜在需求与新进入者

Q： There's a lot of latent demand—people who didn't have money for a fancy camera, never took a film-developing class. New entrants on the market often target users who were not users of the original tool. Where have you observed that latent demand come from in photography?

Zach： Photography is not just art—it's also a document of our important moments and life. A lot of people want a good recording of their moments and lives. They may not have the skills or the equipment. In the past they tried to buy the latest iPhone, hoping for a better camera, but they don't have editing skills. Those are the latent demands we try to address—whether through AI models or AI agents, creative tools that make it easy or even do the job for them, so they can get good photos of their important moments.

Q： At our firm we had headshot photo shoots—photographer, studio, lighting, makeup. Recently more folks just say "I couldn't make it, but I'm going to generate it on Photo." There's an interesting market segment where you need physical presence to take a photo. Have you seen that?

Zach： Yes. And a photo shoot is a set-up environment anyway—you're setting up lighting, you go there. Most of the time you don't have full control because of physical limits. When you do it generatively, there are so many more things you can do—change the lighting, change the angle, even change your makeup.

Q： Matt, you take a lot of photos. How do you think about the old vs. new world of AI photography?

Matt： It's fun. There's the overlap where you can do them together. But there's also where you treat it as a very different medium. When you do things purely in the AI world, you can think about what an image would look like halfway across the world, or in specific studio lighting situations you would never actually be able to do. I find myself sometimes seeing a cool image and trying to think: how would I actually replicate this in AI? What are the right terms to use? You go down a rabbit hole. It unlocks a new area that I haven't been able to explore before.

十六、传统工具的未来

Q： How do you think about the role of traditional tools in the new world of AI creative tools? Are they becoming infrastructure where agents know how to use them, or are they getting rebuilt?

Matt： All these things are just tools. Whoever has a vision and wants to execute on it—what tool is right for them? For some people, it'll always be Photoshop. For a newer generation, it won't be. Maybe it'll be agents interacting with these tools. I think these things will always exist to some extent, but find different uses by different users.

Zach： It's not a new problem. Tools have always been iterating. There's always a new tool coming out, whether AI-based or not. We're probably at a stage where tools are iterating so fast. Some tools may not be needed in the future—they'll be replaced. But a lot will still exist. Even the AI models we have today will be replaced. It's just an iterative process.

十七、自有模型与开源的价值

Q： What's the benefit of having your own model? Is there a benefit to open-sourcing the model?

Matt： One of the biggest benefits of having your own model is the ability to build off of it. Your product has a certain set of users and you want to do the best for them. The best way is by customizing—making adjustments to the model that don't make sense for a general open-source model but make sense for your product. That's not always easy on top of existing models, and may require specialization only you can provide.

Zach： For us, a little different. We don't directly do foundation models—we mostly work on personalization. There are so many cool companies like Luma. We really think personalization is something the user should own. That's the user's model. You build a user's model for them, but they have full control. We want to build the technology to enable them to do that, and let them combine it with any foundation model they like—a specialized model for a particular use case, for example. Disentangling personalization vs. foundation is the goal we have.

十八、未来几年，最好的艺术家与其他人的差距

Q： With advances in AI tools, what will separate the best artists from everyone else in the next couple years?

Matt： From my experience already, it's very obvious. When people use our tools—or any AI tools—you can tell pretty quickly someone who's just throwing in a prompt versus someone who's thought more about the whole holistic story. I get amazed at what very creative artists can do with these tools. Many times you see things that don't seem like they would have been possible. That's the thing that separates it: how do you make something unique with the tools you have access to now?

Zach： It's hard to say there's a good artist and bad artist—but probably good artists and average artists. AI tools are definitely raising the lower bar, so even someone like me can create something pretty heat with the tools. But I also think the gap between the very best and the average is going to be even bigger. Because, as Matt said, it's so much easier for people to express creativity when they have a very good understanding of what they want. They can achieve something they couldn't before. So the gap will get bigger. And of course, they need a very good understanding of all these new AI tools as well.

Matt： One final point. You've seen AI generations that are bad—we've all seen them. I can guarantee the same tools that made those bad generations have also made generations you would think are amazing.

十九、AI = slop？还是用户的镜子？

Q： Something I heard from someone today was: they assume AI to be slop. Anything AI models make would be slop. It's up to the humans to create something out of it. If you just say "I want a picture of a cup of coffee," the first generation is mediocre. It's nothing unique, nothing personal. So it really is up to the user to make it theirs—to put their mark and style on it.

What are your thoughts on personal styles—what constitutes style, what constitutes personalization for users?

Zach： That's such a hard question. I almost feel like there's no good way to define it other than you can learn it from data. It's not just the user's existing data—it's also: when they use it, what's their reaction? Even if you ask the user what their own style is, they can put some keywords there but it's not complete.

If the way to solve the problem is to really define their style and then model their style—that's probably not the approach I would take, even from a research perspective. Look at the data, find the attribution the data shows you, and then you've probably figured out that style. And even then, it's still implicit because it's part of the model.

Matt： I think that's also something that changes over time. Both as a person changes—the stuff I thought was interesting 5 years ago vs. now is very different. And, you know, I'm working on one project one week and another project another week, and I inevitably need a different style. Maybe that's how you define styles for specific tasks, and personalization is sort of the holistic what you as a user tend to like. But yeah, I think it's a bit mixed.

收尾

主持人： Awesome. And with that—well, thank you so much for coming on the pod. This is such a fun discussion. Really appreciate it.

嘉宾： Yeah. Thank you for having us.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的洞察：AI 不是在杀死摄影，而是在把"决定性瞬间"从快门时刻**搬迁**到后期
布列松那句神圣的"决定性瞬间"统治了摄影 80 年——它的本质是：你必须在快门按下的 1/125 秒里完成所有创造性决策，因为**之后就什么都做不了了**。Zach 在对话里轻描淡写地说出了一句颠覆性的话："摄影师在 post-capture 阶段比以前更有乐趣了。"
这句话的真正含义是：**摄影的"创造时间窗口"正在从毫秒级被拉长到无限**。这不是工具升级，而是介质的相变——就像电影从胶片走向数字时，剪辑从"物理切割"变成"非线性"，整个创作的时间结构被重写了。摄影师现在面对的不再是"瞬间艺术"，而是一种类似**雕塑或绘画**的"持续可塑介质"。这是一次悄无声息的范式革命，多数人还没意识到。
## 二、跨领域连接：AI 创作工具正在重演**编程语言抽象层的百年历史**——但跳过了中间步骤
听 Matt 谈"应该把抽象层画在哪条线上"，我立刻想到的是：从汇编 → C → Python → 自然语言编程，软件业花了 70 年才走到"用英语写程序"。而 AI 创作工具正在 3 年内压

item.title: [Business of Sport] From £25m in Debt to £15m in the Bank: How The Hundred Saved Yorkshire
item.dc:creator: Business of Sport
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的发现：拯救约克郡的，不是板球，而是"自我背叛"

整个故事最惊人的悖论在于：一家有 165 年历史的会员制俱乐部，必须先"背叛自己的身份"才能活下去。 Colin 反复强调"不要害怕去会员化"——这句话翻译过来其实是：你赖以为荣的那个组织形式，正是把你两次推到破产边缘的元凶。

更深一层的反讽是：救命的 1 亿英镑并非来自板球本身，而是来自一个被传统球迷骂作"不是板球"的新产品（The Hundred）。也就是说，约克郡是被它最看不起的那种板球救活的。这构成一种近乎黑色幽默的系统性真相——一个组织最深的护城河，往往就是它最大的负债表。

二、跨领域连接：板球的"第五天问题" = SaaS 的"长尾用户"陷阱

Colin 关于"五日制测试赛第五天所有人都亏钱"的论断，几乎是一个完美的单位经济学案例。第五天的存在不是产品特性，是历史惯性形成的成本黑洞——票价压到 10 镑只为填场子，运营、转播、餐饮全员亏损。

这跟 SaaS 行业里"保留那 3% 重度免费用户却消耗 40% 服务器成本"是同一个结构：当某个尾部环节的边际收益持续为负，继续提供它本身就是对核心用户的隐性补贴。 把测试赛改为四日制，本质上和 SaaS 公司"sunset 免费版"是同一种决策——但板球用了几十年都没人敢按那个按钮，因为传统的情感价格远高于财务价格。

再往外推一层：英超 vs EFL、Premier League vs County Cricket、好莱坞 vs 长尾流媒体——所有"金字塔型生态"都面临同一道数学题：顶端的繁荣是不是真在反哺底座，还是只是借底座的合法性收租？

三、最被低估的洞察：约克郡其实在做一场"国家失灵"的私人补救

Colin 顺口说出的一句话值得放大：澳大利亚的悉尼、佩斯球场是州政府和联邦政府盖的，俱乐部一分钱不用出；而英国所有板球场都是俱乐部自己筹钱建的。

这意味着 The Hundred 的 1 亿英镑、去会员化、私人投资者——整套金融工程其实是在替缺席的英国公共体育基建买单。 约克郡不是在"商业化"，它在做一件本该由国家做的事：用资本市场的工具，补一个公共物品的窟窿。

这就解释了一个表面矛盾：为什么一个 32 岁的主持人会觉得自己已经"老"了，而 Colin 却把希望寄托在 10 年后的青少年身上。因为这不是一场关于球的赌博，是一场关于"谁来替英国体育文化付固定成本"的代际接力。 当板球俱乐部开始像主权基金一样思考——成立投资子公司、设 10 年战略、把比赛日变成科技园区式综合体——它已经悄悄从"体育组织"变成了"地方文化的资产管理人"。

约克郡如果赌赢，模板就会复制到 Sussex、Kent；如果赌输，英国可能会失去的不只是一个俱乐部，而是一种"传统机构能否在不靠政府的情况下自我现代化"的可能性证明。这才是这场 2500 万债务到 1500 万现金故事真正的赌注。

📝 转录文本

开场：一个未完成的使命

"We could sit there and do nothing, not being unkind. I could put my coat on, walk out the door and say, 'Right, I've sorted it all out. You got 15 million quid in the bank. Everything's hunky dory,' and walk away from it. I don't want to do that. I want to finish the mission, what I started in 2002. But this is for the good of Yorkshire cricket for the next 50 years. That's why we're doing it."

"Don't be fearful of demutualization. You've been a members club for 160 odd years, but as a members club, we've virtually nearly gone bankrupt twice."

"I would change test match cricket to 4 day. The fifth day in a test match, everybody loses money. The ground loses money, the broadcaster loses money. It's no good for anybody."

"We created The Hundred where we believe that would be an attractive investment for investors. The total investment for The Hundred — if you sold every team — it was nearly a billion pound. Without that, to be honest, that saved the game certainly for the next 10 years."

"I went back to Yorkshire in February 2024 and the debts had spiraled up to 25 million again. They even had an Ashes test match during that time and they still lost money. I still can't get my head around that. If you have an Ashes test match, you should make between three and five million pounds net profit a year. Full stop."

"I find business fascinating."

一、欢迎与背景：板球当下的混乱图景

主持人： Colin, welcome to the Business of Sport. It's really great to have you in the studio.

Colin： Yeah, thanks very much for the invitation, and uh, looking forward to the chat.

主持人： Well, me too. You know, I know I'm the one that's meant to be hosting this, but how on earth do we even begin to look at cricket at the moment? It is a chaotic landscape, really from top to bottom. You are in position at Yorkshire as chair at the moment. Tell us a little bit, just to give some of the context of the challenges that you're facing.

Colin： Basically, I've been in professional cricket now since 2002, when I first got involved with Yorkshire when they were in financial distress for the first time. And then I got back involved with Yorkshire two years ago, 2024, when again they were in their second financial distress. So that's how I come back involved with them.

And yeah, really, I think cricket is in an interesting place at the present time. I think there's some massive challenges ahead at all levels in cricket — international cricket, county cricket, one day cricket, however you look at it. I think there's going to be a lot of challenges in the next 10 years, and the whole game has got to look at that very seriously and how we handle that going forward, because the way people are digesting cricket and following cricket and watching cricket is changing rapidly.

You've only got to look at — even test matches in India, the audiences of test matches in India is dropping. People don't realize that. We're fortunate in England that test matches, we're still sellout grounds when we do have in England international matches, which is great. But that might not last forever. That could change if the audience's views and demands change in the future.

二、世代差异：板球观众正在分裂

主持人： I didn't realize that test matches were declining in India. That's really interesting. Do you think we're in an era of too much?

Colin： I think the problem that we've got in cricket — you can say it's a problem, you can say it's an opportunity — but I think the older audiences like test match cricket. They've been brought up on test match cricket. The younger audiences, different lifestyle, different demands, like one day cricket. You have a result one way or another. It's quick, it's fast, it's entertaining. The younger audiences find test matches very boring.

So if you look at the audience at test matches and then compare that with the audience especially at a Hundred match — totally different audience.

主持人： Would you say 32 is young?

Colin： From where I'm sitting today? Yeah.

主持人： Okay. I love test cricket, but even at 32 I've grown up with it being the primary part of cricket. If you're talking about the real birth of franchise cricket from 2007 IPL onwards — it was the moments in the '05 Ashes for someone around my generation. But now we'll start to see very quickly the first part of the generation that have grown up with the IPL being one of the first things they've watched, with the short form games being one of the first things they watch. It'll be really interesting to see how they prioritize and want to consume and participate in cricket.

Colin： And that is the challenge we've got in cricket for the next 10 years: how do we handle that change? How do we keep the interest watching test match cricket from that younger generation at 32 or even younger? That is a challenge we've all got.

三、改革测试赛：把五日改成四日

主持人： What would you do?

Colin： The main thing — and I said this 10 years ago, maybe I was ahead of my time — I would change test match cricket to 4-day from 5-day. I think 5 days is far too long anyway. Not many go to 5 days when you look at it.

主持人： No, particularly with England.

Colin： Exactly. So if you look at 4-day test match cricket, you play 90 overs a day, it's out at 10:30 in the morning, and you make it more watchable and attractive from a younger generation's point of view.

The other thing we've got to look at in test matches is who's going to play test match cricket going forward. You've seen it over the last two months — you had New Zealand playing Ireland, and you had India playing Afghanistan. Both of those finished within three days and were won by an innings and a lot of runs from a top-quality test match team to a relatively new test match team.

So how many teams are going to be playing test match cricket in 10 years' time? I don't think we'll have as many as we have today. Because what's the point of playing test match cricket if you've got nobody watching it and you're losing money on staging it? All those challenges have got to come through ICC and all the countries who play test match cricket.

主持人： And it's about creating the product for the fans, but it's also about creating the product that makes sense for business as well. Because if you have a fifth day that virtually never gets played, but you're still selling tickets, you're still bringing in all of the food and beverage, the staffing that you're putting on for a fifth day…

Colin： The fifth day in a test match, everybody loses money. The ground loses money, the broadcaster loses money. It's no good for anybody. When you do have a fifth day, you virtually let the crowd in for nothing — £10 a ticket or something like that — just to try and fill the ground as much as possible, because you never pre-sell the fifth day anyway. So the fifth day financially is a negative.

四、传统体育的抗拒与"The Hundred"的诞生

主持人： So you brought that up 10 years ago. We joke a lot on the show about the challenge of big change in sports that we would class as traditional. If you get pushback like that — if things don't change off the back of it — is it because there is just a resilience to doing things, is it because there's a challenge in shifting the mentality that something that has always been should always be?

Colin： I think there's a time and a place for change. When I brought it up 10 years ago, we had other challenges at the ECB and within English cricket. So at that point in time, it wasn't paramount.

The big change that we looked at then was The Hundred — how to bring in a new tournament to attract a new audience. We did all the research and we found that we had a million people each year that watched cricket in one format or another. Problem is, it was the same million people that just got churned around every year. So we weren't attracting new audiences.

When we went out and did the research, we found out that we didn't have families, we didn't have women, we didn't have children. And we asked the question, "Why aren't you watching cricket?" And it came back: goes on too long, difficult to understand the scoreboard, etc.

So when we came out with The Hundred, we came out with a new game that was easy to understand, shorter format, finished in 2 and 1/2 hours. 100 balls, runs, wickets, and a conversation. That's all you need to understand about it.

And the other big thing we did — we got it back on terrestrial television, which helped a hell of a lot with the BBC. So that was the challenge at the time: how do you attract that new audience and increase the audience? Which we did and we got it through and we succeeded with.

为什么 Blast 不够？

主持人： What were the responses of the individuals that you spoke to in that type of survey as to why maybe something like the Blast wasn't servicing them in the way the Hundred has been able to?

Colin： I think the Blast — it sounds horrible saying this, but it's fact — I think the Blast turned into a male social event where the guys would go out on a Friday night or over a weekend with their mates and guys from work. So it turned into a very social male-dominated event, not a family event. And that's where we lost out on the Blast as it was, and still is to be honest.

五、商业逻辑：从产品到投资标的

主持人： Let's duck into the — you brought it up, so let's run with it. There is the fan product element. Now, it's easy to say there's also been a very strong business element. When you set out, how did you position both of those two together? Was the objective early on to recognize this was a really good chance to inject some much-needed capital back into English cricket if it worked?

Colin： Yeah, it was one of the first things we always said — what the game needed was investment coming into the game. And it's still there. That is still a challenge certainly at county level.

So from that point of view, we created The Hundred where we believe that would be an attractive investment for investors to look at in the future. How long that would take, we didn't know at that point in time, but we knew it would be attractive to investors.

I remember standing up when we got it through and we launched it, and I said then: "In five years' time, we could have investors from India, America and all the rest of it to look at investing in The Hundred." And people laughed at me and said, "You must be joking. That's never going to happen. Indians are never going to invest in The Hundred in England." And I got slated for getting The Hundred on the table and coming through. But that was our vision in 2017.

不急着卖：先证明价值

主持人： How important was it to not sell it at the beginning? You create a new tournament — and have investors in from day one. How important was it to prove there was value in that product?

Colin： Yeah, very important. There's no way we ever wanted to do that. The plan was always to run it for five years. Get the audience, get the figures, get everything up and running, get the broadcaster behind us — which we did with Sky, they've been brilliant. So that was always the plan: 5 to 10 years to have something that you can take to market, whatever format that looked like.

主持人： And for those that challenged it, was it the fact that there was already so much cricket they didn't want it to conflict with other formats?

Colin： I think the ones who challenged it virtually were in a very comfortable position with their own clubs and counties. They were profitable, they were successful on the field, they had a good business behind them. So they saw no need to bring anything else to the table. They were happy with what they had.

But it was the other counties who were saying, "Hold on a minute. We need something new. We need something to bring more investment into the game, to bring more capital into the game. How do we do it?" So it took us two and a half years to get it through, but we got there in the end.

六、起源故事：沙漠之春那个早晨

主持人： When you say "get it through," do you mean two and a half years just from idea to approval, where you can then go, right, okay, we're going to create the teams, start selling the product?

Colin： Exactly. It was 2 and a half years from the initial idea, which Sanjay Patel brought to the table with Andrew Strauss, Tom Harrison and myself — sat us down and told us about this idea of The Hundred — to when we actually got the counties to vote for it. Because he had to take it to the stakeholders to get it through and get the 75% majority, which we achieved. That was a 2 and 1/2 year time scale of constantly talking to the stakeholders, updating them where we were, all the ideas we were having. We had meeting after meeting after meeting to get over the line.

主持人： So Sanjay one day was just like, "This is — I've had an idea"?

Colin： Well, when he presented to us, I'll never forget it. We were in Spain. We went to look at Desert Springs — do we buy a ground in Desert Springs for going across there in winter? That was where we started off. And Sanjay said, "Right, Saturday morning, I want to see you at 10:00. I've got an idea I want to throw at you three."

He got a room in the hotel. He presented this new idea of The Hundred to us, and I'll never forget. We all looked at each other and said, "Really? How does that work?" And slowly, bit by bit, for three months, he then virtually put some flesh on the bones of what it looked like and how it would work, etc. And yeah, we all signed into it very quickly.

主持人： In that first conversation, was there a change in format — i.e. shorter overs?

Colin： Yes. From day one, it was 100 balls, and it was not six-ball overs. From day one, that never changed.

主持人： I can imagine you guys sitting there going, "Well…"

Colin： We all said, "How does that fit in with the averages, how does that fit in with your career record and all that sort of stuff?" — which is the first thing a cricketer thinks about. And he said, "Don't worry about all that. We'll get all that sorted, etc., and we'll make it work and we'll fit it in with MCC laws and regulations." And yeah, we did all that. But he never changed it from day one.

七、新观众的到来与"纯粹派"的反对

主持人： Okay, so you get it through. We now see the real value of it. We see it being a really good product where — if you're a cricketing purist, you still get people pushing back on it. I still have them. And generally most of them — do you know what's interesting? I say, "Have you been?" "No." "Well, go and experience it." Because fine, it may not be what you've grown up with, but it's an amazing atmosphere. I was at a Blast game 24th of May — virtually no one there. Apart from the MCC members, virtually no one there. And last year, going to some of the Hundred games was great, like really good.

Colin： Well, that's one thing that we have seen change — they do like the shorter format. They don't like it going on for too long. That new audience has arrived: the families, the women, the children. And the old — we used to call them "pale, male and stale" traditional cricket supporters — are not there.

Because they've never even looked at a lot of them. "I don't like it." First reaction is "I don't like it." "Why don't you like it?" "Well, it's not cricket, is it?" That's the first reaction you get from a traditional cricket supporter. "It's not cricket. It's not proper cricket." "Of course it's cricket." "No, it's not."

So that's the thing which we always said from square one — traditional cricket members, we believed, will not go and watch the Hundred. And we've proved that.

主持人： And you don't care, because really what we're looking at doing is broadening the whole point of this — to broaden the reach of the game.

Colin： Yeah. And it's worked.

八、十亿英镑的产品：The Hundred 的财务影响

主持人： So the product's worked. Tell us how important it's been financially.

Colin： If you look at the figures that were brought in last year, it surprised everybody — surprised me. When you look at — if you like — the total investment for The Hundred, if you sold every team, it was nearly a billion pounds, which is phenomenal. To get 49% in of, you know, £500 million pounds — without that to be honest, that saved the game certainly for the next 10 years.

What we've got to do is make sure — and the game's got to do it as well as the governing body — we've got to make sure we don't waste it. We look at how we use it. The majority, like we have at Yorkshire, firstly we paid down our debt. It's allowed us to pay down all our debt, so we have no debt now. That saves us £1.5 million pounds interest per year.

So the investment that's come into the game through the Hundred is phenomenal. You would not have got that from any other angle, shape or format. From that point of view, it's been a savior.

It's given more money to the recreational game, because 10% of everything that came in was always designated to go to the recreational game. So that's going to help the recreational game over the next 10 years. It's been a godsend to be honest.

控制工资通胀的隐忧

It's allowed the clubs to look at what they've got, what they want to do, how they want to use that. My big fear going forward is that we don't have a wage inflation because of the money that's come into the game. That's got to be handled and managed properly.

主持人： That's the biggest challenge the EFL's had with the Premier League, trying to get more money — because the Premier League will say, "Yeah, but if we give more money, it's just going to get put on wages, it's not going to be siphoned off for the things that are going to help you develop as a business." Which is always a fair comeback — but ironic, because they spend equally lavishly on exactly the same things. To stop that from happening here when you have a chance to do that before it's actually got going — how was that money segmented? What has been classed as a good investment and what has been classed as "you're not allowed to use that money"?

Colin： The ECB's put in some controls now — that for you to access your share of the 49%, you've got to give a business plan to the ECB of how you're going to spend your money. Are you going to pay down debt? Are you going to invest in a new stand? Are you going to do this? How are you going to do that?

And the one thing about cricket at the present time — we have got a salary cap, so that controls the salary cap of the county as a whole, which is good. But I'm sure the PCA is going to be knocking on the door going forward and saying, "Hold on a minute. You've got a lot more money. We need a bit more of it." Which I understand that. No issues with that. Provided it don't rocket and go out with the stratosphere basically.

主持人： But also provided you make more money, right?

Colin： Yeah. Because just because you get a lot more money coming in, it doesn't mean you're going to make more money at the bottom line going forward if you don't spend it wisely. Your operating costs can soon shoot through the roof if you're not careful, saying, "Yeah, I'll have this body, I'll have that body, I'll do this, I'll do that." And by the time you're finished, you end up losing money. So you've got to be very, very, very careful with your business plan of how you're going to run the club and the county going forward.

18 个郡的激励问题

主持人： How important and difficult was it in those first stages, where creating the business plan you incentivized 18 counties — not just eight who had a franchise attached to them?

Colin： With this new tournament, we said to all the counties — all 18 plus MCC, so there was 19 shareholders — we would guarantee you a lump sum every year from this new tournament, which off the top of my head I think is one and a half million a year. We guarantee you that extra money, and that swayed a lot of them straight away, because you were giving them one and a half million unfettered per year in their income streams. That convinced a lot of them.

主持人： Can we just put that into context? In sport we often get pretty careless with our numbers, and money suddenly doesn't mean a huge amount. One and a half million for a lot of the counties is a huge amount of money, right?

Colin： It was saving grace for about six of them, to be honest, because they were so close to going under, some of them. So that was a big incentive for a lot of the others in the middle. It was nice to have for the big ones at the top. It didn't matter — one and a half million to ones who were doing 20, 30, 40 million pounds revenue per year, it didn't really matter. It was nice to have, but it wasn't the be-all and end-all. So we pitched it what we thought was right to make it attractive for the counties to vote for it and to help them survive in the future.

九、约克郡的故事：从 2500 万债务到 1500 万银行存款

主持人： Can we then use this as a seamless transition into Yorkshire and how you have uniquely, in the context of the other counties, approached the sales process — selling 100% of your stake?

Colin： I went back to Yorkshire in February 2024, and the debts had spiraled up to 25 million pounds again. They got it down to 15 million in 2020. So in four years, it had spiraled by 10 million pounds of debt.

主持人： How?

Colin： Good question. Mismanagement. Cost of the racism scandal that fell on Yorkshire in 2020-21 cost Yorkshire a hell of a lot of money on legal fees, compensation for people who got made redundant and lost their jobs. There was a lot of poor decisions made in Yorkshire over those two, three years which cost the county a lot of money.

They even had an Ashes test match during that time and they still lost money. I still can't get my head around that.

主持人： And historically that wouldn't have been the case. Ashes…

Colin： If you have an Ashes test match, you should make between 3 and 5 million pounds net profit a year, full stop. Yorkshire had an Ashes test match in '23, I think it was, and they lost £3 million. So that's the poor decisions that were made running the club.

拍卖那一夜：在巴巴多斯凌晨四点

When the idea of selling stakes in The Hundred came along — as I said, that was always our intention when we launched it — it was like a savior to us at Yorkshire County Cricket Club.

So we looked at it and we thought, "What kind of value are we going to get?" When we started this process off with Raine and with the ECB, people were talking about the most you're going to get for 100% is going to be 20 or 30 million, really, if you're lucky on a good day. So we looked at it and said fine, if we have our 51%, that'll give us 10 million, so we'll be able to knock that off the debt. So we're in a better position.

That's where we started off in December '24. Sanjay and myself looked at it and then we got the board together and said, "Right, this is possibly where we can go if we get those kind of figures." All the board were the same idea: if we can get an opportunity to get income coming in at that kind of level — we've never had it before in Yorkshire — it'll get us out of the mire that we are in.

So we took the decision then, before we knew the final figures, that we would sell our 51% stake. We went along with that. The board were happy with that. We're all going in the right direction. And of course then the whole process started, and the figures just kept going up and up and up.

I'll never forget the day when the auction happened for Northern Superchargers. I was in Barbados, funny enough, on holiday, and Sanjay was over here in the UK. I got up at 4:00 in the morning to go through the whole process. And when we ended up at £100.5 million for the Northern Superchargers, I think we just pinched ourselves and just said, "This is unbelievable."

100% 出售背后的两个理由

Because the one thing it gave us was financial stability. We sold our full 51%. Nobody else did. We did that for two reasons.

  1. It allowed us to pay our debt off, the 25 million. That went overnight. Saved us £1.5 million pounds interest operating costs.
  2. It gave us some money to put in the bank behind us.

We've never been in that position before, ever in 165 years. We were able to bring our creditors up to date, which again, they hadn't been able to do that for the last four years. Everybody got paid up to date. We had some money to invest in facilities in the ground. The groundsman got a new lawn mower and everything that he needed. It was nice to be able to say we're in a good position.

And that's where we are now. We've got £15 million pounds as investments, which we've set up as an investment company with — underneath as a subsidiary within the club. We're now looking at and saying, "Right, what do we need for the future?" And that's where we've launched our 10-year plan. If you hadn't have done that, we couldn't be where we are today.

十、去会员化：打开下一个十年

主持人： So we've now launched a 10-year plan of what Yorkshire and Headingley could look like, should look like. But how do we get there? Because to do all that, we need million pounds. So is this right? I'm going to open the box of demutualization and allow you to give a little bit of an explanation before I botch it.

Colin： The big thing about demutualization — and I've been talking to the members over the last 12 months about this — is don't be fearful of demutualization. You've been a members club for 160 odd years, 170 years, and we are where we are today as a members club. But as a members club, we've virtually nearly gone bankrupt twice.

Because the one thing with members clubs, it's very difficult, if not impossible, to get investment into a members club. And the members are not the kind of members who can put their hand in the pocket and find your 30 million pounds overnight, like you do if you're an MCC member. So you can't compare it with that.

As a members club, you're limited to what you can borrow. And therefore if you want to invest in the ground — which we need to do at Headingley — it's okay as it is, but the vision for the next 10 years is what can it be? And this is what we're saying to the members: look to the future, not where you are today.

25,000 座全坐席体育场的愿景

Do you want Headingley to be a 25,000 all-seater brand new stadium — new facilities, new changing rooms, new hospitality, new entrance, all singing, all dancing ground that will compete with anybody in the world? And you as members would have access to all that. We're going to take nothing away from you whatsoever. Nothing. All your rights as a member will be protected. In fact, going forward, we'll even give you shares in the new company. So there's an upside.

What we're saying to you as a member is: we're taking nothing away from you, we're going to give you shares in the new company, and we're going to make sure that we get this investment into the club to protect both the ground and the club for the future. And if we do that, then Yorkshire should remain at the top of the tree both on the pitch and off the pitch. That's the message we're putting across to the members.

We could sit there and do nothing — I'm not being unkind. I could put my coat on, walk out the door and say, "Right, I've sorted it all out, you got 15 million quid in the bank, everything's hunky dory," and walk away from it. I don't want to do that. I've got — I want to finish the mission, what I started in 2002. So that's one of the things I want to do. But I'm doing it for the good of the membership going forward. I'm not doing it for the good of me. I don't make anything out of it and I don't want to make anything out of it. This is for the good of Yorkshire cricket for the next 50 years, and that's why we're doing it.

流程与反对意见

主持人： And how has the process gone so far? Because it's been talked about for a couple of years if I'm correct.

Colin： We started this off a year ago, around about the time we were selling The Hundred stake. We launched our 10-year strategy in April this year. Sanjay put together a presentation for our members and we had 200 odd members who came to Headingley and we presented our 10-year strategy of what Yorkshire could and should look like. Then we followed that through at the AGM in May, talking about demutualization and what it would look like and how it would evolve.

We've started the process off. We're talking to the FCA to make sure we do everything legal and properly to change it from a members club to a limited company.

We've got opposition. There's people who don't like it and saying, "You shouldn't be doing it. We want to stay a members club." If that's what they want, then fine. If that's the way they want to leave it, then fine — stay as you are. But if you stay as you are, in 10 years' time, realize where you will be in the set-up of English cricket. Because you're not going to be one of the big grounds like Edgbaston, Old Trafford, Lord's and The Oval, who've got 25,000-plus brand new stadiums. We're not going to be in that league. If you want to be in that league, you've got to invest to get in that league. And the only way you get the investment is change it to a limited company.

十一、测试赛分配：北方的呐喊

主持人： So am I right in saying this current 5-year cycle of test allocation — you said there are three years where Headingley doesn't have a test?

Colin： Sad to say really — when I got there in '24 and I looked at the allocation, out of five years, three years we don't have a test match. Well, if we don't have a test match, that is a minus £1.5 million pounds per year on our P&L, which is horrendous.

Without The Hundred money, if we'd have carried on with no Hundred money and the allocation we had, we would have gone bust in '29. That's how important the allocation system is from English cricket's point of view and from Yorkshire's point of view.

And that's one thing which I believe has gone a bit astray. Your next year, 2027, we haven't got an Ashes test match in the north of England, which I think is criminal to be honest.

Yorkshire 板球文化的体量

In Yorkshire alone, people won't realize this: on a Saturday in Yorkshire, there are more people playing recreational cricket than the whole of Australia together. That's how big cricket is in Yorkshire. We have more women playing cricket in Yorkshire than any other county by far. We've got 850 odd cricket clubs in Yorkshire. Cricket in Yorkshire is massive. It is part of the DNA. It's part of the heritage. That's why it's important to Yorkshire people.

But to not have test matches allocated to Headingley — when you look at the test matches we've held at Headingley with all the great occasions of Botham and Stokes and all, we always get good crowds there, we always get good matches, it's a good wicket, ticks all the boxes — to not have three test matches out of five years, I found unbelievable.

主持人： I asked you before if there had been a justification for that, and you said there hadn't. But I wonder if you can create that environment — that state-of-the-art infrastructure, the 25,000 seater stadium, the incredible experience — it almost becomes impossible to not bring cricket into that environment. And right now with the challenges that you have with the ground, the infrastructure, it's almost an easy out if asked the question of, well, the ground can't support what the Ashes demands.

Colin： And that's another reason why we need demutualization. So they can't use that against you to say, "Well, you haven't done this, you haven't done that, you haven't got this, you haven't got that." From that point of view, demutualization's important.

十二、生态系统：投资约克郡 vs 投资县级板球

主持人： It is hugely important, but it also needs buyers and investors willing to commit the capital required to make that valuable — right? — to buy into the club as a private entity. The appeal of individual assets is hugely important. But as we see often across sport, that's only part of it, because you're then part of a wider ecosystem. And if that ecosystem is struggling, there's only so much you can do to build it. You can build a brilliant infrastructure and everything internally, but is the quality of the performance you can put on the pitch the same when you're playing other counties? How does that build into it?

So when you're having these conversations, how much of it is about creating a really investable proposition into Yorkshire, versus also trying to look at the wider county cricket landscape?

Colin： It's both, to be honest. You've hit the nail on the head. If you do the new development at Headingley and you end up with a brand new ground, 25,000 all-seater stadium, you have a sky bar at the top of the pavilion, you have new hospitality suites, you have a small conference center — the whole thing becomes a different entity from an income point of view.

By doing that, you end up with a business plan — provided we get a test match every year. We'll miss the odd one and we can handle that. But provided we get a good allocation in matches, that business at Yorkshire can and will generate £3 million a year net profit without any problems if we do that development. Because you've got the infrastructure to facilitate the hospitality, the sky bar, the restaurants, everything that goes with it. We haven't got those ahead of it. So you need those from an investment point of view, which will bring the income in.

和 Sunrisers 的关系

主持人： Sunrisers got the franchise, right? So Sunrisers — how much influence or interaction is there between your — even though you've sold it…

Colin： Yeah, we talk to them on a regular basis. But it's what we said to them from square one: "You know, you bought the franchise. It's your train set. You're now the train driver — with the facilitator." We make sure the ground's there and everything to work with you on that. They're running the team. We have nothing to do with running the team selection. That's all down to them.

Sanjay and myself are on the Sunrisers Leeds board. We've got a board meeting with them in July. You know, carry things on and look forward.

Headingley 作为品牌

The one thing which we realized as well, when we sold The Hundred, was — part of the process was getting investors to come and look at Headingley and what we had. They — and not only them but others — they were blown away by what we had at Headingley, the history, the tradition. When we showed them the space where Don Bradman sat when he scored his 300 and all that sort of stuff, they just couldn't believe they were on that field and in that dressing room where Bradman had sat. We've got all that behind us.

The two good things I think we've got at Yorkshire is we've got two brilliant assets. We've got Headingley Cricket Ground — if you travel around the world and you talk about cricket, most people know Headingley all around the world — and we've got Yorkshire County Cricket Club, most successful one who's won most championships ever. So we've got two fantastic brands — a ground and a club. By pulling those together and maximizing the potential of that, investors I believe will look at it and say, "We need a part of that."

主持人： Did that give you the confidence when going into these conversations that there was investment to be had beyond these franchises?

Colin： Yeah. And the other thing that we did find when we were talking to investors for The Hundred — first question they always ask, "Well, when are you demutualizing? Because when you do demutualize, we'd be interested in talking to you." Not just Sunrisers — a lot from America and all over the place. So we know that the interest will be there as and when we can get to that position.

十三、行业领导：为何其他郡都在等约克郡

主持人： I mean, it may seem naive, but do you speak with other counties about the value of this collectively doing it and creating that infrastructure? What we're really talking about is the future of these historic institutions that are the bedrock for cricketing success in this country.

Colin： I firmly believe that if we get demutualization through, a lot of other counties will follow. I think a lot are waiting for us to have the first battle to see where we end up — on the receiving end or the good end.

But I think the whole game of cricket will be looking at it in future, because members clubs — they're just not investable. And as I said, MCC is different. You can't compare it with that. The Oval's different. You can't compare it with that.

From that point of view, you've got to look at all of them and say, "What's a traditional county club like Sussex or Kent? What are they like going forward?" And they're the ones who will struggle. Hampshire did it — Rod Bransgrove did a fantastic job at Hampshire. Where Hampshire was — well, they were insolvent, they were gone. Rod picked it up and over 25 years built a fantastic stadium at Southampton, invested in it, and look where they are now and what they've done.

十四、为什么从前能跑通，现在不能？

主持人： I'm interested in this as well. What was it that made the structure successful in the past? Let's say in the '70s, '80s, you could run it as a business in that way, compared to what makes it challenging now. What's changed that means that this type of investment is more important?

Colin： I think there's two or three things that have changed since the '60s, '70s and '80s. I think people today going to sporting events like good facilities, like good food, like everything that goes with it. In the '60s, '70s, '80s, you went and, provided it had a bar and it had a hot dog van, you were happy. You didn't need anymore. But now I think the expectations of big stadiums needs better facilities all the way around.

英国 vs 澳大利亚：公共基建的缺席

The big difference I've seen over the years is the investment in all our grounds in England has had to come from the county cricket clubs. If you go to Australia as an example — Sydney, Perth, all the big grounds have been built via the national government and the state government. They've been funded by those. The Sydney Cricket Club hasn't had to put a penny into it. Perth, Western Australia, they haven't had to put a penny into it. It's been built by the state and the national government.

We've never had that in the UK. We've never had a penny put into Headingley from local government, national government, anything. We've had to fund it all ourselves. So we've had to beg, go out, friends, all the rest of it, who can help us doing this.

If you look at the changing rooms and the pavilion at Headingley — that was built by Carnegie University, cost them £35 million. They built it as a teaching venue. We weren't going to say no to £35 million for somebody building what they perceived would be a pavilion and a teaching facility. They've now pulled out of it. We got it for virtually next to nothing. So we've now got to change that from a teaching facility into proper changing rooms.

That's how we've developed Headingley — working with people, businesses, universities, all those kind of things over the years to help us achieve where we've got to now. And we've always been short of money. That's been the problem with cricket.

十五、县级板球的重构：21 支队伍，三个级别

主持人： County cricket, test match cricket — we mentioned the struggles at the beginning that test match cricket has, and the two are very closely linked, right? County cricket is the feeder into the test system. So for us to have thriving test environments, you need to have a thriving county environment. What do we need to do to ensure that the county game or product is of the highest quality?

Colin： Again, I think we're at a place now where we need to look at how we structure county cricket and how we structure that whole game. Personally — and this is just my personal view — I would change it, because I think it's getting ready for change. It's been in its format now for a long while.

Personally, I would play the 50-over competition at the start of the season, April and May, where if you do have the odd bad day — rain and all the rest of it — you can play the next day if it's a one-day competition, which is fine. It gets the players out on the grass and it gets them used to red ball.

Play the Blast as it is now in the middle of the summer. The Hundred will stay as it is. But I would change the County Championship to 21 teams. I'd bring three new teams in. I'd have seven teams per division. So you play 12 matches — six at home and six away.

主持人： Oh, that's brilliant. We'd always say, "Oh, there's too many counties — do we reduce it?" If you went down that route, it's easy to say, "Right, there's too many counties."

Colin： I don't think that is the case. I think what some counties have got to realize is that where they are and how they're structured — would they be better playing semi-professional cricket in a lower division at the third division? So you have like the Premier division at the top with a top six, and the top six will end up where they end up. When I said that, people say to me, "Well, what happens if Yorkshire end up in the third division?" Well, if they do, that's our fault. Can't blame anybody else for it. We know the rules, we know how it's set up.

But I think it would make it more interesting. And if the third division then became semi-professional, it would help those other counties who are struggling from a finance point of view and salaries point of view, to get them playing a red ball format of cricket.

观众与直播的悖论

主持人： What about audiences and demand? Do you think there is the attention?

Colin： Well, that's the problem you've got in county cricket — the number of people who go watch it. And now you can watch live stream, because that's another thing which hasn't helped. It's helped the fan — he can watch live stream Yorkshire play Warwickshire no problem without getting out of his chair. You didn't have that 20 years ago.

A lot of counties are lucky if you get two or three thousand people turn up to watch a county match. Most of those are members, so they've paid the membership already. So it ends up costing you money to open the gates, because you got stewarding and everything else. Most of them bring their own food and the sandwiches and everything else. So from that point of view, county cricket doesn't generate you income.

But it's played at the wrong time. People are at work. So they've tried to fix that by starting most matches now on a Friday. So you got Friday, Saturday, Sunday, Monday. Has it really worked? No. Because people who follow cricket are playing cricket on a Saturday and a Sunday.

主持人： Does the interest you have around Yorkshire for cricket — has that been seen in the attendances at the county games?

Colin： No. If we get 3 or 4,000, that's great. If we play Lancashire, it might get up to 5,000. Or if we play Durham — they're in second division this year, but they'll swim it back. The local derbies, as we call them, Durham and Lancashire, you might get up to five.

The county that's done really well with the county game is Surrey. They've done a fantastic job. Their attendance is really good. But again, if you look at the demographics of where Surrey is and The Oval, a lot's going for them, and good luck to them. They've maximized it.

十六、走出种族主义风暴

主持人： You mentioned that there was a financial challenge with what happened a few years ago within the racism storm. What about coming out of it for just the club as a brand, and being able to ensure that you're putting it back on the right track and something that everyone's proud to associate with again?

Colin： Virtually, we're now in a real good place to be honest. I went back there in '24 and the club was on its knees both on the field and off the field. The heart had been ripped out of the club with all the accusations and everything that went around it, which wasn't good.

Since then, we now put everything in place with EDI and everything that goes with it — safeguarding, the whole shooting match. We've got a new executive in there. We've changed the coaching structure. All those kind of things have helped.

We've now restructured the pathway to get the kids coming into the pathway from all around the county. One of the big things we did was get Moeen Ali to come and join us this year. I don't know if anybody's seen his interview two weeks ago when he said a lot of people advised him not to go back to Yorkshire. What he's seen is just the opposite. He's come to Yorkshire and he's seen we've got everything in place. There is not that racism scandal around us anymore. People have seen we've changed, we've done the right things, we got the right procedures in place. If there are any issues, they're handled properly. They're given to the cricket regulator.

So we're in a good place from where we were. But it's been a challenging two years, three years to get us back to where we were.

十七、青训通道：从"幼儿园"回到精英培养

主持人： For the pathways piece and getting lots of kids playing the game — such an important part. It's more competitive than ever, where there are so many options for children to go and play lots of different sports.

Colin： It's massive. In Yorkshire, the one thing we've always been proud of is producing England players and top quality players. We've been good at that over the years.

We went back there and the pathway had become — don't know how you explain it — the pathway had become a kindergarten. Where people and families were sending the kids to the pathway because it was free. So you give everybody it free, of course everybody turns up. But are they really interested in cricket, or do they just want somebody to look after their kids for two or three hours while they go shopping or do all the other things? That's what our pathway had become.

So again, we had to start again, restructure it, new coaches in, put it into four regions — north, south, east, and west. So it's now in place to get us back to where we were. It will produce results.

打破排他性：Cricket All Stars

主持人： Cricket has always had the challenge, particularly in this country, of access and it being seen as partly exclusive. I've seen a lot of work going on to change that. Do you see a lot more opportunity for children who haven't grown up with the luxury of great facilities and access from a private school system to actually get involved in the game?

Colin： Yeah. And again, this is another thing we did at the ECB in 2017-18. We launched Cricket All Stars, which was a way of getting the younger kids from 6 to 10 into cricket who've never been anywhere near cricket. I believe that program now is producing 100,000 kids playing Cricket All Stars every Saturday at their local cricket clubs. People forget we launched that in '17-'18. Before that, we didn't have anything.

We've done the next program from 10 years old to 15 years old. So there's a program now there for kids to get into cricket who never could get into cricket before. And the local clubs are picking that up and running with it and doing a great job with it.

十八、ECB 的黄金五年

主持人： When you were at the ECB, you oversaw the creation of one of the most successful short form cricket tenures that England's seen in its 50 overs and its T20.

Colin： Well, when I look back — I took over in 2015 and I left in September 2020, five and a half years basically. In that time:

  • The England men won the World Cup in '19
  • The women won the World Cup in 2017
  • We launched The Hundred in a new format with a new audience
  • We launched Cricket All Stars for the kids
  • We won an Ashes in 2015

So for 5 and a half years, we didn't do bad. The game, I think, in those five and a half years came together — both recreationally and professionally — because it was joined up and everybody seemed to be going in the right direction. And you always do better on the back of success. So when we won the World Cup, the men and the women, that gave everybody an impetus of "I want to play cricket." The Hundred certainly helps. All Stars — all those kind of things generated that new enthusiasm for cricket.

十九、结语：领导力与下一段旅程

主持人： It's very easy for us in all of these types of things to sit here and always be critical of things, but I don't think you ever really want to do that. It doesn't help. When you're looking at this now moving forward, I get the feeling that you think if you can get some of these things through that you need to do — from a Yorkshire perspective, but also from a broader cricketing perspective — then a lot of the challenges that the game's facing can be managed. It just needs some really strong leadership at the moment to get that through.

Colin： Yeah, I think the one thing which I've always been able to do is get a good team around me of executives and board people who have got the same vision of where we need to go and where we need to take the game or the business or whatever it is going forward.

And that's really what we need now, I think, is to look at it and say: take a breath. We've got the income over the last year coming in from The Hundred. Don't sit back and just do nothing and relax and say, "We've cracked it" — because we haven't. Far from it. I think we're just starting the next challenge and the next journey. That's where I'm coming from to say cricket needs to look at the next 5 to 10 years and change — and change to get ahead of the game, not playing catch-up.

个人热爱的根源

主持人： You must still love it. This must still excite you, if you'd still be doing this now. As you said, you don't need to be — but there must be something in here which really keeps you motivated.

Colin： I've been fortunate over the years. I was brought up on a farm in Yorkshire, out in the sticks. My first recollection of playing cricket was as a 5-year-old. We had our own cricket pitch in our grass field, and my dad put a cricket bat in my hand. And I remember my brother bowling me out, and I knocked the wickets down with the bat, and my dad came along and whacked me around the ear and said, "Don't you ever do that again," etc.

I've been at cricket ever since then, basically. I started playing league cricket when I was 13. I played for the grammar school, did all that. So cricket has always been my passion. It's always been in my blood. It's always been my love. It's my first sport.

And Yorkshire to me is — well, it's the holy grail. That's why twice I've gone and helped sort them out, because I couldn't see that disappear — which it would have, without a shadow of a doubt. So yeah, I'm passionate about cricket, but I want to see it progress and progress and progress.

主持人： Beautiful way to wrap it up. Look, I really appreciate you talking us through it. There's a lot in there to unpick, but it's great to understand properly what it's like from the inside. So thank you for sharing it.

Colin： No problem at all. Enjoyed it. Thank you.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的发现：拯救约克郡的，不是板球，而是"自我背叛"
整个故事最惊人的悖论在于：**一家有 165 年历史的会员制俱乐部，必须先"背叛自己的身份"才能活下去。** Colin 反复强调"不要害怕去会员化"——这句话翻译过来其实是：*你赖以为荣的那个组织形式，正是把你两次推到破产边缘的元凶。*
更深一层的反讽是：救命的 1 亿英镑并非来自板球本身，而是来自一个被传统球迷骂作"不是板球"的新产品（The Hundred）。也就是说，**约克郡是被它最看不起的那种板球救活的**。这构成一种近乎黑色幽默的系统性真相——一个组织最深的护城河，往往就是它最大的负债表。
## 二、跨领域连接：板球的"第五天问题" = SaaS 的"长尾用户"陷阱
Colin 关于"五日制测试赛第五天所有人都亏钱"的论断，几乎是一个完美的**单位经济学**案例。第五天的存在不是产品特性，是历史惯性形成的成本黑洞——票价压到 10 镑只为填场子，运营、转播、餐饮全员亏损。
这跟 SaaS 行业里"保留那 3% 重度免费用户却消耗 40% 服务器成本"是同一个结构：**当某个尾部环节的边际收益持续

item.title: [Sequoia Capital] Why Hardware-Software Co-Design Is AI's Real 100x: Dylan Patel of SemiAnalysis
item.dc:creator: Sequoia Capital
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的发现：Dylan 的"第一个神经网络"暴露了整个 AI 产业的本质

他讲那个加油站卖香烟的故事看似只是段子——根据进门顾客的年龄、种族、性别提前把脚凳挪到对应品牌前——但这其实是整段访谈最深的隐喻。他不是在比喻，他是在描述一种思维范式：预测式的预先优化（speculative pre-positioning）。

这恰恰就是今天 AI 推理的核心技术：speculative decoding（投机解码）、KV cache 预取、batch scheduling。Dylan 的童年神经网络做的事，和 Blackwell 上跑 DeepSeek V3 时的优化逻辑，是同一件事——"如果你等用户开口，就已经晚了；要在他开口前把答案准备好。" 一个从小在小生意里训练出"延迟敏感性肌肉"的人，恰好成为了世界上最懂 latency-throughput 曲线的分析师。这不是巧合，这是一种认知路径依赖。

二、隐藏的系统性真相：硬件-软件协同设计的"100x"不是工程奇迹，而是反摩尔定律的避难所

主流叙事是：摩尔定律放缓了，所以 AI 涨幅靠 scaling law。Dylan 却悄悄说了一件颠覆性的事：单层优化最多带来 2x，但三层（硅片/内核/模型）协同优化能放大到 100x，而不是 8x（乘法）。这意味着：

AI 的真正增长引擎不是任何单一技术突破，而是"抽象边界的消融"。

这跟生物学中的"内共生理论"惊人相似——线粒体本是独立细菌，被吞入真核细胞后并未被简单"乘法叠加"，而是重组了整个能量代谢拓扑，产生了非线性的复杂度跃迁。DeepSeek 把专家形状对齐到 Hopper 的张量核心、Anthropic 把模型架构对齐到 Trainium 网络拓扑——这是数字世界的内共生事件。

更出人意料的推论：这预言了纯软件公司（model labs）和纯硬件公司（fabless chip designers）作为独立物种将逐渐灭绝。未来五年存活下来的，要么是"内共生体"（Anthropic+TPU、OpenAI+自研芯片），要么是"通用宿主"（Nvidia 故意保持 jack-of-all-trades，避免被某个 lab 绑架）。Cerebras、Groq 这类极端专用方案的风险不在性能，而在它们押注的模型架构会不会被下一次架构跃迁淘汰。

三、跨领域连接：Jensen 的"多极世界"战略 = 美第奇家族的政治学

Dylan 揭穿了一个反共识：Nvidia 给那些"明显不该投"的 AI 实验室撒钱，不是在做财务投资，是在做政治平衡。Jensen 最怕的世界，是 OpenAI、Anthropic、Google 三家垄断模型——因为模型一旦集中，硬件就被绑架（这三家都在自研芯片）。

这种"扶持小诸侯以制衡大公侯"的策略，和 15 世纪美第奇家族操纵意大利城邦政治的逻辑完全同构。美第奇通过资助微小却独立的人文学者、艺术家、小银行家，制造一个去中心化的"文化-金融多极"，让任何一个城邦都无法独大。Jensen 投钱给中国实验室、给 Neoclouds（CoreWeave、Crusoe）、给思考机器、给所有可能成为"第二梯队但不依附三巨头"的玩家——目的不是赚钱，是维持碎片化生态的张力，让 Nvidia 永远是那个"必须的协调者"。

最讽刺的洞察：Crusoe 和 CoreWeave 的核心团队大多来自加密货币行业。当年被嘲笑为"投机泡沫"的比特币矿工，今天成了 AI 时代关键基础设施的运营者。这揭示了一条隐藏规律——所有大规模、低利润、高波动的基础设施行业，都在为下一个未知的大规模基础设施行业训练人才。下次想知道 2035 年的"AI 算力之王"会从哪里来？看看今天哪个行业被嘲笑为"无意义的烧电"。

四、一个让人坐立不安的预测

Dylan 说"2040 年大多数增量算力会在太空"——但更值得警觉的是他顺口说的另一件事：人类大脑的智能/瓦特比我们高几个数量级，但"幸好这不重要"。

这句话是对碳基智能的死刑判决书，而且它被作为一句轻松的旁白说出来。它的真实含义是：能效不再是约束，规模才是。当一种新形态的智能可以无限扩展功率输入（核能、太空太阳能），它就不必赢在每瓦智能上——只需要赢在总瓦数上。这是生物学从未面临过的竞争维度。

📝 转录文本

Why Hardware-Software Co-Design Is AI's Real 100x

Dylan Patel of SemiAnalysis · Sequoia Capital

开场：SemiAnalysis 内部的"工程师 vs 对冲基金"之争

I think it's really fun inside of semi analysis because we have 90 people and like a big chunk of them are technologists engineers across the whole supply chain. Um, and then a big chunk is people who are formerly at hedge funds. And you see these arguments like people are like, "Oh, well that doesn't matter." And it's like, then someone's like, "Well, but cost." And then someone the engineers like, "No, no, no, but this technology is the coolest." And you see this you see this organically like fight it out. Um, and and we're pretty informal and you know, given the fact that I was a for moderator, you can imagine what the the enjoying it.
