不是所有 gigawatt 都是平等的

I do a little bit of a tee-up on this to turn into a question for you. As we talk about this, for me the thing that's going through my head — that is almost an alternative hypothesis for the Crusoe example — I'm going to use an analogy in oil. In oil, Saudi Arabia has way lower cost per barrel to produce oil than a lot of other countries. There's also like the purity of the oil. A lot of Saudi has generally like very low contaminants in their oil which makes refining easier, all of this.

The question for me is — when you look at for every gigawatt that's being put in the ground, if call it the 20 gigawatts coming online today — how much homogeneity do you see in those gigawatts? Is it something like — and I don't, you can tell me whatever metric you think is right — but are Google's gigawatts two times more valuable than say most Neoclouds because they have optical switches and they've been doing it for a long time and they know how to do power smoothing because — I think this could be the alternative hypothesis that some of the people that are at like — the people that are good at building data centers, they should just do it to the max because there's so much demand and there's so much better than it. But then maybe we're starting to see the early signs of the people that are not as good at it kind of getting hit a little. So I don't know the reality here. I'm just curious how you think about this.

Trainium 与 GPU 的租金价差

So so far there are metrics for this, right? Trainium sells at sub-$10 billion per gigawatt rental rate to Anthropic and to OpenAI. GPUs — at least before the craziness of the last six months — usually went around $12 to $13 billion per gigawatt. So the rental rate, and this is from a Neocloud versus Amazon even — and now when Amazon sells GPUs, they'd also be $13 or so.

And my understanding of that also is that those numbers — like Amazon subsidized that a little bit so that the disparity was even more.

It's less than 10. But there's like some weird basically — and like, look, my understanding is obviously like Anthropic played a big role in making Trainium useful in terms of writing all the libraries etc. And everything I hear is that Trainium's really freaking good hardware and it's getting way better. And obviously Anthropic now using it a lot, so hopefully we would see that price go up.

Like per the deal they did was actually like — there was a floor mechanism. And if it didn't do well it would be like cheaper and then to the point where it's cancelable. And you know if it did really well the price is kind of higher. But effectively, less than 10 is where Trainium shakes out at.

Whereas GPUs — I mean, the SpaceX deal again was like 25 or something crazy. Billion dollars per gigawatt, right? $25 million per megawatt right a year rental rate with Google. I was like, that's a crazy divergence. Now obviously if Amazon was selling Trainium today it'd probably be more expensive than 10 because the compute shortages — but you do see this already.

数据中心的 $/kW/month

In the case of data centers, oftentimes a rental price of a data center if you're doing colocation, right — not compute in there but just power — you price it generally on a dollars per kilowatt per month. And so they used to be $60 per kilowatt-hour per month, and now you see things transacting at anywhere from like 120 to 160. But different quality data centers — actually I've seen data centers go as high as 200 when the customer is not such a great credit rating and then the data center is a pretty good one. And I've seen stuff go as low as 100 still, or in India go as low as 80 because the grid's not reliable, the internet connection's not great, and it's a pretty mid data center — but at least it's a data center.

And so you see this huge discrepancy there already. In the case of data center construction, usually the pitfalls — they just fail. There's a lot of people who fail. They're like four guys — they're like, "Yeah, we — here, I bought some turbines, I put the money down for them, I'm gonna build a data center." And then they get delayed, delayed, delayed, and fail. So you have to probability weight, time weight — time lag, the teams that suck versus don't. And sort of, our data center model does that. We kind of track every data center and try and do this for every single one based on equipment that they're using and all these things.

Google 把 1 GW 当成 1.5 GW 用

One of the things you mentioned about Google is — in a gigawatt data center they'll actually put like 1.5 gigawatts of hardware. Because they have such understanding all the way from workload — you know, they're able to slosh the power around. And so instead of constantly a gigawatt of compute which typically runs at like 60 or 70% utilization in terms of power consumption — not utilization of the hardware, someone's always renting it — they're now running it at like that 60 to 70% means it's at a gigawatt and you're using the full gigawatt.

You see people doing deals with — including Google with utilities — where they're like, "Oh well I know this grid can sustainably take a gigawatt, but except for three days of the year you can actually do two gigawatts. So give me two gigawatts and then just tell me to turn off." And so they'll do that. And so these sorts of tricks — and then you need to have supreme management of workload, backup power, all these things — generators on site to figure out how to actually keep it 2 gigawatts sustainably.

When people do this, they're able to charge more. Whether it be: "I'm actually selling two gigawatts despite only having one gigawatt, because those three days a year I'll be able to deal with via battery, gas, etc." Or, "I figured out how to build power on site, now I have a gigawatt where no one else does and so I'm able to do it quickly." Um, it's not necessarily transacting for a higher price. It's that I'm selling more gigawatts. And sometimes there are levers where you're selling more gigawatts is where each gigawatt is selling at a different price.

I think it's more on the data center and energy layer — it's more about just having it versus not, and then that being delayed or not. It's more binary. But on the compute side, I do think there's a lot more interesting work there. A gigawatt given to Anthropic is objectively worth more revenue than a gigawatt given to OpenAI. And it seems that both of them could sell every gigawatt that they have right now given rate-limit problems and token-max limit and all these sorts of things at OpenAI and Anthropic. Especially since Codex 5.5 came out, it's much better. And then likewise, if you gave a gigawatt to SpaceX, you know, they turn —

My guess, my suspicion is that they probably make better use of the hardware than most people. Just like I think people underestimate how much networking experience they have from Starlink in particular and also how much just like power management experience they have from Tesla.

Yeah. People like Brett Mayo are like incredible — pretty good.

Yeah. And so I think that for me, that's actually I think probably the thing that might — I don't actually know the answer but I think that might be missing from the analysis a lot of people are doing.

I think it's also the fact that when CoreWeave builds a gigawatt, even though their GPU compute is objectively better than Amazon or Google or Microsoft's in terms of performance — we've tested the performance and reliability — the problem is Google sells it six months before they have it up. And they need to turn around and take that paper that they signed to get debt with that credit backing and then turn around so they can actually pay for the PO that they've already issued for the order that they've already issued. Whereas SpaceX was like, "No no no, this is running now, buy it right." And it's a big discrepancy when you have a balance sheet to do that versus not. And that also helps your revenue per megawatt be much higher.

Neocloud 为什么存在？

Why does the Neocloud opportunity even exist? Because if you had asked me five years ago, I would have said the hyperscalers are going to own this. And you mentioned just now CoreWeave has better performance than the hyperscalers. Like why does this opportunity exist, maybe at the macro level and then at the execution level?

Yeah. So in 2023 I wrote a report that had Amazon really hate me. It was called "Amazon Cloud Crisis." So I talked about how Amazon was the best cloud because they had their Nitro NICs which offered like tenant isolation. All the hypervisor ran on the NIC and then you could sell all the cores. And they had custom SSDs that they made and they'd buy the raw NAND and have lower cost because they'd buy the raw NAND and build their own SSDs. And they had their custom Graviton CPUs and that drove down cost per core. And so they had all these things that enabled them to sell more cores, have better security, good networking — but this was all for the traditional CPU, better storage for the traditional cloud world.

But in the AI cloud, a lot of this stuff hurt performance, right? These Nitro NICs — bad for performance, still are worse performance, although they've caught up a lot because they've had a couple iterations to improve them. But they're still worse for performance.

A lot of the security stuff doesn't matter because it's not like I'm time-splicing users or splicing a socket into many users, right? No one rents a single GPU in an 8-GPU server. No one rents a single GPU in a 72-GPU rack. They rent the whole rack. And in fact, they rent many of the racks. And then there's no like, "Oh, I rent for six hours and I give it back." It's everyone has these long-term contracts.

So the mechanics of the GPU rental market meant that a lot of the expertise of the hyperscalers fell away. And a lot of the expertise that they did have were actually some of them were detrimental, right? Network performance for Google and Amazon — they had custom networks that were better for traditional CPU and for the stuff that they were doing, but actually worked for AI.

And then in other cases it's like — well, Microsoft would save money by building their own data centers, but their data center teams were actually were not actually that great. And so when it came time to run — you know, when it was predictable building, it was fine. When it came time to actually double your forecast for the year, it's like they fell on their face and they had to go get a bunch of Neocloud capacity.

I think performance — and I think time to market's another one, right? These massive organizations — no one's getting rich from building this data center faster, right? But you look at Crusoe for example — Chase and all the other people on the team — I was going to name some people on the team, I'd rather not — these all these people are getting rich if they deliver this compute faster. They're hyper-levered equity owners.

Hey look, they're also all coming from Bitcoin.

And you're not supposed to say that.

Uh, I mean a lot of the data center — like their main data center guy came from Microsoft.

I don't know, I'm just teasing. But it's, you know, you learn a lot when you're in a very high fluctuation market.

Jensen 在下什么棋？

What do you think Jensen was playing for here? Each chess?

Jensen absolutely hates a world where all the hyperscalers have all the power. There's a reason he's blowing money on like random AI labs that I don't even know if it makes sense to. But he's blowing money and pumping them up and going to everyone around the world and saying, "You should invest in this company" because he wants to create a multipolar world. That's why he loves Chinese labs — because he wants to create a multipolar world.

A world where OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google models are the only models is one in which he's screwed.

Yep.

A world in which the hyperscalers are the only ones building compute is one he's screwed in.

Yeah.

And so of course he needs to point the allocation gun at Neoclouds, help backstop their clusters, do anything and everything. Because while today a GPU sold to Crusoe and a GPU sold to CoreWeave and a GPU sold to Google and Amazon are all the same price for him, five years from now — Crusoe and CoreWeave existing means Google TPU will be weaker and means Amazon Trainium will be weaker, and more inference being done with non-closed model labs is better for the firm.

So I think the Neocloud ecosystem is — it's these people that are wild west. These Neolabs as well — a lot of them have investments from Nvidia. It's the wild west. Some will fail, many will fail, but some will emerge as really great teams. Whether it be — oddly — Crusoe, who's a bunch of crypto guys who then started building data centers and doing flared gas stuff. Or CoreWeave who initially was a bunch of New York hedge fund guys —

They were also — and then they were doing —

And crypto guys. But then they built — you know, there were a lot of people who didn't bubble up like them, started around the same time, just failed, right?

I gotta say both those teams are phenomenal. They deserve a lot of credit, and it's like — that's your point.

Yeah, I mean my point is like — you throw a bunch of bait into the water and the best fish will figure out and survive, right? And sort of the same way with the Neoclouds — and he hopes the Neolabs as well.

We'll see if any of the Neolabs really bubble up, but Thinking Machines has a few hundred million dollars of ARR, right? That's pretty impressive — even though they've had, you know, in the media it's like, "Oh, they've lost all this talent." It's like, well, but Tinker is doing a few hundred million of ARR. That's pretty impressive for out of the gate a product that's less than six months old or whatever. And we hope the same happens to other Neolabs and so — you know, he wants a multipolar world.

结语

Truly, congratulations on the success. Thank you.

Just the last thing I'll say is — I've seen a little bit of this. I think the public, they can probably tell from listening to you how hard you work, but it's clear you've just been working your ass off for more than a decade. And it led to the last few years of being in the right place, right time. But it's unbelievable what you've accomplished, and I know it's just the beginning. So —

Thank you so much. Thank you for doing this.

Awesome.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的发现：Dylan 的"第一个神经网络"暴露了整个 AI 产业的本质
他讲那个加油站卖香烟的故事看似只是段子——根据进门顾客的年龄、种族、性别提前把脚凳挪到对应品牌前——但这其实是**整段访谈最深的隐喻**。他不是在比喻，他是在描述一种思维范式：**预测式的预先优化（speculative pre-positioning）**。
这恰恰就是今天 AI 推理的核心技术：speculative decoding（投机解码）、KV cache 预取、batch scheduling。Dylan 的童年神经网络做的事，和 Blackwell 上跑 DeepSeek V3 时的优化逻辑，**是同一件事**——*"如果你等用户开口，就已经晚了；要在他开口前把答案准备好。"* 一个从小在小生意里训练出"延迟敏感性肌肉"的人，恰好成为了世界上最懂 latency-throughput 曲线的分析师。这不是巧合，这是一种**认知路径依赖**。
## 二、隐藏的系统性真相：硬件-软件协同设计的"100x"不是工程奇迹，而是反摩尔定律的避难所
主流叙事是：摩尔定律放缓了，所以 AI

item.title: [邦妮區塊鏈 Bonnie Blockchain] 破解3種難度的加密貨幣詐騙！第3種超級難防！【邦妮區塊鏈】
item.dc:creator: 邦妮區塊鏈 Bonnie Blockchain
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个让人意外的视角：诈骗的"进化方向"不是更复杂，而是更"无聊"

邦尼姐花了大量篇幅讲三种诈骗层级——"Give me the money"、"Give me access"、"假的确认画面"。但真正值得注意的，是这三层背后有一个反直觉的进化方向：诈骗不是越来越像电影里的高科技黑客攻击，而是越来越像"日常生活的一格普通画面"。

第一层骗你做一件异常的事（转钱给陌生人）——所以容易识破。 第二层骗你做一件看似普通的事（点一下"领取"）——所以更难防。 第三层骗你做一件你本来就要做的事（确认一笔自己发起的转账）——所以几乎无法防。

这是一个极少被点破的规律：诈骗的终极形态，是让"被骗"和"正常操作"在视觉上完全一致。换句话说，诈骗不再依赖"让你做傻事"，而是依赖"让你做对的事，但在错的画面前"。这跟魔术师的原理一模一样——高级魔术不靠快，而靠让你的注意力刚好落在错误的地方。

跨领域连接：冷钱包 Clear Signing 与航空业的"读回确认（Read-back）"

邦尼姐讲 Ledger 的 Clear Signing 时，本质上是在解决一个非常古老的问题：指令的发出端和执行端之间的"信任真空"。

这个问题航空业 70 年前就遇到过——塔台告诉飞行员"下降到 6000 英尺"，飞行员怎么证明自己听到的就是塔台说的？答案是强制读回（read-back）：飞行员必须用自己的嘴重复一遍，塔台再确认一次。Tenerife 空难（史上死亡人数最多的航空灾难）就发生在 read-back 流程被压缩之后。

冷钱包的小屏幕，就是加密世界的"read-back 设备"——它把那个被电脑/浏览器/AI 污染的"听到的指令"，强制读回一次，由人类用眼睛和按键完成最终确认。

更有意思的是：这意味着加密货币安全的终极方向，不是更强的密码学，而是更可信的"人机接口"。这跟核电站、医疗机器人、自动驾驶的安全演化路径完全一致——当系统能力越强，最后那道防线越要往"低科技、可观察、人手按下"的方向退。Ledger 的小屏幕，本质上是一个"反技术加速"的设计哲学。

隐藏的系统性真相：AI 时代真正稀缺的资源不是算力，而是"最终判断权"

视频结尾提到 Ledger Agent Stacks——AI 可以建议，但人类要批准。这句话听起来像是一种保守的妥协，但实际上它揭示了一个正在浮现的全新经济学结构：

当 AI 能完成 99% 的决策链时，那 1% 的"最终批准权"会变得极度昂贵。

你会发现一个奇怪的对称性： - 过去：人类做决定，机器执行 → 价值集中在"会做决定的人" - 现在：AI 做建议，人类批准 → 价值集中在"能合法批准的那把钥匙" - 未来：谁握有"最终签名权"的物理装置，谁就掌握真正的资产边界

这意味着，一个 200 美元的硬件钱包，其实是在 AI 时代抢占一种新的稀缺资源——"人类主权的物理化身"。这跟印章、护照、签证、核按钮在历史上扮演的角色一模一样：它们都不创造价值，但它们决定价值的归属。

再扩展一层：诈骗的真正进化方向，其实是 AI 帮你做所有事 → 你失去对"是不是自己在做"的感知 。第三种诈骗"假的确认画面"之所以最可怕，是因为它预演了 AI 时代最大的存在性风险——你以为你在做决定，但你看到的画面已经不是真实的世界。

冷钱包这个看似土气的小盒子，意外地成了"AI 时代人类主权的最后一块飞地"。

一个出人意料的观察：邦尼姐的"假冒频道愤怒"，恰恰是她内容最有价值的入口

注意视频开头那段情绪流露——"我原本想低调处理，但真的太扯了"。这是整支视频里最有信息密度的一段，但完全不是技术内容。

为什么？因为它演示了一个被普遍忽略的事实：身份本身正在变成一种被劫持的资产。她讲的三层诈骗都是骗"钱包"，但她自己经历的是骗"身份"——而这两件事，正在快速变成同一件事。

链上世界有句老话："Not your keys, not your coins."（不是你的钥匙，就不是你的币）。AI 时代会出现它的镜像版本：

Not your face, not your words.（不是你的脸，就不是你说的话）

邦尼姐用她自己的身体经验，提前演示了未来五年所有创作者、KOL、企业家、甚至普通人都会面对的现实——当 AI 能伪造你的脸和声音，"我是谁"就变成需要用密码学来证明的事情。冷钱包式的"硬件身份签名"，迟早会成为人人必备的东西，而不只是币圈玩家的玩具。

📝 转录文本

开场：被假冒的频道与失真的"我"

有一大堆假冒我的频道 Bobobobo 现在在 YouTube 出现了。

哈啰大家好，欢迎再次回到我的频道，我是币圈邦尼姐。这一集我是比较激动，因为我原本想要低调处理，不想要给他们任何的 tension，但真的太扯了。而且这种假频道是检举不完，检举了也没有不见，然后春风吹又生，叫你乱投资这些杂七杂八稀巴刀的东西，然后应该就是要把你的钱偷走。

那这傻姐的真的很像吗？我自己是觉得一点都不像，当然是因为我每天听自己讲话，就觉得口音不像。然后 AI 假冒的这个嘴型也不是很像，就是那个讲话的情绪跟力道都没有到位。像真人会突然用力大声跟你讲话，这就有张力，是激动的时候也有生命力。反正真人在定格的时候，丑会很丑，但是这个 AI 假冒的好像怎么定格都没有到非常丑。反正目前我看到不管是假冒我还是假冒其他的人，都会有一种"一号表情"，很 boring 很无聊，没有什么情绪，性结没有到位。

但这些假冒的诈骗，只会越来越像。

本集主题：加密货币诈骗的演化史

这就是我们这一集要聊的——加密货币诈骗的演化史，进化到现在 AI 怎么改变这个加密诈骗的玩法。这个市场上面 right now 有哪一些骗钱的方式，然后我们要怎么样去防堵他们、杀死他们各种骗人的可能。就像下雨天过后那个蟑螂跑出来，你怎么样啪啪啪把他们全部打死。

如果你有买过加密货币、手上有任何加密货币，这一集呢，是势必得听、势必得听。

赞助说明：Ledger 限时优惠

哈啰我是 Money。台北时间从现在到 7 月 3 号早上 6 点钟，从下面资讯栏进去购买冷钱包的第一品牌 Ledger 指定的黑色或者是石墨色的触控荧幕机型，就会送你比特币。那记得要点进去看，打到骨折啦！

诈骗的三层比喻：金库、大门、钥匙

好，坏人想要骗你的加密货币可以用哪一些方式呢？假设我们去诈骗的后台战情室偷看一下，可能可以看到坏人用不同层级的方式要来骗你。

我用一个比喻来解释：就想象说你现在住了一栋房子，那它有一道很厚的大门，进去之后呢，你就会看到一个金库，里面就是你的很多很多钱、巨款。诈骗集团跟坏人的目标就是要到你的金库里面偷钱。但是坏人的计划跟偷钱的方式是不一样的——有些人是直接叫你去金库里面拿钱给他，有一些人会骗你给他这个钥匙开门。反正呢，理解不同层级的区别，就是理解现代加密货币诈骗的关键。

那这一集呢，我会教你怎么样识别跟逃过不同层级三种的诈骗，还有怎么用硬体钱包来建立最后一道防线。

第一种诈骗：Give me the money（直接把钱拿来）

第一种类型的诈骗呢，叫做"Give me the money"，直接把钱拿来啊。意思就是他要你到金库里面拿钱，直接把钱给他。

哇，这怎么做？太简单了吧——直接假装成某一个你信任的人啊，就直接跟你要钱了。

假冒名人空投（双倍返还骗局）

那可能会长什么样子呢？比如说你可能看过 YouTube 上面假频道，弄得很真，但它是假频道，播放一个这个 AI 生成的 Elon Musk 在台上演说、口沫横飞的影片。Elon 说呢：今天心情爽，对不对？庆祝 SpaceX IPO 大成功，我们要给大家福利，不管你转多少个比特币呢到荧幕画面的这个 QR Code 钱包，我就有双倍的还给你，大放送。

那很多人就会想说，哇太爽了吧，赶快把自家金库的钱全部捧出来，转到那个 QR Code 里面，要等这个双倍的回收嘛。啊等啊等啊等啊，然后就没有然后了。

助记词钓鱼（假装新手求助）

或者是你可能在 YouTube 的留言区看过有人留言说：啊我不要玩加密货币了，那我的钱包里面有钱，你们自己拿走吧，然后贴出一串什么 12 个、24 个英文单字。那这个就是所谓的助记词嘛。

或者是有人会说：哎我有这个 USDT 稳定币存在我的钱包里面，我的助记词是这一串"什么 Panda Banana Apple Canada Waka Waka"，反正就是列出一个完整的助记词，然后假装问、假装笨说：请问我怎么把钱转到另外一个钱包啊？看起来就好像是真的是新手不小心泄漏了、新手在问一个问题。

其实全部都是陷阱。

因为有人就会看到这个留言，然后想说哇，不要？这个钱包里面有钱耶，不拿白不拿，我来试试看，对不对？然后就复制了那一串助记词，登入了所谓人家不要的钱包啊。一看不得了，天哪，里面几千几万美元的这个稳定币 USDT 就坐在里面，哇太爽了吧，赶快趁别的网友还来不及看到，赶快动作，对不对？

所以可能自己会尝试想要转账到自己的钱包，然后发现：欸这个系统不够交易手续费。比如说在以太网路上面转 USDT，可能就需要以太币来付手续费嘛，那人家这个所谓不要的钱包里面就没有以太币啊。那没关系啊，我为了要转那个钱走，我就自己转一点以太币进来支付手续费，然后就可以转这个几千几万美金走了，多爽对吧？

所以这个人呢，就会去到自己的金库拿一些自己的以太币转到这个所谓"人家不要的钱包"里面。结果一直收不到钱，再转一次，还是收不到。以太币其实一转进去，系统瞬间就把这个以太币自动转到诈骗集团的另外一个钱包里面。

大部分的人根本不会懂自己哪里被骗，因为这个所谓的"诈骗不要的"那个钱包里面 USDT 是真的，但可能装了自动监控的这个 Sweeper Bot，所以你的手续费一进去就会立刻被转走。也可能会搭配什么多签啊、特殊权限的设定。反正诈骗集团他们就坐着看一个接一个被吸引进来想要把钱包里面的钱转走的人，一个一个手续费一直进来。

杀猪盘（情感操纵型）

还有一种是防不胜防啊。比如说你在 Instagram、Facebook 甚至是 LinkedIn 上面，突然收到什么帅哥美女的私讯：啊你好啊，我看你的照片、看你的经历，觉得你好迷人啊，好想认识你啊。结果每天开始跟你早安晚安，问你今天过得怎么样呢，然后就开始分享他的生活啊，接着跟你说他是做投资的啊，或者是做什么基金管理啊，或者是说他是企业家。反正呢就是又帅又美又有钱的一个人设。

当你开始对他这个敞开心扉的时候，他就会突然说：哎我最近在做一个投资，赚超多的，然后你钱转给我，我帮你买。结果你钱转给他，哇——杀猪盘。可能以为是美少女，结果是抠脚大汉用网美图来骗你啦。

这个就是骗你到金库里面拿钱来给他嘛。

如何避免第一种诈骗

当然这种诈骗还有很多种不同的变种，但情节如果是这样走，都是属于这种"Give me the money 把钱拿来"的这种诈骗。怎么样避免这种类型的诈骗呢？

  • 只从你主动选择长期信任的来源去取得各种连结。
  • 有人叫你先转钱给他，一概不要理会。
  • 只要有人要求你说这个加密货币转到这个钱包里面，你就立刻停下来。
  • 帮你代操啊、帮你质押啊、帮你解锁账户啊、帮你领奖励啊？No，都不行。

那你说，如果我真的是想要转币给这个人怎么办呢？任何涉及要转币的这个决定呢，都要换一个完全独立的管道重新确认。就是说假设你收到一个这个 Telegram 的讯息好了，假装是这个某某你信任的人跟你说要借钱，那你要做的是什么呢？不是直接从 Telegram 跟他确定说"你是不是某某某"，而是可能要从一个完全不同的管道，比如说 Facebook、比如说 Instagram，就其他的地方，比如说 Line，对不对？要重新确认一下是不是真的有这一回事，那确认之后呢，通常就会发现说第一个可能是假的。

那这种诈骗的类型呢，就是会让你有一种 FOMO、贪心跟紧张的感觉，或者是觉得说这个机会再不赶快答应就来不及了。

这种是第一种诈骗。

第二种诈骗：Give me access（把控制权拿出来）

第二种诈骗的类型呢，叫做"Give me access"，把控制权拿出来。这种类型的比较阴险，因为它不会直接跟你说"啊把钱转给我"，它会让你觉得你自己只是在做一个很普通的动作。

钱包抽干器（Wallet Drainer）

比如说你打开加密货币的钱包好了，突然发现里面多了某一个什么陌生的代币，里面还写着说：啊恭喜你获得某某某空投，点这里领取。或者说有人空投一张 NFT 给你，然后上面放一个网址叫你去兑换啊、解锁、领奖励。

你想说免费的多好啊，点进去之后呢，这个网页可能要你 Connect Wallet 连接钱包，然后网站跳出一个视窗叫你什么 Sign 签名、Approve、Confirm，然后你以为自己是在领空投，实际上就是在签一张授权书，同意这个智能合约去动用你钱包里面原本的钱。甚至更惨的就是给他无限的授权。

这种工具呢，就叫做 Wallet Drainer，叫做"钱包抽干器"。而且呢，还不一定只被偷一次——只要你这个恶意授权没有被发现、没有被取消之后，你只要再把钱转回同一个地址，还是会再被偷。

假 App、假网站、假冷钱包页面

另外还有更狠的，比如说假的 App、假的网站。比如说诈骗集团可以做一个跟主流正统交易所一模一样的网站，什么风格也很像、字体啊颜色都很像。然后最近有诈骗是直接做假的这个冷钱包 Ledger Wallet App，他可能会突然寄信给你说："欸你的账户有风险啊、什么韧体必须要更新啊、立即启用这个 Clear Signing"。反正就给你一大堆有的没的。要不然就直接跟你说你的助记词需要重新恢复啦。看起来都是很官方。

那用户一紧张点进假的网站，或者是下载假的应用程式，他要求你输入什么 24 个字的这个助记词。对方只要一拿到你的助记词，就可以直接在另外一台装置上面重建你的钱包，把你里面的资产全部偷走。

如何避免第二种诈骗：四件事

那这一种"Give me access 要你把控制权交出来"的诈骗呢，你只需要记住几件事情：

  1. 不要乱签：你只要看到什么 Sign、Approve、Confirm，不要当成这个无脑下一步的按钮，要看清楚说我现在到底是在授权什么。
  2. 不要给任何人助记词：比如说冷钱包 Ledger 的 24 个字的助记词，只能在恢复或者是设定你自己买的实体 Ledger 装置的时候，你自己一个人的时候，在安静没有摄影机的这个空间里面，直接输入到你自己的 Ledger 装置本身。什么网站、手机、电脑、浏览器、什么软体跟你要你的助记词，都不要给。
  3. 任何陌生的空投不要点：通常没啥好事。
  4. 要确认网址是真的：很多正规的这个交易所连结后面会有活动的追踪码，所以很常到底要怎么判断哪一个网址是真来假的呢？你真的要看的是这个主网域。比如说像 Binance.com 就是对的，Coinbase.com 就是对的，Ledger.com 都是官方的主网域，后面接这些活动的参数是可以的；但是你如果看到什么 Ledger-Login.com 或者是什么 Ledger-Claim.xyz.io 有的没的，或者是 Ledger.com 拼错的，这些都是假的啦。

第三种诈骗：假的确认画面（最难防的一种）

第三种类型的诈骗呢，就是"假的确认画面"。

我们一般在网站按确认，很多时候就是无脑按下一步、下一步、下一步、登入、送出。但是如果在处理加密货币的时候呢，在钱包里面按这些 Confirm、Approve、Sign，很多时候就是在对区块链签名，签下去之后呢，它就是一个正式的指令，无法回头。

这一种类型的诈骗呢，跟第二种听起来有点像，但不是很像。现在我们在讲的第三种，就有点像是你在金库门口签一张合约，这张合约本身看起来是非常正常的，只不过呢，这只是一张假的羊皮合约，真正代表的可能是"我同意让这个人动用我所有的资产"，然后你签了就惨了。

网站它用你看得懂的这个语言包装，去执行你根本没有要执行的指令。那诈骗集团呢，就是要利用这个落差，或者是这个页面让你以为你可能只是在转十块给你的邻居，可是其实他要的是你所有的所有权，但是你看不到。

这种状况在 AI 时代只会越来越猖狂。

解法：Clear Signing 与冷钱包独立画面

那要怎么样预防第三种这种类型的诈骗，就是假的确认画面呢？

既然网站的网页可以假冒嘛，浏览器、电脑环境也可能出问题，那最后转账确认呢，最好就是可以回到一个像是冷钱包一样独立又不联网的硬体装置上面。就在冷钱包这种大荧幕上面可以清楚看到真正要签什么内容，然后再亲手批准，这样会比较安全。这个其实有名字，叫做 Clear Signing。

那你可能会问说：欸你刚刚说骇客可以假冒这个电脑网站的画面，那为什么就没办法假装这个冷钱包的页面吗？Good question！因为冷钱包它不无联网啊。

听不懂？好，我们用这个品牌最大、安全纪录最好的冷钱包品牌 Ledger 来解释：

  • 我的手机跟电脑会连 WiFi 嘛，会开浏览器、会下载 App、会外挂、会收 email、点连结。所以手机跟电脑它们会暴露在很多不同的网路攻击里面，任何一步都有可能被假冒或污染。
  • 但是冷钱包的工作流程呢：电脑或是手机负责准备交易，冷钱包负责用私钥签名，签好的交易呢再回到电脑或手机广播到区块链上面。
  • 最关键的就是私钥它只留在冷钱包里面，它不跑外面的这些一般网站，也不会去装什么外挂，它也不会把私钥去交给电脑或者手机。

所以就算电脑或者是手机连到了假的网站、或者是手机画面被骗了，骇客也没有办法从手机里面把你的私钥偷走，因为私钥就是不在你的电脑里面。

而且它不是把电脑上面的画面复制到冷钱包，而是自己有自己的安全画面。这个荧幕画面呢，是由装置里面的 Secure Element 安全晶片驱动的。意思就是冷钱包的画面上面最后显示给你看的交易内容，不是那一台可能被污染的电脑或是手机决定的，而是由 Ledger 装置本身确认之后来显示。这也就是为什么我觉得大荧幕很重要。

实例演示：被骇时冷钱包如何救你

那以我们刚刚第三种诈骗的方式来举例：

假设我原本要转一个 1 万美金到我自己的另外一个 XXX 结尾的钱包，那很不幸的，我的手机或是电脑被害。所以手机跟电脑荧幕上面呢，虽然显示说我要转 1 万到 XXX 结尾的钱包，但实际上面假设我按了确认这个动作呢，其实是要转 10 万到 YYY 结尾的诈骗集团钱包里面。

但这时候怎么防止呢？如果我用的是冷钱包去确认这一笔转账，荧幕上面就会显示说："你即将要转 10 万到 YYY 结尾的钱包。" 那这时候我就会发现说——明明我要转的只有 1 万，明明我要转到的是我自己的这个另外一个钱包啊。那这时候我就可以停止动作了。

这个就是 Ledger 冷钱包 Clear Signing 最大的优点。

如果没有 Ledger 冷钱包，那我按的确认可能就叫做 Blind Signing，也就是这个"盲签"啦。就像有人叫你签文件、内容但是遮住你的眼睛，就说啊你就签这里就好了。那有了 Ledger 呢，就变成 Clear Signing，就是你可以把文件内容看得清楚，然后你自己决定要签哪里。

Clear Signing 现在也已经变成以太坊正在采用的安全标准，因为整个产业都知道嘛，盲签就是加密货币里面最危险的安全漏洞之一。

Ledger 的其他用途

DeFi 直连：Ledger Wallet Direct Connectivity

另外你在跟去中心化金融 DeFi 的这些 DApps 交互的时候呢，Ledger 也可以派上用场。以前我们用这个去中心化的应用程式，可能要经过网站啊、外挂、还有中间各种连接的工具。反正只要流程越多，就越容易被攻击。

Ledger Wallet Direct Connectivity 呢，让我们可以在 DApp 的应用里面直接连接 Ledger。反正就最后的确认跟签署还是回到 Ledger 的装置上面。现在 DApp 像是 1inch 或者是 Velora 这一类的服务，如果听不是很懂也没关系，反正就知道原理就行——把最后批准资产的那个动作拉回硬体钱包上面执行。

网站登入实体钥匙（2FA Security Key）

Ledger 也可以当成登入这个网站账号的实体钥匙，比如说 Google、X、Facebook、iCloud、PayPal、OKX、Binance、Kraken，反正就这些一大堆可以支援 2FA（双重验证）的网页都可以使用。

假设你要把 Ledger 当成这个 Google 账号的登入保护，那我就打开 Ledger Wallet App，进到 App Catalog，安装这个 Security Key App 到你的 Ledger 装置里，再登入 Google 的设定，找到双重验证，接着呢实体安全金钥，然后完成步骤就可以了。它主要就是防止被钓鱼登入。

你想说，哎这好熟哦，这跟我们常用的这个 Google Authenticator 好像一样吧？

  • Google Authenticator 就是手机上面产生一个六位数的验证码嘛。
  • Ledger Security Key 呢，就是实体装置的验证。
  • 差别就是 Google Authenticator 它的验证码可能会被你输入到假的网站，然后被骗子立刻拿去用。但是 Security Key 通常就更强嘛，因为它就是让网站跟你的实体装置直接做验证。

以后登入呢，不只是输入密码，还要拿出你手上的这个 Ledger 实体来验证。那这样就算假冒的骗子用这个假网站骗到你手上的密码，没有你手上的 Ledger，也没有办法登入你的账号。

AI 代理时代：Ledger Agent Stacks

接下来还有一个我觉得很重要的方向，也就是 AI 代理。

现在很多人都开始用这个人工智慧帮自己做什么投资研究啊、整理资料、分析市场，反正就有点像是自己的一个 AI 助理吧。比如说我可能可以问他说："我现在这个投资组合你帮我评估一下，风险是不是太高了？那我要不要调整比例呢？如果要换仓要走哪一条路径比较好？" AI 就会帮我分析，也可以帮我准备接下来的动作。

但问题就是，只要牵涉到真正动用资产的事情，AI 就不应该拥有最后的控制权。

那这就是 Ledger Agent Stacks 背后的核心概念——AI 可以提出建议，但是人类要负责批准，最后用硬体来守住这个执行的边界。

那实际的流程到底会长什么样子呢？假设我叫 AI 帮我检查这个投资组合嘛，那假设呢他建议我把这个某一部分的以太币换成稳定币，他不能直接把我的钱转走或者是兑换。真的要去执行的时候呢，交易还是回到我的 Ledger 装置上面。我就会在硬体钱包上面看到真的要批准的内容——比如说要换什么、换多少、授权什么，那这一笔交易到底在做什么，这样你就看得清清楚楚。如果我觉得合理，那我再亲手按确认；如果我觉得不合理，我就直接拒绝。

这个在 AI 时代呢，只会越来越重要。

限时优惠：三款 Ledger 怎么选？

老手已经知道了，要买 Ledger 的冷钱包呢一定要等折扣季嘛，因为单价不低，而且折扣的价格差很多。像有个限时的活动，台北时间呢从现在到 7 月 3 号的早上 6 点钟，从下面资讯栏进去买 Ledger 指定的黑色或石墨色的这个触控荧幕的机型，就送你比特币。

那这次的活动到底应该要下手哪一台呢？快速讲给你听喔：

  • Ledger Stax：完全旗舰级的体验啦。它是全世界第一台使用这个曲面有机塑料 E-Ink 的触控荧幕的冷钱包，是 iPod 之父 Tony 参与设计的。买 Ledger Stax 的黑色呢，送 80 块美金的比特币，另外还会额外送一个磁吸保护套跟这个 Ledger Recovery Key。
  • Ledger Flex：是我一直觉得用起来最舒服的冷钱包，它的触控荧幕就像是这个 Kindle 那种电子墨水的触控荧幕。买 Ledger Flex 石墨色款呢，现在送 70 块美元的比特币，也有送 Ledger Recovery Key。
  • Ledger Nano Gen5：是 Ledger 目前最入门、最友善的这个触控新机种。它是铝框加上塑胶的背板，画面呢虽然只有单色显示，但是也够大够用。折扣期间呢，买 Nano Gen5 黑色款送 40 美金的比特币，也送你 Ledger Recovery Key。

另外以上所有的产品呢，加购 Ledger Recovery，都会再送你 10 美金的比特币。

如果不想要思考的话，就可以买这个 Nano Gen5 啦；如果想要更高的质感，再去考虑 Flex 或者是 Stax。如果你长期持有加密货币，硬体钱包呢是肯定要投资一个。

延伸资源与结语

如果你想要知道怎么样设定自己的冷钱包，怎么样把加密货币从交易所转到冷钱包，或者是说怎么选这个主机跟备用机量机的搭配，接着看这一支影片。

那下面资讯栏呢，由我们官方 LINE add 还有 Telegram 电报群，如果在设定的时候呢有任何不确定的地方，欢迎进来群组询问。群友现在在币圈打滚多年都是专家啦，当然我们有专门的同事在里面很热心的协助你，里面有很多免费的学习资源。

另外如果哎你感觉到你身边的人或者是你可能被诈骗了，LINE add 有专区可以协助你帮忙报案，当然你也可以在下方留言区讨论。

辛苦存的币呢，千万千万好好保存。

如果你愿意支持我，那你可以用影片下方的官方连结 my Ledger，就会给我一点奖励，那我会非常感谢你。如果你不想用我的连结也完全没关系，但是记得就是一定要找官方的连结。

我们在币圈继续努力，下一次见，拜拜。

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一个让人意外的视角：诈骗的"进化方向"不是更复杂，而是更"无聊"
邦尼姐花了大量篇幅讲三种诈骗层级——"Give me the money"、"Give me access"、"假的确认画面"。但真正值得注意的，是**这三层背后有一个反直觉的进化方向：诈骗不是越来越像电影里的高科技黑客攻击，而是越来越像"日常生活的一格普通画面"**。
第一层骗你做一件**异常的事**（转钱给陌生人）——所以容易识破。 第二层骗你做一件**看似普通的事**（点一下"领取"）——所以更难防。 第三层骗你做一件**你本来就要做的事**（确认一笔自己发起的转账）——所以几乎无法防。
这是一个极少被点破的规律：**诈骗的终极形态，是让"被骗"和"正常操作"在视觉上完全一致**。换句话说，诈骗不再依赖"让你做傻事"，而是依赖"让你做对的事，但在错的画面前"。这跟魔术师的原理一模一样——高级魔术不靠快，而靠让你的注意力刚好落在错误的地方。
## 跨领域连接：冷钱包 Clear Signing 与航空业的"读回确认（Read-back）"
邦尼姐讲 Ledger 的 Clear Signing 时，本

item.title: [Dwarkesh Clips] How Church Wealth Made Corruption Inevitable – Ada Palmer
item.dc:creator: Dwarkesh Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

腐败作为"涌现现象"：没有恶人的恶性系统

最反直觉的洞察是：教皇制的腐败不是道德堕落的结果，而是激励结构的必然产物。马基雅维利家族讨论该给小弟弟Toto买神职付多少贿赂时，语气平静得像在商量买菜——这不是因为他们邪恶，而是因为在那个博弈结构里，"不贿赂"才是非理性的。当公爵看到敌人在操纵教廷而自己不操纵时，操纵教皇就从"野心"变成了"防御性义务"。这是一种道德的相对论坍缩：当所有人都在玩同一个囚徒困境，"清廉"反而成了对自己族人的背叛。

跨领域连接：财富的"引力坍缩"与黑洞物理

教会财富的累积过程，在结构上惊人地类似于恒星坍缩成黑洞——质量越大，引力越强，吸引更多质量，引力又更强，直到任何接近它的东西（野心、家族、王权）都无法逃逸。这同样适用于今天的硅谷大科技公司、常春藤大学录取、顶级风投基金：当一个机构积累的资本（无论是金钱、声望还是网络效应）超过某个阈值，它就从"提供服务的组织"变成了"必须被俘获的资源"。学区房贿赂校长、风投打招呼塞进Y Combinator、富豪给藤校捐楼换录取——这些都是同一个物理定律在不同领域的显现。

隐藏的系统真相：每一代人都觉得"现在更糟"——而他们都是对的

这里藏着一个让人脊背发凉的发现：但丁（1300）、马基雅维利的祖父辈（1400）、马基雅维利（1500）都说"现在的教皇比我年轻时糟糕得多"——而他们三个人都是对的，不是怀旧偏见。这颠覆了我们对"老人怀旧"的默认解释。在一个单调累积腐败的系统里，每一代的"过去更好"不是认知错觉，而是准确的经验记录。

这给我们一个不安的当代对照：如果你父母觉得"社会风气一年不如一年"，也许这不是他们老了，而是某个我们身处其中却看不见的累积过程正在发生。马基雅维利的洞见——所有制度都必须周期性地"回到根基"否则会在自身重量下崩塌——意味着改革（reformation）不是道德事件而是热力学事件：是系统在熵增到临界点时的必然相变。圣方济各延缓了崩塌200年，但延缓不等于阻止。今天我们的哪些机构正接近它们的"宗教改革时刻"？

📝 转录文本

财富累积如何腐蚀教廷

It's important for us to remember that the papacy becomes gradually more corrupted over time. This is because with every generation more people leave donations of wealth to the church. A, you know, widow who has no son and has property decides to piously leave this to a monastery. The church gets wealthier and wealthier. As the church gets wealthier, with wealth comes power. More and more power is in the state. This makes a stronger and stronger incentive for every ambitious family to send their second son to the church.

马基雅维利家书：贿赂神职的日常算计

And this goes all the way down, right? We have personal letters of Machiavelli writing to and from relatives of his where they're debating what is the correct size bribe to offer to buy a priesthood for his little brother Toto. They don't want to offer too big a bribe because it would impoverish the family. They don't want to offer too small a bribe. They've heard that another family that's after this priesthood offered an extra big bribe — that's kind of not fair. How do they respond to being outbribed?

They just write about this as the most everyday normal thing in the world. And this is a, you know, wealthy merchant prince level family. They are in the top 5% of wealth and power in Florence, but not in the top 1%. Right? But for them, it's normal to talk about paying a bribe to get a priesthood. That's just how it works.

囚徒困境：连"防御性腐败"也成为理性选择

And every generation sees the church get wealthier and have more power, and therefore the incentives to corrupt it are even greater. It even becomes a kind of a prisoner's dilemma system, because if you're the Duke and you don't manipulate the papacy — if you don't bribe the pope, if you don't work hard to get your brother to be bishop — and your enemies do, you're screwed. So you even see it as defensive: I must manipulate the church. It's the only way my people will be safe. If I don't manipulate the church, my enemies may manipulate the church and then there's danger.

And this happens all the way up on the scale of kings, where popes can make your enemy the most powerful bishop in your kingdom, or can deny you the right to marry — because inevitably the person you want to marry is a cousin and you're going to need a special dispensation to marry them. And the pope can prevent that and mess with your marriage alliances. You need the pope very desperately if you're a king. You also need the pope all the way down. And that means bribes and other kinds of incentives make the papacy more corrupt with each generation.

每一代人的共同感受：教皇一代不如一代

So the papacy is worse in everyone's lived experience than it used to be even a few popes ago. And you see every generation for 100 years say: popes are much worse now than popes used to be when I was young.

Right? Everybody says that. Dante says that in 1300. Machiavelli's grandparent generation are saying that in 1400. Machiavelli is saying that in 1500. In everybody's lived experience, the popes are getting more secular, more military and more corrupt over time. It's a gradual accumulation, and it comes to a peak — as such things do — triggering the Reformation, when it becomes so bad that there has to be a massive move against it.

马基雅维利的预言：制度必须周期性"返本"

And Machiavelli, in an interesting way, anticipates this. Because Machiavelli says all institutions are gradually corrupted and need to be reformed and returned to their foundations, or they will collapse under the weight of their corruption. He says he thinks that the papacy has been undergoing this, and that Christianity has been undergoing this, and that if not for the fact that St. Francis of Assisi — and also to some extent St. Dominic a couple centuries before his time — reformed the church and brought in a lot more popular support, Christianity would already have cracked under the weight of its own corruption 200 years before. And that it will need such a restoration again, as any institution needs, as city governments need, as republics need, as corruption accumulates over time.

If you enjoyed this clip, you can watch the full episode here and subscribe for more clips. Thanks.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 腐败作为"涌现现象"：没有恶人的恶性系统
最反直觉的洞察是：教皇制的腐败**不是道德堕落的结果，而是激励结构的必然产物**。马基雅维利家族讨论该给小弟弟Toto买神职付多少贿赂时，语气平静得像在商量买菜——这不是因为他们邪恶，而是因为在那个博弈结构里，"不贿赂"才是非理性的。当公爵看到敌人在操纵教廷而自己不操纵时，操纵教皇就从"野心"变成了"防御性义务"。这是一种**道德的相对论坍缩**：当所有人都在玩同一个囚徒困境，"清廉"反而成了对自己族人的背叛。
## 跨领域连接：财富的"引力坍缩"与黑洞物理
教会财富的累积过程，在结构上惊人地类似于**恒星坍缩成黑洞**——质量越大，引力越强，吸引更多质量，引力又更强，直到任何接近它的东西（野心、家族、王权）都无法逃逸。这同样适用于今天的**硅谷大科技公司、常春藤大学录取、顶级风投基金**：当一个机构积累的资本（无论是金钱、声望还是网络效应）超过某个阈值，它就从"提供服务的组织"变成了"必须被俘获的资源"。学区房贿赂校长、风投打招呼塞进Y Combinator、富豪给藤校捐楼换录取——这些都是同一个物理定律在不同领域的显现。

item.title: [All-In Podcast] Nate Silver Predicts: Democrats Take the House, Newsom Is Fading & AOC Might Win It All in 2028
item.dc:creator: All-In Podcast
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

表层之下：这不是一次政治访谈，而是一次"系统熵增"的诊断报告

大多数听众会以为这是关于"民主党会不会赢2026"的预测，但 Nate Silver 实际上在描述一个更深层的现象——美国政治系统正在从"概率游戏"退化为"重力游戏"。他亲口说出那句关键的话："我们能用97%的信心预测50个州中43个州的2028投票结果"——这意味着，作为一个职业概率论者，他承认自己的核心专业（不确定性建模）正在被一种叫"极化"的常数所吞噬。一个统计学家公开承认统计学正在变得多余，这是访谈中最被忽略的"啊哈时刻"。

跨领域连接：加州的"开票延迟"与印度Himalayas山区投票的悖论

这里有一个让人措手不及的对比——Nate 指出印度可以在24小时内完成涵盖喜马拉雅山区投票站的全国计票，而加州不能。表面上这是基础设施落后的问题，但深入看，这暴露了一个反直觉的系统性真相：技术领先国家在民主基础流程上反而走向"低带宽化"。这与航空业有惊人的同构性——美国是发明喷气客机的国家，却拥有全世界最慢的机场出关流程（Nate 提到 LAX 的接驳乱象）。当一个系统的"叙事复杂度"超过其"操作复杂度"时，效率必然让位于程序合法性。加州的开票延迟不是技术故障，是政治系统刻意设计的"信任延展机制"——但这种机制正在反噬信任本身。

最反常规的预测：AOC 是 Nate 的下注，但这不是政治判断，是品牌生命周期判断

Nate 用了一句话暴露了他真正的思维框架："民主党品牌很烂，共和党品牌也是。" 这不是政治学家的语言，是消费品营销总监的语言。他把2028年大选当作一次"品牌再发布"事件，而不是政策对决。在这个框架下，AOC 的优势不在于左翼意识形态，而在于她是一个"未被消费过"的品牌资产——就像 Newsom 已经被"过度曝光成 Biden 的延续品"。这与可乐市场的规律惊人一致：百事可乐永远赢不了可口可乐，但每一代年轻消费者都需要一个"反可口可乐"的符号。Mamdani 之所以能赢纽约市，不是因为社会主义复兴，而是因为他是"非民主党建制派"这个细分市场里唯一在货架上的产品。

隐藏的系统性真相：你以为的"代际差异"其实是"创伤记忆窗口"

Nate 提供了一个被埋没的精彩观察——分界线是"9/11 时正在上大学"的那一代人。这不是简单的代际划分，而是经济乐观主义的最后一个"出厂前安装"批次。在他之前出生的人，资本主义的成功叙事是默认操作系统；之后的人，资本主义是需要被论证的假设。这与日本"失去的世代"、中国"躺平一代"是同一种现象的不同地域表现——全球同步出现的"乐观主义断层带"。如果这个观察成立，那么 Nate 真正在说的是：未来20年的政治不是左右之争，而是"操作系统未升级的中年人" vs "出厂就装着不同OS的年轻人" 之间的认知战争。而这场战争，统计学预测不了，因为它的基本假设——"理性选民"——本身就是1990年代的遗产。

📝 转录文本

开场：极化是美国政治的"重力"

Partisanship is the gravity that dictates every election in the US, right? Like my job, don't worry, I will get criticized if if we say, "Oh, whatever Gavin Newsome or AOC is a 55% favorite," and they lose. But like, but 43 of the 50 states, stated, we could probably predict right now with 97% confidence who they'll vote for in 2028. And it's not because of rigging. Because polarization and partisanship are very powerful forces that we can't seem to to escape from.

广告

Starting a business usually means juggling a bunch of different services just to look legit. Not anymore. With Northwest Registered Agent, you get a complete business identity all in one place. a business address, domain, website, phone number, and more with privacy protection built in from day one. Don't spend hundreds or thousands on services you can get from Northwest for free. Visit northwestregistered agent.com/allinfree and start using free resources to build your business today.

嘉宾介绍：Nate Silver 登场

All right, everybody. Welcome back to the All-In interview program. We are delighted today to have the one, the only Nate Silver on the program. Gambler statistician podcaster Substacker. Go subscribe right now for a Hyundai. Quick Hyundai at Silver Bulletin over at Substack. It's a great Substack. How you doing, brother? Nice to see you with your Nicks hat on.

I'm good. You know, I think I have like one more week to wear the Knicks hat. It's a little bit bulky, not super podcast video friendly, but I I you know, once in 53 years, once in my lifetime at least.

闲聊：尼克斯季后赛的现场体验

Did you go to any of those games?

I went to a bunch of the Philly series. I went to um I think as Jason did, I was in Philly for like the New York City Takeover of Wells Fargo or whatever the hell it's called now, Infinity or something. Um I went to the Trump game, which was not the right game to go to, but I wanted to be there. I wanted to kind of cover it for my readers. I felt like it was a moment where New York might triumph out of this [ __ ] show that it was to get into the arena.

Um it was a You had to show up what 2 hours early to get in 3 hours.

I am I am not a particularly prompt person. No, I got I'm not wasn't going to miss. So I got there at like 6:05 for an 8:30 game. Lined up. You're supposed to get in on 6th Avenue and 33rd and I was like on 6th and 30th or 29th. It wasn't the worst. It wasn't the least efficient process I've ever seen. New York has a way of getting things done and you just kind of like follow people and and once you get in, I mean, it was like literally TSA security. They like literally like look at your wallet by hand and they take your lighters and and and your pens and your vapes and everything like that. And uh once you get in it was pretty normal. I did feel like the crowd was a little bit fatigued by the second half, which was a choppy second half where there were some questionable officiating decisions.

体育博彩与预测市场

Yeah, I actually did you bet any of the games and like did you find the odds were off anywhere?

I had the Knicks to win the Eastern Conference Finals and then after they wrapped up their series, I bet them uh to win the finals against either OKC or San Antonio. So, I actually I the ticket was not cheap. I paid it would not cover Jason's tickets, but I was able to cover uh cover my 200 level bull seat with the profits from from those non-H homer bets. I thought the Knicks were legitimately overlooked in the East. And when you see a team that wins like that game against Atlanta where we were up like literally like 77 to 22 at some point or something like I'm like as somebody who like studies this stuff and builds models to see that kind of signature significance for that level of dominance over whatever it was four rounds of the playoffs like that's that's real. That's like not noise.

Do you bet prediction markets or how do you bet big Tony?

If if you are I don't bet through big Tony. If you are someone who's pursued so I've been limited by draft kings and and MGM a lot of the uh traditional retail sports books limit you the prediction markets do not I can solve for poly market I should say. So if you're a sharp better I have other issues believe me with the prediction markets but if you are a sharp better then it becomes easier to just concentrate your activity on the prediction markets where you know you have very smart people you have hedge funds who are probably making bets in these markets more and more but yeah more more and more I don't want to go through the rigma roll of like can I even get my money down so I' I will admit I've gravitated toward toward those yes

尼克斯队的转折：Mike Brown 与 Karl-Anthony Towns

and uh when you look at this team what do you attribute because obviously during the season it was up and We were 15 games from the end of the season. People were like, "Hey, we got to trade for Giannis. We got to get Cat out of here." But in January, February, we had this incredible run where we had used Cat as the point center. And then we backed off of it and I was on Knicks Fan TV talking with like the real deep folks and there was speculation like they figured it out and they're like, "Let's hold this play." Mike Brown said, "Let's hold cat at the point from, you know, until the playoffs because we know it works so good." But what makes this team so special? Is it the the wings? Is it just Brunson's grit? Is it the three-point shooting across the team? Selflessness, ball movement? There's so many things people talk about here with this team.

I think it's Mike Brown and Cat. I'm not saying the most important components of the Knicks. I'm saying they're those are the deltas where things changed the most. Um on defense, a couple of things. Number one, you have I love Jaylen Brunson. Might be my favorite player. Um, he's not a terrific one-on-one defender. He gets a few seals, but like he's undersized. The low center of gravity somehow doesn't work on defense as well. But like every other defender of the Knicks, including Towns, which is a descent descended point of view, are are at least average, if not better. And so like kind of figuring out that system. By the way, when you rely on a defensive system that requires high effort and transition play, not playing your guys 37 minutes per game against like the Charlotte Hornets in the regular season, probably a smart move.

Um yeah, this is why Tibs had to go when you look back on it. Tibs going who ground these guys into submission for three seasons and they were

Yeah, it was like we were down one, two, or three starters for every round of the previous playoffs.

Yeah. I remember going uh to the Indiana I guess game seven of the semi-finals against Indiana in 2024 and you kind of see like OG was hobbling around out there. I guess Josh Hart kind of valiantly played and you're like these guys are like an infirmary ward.

Um and especially because you're relying on smart higheffort defense. So that part on defense and low key they became like a much better defensive team in the second half, right? Um, I remember also going this year to the Knicks Nets game in December, January, not the most fun part of the year in New York where um where they had lost like seven out of nine games and there was all the talk about like cap being traded and I just wanted to go and check out the vibe. actually bet on the Knicks to win by like 15 points or more because I thought this is either going to be like the beginning of the end for this regime or it's going to be a turnaround game against you know a team that's trying to lose frankly a cross town rebels trying to lose and the fans were like pretty tolerant of cat they wound up winning by like 60 points or something totally absurd or held the nets of one of the lowest scorers in the modern NBA era and like that team from there they by the way they won the NBA cup

yep

but like in the second half they had something like the fifth best defense in the NBA. They had the best defense in the NBA in the playoffs. Maybe not sustainable, but like but that on defense and then Cat presenting look again like I love Jaylen Brunson. The offense can be a little bit predictable maybe in clutch possessions.

And it's amazing that Jaylen Brunson I mean he shot like 53% between 16 and 22 feet which is kind of insane given that you know it's coming. It's like Marian Rivera's like cut fast ball. You know it's coming.

Um, but just to have more optionality and variety in the offense and to have more ball movement. So to have those two spontaneous things happen, the coaching system led improvement on defense plus like Cat having a revelation like I think he's like one of the 15 best players in the league. He was rated as like

yeah, he's a really good player. And so those two things combined, you know, a little bit unusual for a veteran team, granted, but spontaneous improvement on both sides of the ball. Um, coupled with the Knicks, by the way, have the most playoff games out of any team in the past four years, more than Boston, more than OKC, a lot more than San Antonio. There is empirical evidence that playoff experience does matter. Um, this comes up in in poker, too. If you are in your first big tournament and maybe you're rated the GPI number one player and you're playing in the main event of the World Series of Poker, nerves are a thing. Even in golf, nerves are a huge thing. It's a physical ordeal almost and like you're operating on a different operating system. And to have had experience in a lot of high stakes games, not all of which were wins, but like it really does it really does help.

those two comebacks from the Pacers game from the Boston games where we just broke their backs last year that I think made it obvious to anybody when we're down 10 15 points that's not a lead against the Knicks. The Knicks are going to come back. We're just that good. Um and it's just fantastic now that the entire league has to plan how do they beat the Knicks. We've spent the last like five or six years how do we get through Boston? How do we get through this team? Now the entire league has to do that.

进入正题：加州选举与"邮寄选票"争议

All right, we should get started here. We got so much to talk about and obviously you specialize in elections. We got the midterms coming up. We're going to talk about 2028 postTrump world, all of that. But we just concluded an election. An election which I don't want to speak for Dave, but you may have seen or heard the podcast where he was like, "Hey, this is not illegal, but it doesn't feel like a real election." take us through what happened in your estimation in California with Spencer Pratt fraud just mailing ball.

That was just Yeah, that was just the LA election, Jason, just to be clear.

Yeah, just to go with LA, but go ahead, build on it. Yeah. The question that I wanted to talk to you about, Nate, is when you look at the in-person return data and then you look at the early mail-in data and then the late mailin data where suddenly Nipia Ramen has this massive surge and there's this huge statistical difference as the ballots start to get counted in the days and weeks that follow. She wins. it seems very improbable that there's a true statistical difference in the population that might you know be in person mail in early and mail in late and I just wanted to get your read on that.

Nate 的回应：没有舞弊证据，但系统本身有问题

So I'd say I think there's no evidence of fraud is the short summary version. The mistake that can be made is to assume that each ballot like comes in independently when there are systematic differences between groups and when they tend to cast their ballots. So, Democrats have long been encouraged to vote by mail. Actually, it used to be more of a Republican thing, but like under Trump who discouraged mail voting in 2016 and 2020 um and 2024 to some extent, like that shifted.

So, look, I think California's system is completely unacceptable. I've done um some consulting work on like the Indian election where you like literally have polling stations in the Himalayas and they actually stagger their elections. But once election day happens, they count their vote within 24 hours. I think it's ridiculous and very failed state that California takes longer than that.

But if you have one party who systematically tends to vote later, also Democrats in both the LA race and the governorhip had more of a choice to make. Uh you know, it wasn't clear. You know, for a while it looked like Eric Swallwell was going to was going to win and then Katie Porter and then and then and then you know, Basera finally emerges. Bera like look my dad is from LA. I spent a lot of time in California and I am all on board with the criticisms of how the state is being governed at least in parts of the state. I was in um San Diego for New Year's and beautiful time of year to be in San Diego and like you can like literally run on the sand in the beach and everyone's kind of like hot and you have good fish tacos and everything else like that. I kind of understood the California of like my youth or my dad's youth or whatever else and understand the appeal of the state and I understand why LA and SF I think LA in particular kind of never really recovered from the pandemic. We can talk about that.

At the same time, it's a very partisan state. We have very partisan Democrats like Kla Harris and Gavin Newsome who emerged from the state. We saw in the redistricting referendum, I guess it was in 2025, how like you can debate whether it's a good idea or not, but like that traced with the Democratic margin in presidential years very strongly, even though for many years Democrats had been the party against gerrymandering. Um, it's a capital D democratic state. It's not an idiosyncratic blue state like Maine or something. And like so like to expect even a very talented Republican like Spencer Pratt to win the mayorship. I mean even in New York we have a bit more heterodoxy historically. We've had a a you know socialist mayor. We've had Michael Bloomberg and Rudy Giani in recent years too. California is not that way. It's much more of a a machine state.

追问："票仓收割"与统计差异

But when you look at that statistical difference, I I get the idea that Democrats mail in ballots and they show up late. But when you look at the overwhelming votes for NPIA Ramen over Karen Bass that happened late in the counting cycle versus early in the counting cycle and the statistical kind of shift over that happened and you look at the difference between in person and mail in being so statistically different and I get your point about Republicans are more likely to go in person and maybe you can make that leap there but the difference in accounting that happened over time the ratio just seems so different between ramen and bass and then People bring up this idea of ballot harvesting where there were homeless people from Skidro and the number of ballots that came from that particular region in the late mailin was kind of concentrated for NPIA Ramen. Do you get a sense that that actually is a true representation of individual voters and how they individually voted in that election?

I think the California vote count is an accurate tally of the votes that were cast. So, I mean, yeah, look, mail in.

Were they cast by the individuals that were supposed to cast them? Right. This is one of the theories is that there's ballots that are getting filled out on mass in these ballot harvesting efforts or people walk around and they say, "Hey, fill out this ballot for this person and here's a free lunch or I don't know what's going on, but like there's all these videos. I don't know if they're true or not."

I think there is propaganda around this. I also think that like mail and voting provides some verification challenges in different ways. uh you know I don't inherently see anything that wrong with a system where you go to a physical place to vote and you sign um you sign your voter log and and whether you show ID or not and then if you have like a disability or you're really in bad straight you're traveling in Albania or something then you can

选民ID法与党派的关系

that makes sense but I want to ask I just want to ask you the empirical question what is the difference in states or districts or cities that don't have voter ID and those that do politically like is that basically create a huge overweighting for Democrats. So we'll have a model out within a few weeks. Been a little bit delayed by the World Cup and the redistricting stuff. Prediction markets say Democrats are around I think 80 or 85% to take the House and 40 or 45% to take the Senate. Those both seem pretty reasonable to me. I think if anything it's a little bit low on the House. Um I think it might be more like 85 or 90.

My models surprise me sometimes, but like look, we have been through a lot of elections that are inherently hard to forecast. The House is the one where everything kind of points in the same direction. Democrats um are facing a very unpopular president, an economy that voters have a lot of anxiety about. Trump, no new Middle Eastern wars. On top of that, the very very long history of the president's party drawing a backlash and performing poorly at the midterms, the more recent history of incumbent backlash in the US and all around the world, the good results Democrats in like New Jersey and Virginia last year and special elections.

You know, look, the redistricting thing helps the GOP a little bit in the end, but you're fighting against a lot of gravity, whereas the Senate is more of a more of a numbers game. Even in a pretty blue environment, Democrats have to win some seats in red states. It seems to me that taking a big risk in Maine, which is a state that by all rights they ought to win in a D plus8 year and a D plus 10 state, but Grand Platner is only two points ahead of Susan Collins. I think I take that polling at face value. I think he is I take him in at 50/50, but like that's a unnecessary risk potentially. Um but look um the GOP will be able to in these red states a candidate like Terar Rico who is also probably in roughly a toss-up race and and Paxton is a far less than ideal Republican nominee like partisanship is still a powerful force. people are saying why are they kind of attacking Telerico on I think things that are are not above board you know critiquing his sexuality or whatever else cuz Texas is like a really red state like Trump's not popular so you're like you're kind of going for like the lowest common denominator but in our system that works a fair amount of the time so it's just a a a polling and math and numbers game in the Senate we're going to see more money spent than ever whereas the house 435 seats I think the partisan gravity is more likely than not just too much for Trump to overcome.

特朗普能扭转局面吗？

Is there anything that Trump can do or that could change in a macro out of our control perspective that could shift things more significantly for the House back to the Republicans

if he can get gas prices down? It's such a visible indicator. You drive by the Chevron station, whatever else, right? You you literally see it. You know, I think if you look at forecast, there is supposed to be some abatement, but very contingent on how long the conflict war in Iran persists in this quagmire. Um, you know, look, maybe if we uh maybe if we have a a right by by in October or something, who knows what happens then? But no, it's his unfavorable ratings are very entrenched and Democrats are very enthusiastic despite their misgivings about their own party and as much difficult as I think they'll have in 2028 nominating a candidate to vote for your generic Democrat against a generic Republican is a pretty easy ask for for Democrats who feel like democracy on the line and like and like so I think that's fairly baked in as someone who builds models. I don't been on poss. I've been on sports, but I'm always aware that like, yeah, there's the 10% chance that the underdog you never see coming prevails. You know, Cape Verie ties with Spain or something. But like that's pretty fundamentally driven. Whereas on the on the flip side, needing to pick up four Senate seats when you might blow the opportunity in Maine, it's a toss up. probably probably slight edge to the GOP with maybe gas prices and Iran determining whether it's more truly 50/50 or maybe maybe 60% GOP

参众两院的相关性

the chance of winning both is that you know 80 time 40 type situation and you're or is it different because of politics and and the way the statistics work correlated?

Yeah. Yeah. They're they're almost perfectly correlated. So if the odds of Democrats winning the Senate but not the House are like are like 1%. And if you go to Cali or Poly Market, whatever else, they have to be like around 1%. So condition on Democrats winning the Senate, they'll almost certainly have won the House. They'll almost certainly have won governorships, which by the way, these things are important. You know, I think people probably focus too much on the national picture and not enough on on what happens at the state level where people actually have executive power and you're picking the the candidates and the policies for the next generation. State legislatores are important. If I were talking to all the listeners, right, I'd say look for like a a city council candidate for whom your contribution might make a lot of difference. But like no, if Democrats win the Senate, that means it's a really blue year to overcome the fact that you're winning in places like Ohio, Iowa, Alaska, and if that happens, therefore probably the rest of it. All the dominoes fall that way.

Gavin Newsom 2028：上升还是下滑？

All right, Gavin Newsome in 2028. He's an enigma of a politician, worstrun state in the union as far as I'm concerned, but he's so polished and he speaks so well and people seem to like him with that great hair. What should we take from Gavin Newsome's, you know, continued ascension? Am I correct that he's still, you know, on the up?

He's not. So, we have an article up on Silver Bulletin, which we'll publish shortly after we finish this podcast. Newsome has fallen in Democratic primary polls from around 25% to 15%. He's fallen on poly market from like 33% to 22% or something. I think he was benefiting from some degree of name recognition. Whereas now Democrats see a candidate like John Oaf in Georgia um another good-looking young guy but like actually has a credential which will never have of having won an election in a purple state. Um and being a younger fresher face like whatever you say about Graham Platner and Janet Mills in Maine I think Mills in the abstract might have been the face safer choice. I think it's good that Democrats are showing a bias toward younger candidates, right? Like that's a lot of what happened with Zoran, too.

You know, I wouldn't discount, by the way, someone like an AOC. I'm not sure if she'll run. Someone like Chuck Schumer might be very vulnerable in the 2028 primary, for example, she has a lot of time to wait, but like I could see like I again in New York, it's New York, so therefore you can't extrapolate too much from New York. However, people saying we are sick and tired of the Democratic establishment. I'll take a more septous version or a more leftwing version, but not the same. I think ultimately Nuome has like a a hard argument to make. He's argued for continuity with Kla Harris and Joe Biden. He's embracing Joe Biden when that strategy has failed electorally. I would argue in California governance-wise as well. It to me it's like a I think Newsome is actually in a pretty defensive position and that's why you see him having Hunter Biden or something on his podcast.

AOC：黑马候选人？

Yeah. Yeah. And AOC is now 36 years old, so she qualifies. You're you're saying it's not a zero chance that she could become the equivalent of Trump, the outsider or the burn it down, the I'm sick and tired vote.

She's my She's my bet. Yeah.

That's your bet. Okay. What do you think, Nate? Let's talk about AOC. She got that possibility with her charisma.

We've seen in states like Maine, we may see this in Michigan as well, where Yeah. I mean, um, look, for many years in the Republican primary, the notion was that the establishment always won out. That you'd have like the Rick Santorums or Sarah Palins or Herman Kanes or whatever else who would get 40% of the vote, but the same country club establishment types we talked about at the top of the show would prevail at the end of the day. And then it doesn't take that much of perpetually growing dissatisfaction or generational ter like young people in general like don't understand like why the would I care about like a party brand anyway like the Democratic party brand is like a a lame brand as certainly the GOP is.

Um, interesting. And so if you're an avatar for that brand then like you know I look I think the best candidates even Obama in 2008 obviously not even Obama Obama was a highly effective one of the you know generationally charismatic candidate but even he combined on the one hand positioning to the left of Clinton on issues like Iraq. On the other hand, he had a whole whatever we have gay sisters in blue states and red states and we play little league in the blue states and like that whole like postpartisan rhetoric. I think the most effective candidates can exactly what he is. He has made some efforts. I mean he had Charlie Kirk on his podcast. I think it was last year, right? He has tried to moderate. I mean he's against the billionaire tax. Um he's tried to moderate on some of the culture war issues like nominated a institutional candidate from California. She didn't come that far from winning, but she lost all the swing states by by, you know, two to five points. And like I don't believe at the end of the day, the Democrats will be persuaded that like, oh, just cuz you're white and you have different anatomy like that that that one that every Democrat was terrified by in 2024.

收尾

All right, an hour with our guy Nate Silver. Let us know what you think in the comments. We will have you back on when we do our live election coverage for sure if you're available. And the audience always loves when you go.

He gets paid a lot on that night. J I don't think he's coming on all

whatever. I mean he's a fan of the He's a fan of all win. Everyone's like he's part of the family. Oh, come in the next day. Whatever. Whenever you're not securing the bag to secure more Nicks ticket for our repeat next year. Everybody go right now if you hear my voice to the silver bulletin. Pop out a Hyundai and subscribe. You're going to love

95 bucks. Jason,

that's what I round it up to a Hyundai. But yeah, listen. I know that you've probably done the metrics. 94.97 is the right number for the guy at the door. Five bucks for the guy at the door.

Whatever. A little bit off the top. It's all good. And uh yeah, everybody go to Poly Market, place some bets. Our favorite partner, Nate's favorite partner. And uh yeah, we'll see you all next time on the All-In Interview Show.

Thanks.

Great job, Nate. You rock. Thank you so much. That was awesome. I'm going all in.

item.description:
## 表层之下：这不是一次政治访谈，而是一次"系统熵增"的诊断报告
大多数听众会以为这是关于"民主党会不会赢2026"的预测，但 Nate Silver 实际上在描述一个更深层的现象——**美国政治系统正在从"概率游戏"退化为"重力游戏"**。他亲口说出那句关键的话："我们能用97%的信心预测50个州中43个州的2028投票结果"——这意味着，作为一个职业概率论者，他承认自己的核心专业（不确定性建模）正在被一种叫"极化"的常数所吞噬。**一个统计学家公开承认统计学正在变得多余**，这是访谈中最被忽略的"啊哈时刻"。
## 跨领域连接：加州的"开票延迟"与印度Himalayas山区投票的悖论
这里有一个让人措手不及的对比——Nate 指出印度可以在24小时内完成涵盖喜马拉雅山区投票站的全国计票，而加州不能。表面上这是基础设施落后的问题，但深入看，这暴露了一个**反直觉的系统性真相：技术领先国家在民主基础流程上反而走向"低带宽化"**。这与航空业有惊人的同构性——美国是发明喷气客机的国家，却拥有全世界最慢的机场出关流程（Nate 提到 LAX 的接驳乱象）。**当一个系统的"叙事复杂

item.title: [Bits + Bips] Digesting Strategy: What's the Next Catalyst?
item.dc:creator: Bits + Bips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个比内容更耐人寻味的"错位"

最大的意外不在转录里，而在转录与标题之间的断裂。一个名为《Digesting Strategy: What's the Next Catalyst?》的财经播客（Bits + Bips 是知名加密货币/宏观节目），配上的却是一段"What's in my bag"的网红日常 vlog。这不是简单的字幕错配——这是当代信息生态的一个微型病理切片：标题作为"流量诱饵"与内容作为"实际供给"之间，存在系统性的解耦。你以为在消费分析，其实在消费包装；你以为在追问"下一个催化剂"，得到的是一支唇彩。

跨领域连接：唇彩、相机、钥匙——一个微型资产负债表

如果硬把这段 vlog 读成财经文本，会发现它意外地构成了一份"个人流动性结构":

  • 相机 = 生产资料（内容创作者的"资本品"）
  • 唇彩 = 情绪资产（高频使用、低单价、心理刚需，类比散户的"meme 币持仓"）
  • 钱包 + 钥匙 = 传统价值储存与访问权（法币 + 物理产权）

她说"That's pretty much it"——一种极简主义的资产配置。真正的催化剂（catalyst）从来不是新增什么，而是发现你已经拥有的东西就够了。这恰恰是当下宏观叙事被忽视的反面：市场总在追问"下一个推动力"，而最强的 alpha 往往来自识别已经在场却被低估的要素。

系统性的真相：注意力市场的"误投递"才是常态

平台算法越优化"相关性"，错配的长尾反而越显眼——因为正确匹配被默认吸收，只有错位才会被注意。这条转录之所以会被推送给你，可能正是因为它的"反常"触发了某种推荐权重。换句话说，错误本身是一种被市场定价的信号。下一次你看到"标题与内容不符"，别急着关闭——那里往往藏着平台运行机制最诚实的自白。

📝 转录文本

开场

Everyone wants to know what's in my bag, and I'm finally going to show you. This is the bag I carry every single day.

包内物品清单

  • First up, I have my camera because I'm always vlogging.
  • Then I have my favorite lip gloss — I cannot live without this.
  • And of course, my wallet and my keys.

收尾

That's pretty much it!

观看原视频 →
