item.title: [YC Root Access] The AI Agents Helping Home Services Book More Jobs
item.dc:creator: YC Root Access
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、表面看是"AI 客服"，实质是一场"白领工厂的丰田革命"

大多数人看 Aoka 的故事，会停在"AI 替代人工接电话"这一层。但真正值得注意的是文中一闪而过的一组数字：客服岗位的两年流失率接近 100%——这意味着这个岗位的"经济寿命"比一只仓鼠还短。换句话说，他们替代的不是"工作"，而是"折磨"。

更微妙的是接下来发生的事：留下的 CSR 被提拔成"AI 训练师 / Agent 团队长"。这其实是 1980 年代丰田 kaizen 的语言变体——把流水线工人从"被工序奴役"变成"重写工序的人"。Aoka 在不自知的情况下，把日本制造业 40 年前的劳动哲学，移植到了美国客服行业。这是一次"白领工厂的精益革命"，只是这次的 jidoka（自働化）是用 LLM 实现的。

二、最反直觉的洞察：他们的真正护城河，不是模型，而是"美国蓝领的影响者图谱"

听创始人讲 Rescue Air 的 Josh Campbell——一个能"亲手介绍前 30 个客户里的 10 个"的人——你会发现一个被科技媒体严重忽视的事实：HVAC、电工、管道行业内部，存在一个高密度、低噪音、几乎完全离线的"蓝领 KOL 网络"。它存在于 Facebook 群组、Reddit 子版、地方贸易展，几乎不被 SF 的 VC 雷达扫描到。

这与 TikTok 上的网红经济结构完全相反：粉丝少（几百到几千人），但每一个粉丝都代表着一家年收入百万美金以上的企业。换算成"经济影响力 per follower"，一位 HVAC 老板 KOL 的密度可能高于 Kim Kardashian 三个数量级。

跨领域连接：这其实和 17 世纪荷兰东印度公司在阿姆斯特丹"咖啡馆里的船长网络"是同一种结构——信息密度极高、参与人数极少、决定整个行业资本流向。Aoka 真正的不可复制资产，不是它的 voice agent，而是它无意中嵌入了这张网络的中心节点。

三、隐藏的系统性真相：YC 教会的不是方法，而是"反 VC 化的认知操作系统"

文中有一段几乎被一带而过、但极其锋利：

"每个 VC 都在问 market；每个 YC partner 都在问 can you sell it？"

这看似是建议差异，实则是两种完全不同的世界观操作系统： - VC 的认知是"地图优先"（top-down，市场规模决定价值）； - YC 的认知是"地形优先"（bottom-up，客户付费即真实）。

更深一层：当一个创始人在 idea maze 里转圈时，"市场思维"会让他越想越大、越想越虚，最终瘫痪；而"客户付费"是一个布尔型信号——它把高维迷雾问题坍缩成一个 yes/no 决策，从而恢复行动力。

这恰好对应认知科学里的一个有趣发现：人在不确定性下的决策瘫痪，几乎总是因为评估维度过多。YC 的方法论本质上是一种"维度坍缩术"——它不是教创业，而是教创业者如何把高维问题降到一维。

而 Aoka 故事中最容易被错过的对照是：他们曾经拥有 100 家餐厅客户，已经有了 PMF 的初步信号，却主动放弃。这违反了所有传统增长直觉，但完全符合"客户付费的边际美元价值"这一单一维度优化原则——一个电话 $30 vs $30,000，5 个数量级的差距让任何"市场再大也救不回来"的辩护都失效。

一个让人意外的合并洞察： Aoka 的真正商业模型并不是"用 AI 替代客服"，而是用 AI 把"被劳动法、人性、流失率所限制的服务业产能上限"拆除。在过去，一家 HVAC 公司每年能赚多少钱，受限于"今天能雇到几个不发疯的接线员"。AI 把这个隐藏的天花板拆掉之后，整个北美家庭服务业的 TAM 实际上是被低估了一个数量级以上的——因为以前的市场规模是"被供给侧瓶颈压缩后的均衡值"，而不是真实需求。

这意味着：Aoka 不是在抢 ServiceTitan 的份额，而是在创造一个原本不存在的市场层。这是所有 AI 应用公司中最稀有的一种类型——它不在切蛋糕，它在让蛋糕长大。

📝 转录文本

开场介绍

Today we're sitting down with the founders of Aoka. They're a winter 23 YC team that in the last two years has made it to eight digits in revenue and actually growing so fast it's very rare to get to 2x growth in a single 90-day period. And here we are. Welcome Aerve. Welcome Tyson.

Thank you. Thank you. Really happy to be here and excited to be in this podcast.

什么是 Aoka

So off the bat obviously incredible growth — you know, what is AOCA?

At AOCA we are building the AI workforce for the physical economy starting with home services. So the idea is: think of like the entire world of you have your HVAC businesses, your plumbing, your electrical — all your these service businesses — we think that the entire industry is about to change with how AI is going to be working with humans to get jobs done. And that's what we're building.

It sounds like one of the things that they really sort of struggle with is, you know, someone has a problem. They actually call lots of different home services and uh nobody responds.

That's exactly the starting insight that me and Tyson found, which is that — both our backgrounds, so I grew up in Michigan, Tyson grew up in Pennsylvania — both of our backgrounds, we were working in companies like this where we saw that actually phone calls is one of the biggest ways that all these businesses get revenue. And so our insight was: if we can build an AI agent that goes and answers the phone, we can be adding value to these customers in the most meaningful way, which is new revenue.

为什么选择家庭服务行业

And so there's definitely something special about home services that you figured out. Did you expect to sort of end up in this particular vertical? You know, it sounds like you had some experience there.

I think home services was definitely uh, you know, one one of the things where both Aorv and I, uh, you know, from our family backgrounds, we had uh that experience. So my mom was actually an acupuncturist, uh, one of the first ever acupuncturists in York, Pennsylvania. And growing up, I was her one-man CSR army, so helping her answer a lot of phone calls. So we always realized that this was uh an idea that, you know, made a ton of sense in terms of uh automating the the role of the receptionist.

I think what really uh made us discover home services was — we actually explored a variety of different industries. First, we looked into restaurants and through kind of talking to a bunch, like door knocking, we actually scouted 100 restaurants. But home services was the industry that found us in a way, in the sense that in home services, a single phone call can be worth 20 to $30,000. Whereas in the world of restaurants, a single call is only $30 $40. And 85% of the revenue of a home service business comes from the phone calls. Whereas for restaurants, it's only like, you know, 20 30%.

软件占比 vs AI Workforce：从 1% 到 15% 的钱包份额

I want to double click on something that actually I think is a really powerful model broadly. Obviously, AI software starts off as software. In your particular case, there's a public company out there called Service Titan that is just the software, but you know, they're only 1% of wallet spend. I mean, when you're talking about these service providers, you know, they want to do the service. They don't really want to have to manage a call center or deal with all the support needs that — you know, but they are sort of forced to. You talk to me about like, you know, if software in these markets they only spend 1%, what's the expansion? Cuz you're not going to settle for just the 1% given AI today.

It's exactly right. I mean for ServiceTitan today we see spends of like 1% — 1.2 to 2% in that area, sometimes even 0.5%. It's all around that number. And the interesting part is that that's generally what people have spent for software. But the idea with what AI is trying to do, and why we like to think of ourselves as bringing in the AI workforce, is that you are now not only thinking about expenditure as just software expenditure to help your business. You're now thinking about, okay, how much labor do I need? What operations do I need to run my business? And how does that work from that?

And so if you actually look at customer service or customer care, you look at inside sales — those units are actually 3 to 5% of total expenditure into a business, whereas software only captures one. Now if you look at marketing — marketing is such a huge portion of what you need, it's how you get all your leads — that's 7 to 10%. So if you actually just look at some of the different kinds of AI workforces that you can hit into, you're not just trying to see how much of that 1% you can get. You're now hitting into this 15-plus percentage that you can see how much impact you can make into that. So that's, I mean, 15 times as big.

We initially started as a singular wedge, which was the AI CSR — like replacing their, uh, you know, a lot of their call center needs. Uh, but now, you know, over the course of the last 6 months, we're seeing insane ACV expansion, sometimes on the order of 5 to 10x. And if you look at some of our top uh customers and you look at, you know, the amount that they spend on their CRM versus the amount they spend on us, we're already seeing cases where we're able to get to two times that of the CRM. So, we're already proving by uh actually automating a lot of their uh workflow, we're able to be able to capture a much larger percentage of of the value there.

软件吃下 GDP 的那一刻

At the risk of like going VC speak on this, you know, one of the funnier things that — um, you know, we hang out with our friends at um DST for instance, and one of the things they're really excited about is actually exactly this idea: from time immemorial like software has been sort of trying to get into more and more of the pie of GDP. When it does, it's getting that 1%, which is not very much, this very small percentage of like overall revenue that goes to stuff like this.

You know what's great about tech and being working in tech is the street — like they will actually value what we do at, you know, 10x 20x next 12 months revenue. Like that multiple is pretty wild. Like you see non-tech people look at what tech people get and they get very jealous about it, uh, but that's actually exactly what's happening today. It's just matrix math, guys. Like, it's software that's built in a slightly, you know, more accelerated way because it also uses AI, but it's actually jumping into, you know, payroll expenses, marketing, all of these other parts of, you know, share of wallet. A San Francisco based startup prior to maybe even 18 months ago never would have dreamed to be able to crack that part of wallet share. And then being able to multiply your wallet share by 15x and have it be software that is valued at like 20x next 12 months — that's like going to be the avalanche of value. Like if you're trying to make something, this is actually the wildest story in all of like the world right now.

I completely agree, and actually I think it's even more crazy when you think about how early we are in terms of how mature these AI tools are. Like we're still in the very earliest phases where in fact actually if you look at our product roadmap, the most important things we haven't even started working on yet. We're still kind of getting into that and we've already been able to create so much value. And so I mean, to your point about it's truly orders of magnitude level of different, if we can execute really well.

这是抢工作吗？关于 CSR 的真相

I feel like we should address like some of the stuff around — you know, someone who just heard what we just talked about will say like, "Oh, wait. Well, aren't these people's jobs?"

And the weird thing there is, I mean, the attrition rate on customer support is insane.

In a normal um home service business, there's almost a close to 100% attrition rate over the course of 2 years. So that means that almost no one from that initial cohort will still be there. And it's because the job is just — it's not really fun, right? You're dealing with pissed-off customers all the time. The working conditions oftentimes aren't great and you're just overloaded with all these types of calls.

What we really view our solution on the AI CSR front is actually helping those folks. By having an AI that's able to take, you know, the 60 70% of what we call nuisance calls that aren't even revenue sensitive, that actually allows their CSRs to spend more time doing the things that they enjoy and actually, you know, have that unique human advantage for, which is, you know, outbound sales, stuff like that. And so I definitely think that we don't really view it as come in and and and replace a lot of folks. It's really to augment and allow them to scale in a much more efficient way.

Have you seen the movie Office Space?

Yeah. Yeah, I've seen that.

Like the woman who's like — they they put one of the characters next to someone, a woman who's in CSR and who's like "corporate account, speaking, just a moment" and it's just like all day. That's like what they're doing.

That's what I think of when — like maybe a form of white collar torture if you really think about it. And then, you know, the opposite of that is like — I was really taken by your story about, you know, some of the CSRs who work in your customers' operations, like sort of converted from like being tortured in that way.

Yeah. A lot of them, after using Aoka for 9 months now, is taking a lot of the calls that are not as enjoyable and they don't even really want to take — a lot of them have been promoted to different positions in the organization. Some have been promoted to, you know, dispatch functions. Uh and there's a new kind of role that's very interestingly emerging, which is actually the person that's in charge of training and overseeing the AI agents. And so some of the top CSRs, the folk, the ones that really see this and and try to think about what they can do to really help the business — they've embraced this new role of being the person that can really pioneer kind of the team lead for my fellow AI agents.

与丰田生产系统的类比

That kind of rhymes with um, you know, sort of the revolution the car industry went through in the 80s, where, you know, Japanese cars went from like kind of these rust buckets that people didn't like to buy to suddenly they were the most reliable, and, you know, everyone preferred them, and people were sort of worried like, will people buy anything other than Japanese cars? And the way they did that was actually empowering the worker. So, you know, a Toyota production system famously had this thing called kaizen where like the people who have to do the work get empowered to redo the workflow of how it works. And then it transforms this sort of like very rote, you know, uh in a factory it's like how much more rote could it be — it turns it into play. Because like you are actively trying to make a system that you have control over better and better.

And then if you think about like what's happening in terms of transformation of the roles — that when Aoka comes into a CSR situation, a lot of people are quitting. 0% annual retention means like literally if you had 10 people, all 10 people would quit that job on a 12-month basis, right? You know, if you took a couple of those people, you know, they were going to quit anyway, but instead they're wrangling AI agents and they're learning more about the customer.

One of our customers — called a great example of that — their retention rates skyrocketed. Pretty big HVAC business in the Carolinas. And once they started using our uh Aoka agents to augment and and kind of be that first line of defense, they saw attrition rates go through the roof because people are now answering less calls and enjoying that more.

I mean, I think this is an important story that, you know, people should sort of pay attention to.

I say the biggest thing to even think about too is like, if there was some kind of dynamic where people were losing jobs and they hated this Aoka or hated AI agents, you'd see the following in terms of customer value: you'd see that the owners of these businesses love you because maybe you're increasing revenue, and your customer service teams hate you because they're kind of like, "Oh, these guys are taking my job." In reality, actually it's kind of funny — like, I mean, the owners obviously love us for bringing them revenue, but it's also the customer service teams, they are super happy using VOCA. It's like VOCA has actually been great for their businesses and they're able to now no longer be, you know, yelled at or be judged by making sure they answer every little call, but now they can go focus on doing more important things in the company. And we actually see that dynamic play out. So definitely at this stage, it's it's super win-win and we foresee it being even more like that over time.

Well, thanks for sharing the good news. I think there are a lot of AI startups out there who will be watching this who I'm like, "Oh actually this is sort of the example for what we should, you know, try to make happen," and it's certainly possible. So thank you for making that happen.

联合创始人故事：扑克桌上的相遇

Switching gears a little bit, I love to hear about how the two of you came together as co-founders. It sounds like both of you were at MIT and you met at a poker competition.

This was many years back and it was kind of a casual um poker competition with uh just a group of folks. I remember uh meeting Aorv. I was getting ready to leave. So I, I uh decided to to go all in with nothing. And then a poor uh looked me dead in the eye. I didn't really even know him at this point. He looked me dead in the eye and he said, "If I fold, will you show me what you have?" And I was like, "Yeah, sure." And he's like, "Okay, well then I call." And then he just won all my money.

Oh man.

And I I remember leaving that day um really frustrated.

But then uh we ended up becoming, you know, super super close friends. Um lived together uh in uh in San Francisco and and throughout New York for over 5 years. Just really got along, uh, had a lot of similar passions around uh AI in particular. And we we worked on a lot of different AI projects together. Uh, even even living together. We had built this one application when we were playing basketball one day. We're like, "Hey, who, like, what is actually our vertical jump?" And so literally the next day we decided to build a quick AI application where you take your phone and then you can video yourself jumping and it instantly tell you the vertical.

Nice.

And so we did a lot of these things together and and then ultimately um decided to build something.

在 Retool 和 Nuro 的早期经历

I mean both of you guys kind of cut your teeth on building pretty impactful things for, you know, Nuro, for Retool. Do you want to talk about those things at all?

Honestly, that there was a lot of really great work that was happening there. I mean, I can I can touch on Retool for example. I mean, I think Retool when I joined the company, they were very much like under 50 people, like blitz scaling, and I think all the way from, you know, figuring out what does our motions look like on product and think about for-deployed engineering and all this stuff. It's like I was one of the first people to kind of be hired into forward-deployed engineering, growing out that team, figuring out how do we see success for customers, how do we scale those operations — like all sorts of different kind of work like that.

So I think it was pretty great. And honestly, I'd say one of the biggest and most prideful things — and I think we've kind of kept these values into Voca as well — is that we hired an insanely strong and talented forward deploy engineering. I think some of the people there have gone on to be other founders today, Series A, Series B founders doing really well. It just shows that when you hire a great team and you can execute really well, these people will do great things in general. And we we see that as well in Aoka, where we think the people that are working here at Voca have created something incredible and they will themselves be future incredible founders one day.

Yeah, the FDE model is pretty awesome. My high school and college buddy I think was basically the first FDE in practice. Stephen Cohen, one of the co-founders of Palantir — it was before the title was created but that's basically what Stefan did. And then uh our buddy Sham who now runs all kinds of things at uh Palantir still, he sort of formalized it into like a training program.

It makes sense, because I think for every business the most important thing is customer value and customer obsession. So if you can get someone who can understand the product and be technical and on the engineering side, and also interface with the customer — they are potentially some of the most important people in any business because they're figuring out how does this product actually drive you to success. Truly it's an incredible model and I think it's worked really well. Well, you obviously need to get the right people and it's, it's just amazing.

For Nuro, I think one of the most — I mean, I worked on a lot of projects there for, uh, around 3 years. Some of the most interesting things at Nuro that uh probably impacted uh kind of my thinking and our thinking of of Aoka was some of the work I did with uh uh the human-in-the-loop stuff. And so the team I was on, it was called uh kind of supply. We built a lot of the internal tools powering uh kind of the operations of of Nuro. One of the things was uh this program called Guardian — when the autonomous vehicle was uh in trouble, allowing a remote operator to kind of remote in and save the day. That was something that we actually took kind of a similar lesson from and incorporated it into the Aoka product as well.

What were the tactical things there?

It's like trying to figure out, you know, when should a human get involved. The most important things was understanding like when, and also uh providing the context — understanding when the autonomous vehicle is in trouble and ideally anticipating a few seconds beforehand, and then being able to then give the human operator that's going to step in a bit of a preview into like the context of what's happening. You're not just immediately thrown into a situation where you have to kind of save the vehicle.

That's funny. I mean, I guess in AI people are always uh doing context engineering for LLMs, but you're doing context engineering for humans in the loop then.

Yeah, exactly. There's so many parallels between the world of autonomous vehicles and uh, you know, AI agents we're building at Aoka.

进入 YC 与早期的 idea maze

I think you got into YC twice. Uh the first time you couldn't come over yet and then second time you guys did. Talk to me about your time in YC.

So, I would say when starting the company, I would say the most important thing was me and Tyson. We were roommates for so long. And we wanted to work together and we were — we had very similar ambitions and all that. I think in the early days we were kind of exploring a few different ideas, hacking on things. We we always used to hack on a variety of different projects. I mean all sorts of different things.

But finally, I think the the place where we got really excited where we thought actually YC would be great was going to be um when we were thinking about the fact that — this is actually pre-ChatGPT, but after the model GPT-3 Da Vinci was out and I think people started talking about it, but the hype wasn't fully there — we started kind of realizing, wait, these AI models kind of sound like a human. It's not perfect and hallucinates like crazy, but it does sound like a human.

And for me and Tyson, I mean, from both of our backgrounds, we kind of, you know, growing up in Michigan, Pennsylvania, it's like we literally did the job of what these agents would often have to do or what these agents could do — where we just had to just, you know, talk to these customers, close customers, etc. We really had this like kind of hunch where we were like, okay, these things aren't yet able to speak, but once they can speak, the entire what we call like the physical economy, the entire all these service-based businesses will have an insane unlock because of the fact that now you have someone that can answer your phones, that can go reach out to customers, do all this thing. And so I think we had a pretty early hunch — that's when I think me and Tyson were kind of like, let's do this.

And slowly when it comes to YC, I mean, I think without YC we would not have been successful. I think it's just very clear. In fact, when we first got into YC, me and Tyson were kind of like, you know what, do we need YC? We don't really know what we're working on yet. Like, like, should we even do it? And I think we actually decided not to do it cuz we were like, you know what? Still working at a company that's not going to wrap up by then. Um, let's just, you know, figure it out ourselves. There's some VCs who are reaching out, you know, wanting to invest. We don't necessarily need YC.

But that's actually when we were actually doing the actual exploration, is when we realized that there's so many things about starting a company that genuinely we would not have learned. I would say the two biggest things I can think of are: customer obsession — which is just being maniacally focused on your customer — and focused on momentum, which is just constantly be pushing yourself. And I think YC gets you into that environment.

And so for us, frankly, there was many places where me and Tyson were thinking like, okay, you know, we have all these job offers. Do we, you know, we've tried for a bit. Do we go back and then maybe try again in a few years or do we keep executing? And the fact that we kept executing and did YC is probably the only reason that Aoka is able to be how big it is today.

合伙人与 Dalton 的 office hours

I feel like tangibly the two things would be — like obviously you're working with a partner is cool. I mean I'm working with 25 companies right now and I just always reminded of like, when you're in the maze trying to like find your way to product-market fit in the idea maze, you're like, "Do we turn around? Do we like explore this other part of the maze?" And then, you know, being having someone next to you who's like, "Oh no no, keep going."

"Like, you don't know that yet" — or, you know, or it's like, "Oh yeah, it's time to not do this anymore." Like that, you know — was that a part of like what you experienced?

Definitely. I think there were so many instances where — and I, you know, were roommates — uh, and we would get so excited. There would be so many of these kind of like, "Man, like this is it" — where like we're on to something huge, like we we this can be huge. Literally like 2 days later, as we like discover more about that niche or like talk to more customers, then we fade. It'd be really disheartening cuz it feels like you're pounding your head against this wall and you're working like, you know, we're basically working 24/7 every single weekend.

I think it was really uh nice to be in that environment where there's other people doing the same thing. Every time we had the opportunity to speak with our with our partner at the time, Dalton, it was really elucidating. I think having him kind of ground us — and uh it's just like every time we left uh speaking with Dalton it was like, okay this is what we have to do, these are the actual core tenants to focus on.

As said, I think like speed is is everything. Like I think there's many areas where we could have probably built a decent business if we had stuck to some of these other verticals like restaurants that we had found some initial traction, dental — but it was really the relentless idea of like, okay this is good, but how big can it be? Like when we switched into home services, we had a hundred restaurants that were using us. They were liking us decently. We even had some national restaurant chains. But after we saw the opportunity of home services, we just realized that even though we had 100 of these restaurant customers, that wasn't enough. Like that's that's not going to become a generational company. And so having the speed to kind of go quickly and then also be able to say no to, you know, good markets and good ideas to pursue even, you know, bigger ones — I think that really was what allowed us to succeed.

VC 思维 vs YC 思维

Maybe one quick thing to add to that is — I think the the thing that I really like to think about when it comes to this is about customer value. Again, I will say in the early days, you know, I mean, we worked at Retool and Nuro, a lot of our friends were VCs knocking our doors. It's like whenever we'd kind of talk to a VC even casually and and we'd kind of be like, "Oh yeah, here's what we're exploring." The thing that every VC person would tell us — that YC wouldn't, that I think YC was correct on — is that every venture capitalist is always thinking about market.

Whereas every YC partner that we would ask advice for is always thinking of: can you sell it? Will they pay for it? How much do they actually like it? Because I think selling and paying is a proxy for value.

And so for us actually what was interesting was that, you know, even in home services it's like in the early days it's not like VCs were always like super excited — early days they they hated it. But but the thing that I think me and Tyson saw, and that's actually candidly because of a lot of learnings we got from, you know, places like YC, is that actually the amount that these home service customers love our product — it's producing 80% of revenue for their business are coming through phone calls. So the amount that our product is helpful is just so high that actually, let's not look at classic things about, "Oh what is this market? Should we go work on it?" That's kind of like — that's the VC's job to go do that. Our job is to go follow where there's customer love, where people are going to pay us more, and that's going to be the more exciting opportunity for us.

And yeah, frankly, I think we would have — me and Tyson could very well have been stuck in this maze of like trying to play the game of VC of what idea to work on, as opposed to just following the customers, if it wasn't for a place like YC.

Rescue Air：第一个客户的"光球时刻"

And I think one other YC principle that really stuck out in the early days was understanding the way that you can add value is really directly correlated to the customer pain. And I think in the early days, like I remember one of our earliest home service customers, Rescue Air, they had this pain that was so great. When we spoke to them, that's all they could tell us about — which is how much they hated their answering service. And yet, they still paid $3,000 a month for this answering service. It didn't integrate into their CRM. It had long hold times. It was inconsistent. And yet, they still paid it.

And the YC principle that it always takes me back to is like: if the pain is big enough, the customer will try to find a solution to it even if it's not perfect. And so that's when it was like — that kind of was a bit of a light bulb moment was like, wait a minute, these guys are spending this much money and it's like the the solution is this bad. Like there's definitely something here to to kind of tease that, like tug on that thread.

Basically, all of these things — like you can read about it, you can watch videos like this — and, you know, but when you're actually in the idea maze and you're like breaking your brain about stuff, having office hours with someone who's seen so many things is basically like a — it's almost like a GPT-5. It actually does have this like model router that tries to help you figure out like, okay, do I have the answer or do I need to, you know, use medium or high reasoning, or do I need to use Pro? If you're doing it on your own, it's like trying to use like GPT-5 Pro for like every decision.

Exactly. And you kind of like spin and overthink and then it's like when you use GPT-5 Pro for uh, exactly. What happened to you like, "we have a customer who's experiencing this extreme pain" —

Like in office hours, when we hear that, you know, I would be like, "That's sick. Like, do that. Like you have — you now you got something. All you have to do is like start pulling on this thread and you're going to have this billion-dollar company," which has turned out to be true at this point.

We're not quite a billion yet, but we'll get there.

I mean, we can see the trajectory. Seems like — seems you're running in the stars to me.

从 Demo Day 到产品扩张

So, you figured out this product, you've got product-market fit. Um, it's working. Walk us through — you did demo day, you did YC. How much did you raise? Like, you pivoted in the batch and found this amazing thing. Walk us through, like, you're in the idea maze and then to here, you know, especially around the product.

On the product side, basically the day-one value was focusing on one problem that's like a hair-on-fire problem these customers have that we can provide the most amount of customer value, and that was building the AI CSR. And so this is essentially someone like an AI agent that goes in, answers phone calls, books customers, closes revenue, etc.

And when we realized that that was actually very valuable, we kind of were — was like our immediate thing was let's get this in the hands of as many customers as we can and let's perfect that. And I think when we started seeing that that was going well, that's when me and Tyson kind of realized, we were like, okay wait, this can be onto something quite interesting. That's when we actually focused a bit more on hiring, got a great team and things like that too.

But then the next phase of the company started occurring, which is what's happening right now. And I think we're kind of in this phase where we're essentially — the product is significantly deeper than voice. And so that voice, that AI CSR, was the initial wedge. What we are doing now is like what we like to call the AI workforce, because it's not just that CSR agent. We're doing all sorts of things. So, we're kind of doing proactive reach-outs. We can reach out via text. We can do targeted marketing to different people. We can have like a coach product that goes and coaches to see, how are you doing on call performance? How is your text doing and things like that? And so now it's like just building each of these agent flows.

团队与文化

And I would say the biggest reason we're able to do that — and that's what's causing a lot of these, you know, ACV expansions and things like that — is actually because we also have just an incredible team. And so I will say it's like we're kind of in this like complete green area where you have this industry where there's not that many technical people. You have to create an incredible product because now you have this entire AI wave that's about to completely change their industry.

And so that's where we were able to kind of have some of the best people working here. And so I think for example, I'll just highlight a few people cuz they were also YC people — but there's like Barrett who I think he was YC in 2015. Without him, the company would not be where it's at today. He's like one of our product leaders. He kind of goes in and figured out essentially how to kind of help scale some of these, go from the CSR to building the rest of the workflow as well, led some of that work. There's Drago who, for that CSR coach, that like the coaching product, he's kind of taken a big lead on that, and he was YC 2020, incredible person there. There's also Ditia who is actually the same batch as us. He was building like an API infra company and he's just been one of the most incredible engineers too.

And so in fact actually we just have this incredible team and we are building out essentially the rest of the workforce layer. And so what I think is the most exciting part about VOCA is not even like how the product looks today or how much we've grown, but it's how strong of a team we have and how much there is still to grow.

I mean my candid plug would also be, if there's any folks — we love ex-founder people, want to be founders. We probably have one of the most founder-like early-stage cultures, especially in New York. And so if anyone's excited, I think VOCA is definitely looking.

I can definitely say like working at Palantir very early uh jump-started my whole life in a lot of ways. You know, being able to work at the next uh, you know, future $300 billion company in Aoka — that, I mean, that's going to be a pretty killer experience.

And we give a lot of autonomy too. Like there's, you know, there's so much to do. We're the leaders in home services um, but there's, you know, several other verticals that we're looking to expand to. So just the scope of really being able to to work uh here I think is is unparalleled actually.

从成本中心到收入引擎

Super interesting how you take this sort of CSR function that's kind of a cost center, like cost of doing business, very very high turnover, terrible job, like nobody even really — you know, you have to do it but you don't want to. And then you've sort of switched it and now it's actually like — sounds like it's putting more money into customers' hands —

Core revenue. Yeah.

So it's not just like cost center "I have to do it." Now it's uh —

That's actually when we do sales — I mean, our for sure. Yeah. You'll get some cost uh reduction. We've even seen cases where a company that had 100 CSRs is now at 30. Um and and, you know, a lot of that is natural attrition given seasonality, and a lot of is like folks get promoted. The value probably always lead with is this is going to be a core revenue driver. This will be putting multiple millions more, um, by, you know, people who spend so much on marketing — by being able to capture every lead.

深度客户洞察：飞到客户那里和他们一起工作

You were saying you found a customer and they're starting to pull the product out of you. I feel like there's a teaching moment there where it's like some people might overthink it or maybe they not spend the right amount of time. Like it sounds like what you did was fly there and sit with the customer and like literally work out of their like office, you know? What was that like?

The customer obsession is is I think a core part of uh what makes the Voca special and a core part of the DNA that we've been able to to carry, you know, since since we started. A lot of the best ideas that we have come from understanding customers at a very deep level. And I think we can only get to that point by having that level of obsession where literally now even like basically all of our uh major deployments will have several team members fly to that customer for a week.

What I like to call it is like all these customers, they're especially the large ones, they're successful uh for some reason, and usually it's like they might have found one or two secrets that no one else in the industry has figured out.

Walk me through like the story of it. Can you place me in like a time and a place? What did it look like in that CSR room and who did you talk to, and like, you know, when you came away from that, is like how did that influence your first products?

The one that kind of comes to mind is probably our our first customer, uh, Rescue Air. Uh when we first walked in, they were two owners, Josh and Michael. They had known about us because one of our restaurant customers uh just like said, "Hey, you guys should talk to these guys. They're buddies of mine." I showed them the AICSW — uh, for me. Uh, and uh basically all these guys could talk about was how bad their answering service was.

So they introduced us to the operations manager, the guy by the name of Andreas. And Andreas is this like tall 6'2 Greek guy. He's like, "Hey, this is my CSR room." And he showed me folks, there's like six people. And it just turns out that the room was buzzing. But the issue was there was too many calls because it was a heat wave in Dallas in the summer.

And so the the call room was buzzing and, you know, people were trying their best to handle the calls. But it turns out a lot of the calls just weren't able to be taken. "Well, what happens to all these calls? Like, you know, where do they go?" And they're like, "Well, we have this local answering service that we're using to handle all of our overflow and after hours, like weekends, nights, where they don't have any of their CSRs to take care of them."

And so then we kind of said, "Well, like, you know, does it work well?" And that's when we didn't even have to say anything. They basically continuously were like, "You know, this is actually the number one thing that irritates us. We've literally switched to the fifth answering service in the same year. Five different ones." And it's like he's like, "It's always the same thing. They start out strong, but then quality diminishes."

And so understanding that, then we got deep into the workflow. It's like, well wait a minute, what does this answering service actually do for you? Does it actually book jobs? They're like, "Well, no, all it does is it waits 2 minutes on average to answer. It doesn't actually book the job. It just takes a message." And so we're like, "Wait. So basically, you're paying $3,000 a month for a glorified voicemail," which also, you know, sometimes even irks the customers because like the CSRs they have are, you know, not the friendliest.

They're sitting in a 95-degree room and they're hot and —

And those answering services are answering phones for, you know, Rescue Air, the vet, the librarian — like it's not personalized at all.

And so yeah, that — that maybe that's a bit of a kind of the inner look into what that initial interaction was like.

"Feature 不算完成，直到客户满意"

And then what was it like, you know, you went back, made a version that, uh, you know, and you went back and showed it to them — what happened?

There was these moments where it felt incredible. And I think that's — it was not perfect. You know, in the early days, AI is hallucinating, there's all these sorts of issues, but how does it handle this edge case? How does it deal with this workflow, this workflow? But in the cases where it did well, it was incredible. And then it was up to us to go figure out how to like really fine-tune that product.

And then if you look at a lot of the other agent workflows we've built or things like that from there, it's really just came down to being like, this is customer X. Nothing in a VOCA is built until customer X or customer Y is using that and they're happy with that. Until that point, it's like there's no — you're not feature-complete until a customer is happy, as one of our tenants.

And I think the reason we have that is because we hear our problems from them. It's our job to add as much value as we can to customers, and we hope that that'll show increases in our contract sizes and how much revenue we also get too.

爆炸式增长：从 7 位数到 8 位数 ARR

These maxims are like easy to say, very hard to do, and you're doing it. Um and, you know, from here it's sort of resulted in this absolute like growth explosion. Like last year when you hit uh, you know, your first seven figures ARR overall, and then uh now you've basically doubled to 8 figure ARR. Like doubling to get there in the last 90 days is very explosive growth. So how'd you do it?

It was a combination of things. Um, but I think one of the big reasons was — it kind of goes back to the old like YC axiom around like don't try to get a 100 customers that like your product, try to get three that love it. And so we went uh to the lab and and just like had three essentially early design partners that we absolutely poured our heart and soul into making something that they loved.

And the interesting thing about home services that it's extremely interconnected. A lot of the early uh traction was referrals. You never think about it, but in home services there's this influencer network where folks like a Josh Campbell from Rescue Air — they are like kind of super connectors. Everyone looks to them to say what's next, like what is the next big technology. And so, you know, I think Josh personally connected us to uh something like 10 of our first like 30 customers.

Then we started getting uh a lot of uh chatter about us on these Facebook groups where all these contractors are from. And then we layered in um kind of the conferences, the trade shows — with virtually zero sales and marketing. We were able to get to that first like, you know, million, 2 million.

From there on it was just continuing to to kind of scale. The last half of it has really been driven by the enterprise and and private equity adoption, because these, you know, PEs consolidating this uh industry uh a ton. We figured out like how to how to get that motion down, how to do enterprise sales. And then once we had that kind of continuous inbound from from the mid-market, then we're able to understand and really figure out how to attack the enterprise. That's when things just like continued to boom.

And again, like, you know, we we've been growing fast, but we really think this is just the beginning. Like this market is absolutely massive. The exciting innings are are yet to come.

先做不可扩展的事，再扩展

Basically do unscalable things uh to get to the first million dollars a year in revenue, and then uh the trick is scale it after that.

Yes, exactly. I think a lot of unscalable things we did with the Facebook groups and stuff. I think we would personally ask our customers that were like really happy — anytime there was a AI-focused thread to kind of talk about us — and you know, that led to a lot of things.

Yeah, I think scaling. Yeah, we ended up starting to scale sales quite heavily. But the key is, I think the thing that we did right is is scaling before you truly find PMF — and I think that's one of the biggest mistakes that can kill your business. We literally got to around a million, maybe over a million before uh we brought on uh kind of our our first actual sales team. And then it was like, hey, we figured out how to do it, and then we scaled it.

That's the right way to do it. Like often people try to scale their sales team having not even done the sales themselves. Like without founder-led sales you can't do it. And so totally.

未来展望：从 System of Record 到 System of Intelligence

If you're right about going from 1% wallet spend to 15% wallet spend in this, you know, very very large home services market — uh, you know, what does a Voca look like, you know, next year and 5 years from now?

It's almost like there's this concept of system of record that, you know, everyone talks about when it comes to CRM and things like that. There's almost like going to be this — what a Voca will represent to these industries is almost a system of intelligence or system of action, where your core workflows, how you close your customers, how you do your marketing, how you do all the core operations work, is going to be centered around Aoka. And it'll be the biggest platform that we think these people will use, be significantly more valuable to these customers than I think even a ServiceTitan could be. But I think, you know, ServiceTitan has its place too, it's a great company, but I think Aoka will have a great place as well, and I think it's going to be very big.

And I think the most exciting thing also, by the way, is that home services — how that's going to be done will also be very similar to maybe adjacent industries or other industries too. And so when we think of like a Voca, we're already hitting a market size that's just so massive, and the fact that it's just the beginning with that market is just so exciting for us to hear, where we think that if we execute flawlessly and do really well, the opportunity for this business — being a unicorn is not at all the goal. The goal is to see how great we can be. It's like — can never say anything about valuations and pricing, but quite big.

Well, that's uh all we have time for today, but we'll see you guys next time.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、表面看是"AI 客服"，实质是一场"白领工厂的丰田革命"
大多数人看 Aoka 的故事，会停在"AI 替代人工接电话"这一层。但真正值得注意的是文中一闪而过的一组数字：客服岗位的两年流失率接近 100%——这意味着**这个岗位的"经济寿命"比一只仓鼠还短**。换句话说，他们替代的不是"工作"，而是"折磨"。
更微妙的是接下来发生的事：留下的 CSR 被提拔成"AI 训练师 / Agent 团队长"。这其实是 1980 年代丰田 kaizen 的语言变体——把流水线工人从"被工序奴役"变成"重写工序的人"。Aoka 在不自知的情况下，把日本制造业 40 年前的劳动哲学，移植到了美国客服行业。**这是一次"白领工厂的精益革命"，只是这次的 jidoka（自働化）是用 LLM 实现的。**
## 二、最反直觉的洞察：他们的真正护城河，不是模型，而是"美国蓝领的影响者图谱"
听创始人讲 Rescue Air 的 Josh Campbell——一个能"亲手介绍前 30 个客户里的 10 个"的人——你会发现一个被科技媒体严重忽视的事实：**HVAC、电工、管道行业内部，存在一个

item.title: [邦妮區塊鏈 Bonnie Blockchain] 為什麼有錢人都想去百慕達？全球富翁看到了什麼？David Parker【邦妮區塊鏈】
item.dc:creator: 邦妮區塊鏈 Bonnie Blockchain
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

百慕大悖论：一个用"风险"做主业的避险天堂

最反直觉的观察是：百慕大被称为"世界风险之都"（Risk Capital of the World），但它真正售卖的产品恰恰是"确定性"。承担全球 36% 的再保险——意味着它在账面上吸收了世界上最不可承受的灾难（飓风、地震、网络攻击、长寿风险）；但与此同时，它向高净值人群、家族办公室、加密公司兜售的，是"稳定、安全、可预测"。

这不是矛盾，而是一种极其聪明的风险套利结构：百慕大把"波动性"打包成可定价的金融产品卖给全世界，再用赚到的手续费、人才与监管声誉，反过来为另一群客户（私人财富、数字资产）提供"反波动性"庇护。换句话说，它一边做世界的避雷针，一边做世界的避风港——两个看似对立的角色，其实共享同一套基础设施：精算、合规、信任。

隐藏的跨领域连接：再保险逻辑正在"吞噬"加密世界

David 提到一个容易被忽略的细节——Realm、Meanwhile 这类公司，是"完全用数字资产运作的保险公司"。表面上这是"insurance × crypto"的跨界故事，但更深一层的真相是：

再保险业的底层逻辑（长尾风险、资本池、概率定价、跨境合规），与 DeFi 的底层逻辑（流动性池、智能合约、链上清算）在数学结构上是同构的。

这意味着百慕大不是"顺便也做加密"，而是它三百年来积累的精算文化，正在成为加密原生金融唯一能"成年化"的土壤。伦敦有 Lloyd's 但监管太重，新加坡香港有金融但缺乏巨灾承保的肌肉，开曼有牌照但没有产业生态。百慕大可能是地球上唯一同时拥有"风险定价文化 + 加密牌照 + 美元时区 + 英联邦法律"的 21 平方英里。这种组合不是政策红利，是地质级的稀缺。

你没听出来的潜台词：地缘政治的"分散投资"，正在被人当作资产配置

最值得划重点的一句话是 David 谈家族办公室时说的：

"我们不是让你关掉香港搬到百慕大，而是问你——除了香港，你还同时在哪里？"

这句话翻译成大白话是：国籍、税务居民身份、资产托管地，正在从"选择题"变成"投资组合"。过去 30 年的全球化让富人相信"选一个最优解就够了"（伦敦 / 新加坡 / 香港），但当 Mark Carney 这样的人都在说"全球化已被武器化"，富人的反应不是去全球化，而是把自己也做成一个对冲基金——身份分散、司法管辖分散、银行系统分散、甚至币种分散。

百慕大卖的从来不是岛，是"地缘政治的非相关性资产"。这跟现代投资组合理论（MPT）里马科维茨说的"加入一个低相关性资产能在不降低收益的前提下降低组合波动"是同一个公式——只不过资产标的从股票债券，换成了护照、居留权、监管管辖。

一个让人不舒服的延伸推论：未来十年，"身份"会像股票一样被定价、被组合、被再平衡。而百慕大、瑞士、阿联酋、新加坡这些"小国"，本质上是在做一门新生意——主权即服务（Sovereignty-as-a-Service）。

📝 转录文本

开场：百慕大是什么样的存在？

Some people refer to Bermuda as the risk capital of the world. Bermuda has one of the highest GDP per capita. 36% of the reinsurance market globally uh own Bermuda. A lot of family offices choose either Hong Kong or Singapore. In Asia, there's no beaches in Hong Kong. I live in Hong Kong. Not sure. Okay. Okay. How much to apply for a passport?

We're back at Consensus Miami day 2. Co-hosting with me is Bonnie Chang, and we're here today with David Parker. He's a head of business development at the Bermuda Business Development Agency. Welcome to the show, David.

Thank you very much. Very good to be here.

百慕大的经济基本面

David, uh Bermuda has the highest one of the highest GDP per capita.

Yeah, the GDP per capita in Bermuda is about $130 to $140,000. Um the economy is extremely stable. Uh growth is good. Uh the Bermuda economy in many ways is is tied to the US. It's just a few hundred miles off the the coast of the Carolinas. Um we uh at that said uh it's it's not part of the USA. Of course, it's a British territory. Tends to punch very well above its weight from an economic trade and business perspective.

And you guys are very famous for reinsurance.

We certainly are. Uh about 36% of the reinsurance market globally uh from Bermuda. Um we have uh a long history in the risk and insurance space. In fact, some people refer to Bermuda as the risk capital of the world.

为什么是百慕大，而不是伦敦或纽约？

Why Bermuda? Why not London, New York? You were in Hong Kong.

I certainly was. I think uh one of the benefits of Bermuda is first of all, as I've already mentioned, the proximity to the US, the world's biggest economy. Uh just 6 hours flight time into London. So if you're looking to do business in the Western Hemisphere, it's perfectly placed.

Uh Bermuda has a globally respected regulatory environment from an insurance perspective. It's also regulatory compliant with the US, with the UK, uh with the EU. Uh and I also think that a time of a lot of volatility uh in the world, Bermuda offers certainty, it offers security, it offers safety, it offers consistency.

"避税天堂"的真相与税改

and it's been called the tax haven.

It's certainly not a tax haven. Uh Bermuda has recently introduced the corporate income tax. It's compliant with the highest standards of international tax compliance. That said, uh it certainly offers many low tax benefits, uh tax efficiencies, if you want to put it that way. So I think we have the best of both worlds. You know, we offer tax efficiencies, but we're not a tax haven. We are tax compliant.

You recently changed a tax rule.

Absolutely. Yes. So the corporate income tax was introduced uh last year uh has actually been uh extremely successful for Bermuda. I think it's reinforced its global standing uh and reputation. But as part of the introduction of the corporate income tax, um a range of tax credits were also introduced. So when businesses in Bermuda create economic substance, when they create new jobs, when they invest, when they uh develop uh local talent from a skills perspective, they can benefit from that from a tax perspective. So I think that's starting to create what I like to talk about as a as a virtuous cycle economically.

去全球化时代，百慕大的定位

At the World Economic Forum earlier this year in Davos, a common reoccurring theme among politicians that globalization has been weaponized. Mark Carney PM of Canada has also said that u he expects a new world ordering in which globalization could be reduced. Well, I think I'm paraphrasing, but that is kind of his kind of his mantra here. How is Bermuda positioned for a world that may be less globalized and more protectionist?

I think Bermuda is is neutral. Uh we work very closely with markets in the US and in Europe as I've already talked about, but we also have very strong links with with Asia. You we're seeing a lot of investment coming into Bermuda uh from Japan for example in the life insurance industry. Uh we have very close links with Hong Kong from a digital assets perspective. Uh I've already mentioned that our insurance industry is compliant with a range of leading markets globally.

So I I don't think from a Bermuda perspective we've been too much affected. If anything, Bermuda offers an advantage as I talked about earlier, you know, that that that certainty, that security if you like at a time of great change internationally.

生活成本与关税影响

That's right. And the cost of living crisis has been a theme for citizens all around the world from America to Europe. some parts of Asia now especially with the oil price going up Canada especially what is it like in Bermuda

I don't think Bermuda's been affected uh so much from a trade perspective because Bermuda's economy is largely service sector driven um but that said um there is a cost of living in Bermuda it's a beautiful island in the north of the Atlantic it's very exclusive uh we pride ourselves more on quality rather than quantity so we don't We don't tend to attract high volumes of business or high volumes of tourists. I think it's a niche market for the right business with the right fit or for the right tourism that's looking for something very exclusive.

So tariffs haven't hit Bermuda.

Tariffs haven't hit Bermuda.

Not at all.

No.

百慕大想吸引哪些产业？保险 × 数字资产的融合

What type of businesses are you trying to attract?

It's a great question. I've already talked about the fact that uh we're a worldleading jurisdiction for insurance. uh but of course uh no jurisdiction should be too heavily reliant upon one single industry. So a big focus from a BDA perspective has also been uh economic diversification looking at other markets other industries where we can develop global leadership.

Uh Bermuda has become a hub for example for private wealth. We have our economic investment residency certificate. Uh that's attracted a lot of high net worths to consider Bermuda as part of their portfol global portfolio of assets and investments.

Um digital assets were here at consensus. Let's talk about that. Bermuda introduced the uh digital assets business act in 2018. uh we now have a large number of companies including major players like Kraken uh Coinbase Circle for example with a significant presence in Bermuda.

What's also interesting is the convergence that we're now starting to see between different industries. I talked about Bermuda strength in insurance. We're now seeing a lot of insurance players, insurance carriers in Bermuda uh using digital assets. uh with us as part of the delegation uh here in Miami this week for example is Realm Insurance which is an insurance carrier that operates exclusively through digital assets. Another good example uh would be uh Meanwhile uh this is a life insurance company attracted to Bermuda because of growth in the life industry but at the same time all the product offering is via digital assets.

So what we're starting to see now as we focus on new industries, whether that's private wealth and family offices, whether it's digital assets and fintech, uh we're starting to play to our strengths as an insurance hub and see convergence between those different industries. And I think that makes Bermuda um you I would go so far as to say unique. I mean, some of these companies when they do a global search and they're looking at the expansion, they're looking at their global footprint, conclude that the only jurisdiction that has a fit for what they want to do in terms of their growth is is Bermuda. Next Fore. Next. Next.

对家族办公室：不是取代香港/新加坡，而是分散风险

A very quick follow-up question. A lot of family offices choose either Hong Kong or Singapore in Asia. How would you convince them to go to Bermuda?

Well, we're not trying to con uh compete with Hong Kong, Singapore or other international hubs. Um what I'm finding when I engage with family offices is they're looking to spread risk. So rather than have all assets, all investments uh in one location, rather than have residency in one location, they're now looking at multiple locations globally. So we're not necessarily saying to them, you know, close down in Hong Kong and move to Bermuda. What we're saying is how are you managing your risk globally? What other jurisdictions as well as Hong Kong, not instead of Hong Kong, are you considering? And how might Bermuda form part of your future plans?

There's no beaches in Hong Kong.

I I lived in Hong Kong. That's not true. Okay. Okay. Hong Kong, but I can tell you that Bermuda has some of the most beautiful beaches in the world.

What is that? Pink pink beach,

the pink sand, the turquoise sea, the palm trees. So,

关于资本利得税与监管平衡

but just going back to the tax haven uh status question for both

we're not a tax haven.

We're not you're not a tax haven. There's no capital gains tax, dividend tax, right? Correct. What is your view on capital gains tax overall for an economy? Not just for nuda, but for the growth of an economy. There are some jurisdictions where there are no capital gains taxes in the world and then you have the opposite end of the spectrum where in some European countries they're talking about taxing unrealized capital gains. U Camela Harris is also talking about that before the election of the US. Of course, that was never implemented. How do capital gain taxes benefit?

I'm not a I'm not a tax expert, but the way I look at tax is it's a source of income and well- reggarded governments use that income to drive economic benefits for its population. Be that improved education, be that improved healthcare, be that improved transport and infrastructure. But of course if you if you cross that threshold too high then you know whether it's individuals or organizations that are looking to manage their tax exo exposure going to look elsewhere.

So I think it's getting the balance right. Uh and I think Bermuda uh the government of Bermuda has has got that spot on.

Likewise uh we talk about tax. I think I could say the same thing from a regulatory perspective. you know uh with regulation on the one hand you're looking to uh protect against anti-money laundering protect consumers but if you go too hard on that you kill innovation on the other hand you're looking to embrace innovation if you go too hard on that you could be potentially exposed and I think again what Bermuda uh has got right uh is balance you know ensuring that there's robust regulatory regime in place but at the same time ensuring that we have uh an innovationfriendly ecosystem where entrepreneurs, founders, innovators can thrive.

在百慕大生活是一种什么体验？

Um, one last question. Um, what is it like living there?

It's absolutely beautiful. Um, I relocated to Bermuda from Hong Kong uh in December 2024. Um, it's it's it's small. Um, I think you have to get to to know people uh to really appreciate everything that Bermuda has to offer.

I always say that uh there's been rarely a day since I relocated to Bermuda where I've not discovered something new, you know. Um so for such a small jurisdiction, this tiny islands in the North Atlantic, that's pretty special. If you go to, you know, Hong Kong, a major global city, you know, the the big urban spraw, and of course all those islands made up of natural beauty, you expect to discover something new every day. If you land in a tiny islands in the North Atlantic, you probably think, well, 1 to two days and I'm done. Over 365 days later, I'm still discovering something new about Bermuda every day.

What was the latest discovery?

My latest discovery I I think culinary has been really interesting. I mean, Bermuda offers a One of the things I like about Bermuda is you got some influence from America, you've got some British influence. It's not too far from the Caribbean, but then of course you've got that local culture, that local cuisine as well. And I think the fusion of all of that is something that I didn't expect. Uh, and I love we have some of the finest uh, restaurants and places to eat. Uh, and I'm still said I'm discovering new places in Bonudo. Every day I'm discovering new new tastes, new places to eat, new culinary delights to enjoy.

结尾彩蛋

All right, final question for me. How much to apply for a passport?

You talk about that offline. Excellent. Thank you very much. Very good to be here.

观看原视频 →
