主持人开场

Welcome to the Microscopic Podcast. My name is Alex Adrenov and today I have the honor of welcoming Simon Dixon. Today is the 25th of June, 2026. Welcome to the podcast, Simon.

Simon: Thank you so much for having me. Excited to be here.

Alex: So Simon, since you're also for the first time appearing on our podcast, I would like to dig a bit deeper into your way of thinking, your philosophy, the way you see and perceive the world — so that our audience gets a better understanding of your main ideas and main takes.

一、父亲的退休金与"钱去哪了"

Alex: One of the first questions that I was actually wondering is a bit more personal. It's about the episode where your father's pension was wiped out in the dot-com crash. What did this teach you in that moment about the system we live in?

Simon: Yeah. So I got into this game of following the money because my father — who was born in '33, so he lived through the Great Depression and World War II — kind of made himself from complete rags to clawing up to becoming a millionaire through blogging. And then he lost it all at his retirement age in the dot-com boom and bust.

So he went through that whole cycle, and I remember what an event it was. He was looking back at his whole life saying, "You know, I came from nothing. My father was a bus driver. There were seven of us — seven of us lived off a bus driver salary. I pulled my way out of it, and now where is the money?"

And he asked me to answer that question, because he just didn't understand it. He saw it as a digital entry on his stock brokerage account. And then it was gone. He was like, "Well, where is that money? Who made that money? What went wrong?" He didn't understand how markets fundamentally work.

That was 26 years ago. And I've obsessed over the topic of trying to answer that question. I think I've reached an answer, but I still obsess over it.

学院之路与"被赞助的课程"

That kind of took me to academically doing economics up to Master's level, realizing they don't give you the answer. What I discovered is that the university curriculum is sponsored by those that have vested interests in deviating your attention away from the system — the same what I call financial industrial complex (FIC).

So then I went into not-for-profit work and started teaching people about how the banking system works. But this was after working in the banking system.

从券商、做市商到投行的"内部觉醒"

  • I got a job as a stock broker — realized that my job was just to say whatever you need to say in order to sell stocks.
  • Then I decided to learn how the markets work. So I worked as a market maker, where my job was to manipulate the price of stocks in order to fulfill institutional investment banking orders.
  • Then I went into corporate finance, which was helping companies go public — and understood the whole game of how taking companies public is actually a vehicle for subordinating entrepreneurs and installing boards, so that you can control businesses in order to maximize returns.

After that game, I got into the not-for-profit world. Realized that all politicians are owned by the same financial industrial complex. Around the 2008 financial crisis, I was asking politicians to change the system, but realized the people that were investigating the system were ex-bankers that were sponsored by bankers. And so they ain't going to change it.

试图创办"非部分准备金"银行

So then I went around trying to create a bank. I wanted to create a non-fractional reserve bank called Bank to the Future. And the Bank of England told me that I could only build it on top of a bank that already was doing fractional reserve. So you couldn't have a model where you didn't leverage up client assets.

发现比特币

So then eventually I found Bitcoin. I started investing in over a hundred of the different Bitcoin companies — and then one by one they all became owned by FIC. Because companies like Coinbase went from a small idea to help people get Bitcoin, to floating on the stock market for $100 billion. One by one I realized all of the companies I'd invested in were subordinate to the same financial interest.

So then I just started creating content, sold my businesses, and started working on becoming as sovereign as possible — trying to help people understand how to sit outside the system so that this financial industrial complex can not get any of your energy, and you can boycott the system. You can sit outside the system and try and become more sovereign.

That's my 26-year journey of trying to answer the question of where did my father's money go.

One thing that was very fortunate is that I got into Bitcoin at $3. And I was teaching people: own more Bitcoin every month, self-custody it, run a node. That obviously took my father out of dependency, and I was able to give him the best life possible until he sadly passed away shortly after COVID.

Alex: Oh, my sincere condolences about that. And it's a beautiful circle of life — that you've been able to tie back: he lost everything, you lost also quite a lot too from what I understood during that Bank to the Future episode (I think was a quarter of a million pounds if I'm not mistaken), and were able to raise from your ashes.

二、做市商如何"合法地"操纵市场

Alex: You said one thing about the time that you had as a stock broker was about manipulating stocks, or at least having an influence on the price. How did that exactly work?

Simon: Yeah. So I was a market maker for small-cap stocks on the London Stock Exchange — and a market called the Alternative Investment Market.

When I was a stockbroker, my job was just to sell stocks. You used to ring through to the market makers and try and get them to fulfill an order. Interestingly, the broker I worked for was the one my dad lost all his money in, so I managed to get a job there.

Someone would call, they'd want an order, I'd have to find the stock for them. So I'd call the market makers in London and they would have to give me a bid and an offer — they were obligated to always give me a price.

"中国墙"形同虚设

Now when I worked as a market maker, I realized that there's meant to be these things called Chinese walls in the investment bank. That is: when you work in corporate finance, you're doing mergers, acquisitions, takeover deals — and the traders / market makers are not meant to know about that, because they can build a position, manipulate the market knowing that a deal's going ahead.

But every market maker knew about every deal. The Chinese wall failed.

销售交易员与"蜡烛图陷阱"

On the other side, there's what's known as a sales trader. When Goldman Sachs needs to fulfill an order — because they're investing at such size, they need it to be a secret order that is fulfilled over time. So the sales trader would contact me as a market maker, and if I would take on at risk a bunch of the stock that he was trying to buy, then my job was just to push prices up and down until we could get people to sell their stock.

You would create all these things that look like candlestick charts that people are analyzing. But at the lower liquid stocks we were dealing with, where we could basically control the whole market with a few other market makers — often Goldman Sachs would leave orders with me and three other market makers, and then you can see what they're doing. There's these unwritten rules: you can push up a price at a psychological level where everyone will have their sell orders, you can pick up a bunch of stocks, and you can crash it down, hit their stop orders.

This is the process of trying to get lots and lots of retail investors to sell their stock, so that you can then give it to the institutional investor. Then the institutional investor could publish research later, would then make its way to the media, and then the media would then be where the stockbrokers fulfill orders for retail investors.

So institutions are always ahead of retail. And market makers are able to play that game in order to get the stock for the institutions.

三、IPO 作为"驯化机制"：以 SpaceX 为例

Alex: I'm curious — before we go into the deeper side of it, because you've also mentioned about IPOs — how would you analyze what we've seen as probably one of the biggest IPOs ever, the SpaceX IPO? What interest it served, and from an insider perspective, what exactly happened in the background?

Simon: An IPO is a mechanism of subordinating a company to the financial industrial complex. What you're doing is you're taking a business and you're turning it into a product — and that product is a share.

承销 → 董事席 → 投票权

Our job would initially be to underwrite that offering, where we would get discounted stock and then we would guarantee a certain amount of stock we had to purchase. Then we would go out to our network of institutional investors and try and sell it to them. With it, we would construct lots of rights — and the more we could sell, the more rights we would have in the company. If we got sold enough, we would then get a board seat.

When you got a board seat, it means the executives that are managing the company have to answer to a board. Those boards represent the shareholders, and the shareholders have the voting rights.

So the game of investment banking is to: 1. Turn as many businesses into products, 2. Get as many board seats as you can, 3. Get as many voting rights as you can.

The best investment banks have the most board seats, the most voting rights, and are productizing as many of those companies as possible. Once you do that, you own a piece of the business — or proxies for institutional investors — and you have a board that fulfills your goals. The executives then work for you.

用债务、期权、抵押贷款层层驯化

Then what you do is you load them up with debt. You want to make it so the company has a bunch of debt, because once you can use the debt capital markets and the equity capital markets, you can subordinate them further.

Then you want to load the entrepreneurs and the executives up with options, so that they are rewarded to follow the behavior that you'd want them to follow in order to get paid when those options become shares.

Then you load them up with collateralized loans — you give them a loan against their stock that they personally own. Once you've done that, they are fully subordinate to the financial industrial complex and they can no longer speak freely. They have fiduciary duties to their shareholders, and their wealth is dependent upon compliance with these levers.

与"深层国家"的接口

In the more larger companies that are connected to military contracts — like SpaceX, where they depend upon Pentagon budgets and government contracts — that goes into the deep state. The deep state is where you have these blackmail operations like Jeffrey Epstein, and MI6 / CIA / Mossad operations, in order to make sure that everyone is subordinate to your power structure.

Those military companies are also subordinated to the same financial industrial complex, as are technical companies. Now technology is becoming the new lever of power: who owns the data, who can control the chips, who owns the control flows, what countries are sovereign and how do we co-opt them in order to allow for this flow.

Elon 的万亿净资产其实是"合规净资产"

If anyone goes against your agenda, then you have a network of short sellers, hedge funds, and derivatives contracts that can destroy your value if you get off track.

If Elon goes too far off track, you can destroy the value of his trillion-dollar net worth — because it is not a trillion dollars of gold that he can send anywhere without government permission. It's not a trillion dollars of Bitcoin that you can just send. It is a trillion dollars of net worth that is dependent upon compliance with the financial industrial complex.

This is the game of investment banking. You build all the products around it to subordinate governments, companies and individuals — and that is the complex that I call the FIC: the Financial Industrial Complex.

四、FIC 的演化史：从荷兰帝国到美利坚帝国

Alex: Let's break it down a little bit and make it more concrete. Where did this FIC start? Who are the biggest actors and what are their interests?

Simon: Well, it started where you're from — the Dutch Empire.

三大支柱：央行、有限责任公司、政府储蓄罐

The Dutch Empire created the three pillars of this: 1. A central banking system, 2. The private limited liability company, 3. And the government as the piggy bank.

You use the bond market and the equity market to tokenize or securitize every company. You then use the government in order to subordinate them to bondholders through the debt capital markets. And then you control the money printer that creates all the money — the central banking system, which effectively is owned by the banks, and those banks create currency every time they issue a loan.

The mechanism: leave all the people with the debt through the debt markets where they pay the yield, transfer the government's value over to the private corporate interest (which was the Dutch East India Company), and then use their military and naval fleets to steal resources from the world — by having the strongest fiat currency and inflating away other people's currencies.

The gold standard was a mechanism for making sure that the Dutch guilder had the strongest currency, while you invade other countries through the strongest naval fleet to steal their resources and put them over into the Dutch East India Company.

荷兰 → 英国 → 美国的"外包链条"

The Dutch Empire started to outsource the labor to the British because they were the cheap labor. The British learned from the Dutch and then they created: - The Bank of England, - The British East India Company, - The largest naval fleet in the world.

Then they bankrupted the UK government. Once they fully bankrupted the UK government, World War I happened — which needed the Federal Reserve. So then we transferred all the value over to the American Empire. The Federal Reserve was created. The private interest was the military-industrial complex.

Alex: Sorry, why did they need the Fed specifically? The US was not even that important back then.

Simon: Central banking is a mechanism for bankrupting governments and transferring all the value over to private-public partnerships. And the best mechanism for doing that is war. If you can manufacture war, then you have the ability to fund both sides of the war — and the winner is the one that accumulates the assets from the loser.

一战的金融剧本：同时孵化共产主义、法西斯主义与犹太复国主义

If you look at World War I: - The Federal Reserve was created in 1913. - Wall Street set about funding different ideologies. During World War I, Wall Street funded the Bolshevik Revolution in Russia, which overthrew the Russian Tsar and replaced it with the Western Marxist ideology called communism. So the Soviet Union was born out of a revolution that was funded by the same forces that created fake capitalism in America through a debt-based Ponzi scheme called the Federal Reserve system. - The governor of the German central bank (Reichsbank) was one of the brothers of the Warburg family — which was the same family as the governor of the Federal Reserve. - During that war, you also had the Balfour Declaration — where the Rothschilds got from the British government the commitment to create a Jewish state. That happened in 1917, the same year as the Bolshevik revolution, in order to acquire the resources on oil from the Middle East and from Russia.

They installed communism in Russia and used Israel to create a militia group to destabilize the Middle East — so that they could steal all the oil that was needed and partner with Middle Eastern countries. That was World War I in a nutshell.

大萧条、纳粹融资与 BIS 的双面游戏

Then you had a pump and dump cycle where you get everyone to lever their assets leading up to the '20s — and then the Federal Reserve rug-pulls the system. You create a Great Depression, you confiscate all the gold (which was 1933), and then you fund fascism.

Wall Street funded Hitler. When Hitler invaded a country like Czechoslovakia, he would call up the Reichsbank, the Reichsbank would call up the Bank for International Settlements (BIS). The BIS didn't store any gold — it used the Swiss central bank, the Bank of England, and the Federal Reserve to store its actual gold. So then the Reichsbank would call the BIS, the BIS would then call the Bank of England and say, "Hey, that gold is now owned by Nazi Germany." And so the Bank of England would store the gold for Nazi Germany while fighting a war with Nazi Germany.

That kind of tells you the craziness of it. It was that gold that propped up the system and funded the further expansion of the wars.

BIS 与凡尔赛宫的回声

The reason I covered that story is because the Bank for International Settlements was set up for one purpose: to take Germany's debt and manage the reparations that Germany needed to pay from World War I. It was signed in the Versailles Palace — exactly where Trump signed the deal with Iran right now.

The debt was priced in gold. So Germany needed to repay gold, and the only way to repay gold was to print the currency that they controlled at the German central bank. That created hyperinflation. It created the degeneracy of the Weimar Republic, and then that led to an uprising where Hitler arrested the Rothschilds and launched his own central banking system and a labor-backed currency.

That was a resistance against the Western banking system. But then Wall Street managed to fund it, and then infiltrate it with the Bank for International Settlements, and basically confiscate the gold — that then led to World War II.

战后新秩序：IMF、世界银行、石油美元

The winners of the war rewrite history, make it out like it's something different. And then we set the next world order: IMF, World Bank — persuading every country to stop converting their currency into gold, and instead convert it into dollars via the IMF. The promise was that if you did that, you'd get a loan from the World Bank denominated in dollars, which would then destroy the currencies of everyone that took those loans — because you'd launch a currency war.

The promise was that the dollar would always be backed by gold. So then they launched the Cold War, the Vietnam War, the Korean War. The Vietnam War bankrupted the US government. They came off the gold standard, assassinated King Faisal of Saudi Arabia, then went into the petrodollar — forced them to price in dollars. But the difference was that they had to reinvest it in US debt, and then the yield in the Federal Reserve system would purchase equipment from the military, and then America would set up a base there.

That set up the petrodollar and OPEC. And now we're at the final stage of the bankruptcy of the petrodollar — so we closed the Strait of Hormuz in order to reset the world order, break the petrodollar, and move to the next order — which is what the FIC is repositioning for right now.

五、从军工复合体 → 金融复合体：1971 的拐点

Alex: You also argue that the power shifted at some point from a military-industrial complex to the financial one. When did that really occur, and who were the most important actors?

Simon: Complete dominance happened around '71 — with the coming off of the gold standard. That's when the FIC financialized, securitized, and privatized everything to a massive degree.

But really the FIC were always in charge through the central banking system, because they got to create the fiat currency to steal resources in a Ponzi scheme. There's never enough interest to pay off the debt, so you're always rolling over the debt until the government's bankrupt. That's the game. And you do it slowly, so people don't notice it.

跨国资本如何凌驾于政府之上

Really, with the creation of the BIS, the IMF, and the World Bank, that created the construct of transnational capital that sits above governments: - Governments were subordinate to transnational capital through bondholders. - Companies were subordinate through the equity capital markets. - People were subordinate through borrowing to create fiat currency through the banks.

What defined that was having the strongest currency backed by gold, so that others would destroy their currency and you could weaponize currency wars.

71 之后：石油美元、隐蔽战争与 Safari Club

In '71 we came off the gold standard because everyone wanted their gold instead of dollars. They wanted to exchange their dollars for gold. America defaulted on that promise.

From that moment we entered into needing to engineer a covert war to make sure that there was constant demand for US debt and US treasuries — and that was the creation of the petrodollar. That's why you needed the regime change in Saudi Arabia.

George Bush set up a covert operation called Safari Club. Safari Club groomed people like Osama bin Laden — that led to 9/11 and various other CIA assets that would create all the wars in the Middle East, that would then lead to the forever war model, and everything that we're resolving and reversing today.

The idea was: if you price oil in dollars, you then need to reinvest it in government debt. That government debt would fuel the military-industrial complex. By having the most stable currency, everyone would need to lend it to the US government — while they launch wars against your country. And then you would be forced to spend those dollars back in the American stock market. Then that would be tied loans that would lead to military bases being set up in your country — because you either get regime change, a dictator installed, or you negotiate and do what Saudi did: set up something like the petrodollar.

反转的时刻

This all functions as long as the dollar is going up in value. Once people stop having faith in US treasuries, and the banking system stops using US treasuries as collateral, then that weakens the dollar and reverses all the chain. People end up going into gold, or the central banks because they don't want to hold treasuries. You end up having wars that lead to reasons like confiscation of treasuries — like happened to Russia — and then the whole trend reverses.

This is normally engineered by the FIC because they control the MIC.

金本位脱钩 → 中国崛起 → 多极化

But what actually really created transnational capital dominance to the hyper-concentrated degree we have today — it was the coming off of the gold standard. That led to the financialization and securitization of every company. From that, the FIC was able to think of the world in terms of global rather than nationalistic — and so they built up China, built the world's largest manufacturing base, and through globalization built China's wealth. Which is actually national wealth, because China is actually controlled by the government — one of the only countries in the world that's controlled by the government.

So they can't penetrate China. And so as China grew, the order changed. Now the American military can't even produce a weapon without China. We end up in a changing order at that stage.

六、Graeber 的"军事–铸币–奴隶复合体"

Alex: I'm not sure if you've ever came across this book of David Graeber, Debt: The First 5,000 Years?

Simon: I haven't actually read that one yet. It's on my reading list.

Alex: I would highly recommend it. In one chapter, there's something that I think would really tie in to a lot of what you're talking about — what he called the "military-coinage-slavery complex."

He uses the example of Alexander the Great, who through his conquest would require I think half a ton of silver daily to pay his army. Funnily enough, the word soldier itself is derived from solidus, the gold coin used to pay the troops.

The idea: he funded the cost of conquering Persia, then the Persian mines forcing enslaved war captives to mine and extract those precious metals, to then mint those bullions into coins. Wherever those coins would be used by the soldiers, they would create economies locally. This was the self-reinforcing cycle.

Today you could make a parallel in a much more sophisticated way — about the dollar, and about what you just said about the debt slavery process of tying people (executives, entrepreneurs, countries on a nation-state level), and now through the digital sphere with stablecoins, which we haven't talked about yet but which would be the next iteration of a much more sophisticated global scale.

七、今天的 FIC：BIS、央行、ETF 帝国与主权基金

Alex: The FIC today — is it governed by BlackRock and the other Big Three? Or is it much more complex than this?

Simon: A lot more complex.

巴塞尔的"飞地"：BIS

The Western central banking system has its own jurisdiction in Davos. The BIS — depending on the GDP of your country, you become a larger shareholder in BIS through your central bank. So the largest shareholder in BIS is the Federal Reserve. But as you manage capital outflows, you can change the GDP of a country. America has gone from like 70% of global GDP to about 25%. So the Federal Reserve is becoming less and less powerful in the BIS system over time.

三种央行模型：国有化、私有化、混合

Then you have different types of systems: - China: member of BIS, but it is a nationalized central bank that is owned by the government. There's state banking, mini banks, community banks — thousands of community banks in China, which is a fully nationalized banking system. The PBOC and the banking system is fully nationalized. - America: on the extreme, the Federal Reserve is owned by the banks, and the banks are private companies that are all public. Those public companies receive constant flows of capital via passive investments — mainly ETFs, pension funds, endowment funds, and foreign capital.

The owners of those banks as public companies is managed now by ETFs and asset managers, because now 50% of the stock market is ETF investments. The largest managers of ETFs are BlackRock, State Street, and Vanguard — collectively they manage approximately $25 trillion of assets. BlackRock about $14 trillion of assets.

主权财富基金的桌位

They then partner with countries that were able to maintain their own resources. A country like Saudi Arabia, UAE, Qatar, Norway, Singapore, and China — they all have the largest sovereign wealth funds in the world. They get a seat at the table co-investing with the FIC. But they represent their own — because Saudi is an absolute monarchy that owns its own resources. It's not private like the Federal Reserve system, it's not CCP nationalist like the Chinese system.

The FIC has partnered with sovereign wealth funds, and the central banking system is the mechanism for trying to get more resources and change those games. Every war is a constant state of fluid capital that's changing power.

Right now, Middle Eastern power is on the rise significantly as a result of the closure of the Strait of Hormuz. Even though it appears that it's declining, it's actually growing significantly as a result of this.

Saudi Aramco → BlackRock 的董事席

An example: with the rise of the petrodollar in Saudi Arabia, the partnership with China where China became the largest purchaser, America going to an energy exporter — that completely changed the petrodollar dynamics. Saudi got more power. What did they do? They floated through the investment bank part of Saudi Aramco — 1 to 2% of Saudi oil production. That gave them leverage with the investment banks in order to get a board seat on BlackRock. The CEO of Saudi Aramco became a board member on BlackRock. So now he can represent Saudi interest. So now the company BlackRock — that has 20,000 board seats on every major company — can manage a portfolio of where the war is, when the war goes or not.

Through managing technology like Aladdin — their AI technology that sovereign wealth funds use, treasury departments use, central banks use, pension funds use. You can scenario-plan with BlackRock's technology: if the Strait of Hormuz is going to close, and Iran retaliates, and these central banks start buying gold — what's the ideal portfolio and capital allocation?

Capital is plugged into the same system that then coordinates via ETFs and capital investments. Those flows is where you try and get leverage. How do I get more power over capital flows? That's the game. That's a constant state of tension.

People want an easy conspiracy theory of 300 people sat in a room in an organized cabal. To a certain degree, there are powerful people, but that changes all the time. Getting back to UK — yeah, so that's the next: civil unrest, digital ID testing. Let's use social media, they create ops. I'll go through two ones — because one that relates back to where we started, Netherlands, where this whole thing started: the Dutch Empire.

You know you are in the asset stripping phase by FIC when you get tax policy that doesn't make sense. You decrease the growth, you increase the inflation, you increase the taxes on the people that remain, you send the millionaires and billionaires into another jurisdiction, and then the corporates are exempt from that tax structure by using elaborate tax structuring — where corporations are exempt through organizations like BlackRock and you contributing to your pension. The FIC receive the yield on it, and then you pump that into the stock market. That's asset stripping.

When you do things like tax on your assets, you make it where everyone has to sell their assets in order to pay their tax — or borrow against their assets, which makes them a collateralized debt obligation for the FIC. That's the asset stripping phase.

Europe is being systemically asset stripped, and that's why the war with Russia and Ukraine will continue probably for another 3 years — because that's how you justify reallocating all of that investment, not into productive infrastructure, but dependency: energy dependency, then military.

America's now selling them the energy.

This is systemic portfolio management where you hedge your bets. You use derivatives to profit from both sides, and then you use your portfolio of companies in order to manufacture the narrative that is needed. A classic divide and conquer operation.

Europe has the stocks doing well, the debt is going up, but eventually it will lead to austerity, removal of benefits, and the distressed acquisition. In your case in Netherlands, you have one of the key nodes of the AI infrastructure which they want to acquire — that's a very important part of the whole AI chip manufacturing, ASML, part of that structure.

十一、终局：你将一无所有并感到幸福

Alex: Maybe like, if we are already on this topic — because it's also tying in what you said. It was also what von der Leyen brought up I think almost a year ago about the Savings and Investments Union (SIU), which would actually then be a way to funnel the European savers' money into different programs and different funding schemes — one of them being the funding of EU defense and the ReArm Europe 2030 program. 你将一无所有：通胀 + 强制变现 + 代币化托管

You will own nothing by inflating the cost of living crisis — where anyone that's got assets has to sell them to live. Anybody that's got assets is managing them through the FIC and BlackRock. You tokenize them all, so that you end up with a token and they end up with the asset in custody.

When you manufacture crisis, each crisis leads to concentrating wealth upwards. If you manage to get on the right side of that, they want to manage your assets for you. So they get the voting rights through the ETFs.

并感到幸福：UBI + 可编程货币 + 强制电动化

Then they want to make you happy. How? Well, they can do that by giving you a universal basic income. They'll sell Elon saying "universal high income." You don't need to work. We'll give you AI that will get the costs of everything down. But if there's no jobs, we'll put you on a universal basic income and you can live in a utopia and you can rent everything from us. You will be given enough universal basic income to pay the interest on the debt — to make sure you can buy the products we want you to buy.

But it also needs to be programmable, so that we can penalize you for spending money in the wrong direction, and reward you for spending money in the right direction. We can manage monetary policy by making it where you don't own a house, you have to buy Elon Musk's $7,000 electric house. You don't own a diesel car, you own an electric vehicle — where if you don't comply, we can shut down your car.

Everything is about controlling the choke points to ensure compliance — because when you're creating this level of wealth inequality, you know that the biggest risk to you is the people uprising in a communistic/socialist movement that takes away private power. That then needs to be where you either wait for it to happen, or you manufacture civil unrest in a controlled environment and build the control grids.

That's how you will own nothing and be happy if they achieve their plan.

十二、Bitcoin 的位置：在控制网格之外，自我托管为王

Now that ties nicely into Bitcoin. I know that people are very mixed around Bitcoin. The reason is because it's digital — and because it's digital, it plays into those control grids. Those that love that realize you need to exit the system.

If you want to hold an asset, you've only got a few ways of doing it:

选项一：现金

What's the problem with cash? It gets devalued over time. So you lose your purchasing power. But it's nice for short-term expenses — if you happen to not lose all your value in the time before you spend it.

选项二：黄金

Gold has preserved its value for 5,000 years. Its supply only increases at an average of 1.5% to 2% historically. But most people when they need to store it at scale end up giving it to BlackRock, and they end up with paper gold instead. Or they end up with a tokenized version of gold, which is "you will own nothing and we will hold the gold for you."

Alex: Or you buy physical gold at GoldRepublic and we store it for you on your behalf.

Simon: Correct. So then you need to come up with a mechanism for storing your own gold — or you come up with the best type of custodian where you think they'll give you your gold under the set of legal protection. But it's still difficult, and most people end up storing their gold with someone else under the best set of circumstances where they think they may be able to get that gold in case they need it.

Or you have a safe in your house, you own your own gold, and you're autonomous. The downside of that is that at scale it's a big risk, and most people can't do it. And then you don't have to pay the fees to store the gold.

选项三：Bitcoin —— 数字主权

So Bitcoin then basically said: you can own your own value, but it's digital. You can send it to whoever you want as long as you self-custody it. The monetary policy is enforced by a decentralized network of miners and nodes that can never change it. There'll only ever be 21 million Bitcoin. People lose it over time when they lose access to the keys. Effectively 20 million bitcoins have already been mined. There's only another million to be mined. You can plan out the monetary policy any time into the future and know that more and more people will probably lose those Bitcoin because they lost the key, or died, or didn't plan how to pass it on. So it is a fixed supply.

比特币的拓扑：白皮书 → 矿工 → 节点 → 自托管

  • Open-source code: an anonymous developer released a white paper called Satoshi Nakamoto, and a bunch of developers came around and started being involved in coding it. The early developers were people like Hal Finney, Gavin Andresen and various others.
  • Mining is geographically distributed: Iran is the largest sovereign Bitcoin miner with nuclear energy mining Bitcoin. UAE is another big Bitcoin miner. Russia is. America has about 20 different public companies that collectively manage 40% of the hash rate. China has 21% of the hash rate. The network can never be taken down. It is now the largest distributed supercomputer that exists in the world today.
  • Nodes: the rules are enforced by anybody that wants to run a node — and it's cheap to run a node. As long as you self-custody your Bitcoin, you can connect it to a node, verify your own transactions, store the entire history, and contribute to enforcing the rules whenever any of the coders try to change it, or the miners want to signal a new change. There's 25,000 of those nodes around the world run by anyone that wants to run one. 25,000 sovereign people that can control their own value, send their own value, and know that the monetary policy will never change.

But it is digital. It does rely upon energy. So there are choke points.

十三、Satoshi 之谜：Len Sassaman 与 David Chaum 的荷兰渊源

People look at choke points and say, "Okay, so Satoshi Nakamoto, this anonymous person created Bitcoin. Why?" You can theorize. I've got my own theory of why.

If you go back to that time, there was a movement to try and create digital currencies from the cypherpunk movement. One of those attempts was DigiCash. DigiCash was created by a guy called David Chaum. David Chaum was in Netherlands teaching at a university two other cypherpunk cryptographers. One was called Len Sassaman.

Len Sassaman was assassinated. What? Sorry, let me correct that. He killed himself and allegedly committed suicide.

Alex: He got "suicided."

Simon: Well — again, my belief. I want to be respectful of the dads and his family. He allegedly committed suicide one month after the developer that Satoshi Nakamoto handed the project over to — Gavin Andresen — met with the CIA. One month after, Len Sassaman lost his life. And so did Hal Finney, the first one to ever mine a Bitcoin and contribute to that.

Gavin Andresen is still alive today, but he went off into another project. He forked off Bitcoin and tried to create an alternative version of Bitcoin, and left the Bitcoin community.

The point is: I personally believe that Satoshi Nakamoto was Len Sassaman. There's significant evidence to that effect. And I believe that it was created from his mentorship by David Chaum in Netherlands, and Hal Finney was the one that most likely was involved in the coding while Len Sassaman probably released the white paper. Len Sassaman and Hal Finney are no longer with us today.

一个反直觉的假设：Bitcoin 是稳定币/CBDC 项目的"叛逃产物"

What was David Chaum doing? David Chaum and DigiCash was shut down because they tried to create private transactions on top of the banking system — and because the banks could shut off the choke point, they were able to disappear that project.

I personally believe that they set about iterating through central intelligence stablecoins and central bank digital currencies as digital currencies — and I believe that Len Sassaman may have been involved in that project, and he released the white paper as a breakaway movement from those digital currencies and digital fiat currencies that are now stablecoins and CBDCs. He released the white paper as a rogue agent from that project — because otherwise, I don't think it was meant to be open-source code on a decentralized network with node operators where anyone can run a node.

That's the antithesis to the digital currencies that are currently being confiscated — like Iran stablecoins. You can call Tether and have them frozen, and Scott Bessent did it. So I believe it was a breakaway movement, with many many infiltration attempts to try and bring the project closer to centralization. Because I was involved in 2011 when Bitcoin was $3, and got to meet many of those developers, and was invited to many of the early squats where Bitcoin coders were working on the code, I've seen many many of the infiltration attempts by central intelligence to try and infiltrate the network.

Still those exist to this day. But Bitcoin, I believe, has maintained and preserved its resistance against those. It still is open-source code with a distributed supercomputer, the largest in the world. The rules are enforced by anyone that wants to run a node. Anyone can own their own value still today in self-custody, send it to anyone they want without a government, without a bank. The money supply, I still believe, will never ever change.

十四、隐私问题：CoinJoin、Lightning 与"主权 vs 屈从"光谱

Alex: What about the privacy though? Because Bitcoin is pseudo-anonymous. People hold Bitcoin — I hold Bitcoin, Bitcoin is the reason why I got into finance — but apart from holding it and all the features that you just described, there's for now not much more I can do because it's locked either in the wallet or, in some cases, an exchange (which is not recommended). It doesn't really move, and it can be traced through KYC/AML procedures.

What is your conclusion to that? Is there maybe another layer on top of Bitcoin that will solve this, or is this not a problem to you at all?

Simon: The on-ramps and off-ramps mean that whenever you engage in fiat currency, you have to play fiat games — which is KYC, AML and all that. But I can send my Bitcoin to anyone I want to, and no one can stop that.

Now, law enforcement exists. I still need to play within the confines of the law. If I break the law, I break the law, and I need to face all the consequences of that. If somebody wants to steal my physical value, they can try and steal it.

CoinJoin 与 Lightning

Part of the benefits of self-custody is that there's a couple of other features. You can run a node. You can also use a CoinJoin — and that's completely legal. One of the first bits of technology that the developers worked on was CoinJoins — combining transactions with millions of other transactions so that you can have private transactions.

The next was to set up Layer 2 — where you can take the value that you want to spend rather than save, and you can transact in a Layer 2 network like Lightning. This is the path that the open-source community has taken us down. If you want to obfuscate your transactions in CoinJoins — as long as it's legal in your country — you can do that. At the moment it is completely legal to do that.

It doesn't circumvent the law. It allows you to control your rails within the legal system that you decide to live in.

主权 vs 屈从光谱

Remember: I often talk about there is complete subordination on one side of a spectrum, and there is complete sovereignty on the other side of a spectrum. My life has been going from complete subordination to as much sovereignty as I can get.

I still live on an island which still has a government, and we have a community, and I need to play within the rules of that community of social behavior and social consensus. I need to pay the tax and my contribution in the community that I live in. If I don't play within the confines of the rules of this country, I have to accept the full consequences of the jurisdiction and the legal system by which I live in.

I'm still subordinate to the social contract with my wife. I still may have a business that has shareholders. I still may have some money in fiat currency, which means that part of my wealth is subordinate to the bank — and I fully expect them to give me all sorts of problems every time I try and spend it at scale.

These are all subordination vehicles. My and your job is to wake up every week and say: if I take that action, am I more subordinate or more sovereign? And who am I subordinate to, and how do I move towards sovereign in that aspect of my life? That's the job.

But nobody is fully sovereign. If you believe in God, then you are subordinate to the rules at which God governs the world. If you are atheist, you are still subordinate to those structures of what you think the rules are of the world. We are all subordinate to something or someone — but some are completely subordinate debt slaves.

奴隶制的演化

That's what the FIC wanted to create. The concept of debt was originally a mechanism for getting somebody using their labor to repay the debt — so you actually could use them as slaves and they could work off their debt.

It wasn't what we ended up creating as slavery in the West, where we actually took Africans from their island, destroyed their currency, shipped them in chains over to here and say "you are mine." Originally, slavery was the concept of: you took out a debt and you couldn't repay that debt, so you were able to work off that debt through your labor and your body. Originally, it led to industries like prostitution and all sorts of stuff because women used their bodies in order to pay off debts. But it was working towards a debt of becoming free from your slave master.

It was only the Europeans and the Americans that created a version of slavery where you literally just take people in chains and say, "You're mine, with no possibility to work it off — you're owned by me."

They replaced that when slavery became illegal in most countries. They replaced it with debt subordination — make you a collateralized debt obligation where you earn just enough to pay off the interest, and you work your whole life just to pay the interest. As you progress, you take on more debt and pay more interest, and you do that forever. That's the slavery that they created to replace the European and American version of slavery that they did to the Africans.

十五、操作上的"完全主权"路径

Alex: So if I resume it properly — the sense of privacy in that sense that you have through Bitcoin is not as important as the ability to transact between other addresses (from me to you or from anyone), and thereby gives me a vehicle to operate within different systems where I will be subordinated through a system of choice. Is that a correct way of —

Simon: If I'm willing to remove all my subordination vehicles — i.e. not hold my Bitcoin with a custodian, not hold it in the exchange, self-custody it, run my own node, and use a CoinJoin wallet connected to a node in self-custody — I've got fully private transactions, and I can send that to a Lightning wallet and spend it in complete privacy within a Layer 2 network.

I know that's a lot of geeky things there. But if you want to be sovereign, then Bitcoin offers it.

If you want to have paper Bitcoin in a BlackRock ETF where Coinbase custodies it and leverages it up and creates more paper Bitcoin than actual Bitcoin, and you want to be subordinate to BlackRock, Coinbase, Fidelity, and you want to give Larry Fink power — then you can be fully subordinate and own Bitcoin. But the minute you want it private, in self-custody, where you can send it to anyone — that all exists.

Alex: But you will still be flagged if you mix it, even with CoinJoins — it will be marked as a [tainted coin]?

Simon: Yeah. So when you go back to the fiat currency world, you have to pay fiat currency gains. If you want to spend that as fiat currency, then you're back to the same rules where whatever you spend in fiat currency, they'll want to know how you achieved that wealth and whether you achieved it legitimately. When you off-board into fiat currency spending, then you need your documentation, because you're in fiat currency world.

But when you want to stay out of fiat currency world, you stay out of fiat currency world. If you use a CoinJoin and you can prove that you received that value completely legitimately in the fiat currency world, then you can spend it.

Alex: Hotel California. Sorry, Hotel California.

Simon: Yeah. Remember, this is about building sovereign wealth outside the system — where you don't have to answer to DTC, BlackRock, any custodian. When you want to spend it, if you've got significant value because of the fixed supply, and you've had a long enough time frame to play out that thesis — I can't guarantee it will happen, not financial advice — you can go wherever you want. The value comes with you.

Whereas they want you to have subordinated wealth — wealth and a job that is connected to geographical boundaries. Therefore you're stuck in that country and subordinate to their system, with a physical business, a physical job in the physical world with physical debt. This goes wherever you want it to go.

十六、Wall Street 的 Bitcoin：Saylor 与 Strategy 的真正角色

Alex: So this is sovereign Bitcoin. And then there's Wall Street Bitcoin — which is Michael Saylor, treasury companies — and maybe perhaps a bit of context about also how the FIC then is trying to rein in this sovereign Bitcoin that we just described. How would you paint this picture?

Simon: Okay. The FIC doesn't mind two types of people, but they don't want the third type.

他们不介意的人

Type 1: Broke people in debt with freedom of speech. They don't care. You can yap away. You can talk about Epstein. You can say whatever you want. Some countries you can't, others you can. You can complain about everything. You can say that Trump's a pedophile in Epstein's network — whatever you want to say. They don't mind, as long as you're broke and in debt and keep yapping away. Freedom of speech is no threat to their power.

Type 2: Wealthy people that are captured by the system. Elon Musk is an extreme example — people where they've got collateralized wealth in DTCC-owned custody structures.

Alex: So what is DTC?

Simon: Basically you don't own your shares. If you own a share, you own a claim from the broker that you own that share. That broker has a claim against DTCC, which is a central clearing which is owned by the brokers. So it's all promise that you own that share. In the bankruptcy process, in the breaking of the system, DTCC owns the share. The broker owns a claim against DTCC, and you own a claim against the broker. So you're layers removed from your share — but you don't own them. You just log in and it looks like you own it.

Same with gold in custody. You log in and it looks like you own it — but what legal ways do you have to get it? That's the contract. Same with Bitcoin in custody, any of these custodians.

They don't mind you having all your wealth in custody — using it as collateral, leveraged, with lots of equity that they control and debt — and very powerful, and very high net worth, so that they manage your net worth for you. They don't mind as many of those as possible. They'll capture as many of them — where you're on the same team either through shared interest, leverage, or blackmail, whatever they need to do depending on how powerful you are and what game you're playing in.

他们不要的"第三种人"

What they don't want is people that are free to speak with wealth outside the system — that are willing to speak against the system, reallocate capital, and actually drive people to taking capital either away from here or galvanizing these people. Those are the people that they don't want.

Wherever there were the biggest uprisings in those came from: the gold community, the cash community, and the Bitcoin community. So they set about: - Eliminating the cash community by making everything digital banking, - Centralizing all the gold through central banking, - And now they want to do the same to Bitcoin.

Operation Chokepoint 2.0 与对 Bitcoin 的"产品化收编"

They created an operation called Operation Chokepoint 2.0, where they created companies that you could destroy to remove credibility, manufacture a crisis, take out all the banks that were servicing the crypto companies — and replace them with Wall Street.

So then you got the birth of the FIC players like Goldman Sachs, JP Morgan creating options markets, derivatives markets, perpetual markets. Silicon Valley invested in all the companies. The companies that were willing to be co-opted became public and subordinate. They took a bunch of the Bitcoin miners and tried to make them public companies. They then took key people and co-opted them.

They took a model like Michael Saylor and Bitcoin Treasury Company to create vehicles for using financialization, arbitrage and securitization to centralize as much Bitcoin as possible. Larry Fink and all the others created ETFs where they hold the Bitcoin and you get a paper version of Bitcoin.

Then they started to create subordination vehicles through Cantor Fitzgerald by creating Bitcoin-backed loans — so you have to custody your Bitcoin with them, and then they give you some fiat currency so that you don't sell your Bitcoin, but they hold it for you. Then they created companies that did that. They went around and took key Bitcoiners and said, "Hey, why don't you put your Bitcoin in a company? We'll then take the company public, and then you can take all your businesses and reverse-merge them into this public offering." They did that with David Bailey, with Adam Back, with Jack Mallers. Then they could take key parts of the infrastructure and get them within the FIC control grids.

Then they would try and make it where: - You own a Bitcoin ETF instead of Bitcoin, - You would be incentivized to borrow against your Bitcoin (so you were leveraged to them and hold it in custody), - You would buy a Bitcoin treasury company instead of Bitcoin.

They can build out their hedge fund network, derivative network, and use Strategy as an arbitrage vehicle for trying to create an environment of controlling the short-term price of Bitcoin using paper Bitcoin — just like they did with gold.

他们最不希望你成为的那种人

In the long term, they can't hold that resistance — because it costs a lot and causes systemic risk. Eventually people want their physical gold, physical Bitcoin, or cash out of the bank. That creates a run structure on the custodians, the leverage institutions, and the banks. So you just need a long-term game.

What don't they want? They don't want you being a long-term investor where you own more Bitcoin in self-custody every month. Instead, they want you speculating on alternatives. They want you engaging in perpetual futures. They want to turn you into a degenerate gambler.

So Silicon Valley funded all the alternatives to Bitcoin — created foundations, created alternative models like proof of stake rather than proof of work (where they can control it based upon staking), created the degeneracy industry of meme coins, NFTs, alternatives to Bitcoin, Bitcoin hard forks. They infiltrated developers to say, "Let's create divide and conquer within the Bitcoin community. You create one that's optimized for cash, they create one that's store of value. We'll create a fake version of Satoshi Nakamoto called Dr. Craig. Then we'll get Epstein operatives like Brock Pierce in order to try and infiltrate and invest in companies."

They did everything they could because they want you: - Wealthy under their control, or - With no Bitcoin, a degenerate gambler.

But they don't want you Bitcoin and self-custody, running nodes, through CoinJoins, where you take some of your fiat currency every month and take it out of the system, and you build wealth outside the system with a 10-year, 20-year, 30-year plan in order to be sovereign. That's the exact type of person they don't want. So they attacked them, and many people fell for it.

Right now they've got approximately 3 to 4 million Bitcoin in FIC vehicles, and the rest of it is still in self-custody. Fortunately Bitcoin was designed as proof of work — which means owning the coins doesn't allow you to control the network. You can leverage them to control the price in the short term — you can't control it in the long term. You can try and use it to leverage price short-term to get people to sell their Bitcoin, so that they end up with as much Bitcoin as possible. That's exactly what they're doing today. They're using financial engineering to get as much Bitcoin as possible, suppress the price so that you get impatient and you sell it — and they end up with the Bitcoin, or you get margin-called and they end up with the Bitcoin, or you end up with a Wall Street wrapper instead of Bitcoin.

十七、Strategy 不会破产 —— 它太"有用"了

Alex: So now in the case of Michael Saylor and Strategy — what is now the situation exactly? Because a lot of the speculation: "Oh, this model is now falling apart, the house of cards is collapsing, they will be forced to sell Bitcoin," and so on and so forth. What is your view on what's going to happen next on that question particularly?

Simon: I think most people have this one wrong. I'm not a shareholder in Strategy and I never will be — and if I ever change that, I'll disclose it publicly. None of the other ancillary financial vehicles around the company as well.

Strategy is doing what it was designed to do, which is create an arbitrage vehicle for manipulating the short-term price of Bitcoin for the hedge funds and Jane Streets and appointed representatives into the Bitcoin ETF. The goal is to issue as much debt, as much equity, and as much structured products to centralize as much Bitcoin as possible.

Strategy is doing that. It has convertible debt, preference share and equity, a structured product, and a number of structured products that allows you to arbitrage and centralize as much Bitcoin as possible for the FIC.

为什么 FIC 不会让它倒

Most people are focusing on the wrong thing. They think it's going to go bankrupt into Chapter 11 and go into a death spiral. Why would FIC want to do that? They got the perfect vehicle for trying to capture as much Bitcoin in there as they can.

Strategy is perfect for it. Whenever Stretch trades at a discount, you can increase the yield and you can manufacture a narrative that they're going to sell the Bitcoin. You can sell some Bitcoin and crash the price and then buy it back cheaper. You can dilute shareholders by selling more equity and buying more Bitcoin — and decreasing their Bitcoin per share when it's trading at a discount, or increase their Bitcoin per share when the equity is trading at a premium. You've got the perfect arbitrage vehicle for trying to centralize as much Bitcoin as possible.

If I were FIC, I'd keep that going for as long as possible. I'd try and get as much of the debt, as much of the preference, and as much of the equity within an ETF. So I'd use the ratings agencies to try and get Saylor to say, "You do as we say, and you'll be included in the S&P 500. You don't do as we say, and we'll subordinate your net worth." Then you have to change your behavior in order to change the levers — so that eventually, when the price of Bitcoin comes back up, you're included in the S&P 500, and then we'll give you access to the passive flows so that BlackRock becomes the largest shareholder (which Jane Street was). It's hook, line, and sinker — Strategy. I would keep that alive for as long as possible.

No personal attack against Michael Saylor. He works for his shareholders. His shareholders have board members. He has to, by fiduciary duty, maximize the value of the company for his shareholders. And public companies are subordination vehicles. So he works for FIC, and FIC can destroy the value or increase the value in order to change his behavior — to centralize as much Bitcoin as possible. It's the perfect vehicle, and you resist by not buying it, by not participating.

抵制是个动词

This is the beauty. We get to boycott the system. - You boycott the Federal Reserve system by owning Bitcoin or gold. - You boycott the custodians and BlackRock by owning it in self-custody. - You boycott the banks by not borrowing against it. - You boycott Silicon Valley by investing direct into localized businesses that build decentralized structures that fight against their centralized structures. - You boycott this centralizing agenda by working on more community structures, more decentralization, and building wealth outside the system.

What is it in the end? It's a boycott vehicle. And it doesn't matter what the price is — because the lower the price, the more fiat currency allows you to buy more Bitcoin. So you measure your wealth in Bitcoin, measure your wealth in gold. As the price goes down, you get more of the gold or fiat or Bitcoin for your fiat currency. As the price goes up, your power within the fiat currency wealth / net worth increases — which allows you to exert more influence from outside the system.

十八、稳定币 = 私有化的 CBDC

Alex: And how do the stablecoins tie into that? Because until recently the Genius Act was actually solidifying certain regulations and laws around this. Also a lot of people celebrated the MiCA regulations in Europe. But eventually what it does — kind of like how I see it — is kind of a CBDC disguised under a dollar (or just put a different shape to it). It is, in fact, a vehicle as well that could enable those power dynamics to actually get hold on the dollar — which is a synthetic dollar actually on a global basis — where countries that do not have better currencies than the US dollar (which is the case of many countries that are underdeveloped) would then also be in this grid of control and influence that the dollar can expand on globally. How would you analyze this development?

Simon: Exactly. The stablecoins are just a privatized CBDC. Different rules, different beneficiary, but plays into the same FIC control grid.

两条平行的控制网格：CBDC 与稳定币

CBDCs play into the central banking system — and they're building a network of CBDCs that connects UAE, China, Hong Kong, Saudi Arabia, and Thailand via the mBridge project, which was created from the Bank for International Settlements that circumvents Swift. The Swift system — the Fed has already created [an equivalent] for wholesale settlement called FedNow, on a blockchain with nodes on top called stablecoins.

Stablecoins will then be integrated with CBDCs — so that you can create divide and conquer as you transition away from the dollar-dominated world. You have two control grids: the network of CBDCs, and the dollar-programmizable stablecoin.

While mBridge was building the network of CBDCs from the BIS, the Fed, the FIC and the TIC were building stablecoins via Silicon Valley.

稳定币的"起源故事"：从 Brock Pierce 到 Tether

If you look back at where did stablecoins come from — it came from a Jeffrey-Epstein-connected guy called Brock Pierce, who in 2014 infiltrated the Bitcoin Foundation, sent it bankrupt. He was the chair and then handed over power to the Bitcoin Innovation Lab (BIL), which was part of MIT, that later was headed up by Gary Gensler — the head of the SEC, who was under the SEC during Operation Chokepoint 2.0, where the only person he met from our industry was Sam Bankman-Fried.

He created a stablecoin that later was rebranded as Tether in 2014 and sold to a company called Bitfinex. Bitfinex then built out this network of stablecoins. Later Alameda Research and FTX, under Sam Bankman-Fried, connected Silicon Valley Bank, Silvergate Bank and Signature Bank via an alternative payment rail stablecoin.

Peter Thiel：TIC 的关键节点

Who took down Silicon Valley Bank? Peter Thiel. Peter Thiel came up on a Space and said, "I'm withdrawing my $19 billion of deposit, and every startup should do as well."

Peter Thiel was the one that was connected with Jeffrey Epstein — communicating via email on how to destabilize the Bitcoin community, split them up into store of value, medium of exchange, and unit of account: divide and conquer.

Peter Thiel was the one that invested in Blockchain Capital — which was Brock Pierce's VC fund — that later then invested in Coinbase and Blockstream and various other companies. Peter Thiel is the one that took down Silicon Valley Bank. Peter Thiel has now launched his own bank after Silicon Valley Bank that uses blockchain technology.

The stablecoin was always the goal. Len Sassaman was the resistance against that project — that released the Bitcoin white paper and open-sourced Bitcoin in a decentralized network run and enforced by nodes. Because what was DigiCash? Private stablecoins that depended upon banks. Stablecoins depend upon banks that then buy treasuries — and those treasuries are lent back to the government. Much like the petrodollar, it's a mechanism for enforcing the treasury-backed system.

Genius Act 与 Clarity Act：把数字美元焊进银行体系

What did the FIC do? They co-opted the Trump administration — said that America will become Crypto Valley and crypto center of the world — and created the Genius Act.

What did the Genius Act do? It gave any bank that has reserves at the Fed the ability to use those reserves as collateral to issue a stablecoin. The largest reserves are JP Morgan. Then JP Morgan, Bank of America, and Citigroup lobbied — as well as Coinbase being co-opted by Larry Fink. And the company that I created, Bank to the Future, ended up being sold to Coinbase, which then created tokenized securities, which is now lobbying for the Clarity Act.

Clarity Act and Genius Act combined give an advantage to banks to issue yield. So then one of the companies I invested in called Kraken in the early days has now got a banking license, so that they can issue stablecoins — and they're now part of FIC.

That's what those operations were: to centralize and create a digital dollar that is privatized and programmable, integrated into Palantir's artificial intelligence and Elon Musk's social credit score — that the FIC allowed Elon to borrow against his Tesla stock to buy X. Gave everyone freedom of speech but not freedom of reach — so that we could all talk and be radicalized into the version of ourselves that the algorithms control, and set our social credit score.

They were all Peter Thiel. David Sacks became the czar of the Trump administration for pushing forward the AI and crypto policy.

十九、Cambridge Analytica → Libra → FTX → 司法掮客网络

Alex: What about Cambridge Analytica back then? It's kind of like what you describe during the Brexit. Is that in the same ties, or what's the story behind that?

Simon: Cambridge Analytica was an AI tool for manipulating media to control — using the TIC — in order to control political outcomes that was used by Facebook.

And what was Facebook? Facebook coded a project called Libra, which was a stablecoin. That stablecoin was then sold to Silvergate Bank, that created that network that connected Signature Bank, Silicon Valley Bank and Silvergate Bank into FTX and Alameda Research.

The board members of FTX were Sullivan & Cromwell. The bankruptcy Chapter 11 lawyers were Sullivan & Cromwell. Sullivan & Cromwell were the main lawyers throughout history for the CIA, Mossad, and MI6.

FTX was used to launder money via this network to Ukraine for the corrupt part of it.

When Scott Bessent wanted to target the Iranians, he launched a currency war to destroy the value of Iran's currency, and then confiscated the stablecoins — but Iran ended up with the Bitcoin, but not the stablecoins. Starting to make sense.

Stablecoins and CBDCs was always the goal. And Bitcoin was the breakaway open-source movement for those that wanted to resist against the digital control grid that Elon Musk paid $250 million to the Trump administration to create.

JD Vance 与 Palantir / Peter Thiel

Who groomed JD Vance? Palantir and Peter Thiel. JD Vance is not his name. He was magnificently given a VC capital of $50 million by Peter Thiel, and was politically funded $15 million by Peter Thiel. We're being told that he's creating peace in Iran because he's being groomed for the next president.

Because we've moved from MIC to FIC to TIC. All of Trump's policies: - Department of Government Efficiency — give all the data to TIC. - Tariff policy — split the world up into multipolar world and support China. - Epstein files — crashed the dollar in terms of its credibility, and all the mechanisms that propped up the dollar in the deep state. - Closure of Strait of Hormuz and war with Iran — reset the world order into multipolarity.

北京的"卡位"

And what happened right before that? All the executives of TIC and FIC meet in Beijing with Xi Jinping. And Xi Jinping said: - "Yes, Elon, you will be able to get access to your rare earth minerals, provided you play ball." - "Yes, Tim Cook, you will be able to manufacture your iPhones in China, provided you play ball." - "Yes, Visa and Mastercard — as the CBDC network takes away control from the Swift network, we will allow you to charge fees by integrating into the CIPS network." - "Yes, Nvidia, we won't buy your chips — but we will continue to create our own version via Huawei that does it 10 times cheaper at 90% of the efficiency." - "Yes, Trump, we won't sell our treasuries to destroy your bonds and lead you into quantitative easing and having to purchase yields again." - "Yes, Trump, we know that you've got derivative contracts that far supersede the amount of gold — and all the gold is in Shanghai." - "Yes, Trump, we won't engineer or burst the bubble of the AI capex that you're currently overspending into, and release another DeepSeek moment that destroys the valuations — until you've managed to get all the money you need to build out the AI data centers and build the control grid." - "And yes, the second we want to rug-pull that, the Fed and PBOC can do it in a second."

We control the market. PBOC and the Fed and the BIS control the market, and they're splitting up the world — and they can rug-pull it the second that they want. So they're engineering via ETFs capital outflows into emerging markets. And at the moment to reset the world order — close the Strait of Hormuz, which required cooperation from America, Iran, and Lloyds of London in order to close it. Because in order to get the insurance to get a shipment through, you need guarantee by the IRGC they won't target you, and you need guarantee by America that they won't target you on the other side of the blockade. Lloyds of London control the gate.

So you reset the world order into multipolarity and build a global control grid. Every capital flow in the world is telling me that that's what's happening when you follow the money.

二十、收尾

Alex: Well, it was a lot to process, Simon. But I'm glad that you laid this out, and I think it's crystal clear. In the coming conversations that we will have, we'll be able to dig deeper into some of those pockets.

I want to thank you for your time, for your knowledge, for all your explanations, and being able to put it so clearly as you did.

You also have a YouTube channel that I advise people to follow. You're also very active on X / Twitter. Is there anything that you want to share to the audience as a final thought?

Simon: I try to go with real-time analysis on X at @SimonDixonTwitter. I commit to once a week going live and following the monetary flows — and trying to make predictions or analyze what I got wrong every week in terms of this model. Sometimes that takes like two to four hours.

So then on my blog simondixon.com I plug it all into AI and my team creates 5-minute videos, 20-minute videos, and text summaries and documents — under, you know, completely free. All of this is no sponsorship, no business agenda, no upsell, no monetization. I just want more and more people to be sovereign.

So I created that within simondixon.com for those that don't have the time to go through these really long rants. And then I also upload any interviews I do, like this one, on Simon Dixon 21 on YouTube.

Alex: Well, again, thanks a lot, Simon — and I hope to be able to follow up with future interviews with you very soon as well.

Simon: Okay, thanks for having me.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的观察：Bitcoin 的"创世神话"可能是一次**叛逃**，而非**起源**
大多数人把 Satoshi 视为"无中生有的创世神"。但 Simon 在这次访谈中投下的真正炸弹是另一种叙事：**Bitcoin 不是密码朋克运动的"开端"，而是一个内部反叛者的"叛逃产物"**——Len Sassaman（David Chaum 在荷兰的学生）很可能是从 CIA/央行正在孵化的"数字法币 / 稳定币 / CBDC"项目中带着白皮书出走的"内鬼"。
如果这个假设成立，整个加密世界的拓扑就完全反转了： - **稳定币和 CBDC 不是 Bitcoin 的"模仿者"或"竞争者"，它们才是"原版"**； - **Bitcoin 才是那个意外泄露出来的"开源叛变分支"**； - Genius Act、Tether、Libra、Circle 这十几年的"创新"，其实是**原始项目沿着主线缓慢回归既定轨道**； - 这也解释了为什么 Hal Finney、Len Sassaman 几乎在同一窗口期"消失"——叛变者总是被回收的。
这把"Bitcoin vs. 法币"的二元对立改写

item.title: [Business of Sport] What Does Manchester United’s £1BN Debt Really Mean?
item.dc:creator: Business of Sport
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、被忽视的真相：曼联其实"自己买了自己"

大多数球迷把格雷泽家族的杠杆收购理解为"美国人借钱买球队"，但更精确的描述是——曼联用自己的资产作抵押，借钱把自己买下来送给了格雷泽。这是一种近乎"经济学魔术"的所有权转移：俱乐部的现金流、品牌、老特拉福德的砖头，全都被用来偿还把自己"卖掉"的债务。

这里有一个令人意外的观察：在过去20年里，曼联付出的利息总额（保守估计超过12亿英镑），几乎等同于他们如今要建造新球场所需的全部预算。换句话说，格雷泽时代的利息支出，刚好"吃掉"了一座未来的世界级球场。这不是经营失败，而是一种结构性的价值蒸发——球迷以为自己在为冠军付费，实际上他们在为一笔20年前的并购贷款付费。

二、跨领域连接：赛马、对冲基金与"小字条款"

视频中最被低估的细节，是弗格森与马格尼尔之间那场关于赛马 Rock of Gibraltar 的官司。表面上这是体育八卦，但它实际上是曼联命运的真正分水叉——如果当年弗格森没有和爱尔兰亿万富翁们因为"种马繁殖权"翻脸，那么接管曼联的会是赛马大亨，而不是来自坦帕湾的房地产商。

这里藏着一个深刻的系统性规律：重大资本事件的走向，常常不是由资本逻辑决定的，而是由"无关人物的私人关系"决定的。就像 1494 年托德西利亚斯条约用一条经线决定了南美洲的语言版图，曼联的所有权也被一匹马的"小字条款"重新定义。

更耐人寻味的跨界连接：Citadel（肯·格里芬的对冲基金）既给了格雷泽当年14.25%的 PIK 贷款，如今又在为切尔西的波切蒂诺合同提供融资。同一只"影子资本之手"，二十年间反复出现在英超的关键交易里——足球的"主权"，其实早已被几家华尔街机构悄悄分发着。

三、隐藏的系统性诊断：曼联得的不是债务病，是"时间错配病"

阿森纳和热刺的债务高峰，是为了换来未来三十年的现金流；曼联的债务高峰，是为了支付过去二十年的所有权交易。这是同一种财务工具，但服务于完全相反的时间方向——一个是"向未来借力"，一个是"向过去还债"。

这里我想给出一个反直觉的结构性诊断：曼联真正的危机不是"债太多"，而是球场翻新和债务到期的"时间窗口"完美错开了。当其他豪门用低利率红利期（2015–2021）锁定了基建融资时，曼联恰好在用高息再融资偿还旧债。这是一种"代际套利失败"——你借钱的时刻决定了你未来三十年的命运，而曼联连续两次踩在了周期的反面。

这让我想到一个不常被提及的类比：曼联的财务结构很像 1990 年代的日本企业集团（zaibatsu）——表面庞大、品牌强势、现金流稳定，但内部交叉持股和债务循环让它失去了"重新选择未来"的能力。当新球场需要 20 亿英镑时，曼联要建的不只是一个体育场，而是一艘"逃离自身资本史"的方舟。而拉特克利夫所谓的"环绕隔离"（ring-fencing），本质上是承认：这家俱乐部已经无法再以一个整体的方式去拥抱未来，必须把"未来"切割出来独立融资。

这才是 10 亿英镑债务背后真正令人不安的事——不是数字，而是它所暴露的"自我吞噬式资本结构"。

he can take it off the stock exchange. Yeah.

Yeah.

So this really is what's known as a leveraged buyout. And what that means is you use the assets of the company that you're buying to secure the loans that you take out to actually buy the club. So as Man United fans have had an issue with for many many years, you basically — Manchester United bought itself — and uh they then went from a debt-free organization to a significantly laden-with-debt organization.

You have different types of loans and that was used here, and we're going to get back into our old friend the PIKs. So there was about 260 million that was what we would call a traditional bank loan. Yeah.

六、PIK 贷款是什么？

And just be clear on PIK notes — we mean that we have covered it on the show before.

So the PIK note is — it's basically a recapitalization of interest onto total debt. So let's say I have — let's say I have a 200 million debt and it's a 10% interest rate.

That will then become 220 million.

But that's a 220 million total debt. I'm not paying that extra 20 million next year on to 220. So then the next year after that 242, and so on and so on.

So it's a — you know it can it can be good in lots of ways if you have short — uh if you have poor cash flow in the early stages. However, the total cost of the debt is hugely significant compared to traditional forms — or should we say more basic traditional forms — of debt financing.

So, they were 14.25% when they got these these PIKs. Um, and they were pretty punitive because they increased to 16.25 if it wasn't paid off in 2010.

It's also worth saying the Glazers put in 270 million of their own money that was raised through sales of various assets. So you had 260-odd million of bank loans, 270-odd million of PIKs, 270-odd million of Glazer's own cash, and then the PIKs were paid off in 2010 at the cost of about 250 million. However, the means with which they were paid off — no one really knows what was actually what lever was pulled to enable that payment to be made.

Yeah, right. And that's despite the fact that they are the third highest revenue club in the world, or the fourth highest revenue club in the world, whatever whatever it might be.

So in other words, the interest payments that are coming out of the club to pay the interest on the debt — let alone forget repaying all this debt — has to mean that this club, effectively the club has been refinancing these loans. With the loans being refinanced through the club.

but then there's a cast as because ref — I think —

well so so they have a — they have a form of revolving credit facility right now, right? On top of all of this.
