关键成绩： - Scored 51 points on the Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index — the highest score of any open-weight model ever - Stacks up nicely next to the frontier models - Beat GPT 5.5 on the frontier SWE coding benchmark - Trails Claude Opus 4.8 by less than 1 percentage point - API usage cost much cheaper — 85% cheaper in fact than GPT 5.5 for comparable performance

Z.ai founder told Elon Musk open-weight Fable capabilities will be here sooner than Q1 2027.

Gavin, in other words, all this hand-wringing, all of these legal restrictions, self-imposed restrictions are now completely or close to completely moot if they're going to have a model in Q1. Gavin：蒸馏（distillation）已经发生，未来属于组合模型

I do think how good GLM 5.2 is has challenged some of my beliefs. And there was a great post from a TPU engineer that for sure distillation has happened. There's been an immense amount of distillation. No question.

Please explain to the audience what that means.

Distillation is when you have — you know, we all have seen videos of these Chinese iPhone farms. Just picture a farm like that. Tens of thousands of phones, iPads, and computers that are asking the cloud API through masked accounts very specific questions, and then these what's called reasoning traces are being harvested because if you're on the API, you get to see every token.

And those reasoning traces are then fed back into the model during the reinforcement learning process and probably during the pre-training process. And that is a way that you can get really, really close to the frontier at a fraction of the cost. And this is for sure going on and has gone on for a long time.

It's a cheat sheet. It's a cheat sheet for other models to catch up.

It would be like asking Google every question you could ever imagine, every search imaginable, getting all the results and then putting your own search engine together to make it very simple.

Exactly.

But now that this model is so good, it is good enough to do its own RL, and the cat may be out of the bag. Now I don't think we really know how good Mythos is. We don't really know how good the next OpenAI model is. We haven't seen the next SpaceX model. So maybe that gap opens up again.

But either way, I profoundly believe the future is composable models and every enterprise — you're going to have a what Andrej Karpathy called "a council of LLMs." You're going to have Grok, you're going to have Anthropic, you're going to have OpenAI, Google. You're going to have at least two of those. I would argue Grok should always be one of the two because of its dedication to the truth and it will tell you as a business owner a politically inconvenient truth that you need to know for your data.

But you're also going to have your own open-weights model that you trained on your data. And you're going to put those two together — the frontier models and your own model — and you are going to get real Pareto-dominant outcomes. And half the queries are going to go to the open source model — maybe 85% — and only the hardest ones are then maybe they all go to open source first and only the hardest ones are then checked by the frontier models.

So I think this is the future. It's coming. And a misconception that a lot of people have is that open-source models are somehow bad for AI. They're awesome for the AI infrastructure providers. They just shift economic value from the margins of the frontier labs to the infrastructure. And that's not bad for AI.

That's great for them.

It's great for them. But I do think there's still a role for these frontier models, and it may be true to date frontier tokens are capturing 90% of the economic value and open-source tokens are probably 80%+ of tokens processed — and those ratios may be here to stay — but I just think composable models are the future.

But that is the situation we're in right now is that Fable has been rolled back and GPT 5.6 is trying to navigate these new approval hoops. So we now have a Chinese open-weight model that is as good as the currently available models from OpenAI and Anthropic.

And look, this is a point I've been making really since I joined the administration — that we are in a very competitive situation with China. I've been saying this from the beginning. Our whole AI strategy from the get-go was about winning this AI race, defining it as a race, as being globally competitive. And we cannot afford to do things unnecessarily that slow our companies down.

I hope that — David, can I ask — do you think Dario got exactly what he wanted? It seems to me there's some chance this has been a very calculated strategy to provoke the US government into doing what they just did, and this is what he wants. He has a regulatory moat now. He can keep his future models behind this, give it out to Glass Wing, use it to distill it for himself. Do you think this is what they wanted?

I think that on a certain level it is what they wanted because they've been advocating to have a federal regulator — basically a new agency. In fact, Dario posted a blog just a few weeks ago saying he wants an FAA for AI. They wanted government approval — a government approval process for AI models. And so, in a sense, they've gotten exactly what they wanted.

Now, that being said, I don't think they're happy about the fact that Fable has been rolled back. So, in a sense, you could say that Dario got hoisted on his own petard here, or it could be an FAFO situation.

But look, my view on it is we should not reward Dario by giving him exactly what he's always craved, which is some sort of labyrinthine government approval process that does reward regulatory capture. So I hope that very soon now, I do think that as long as Anthropic has resolved the jailbreak issue, then I do think they should be allowed to come back to market. And similarly for OpenAI — I don't think we should be delaying them unnecessarily. We do not have months to give away in this race.

And let me just say one other thing which again is something I've been saying for months — which is with respect to risks like cyber, it is undoubtedly a risk. But what is the response to that? The only thing you can do is go out and find all the vulnerabilities first yourself. The white hats find all the vulnerabilities and do a big upgrade cycle. Roll out the patches before they can be exploited.

The reality is that if you just clamp down in a way that doesn't even allow these models to be used, the Chinese are going to have these capabilities imminently anyway. They're already at Opus 4.8 level. And the founder of Z.ai, he said that before Q1, they'll have Fable level capability.

I believe them because the Chinese have been, I'd say, 9 months behind our models, plus or minus 3 months depending on capability. But when they know there's been a breakthrough around something like cyber, they can deploy more resources against that particular problem and catch up faster.

So, like I said, we're on a shot clock here. I've been saying for months that we're on a shot clock. We have to do smart things. We can't just slow everything down because that will not slow down the Chinese. They're not under our jurisdiction. We have to basically get these tools in the hands of our cyber security industry. They're the force multiplier. They're the enabler. We have to basically go out and do this big upgrade cycle quickly because we only have a few months left.

They're six months behind on the model and they're 24 months behind on the silicon yet they're only a few months behind in total. So what game are we playing? This is insane. We are going to lose if we keep doing this stuff to ourselves.

Sacks：中国押注华为芯片、AI 即包装出口

So let me make a point about that. So on the silicon, there's been a huge push in China by the government to push their AI labs to develop and train on Huawei chips. And look, you can take these claims with a grain of salt — maybe they're not true — but it was claimed that DeepSeek V4 was trained on Huawei chips. And now Z.ai — they are saying that the GLM 5 family was trained entirely on clusters of Huawei Ascend 910B chips.

So now look, maybe they're lying, maybe they smuggled in some Nvidia chips, but the claim is that this was all done on indigenous chips. And what I believe is that China is engaged in a strong indigenization push right now. They want to prop up Huawei as the national champion. They want all their companies using Huawei chips.

They still need to scale some of the manufacturing, but they're going to do that pretty quickly. And then what they're going to do is they're going to take these Huawei chips — Huawei-optimized models — remember that GLM 5.2 inference is optimized for the Huawei chips. Okay, we know that. And they are basically going to package these things up. They call it "AI in a box." They're going to sell it at a fraction of the cost globally.

Which is what they do with every technology, right? Better, cheaper, faster.

Or almost as good. And that's another thing — as I've been saying since the beginning of this administration — we have to be pro-export because China is going to be there within one or two years. As I said, we're going to be kicking ourselves because we could have had the whole global market to ourselves. We invented reasons not to sell abroad to our friends and partners. And now China is going to be there imminently.

Yeah. And with a lower price segment.

When you play with this new GLM, you get some really interesting responses. I asked it about the country of Taiwan, was not pleased and didn't give me an answer. I asked it about Tiananmen Square — no answer as well. I'm using the hosted version at Z.ai. But when I asked it places to visit in Paris, it did an exceptional job. Except when I said "make these ideas into an infographic and make me a 3-day agenda," it was like, "Hey, we don't have enough time to do that." And it said use the other model because this model's too busy. But you can go play with it at z.ai.

One quick point about the censorship. There's no question that these Chinese models have, you could say, censorship — and there's political bias in there out of the box. But American companies have taken Chinese models and then essentially worked around and basically fixed the censorship inside their own forked version. So I think Jal, you're absolutely right about the censorship, but it's not a fatal problem. It's something that American companies can fix when they take an open-source model and fork it and customize it.

Yeah, in the hosted version, you're not going to get a great answer of what agenda you should use for tourism in the great country of Taiwan or your visit to Tiananmen Square.

话题三：Micron 财报炸裂与 HBM 内存的全球瓶颈

All right, let's keep moving here on the dock. Micron smashed their earnings. If you don't know Micron, they are one of only three companies on Earth that make high-bandwidth memory. These are specialized chips. They sit on top of the Nvidia GPU and their entire 2026 supply is sold out and has been for some time. SK Hynix and Samsung also make HBM.

Micron earnings： - Revenue up 4x year-over-year — $9 billion to $42 billion - Beat expectations by 16% - Big jump in guidance for Q4 — $50 billion versus $43 billion - Their stock is up 10x

Shout out to Gavin — in our 2025 prediction show, he gave a call on HBM makers like Micron as the best performing asset since that time, up 14x.

I'm not crying in my soup.

You're not crying in your soup.

I got a ton of information here. I think I'll just end on the Apple price increases. Everybody knows Apple has been a beneficiary of the run-local-models movement that I'm part of, and people are buying 128 gig, 256 gig MacBook Pros, Mac Studios. But the gig is up apparently because now Apple, which had not passed on those costs to customers, is having to pass those increases on. So everything from the new MacBook Neo, which is their $699 laptop kind of competing with Chromebooks, is now $799, up 14%. Mac Studio up 25%. The costs are going to be very significant. Inflation has come to the desktop.

Your thoughts, Gavin, on Micron and the impact on the industry — is this a temporary bottleneck or does this mean everybody has to get into this business quickly?

Gavin：DRAM 是 AI 革命的"代谢限速酶"

No. Well, DRAM is the most important bottleneck. There's a whole segment of people on X who are very focused on bottlenecks. I call them the "bottleneck bros." They'll do some work with Claude, find some esoteric Japanese company. The bottleneck that matters is DRAM.

DRAM and HBM DRAM — this is the most important bottleneck simply because memory capacity and bandwidth are foundational to the performance of every AI model. So, this is the most important bottleneck. Elon is focusing the TerraFab on memory because he sees it as the most important bottleneck. Not lasers, not capacitors, not power supply semiconductors, not NAND flash, not HDDs — DRAM.

And I think this bottleneck is going to be with us for a while and it is kind of astonishing.

A few thoughts — what was important about the quarter: they announced that they have these SDAs, these supply chain agreements, that have a floor and a ceiling for prices with an increasingly large group of large customers. And this covers essentially 50% of their revenue, I think, with just four customers. And the floor pricing in these new contracts is ahead of prior cycle peaks from a gross margin perspective. And so this is really, I think, maybe end up being very transformational for the industry.

Most other parts of the semiconductor supply chain have rerated. Lam Research — the wafer fab equipment suppliers — they all trade at huge premiums to DRAM relative to prior cycles and their business models have improved. But so has the industry structure and business models of DRAM because HBM DRAM is increasingly a customized chip.

But as far as other people being able to do this — look, CXMT is going public in China. They are going to — they may be the cure for Apple's ills. They will flood the market with to some degree cheap consumer-grade DRAM. But for the DRAM you need in these AI servers, there are three companies that can make it. It's really hard to do. This is as close to magic as science can get.

And TerraFab is going to be an important part of this solution. But these stocks still trade are cross-sectionally cheap relative to the rest of AI.

Something I've been thinking about — memory and DRAM is probably going to be 30 to 40% of all hyperscaler CapEx next year. The hundreds of billions of dollars that are spent going straight to DRAM. It's wild.

But this may actually be very valuable for society because it is probably going to inflate the costs of building a gigawatt data center to the point where even for the hyperscalers, economics matter. We're caught in this prisoner's dilemma. And this may give us as a society time to adapt — to adapt what our friend Brad Gerstner calls the social contract.

So the high iPhone prices — one, CXMT is coming for consumer-grade DRAM. But two, this may be good for AI. It may be good for us as a society.

And making it is just really pure silicon, right? Like making memory is just incredibly refined silicon.

And that might be the pre-bottleneck. Making the HBM DRAM, making what Nvidia calls SOCAMM, making LPDDR — these are the types of DRAM that are really hard to make, not consumer-grade DRAM, and they are increasingly what you need in these AI data centers.

Yeah. Semiconductor grade. Yeah.

Sacks：HBM 堆叠工艺与全球电子产品涨价

So my understanding of HBM stands for high-bandwidth memory. Again, this is part of the GPUs that go in the data center to run AI. Is that you take the DRAM wafer or die and you actually stack them. And so I think HBM 3 is like 8 — it's stacked 8 dies high — but now they're increasing to 12 and even 16. And to basically stack them and then package them all together, that's an advanced technology in and of itself.

So you're seeing now, like Gavin's saying, there's only three companies that can do it. But also, this is creating significant price pressure for all the consumer electronics businesses. Apple had huge news today where they announced massive price increases. And again, it's because DRAM now is less available because it's just being hoovered up by all the data centers.

And if you're a data center and you need to buy GPUs, those chips are using immense amounts of DRAM because one HBM chip is using multiple stacks of DRAM. So it's just getting slurped up and then it takes a couple years to ramp up new capacity. So these companies are going to do that, but that could take a while.

We saw that in New York — remember that was that Micron plant that was had just broken ground and then got shut down the same day because of some crazy environmental issue. So it's not easy to ramp this stuff in the US. Although Micron is the one provider that's in the US. SK Hynix is in South Korea. Samsung's in South Korea too.

But anyway, we're going to see more of this — AI tariff they're calling it — it is in this narrow area of consumer electronics where there's competition for DRAM, it is leading to price inflation.

Now, Microsoft raised the price of the Xbox. You know, it's coming for the Switch. It's coming for the PlayStation. There's demand destruction because of prices in consumer whereas AI demand is relatively price insensitive.

David, I would modify one statement. It's hard to build a Micron. It's hard to build a new fab in a deep blue state. You can build fast.

Why were they trying in New York? It's kind of crazy.

New York gave them all these incentives, but none of those incentives matter. It's a little bit like solar power. You can be as pro-environmental as you want. But if you can't build and install solar because of regulations, it doesn't matter. So maybe that Micron plant ends up getting built in my home state of Texas.

The incentive game has kind of flip-flopped. It used to be the states were courting the factories and the fabs. Now it's the fabs are like, "Which state can actually build this? We'll pay you whatever you want. Just tell us where to send the envelope. We'll drop a couple of envelopes off. It's not a problem."

Gavin, can you say how long it's going to take to stand up the fab at TerraFab?

Well, I mean, if it was a normal fab, it would be a 2, 3, 3-and-a-half year process. But we've seen what Elon has done to other construction processes, and he's starting with some advantages with the Intel partnership. So I don't think anyone knows, but based on past history, he's probably going to stand up TerraFab faster than other fabs have been stood up. But this is really hard. It's the intersection of magic and science. You can't believe how complicated this is. So it's going to be hard. But he has a track record of doing what Jensen called impossible — superhuman. And so we'll see how long it takes.

数据中心成本：从地面到轨道

You know, one other point here that I guess is — it might be relevant to SpaceX AI, although it's don't have to limit it to this — there's an assumption that over time it would get cheaper and easier to stand up new data centers. But what you're saying is actually it might be getting harder. It might be getting more expensive. Because there's competition for these components. The memory is getting more expensive. I'm not sure that the GPUs are getting any cheaper.

I guess some of these transformers, the switch gear —

The energy might be getting cheaper, and then the entitlements are getting harder and the political situation is getting harder. There's very few places you can even stand up new data centers. So, is it the case that actually it's going to get more and more expensive?

100%. So to stand up a 1 gigawatt data center, it's $35 billion in semiconductors — Nvidia semiconductors — and it's $25 billion of power and cooling equipment. And that is clearly inflationary because a lot of that $25 billion is the human labor required to install it.

So the calculation that needs to be done for orbital compute is — it's $35 billion of silicon in each space, in literally outer space and orbit and on ground. But if you can get the cost of launch significantly below that $25 billion, then the math starts to really mass.

And when Starship is reusable, it's going to cost $5 billion to put a gigawatt of compute into space. And something that drives me crazy is people picture these Pentagon-ish data centers. No, it's racks in space linked with lasers. It's kind of a virtual data center in space.

Wait, $5 billion. Is that $5 billion of launch cost or what?

$5 billion of launch cost. Now you're at $40 billion to put the gig into space. You're at $60 billion terrestrially. And the $25 billion that is power and compute is clearly inflationary. So it may be that in three or four years it's $70 billion versus $40 billion. And that $5 billion as Starship becomes rapidly reusable is likely deflationary. So this is the economics that underpin orbital compute from first principles.

And then on an ongoing basis, you are paying maybe a billion dollars a year for the power to run those chips and cool them.

Chamath：地面算力将被争夺，SpaceX 获得结构性优势

If I had to make a guess, I think what's going to happen is that since 2021 about 40% of all data centers get contested. I think that number is going to go up. So Sax, I suspect that whatever forecasted energy consumption that we are looking at in AI is grossly imbalanced. There is very, very meager supply and there's effectively infinite demand.

So that probably pulls forward the economic equation to want to go to space. But then again, that's going to prefer SpaceX and their compute stack and their compute decisions over the hyperscalers and over anybody else. And so you're going to have a cost of an output token, I think, terrestrially, particularly from the hyperscalers, be a little economically lopsided versus SpaceX. Once they get it to scale — now that's the key statement.

Whatever is left on the ground, though, will be incredibly, incredibly valuable. It'll be a diamond. These are diamonds. And the thing is, you have to find reasonable size, right? You can't have a 10-kilowatt diamond. That's like a little pebble — nobody cares. But if you're in the reasonable hundreds of megawatts to gigawatts, man, those are like Hope Diamonds.

Those are just — lock them down. Which I own.

"Modular data center hardware for artificial intelligence computing comprised of network of computer servers, computer hardware for artificial intelligence processing, computer network hardware, electric power distribution units and cooling systems sold as a unit. Self-contained modular computing hardware systems for artificial intelligent workloads..."

Essentially —

Just to explain what this is.

By the way, that description — I don't know what it I still don't know what it is.

Essentially, what people are saying is this is going to be a giant battery pack with GPUs at the Supercharger stations which have already been approved. That's the back channel. Chamath, go ahead.

Chamath：模块化数据中心的产业逻辑

You have a couple of issues right now to turn on compute terrestrially. So, assume you have land — that's relatively straightforward. Assuming you can get it zoned, less straightforward. Assuming you can get power, very difficult. Then you have a very critical design decision.

So all of these folks publish these things called the basis of design, and your BOD essentially tells you here's the Anthropic spec, here's the OpenAI spec, here's the CoreWeave spec, here's the AWS spec, here's GCP. And you get these 50- and upwards of 500-page documents of all these technical details.

The issue that we have is — I don't know if you guys have used OpenAI or Anthropic recently where you get the whole thing of "come back later." That "come back later" is completely unacceptable. It just means that they have no compute.

So in trying to find — surge pricing is coming.

So in trying to find this solution, what is happening is these basis of design — the restrictions, the specificity is being relaxed. And one of the key constraints is we've moved to an architectural model from Google and Nvidia that has said look, we have to liquid-cool these racks. These are very complicated big-girth supercomputer racks essentially. And now we're also going back and saying, "You know what, maybe some of these older stuff that's a little bit less proficient and a little bit less useful, but we can air-cool them — is useful." And everybody's like, "Yeah, you know what? We should try to use everything that's available in that second class."

What some very smart people are doing is like, "Wow, well, here's a shipping container that you can just drop on a concrete pad somewhere, plug in the power, and let it rip." And so, Jason, what you're seeing is that level of investment that's happening.

So there are companies like Dell makes these racks, companies like Vertiv makes these modules, and I think if Tesla can make them available, folks like us for my data center project, we would be enormous buyers of these things because we would just literally prefab them in a warehouse — get the chips, prefab them, truck them to the place, crane them in, turn it on, off to the races you go. You have like a 90-day build cycle, which is unheard of.

So that's where these Megapods are coming from. I hope it's not entirely consumed by Tesla and SpaceX internally.

Well, that's the rumor — that he's going to put them at the Supercharging network where he has a lot of land and he's got power there already and those are lightly used in some cases.

There are other requirements. The problem with some of these things is that you need to have certain levels of access. There are certain InfoSec requirements. There are certain liquid cooling requirements that make many of the workload applications unfeasible at a place like a Supercharger center where random bumblers are like traipsing around — security guards putting it off to us.

So guys, I've got 500 properties with lots of energy, lots of mechanical cooling systems and lots of LNG access — or sorry, not LNG, natural gas — sorry, already piped in. No, no, hold on, hold on, hold on. It's not that easy. Okay. If you guys want to build a cloud and contribute it to a pool, you're going to have to sign up for liquidated damages. And you're not going to sign up to —

SLA. I'm not a hyperscaler. I'm just using it for myself.

Yeah. Rack and stack.

Oh, okay. Sure. You can use it for yourself. I'm saying the more interesting opportunity is when —

Whatever Travis says. Let's talk about the interesting stuff.

No, no. I'm just saying like — what I thought you were going with this, Travis, is like you could build a synthetic pool and contribute it to a neocloud. And all I'm saying is to do that is a leap of things that you'd have to do that you probably don't want to do because it veers you away from what you're doing.

I think the security thing is the main issue around non-data-center type real estate, to be honest. It's the big one. Where you go into a room, the door closes behind you that you got into with your fingerprint. Then you go into the next part of it again with your fingerprint only when that door closes.

Just out of genuine curiosity, how much does one of those man traps cost? Like, why can't you put some of those in your —

Yeah, maybe you could.

Are you interested in putting in a man trap?

I'm just trying to help trap some men.

I'll try to interpret where Chamath's going a little bit — is like a Megapod that's sitting at a public charging situation. Because of the physical access of it, there's probably some stuff you've got to do if you're going to resell it. But if you're going to use it for yourself, you can use it for yourself.

The real value I think in the Travis example, which I find super exciting, is if you can contribute it to a distributed training pool or a distributed inference pool that has a lower SLA, which is more of how this community-based oriented work. Jason, I think you've talked about Bittensor. I think Venice is a project. I think Plurales is another project. Travis, that's where I think it's super exciting where —

Travis：分布式训练 vs. 分布式推理

But the one thing I'll just throw out there, guys — the distributed training stuff. If these things are far away from each other, meaning not right next to each other, they're so less efficient. The efficiency drops dramatically. You want these things to be right next to each other physically. You get multiple orders of magnitude — at least an order of magnitude — type efficiency, maybe plus plus. Like it's a big deal.

So like you could have two facilities — passing the jobs from one GPU to the other is super important and if it's on a peer-to-peer network it's going to have lag.

Honestly even if it's on your own fiber connected like 2 kilometers away — you're screwed. It's not a thing.

This is 100% true. But everything that cuts against training works for inference.

With respect — well, latency is super important and I do think distributed inference — distributed inference clouds are coming.

Yes, I get that.

Gavin：推理的分解（prefill / decode）与硬件复用

And to riff on what Chamath said in all of this — there is actually a startup that is trying to put four GPU units with kind of a battery on people's houses and give them a discount on their power and then you can do inference for that neighborhood from those four GPUs. And it's like lock-sealed so nobody can get in.

But there's another dynamic that I think we should talk about with all of this — and can play into the Megapods and other people are kind of working on data centers. You know Crusoe is working on modularly assembling data centers — kind of like a data center and think of it as like an 18-wheeler shipping container.

But that is the disaggregation of inference into prefill and decode. When a model is answering your question, it's doing two things. The prefill part is understanding the question and its answer thus far. And think of that as: the more you can remember, the bigger your memory capacity — literally, the more words you can remember the better.

Decode is the process of generating the next token and that is a memory bandwidth-bound problem. And think of it as: the faster you can speak, the better.

And these two types of inference are increasingly being disaggregated. And Chamath was an investor in Groq, which Nvidia bought. And they're going to use this — Cerebras is the other solution today that is available. And you can put Groq or Cerebras decode-optimized chips — they both say you can do more than decode on them, and that's true — in front of old Nvidia GPUs like H100s. So you can lift H100s, A100s out of some old data center, put them in one of these Megapods, you know, a rack in a shipping container, put a Groq or Cerebras in front of it, and you can get a very competitive solution.

And so I do think the disaggregation of inference, we're going to be using GPUs for 7 years, 10 years, 12 years, and that's great because it lowers the cost to finance them, which makes this AI revolution more financeable.

Chamath, you want to riff on Groq? Should we —

I really agree with everything. You're incredibly well-steeped in the space. It's so exciting. I don't have any investments or anything in the distributed compute space, but at the intersection of competing against China, having a vibrant American open source community, having a bunch of distributed models for purposes of free speech and otherwise that Travis mentioned earlier, I do think this idea of distributed inference has a real place in the American ecosystem.

I don't exactly know where and how and how homeowners would get paid, but whoever figures that out as a pan-American idea is really onto something.

And yes, Travis — obviously slower, but people are contributing compute to this that's like kind of surplus compute that's available to —

Unused computing — unused compute.

And then there's Targon, which is just straight up people are putting and you can rent H200s by the hour for three bucks, four bucks, and it's permissionless. Anybody can contribute provided — and this is kind of the magic of Bittensor — there are validators that make sure you're putting in what you're say you're putting into the network.

And so if somebody just had the hardware and Tesla said from now on every Powerwall — and I don't put it past him to do this — you buy a Powerwall, we give you a discount on it. Every Powerwall has our GPUs in it. Part of the offering is you can't buy a Powerwall without putting GPUs in it. And when you're not using it, we will pay you for your battery. And you put a Starlink on your roof. And now you've got a distributed system where you get a couple of these Powerwalls with their own silicon in it and a Starlink. And now Elon's created an infinite number of home battery backup systems. People get their battery system for free.

He gets the exclusive for 20 years.

You guys see that there's a rumor that Elon's going to buy T-Mobile?

话题五：IPO 市场、Cerebras 跌破发行价与 Anthropic 3 万亿估值

By the way, we should — because Cerebras has blown up and gone through deal price — we should talk about Cerebras a little.

So, Cerebras had an incredible IPO. Give us an update on where these IPOs are happening. Obviously, SpaceX had an incredible IPO, but has retreated from this otherworldly $200 share price. So to the extent you can talk about these two as well as the two IPOs to come — $4 trillion in backlog.

We've obviously got OpenAI and Claude — I'm sorry, Anthropic, which makes Claude. Both of those will be worth a billy plus. You put all this together — $4 trillion of new offerings plus Cerebras in there in the mix. How does the market manage this much new inventory being put on the market? Obviously the float to SpaceX is notably small, but over time people like yourselves and other insiders, founders fund, etc. will be unlocked and have the ability to distribute to their LPs.

So this is going to be a moving target I think in terms of share price and can the market absorb this? Where does the money come from? Retail? Or does it come from people selling their Bitcoin and moving it over to something more exciting? What's the dynamic here in the market? I know we're in uncharted territory, Gavin.

Gavin：Anthropic 值 3 万亿美元

Well, for sure there's no precedent for any of this, but a few things I would say. In no particular order — I think Anthropic is worth $3 trillion today and it's very important.

I'm sorry, did you say Anthropic is worth $3 trillion?

Yeah, I think that is roughly where it would probably trade as a public company.

Wow.

Yeah. Look, they're going to end this year well over $100 billion.

Holy [ __ ]. What's the '28 number? What's the '28 number? Is it 200? Is it 300 billion?

It's probably not going to trade at 10 times that number and it will be very profitable at that scale because it'll be inference dominated and people reporting they have 85% gross margins on inference.

But in terms of the market absorbing this — like the market's already absorbed it. It's just shifting from private to public. And so in the scale of global capital markets, these seem like really big numbers. You're just moving from the private markets to the public markets which are even bigger.

SpaceX IPO 与员工解锁结构

As far as SpaceX specifically — I think one of the more important things is everybody who's a SpaceX investor or employee has had a chance to sell every 6 months for the last 10 years. So they may not be the wall of liquidity that some people are thinking about.

I read this New York hedge fund short report that you could just short SpaceX on the lockup because so many people are going to sell. Really? Well, everybody who's on the cap table — they had an opportunity to sell. And almost half the employees at SpaceX bought on the IPO.

But Gavin, one thing though, I got to say — so I've had SpaceX since 2018. What? But their little liquidity thing every year — like last year it was like 350, 350 billion last year. So you got an 8x in one year. You could have a lot of people selling, right? They were doing little 20% up, 30% up clips for many years. And then an 8xer could create that liquidity.

Maybe, but we'll see. That's possible. But the employees are buying at the new price, and they're probably at some level one of the biggest pools of ownership that's going to unlock. And then a lot of people probably own SpaceX through SPVs, and those probably don't unlock or get distributed anytime soon. So I just — I would be careful with assuming. I don't think Elon's a seller of his.

No, he's definitely not, obviously. Yeah. Yeah.

And a lot of us aren't sellers. And that's great. Yeah.

Cerebras 跌破发行价的剧本

Yeah. I mean a lot of people who had large SpaceX positions were large buyers on the IPO on Cerebras. So Cerebras has had a tough two days since they reported their first quarter as a public company.

And I think there are two things that are worth discussing here. One is there is a whole generation of portfolio managers — there's a lot of people who are advocating for kind of squeezing the blood out of the stone on IPO prices. And the flip side of that is that there are a lot of portfolio managers who if a stock breaks deal price, they sell it no matter what. They consider it a promise that was broken.

And so this is what has happened with Cerebras to some degree over the last two days. And this may seem irrational but there are people who run giant funds who I know personally where if a stock breaks deal price, they sell no matter what. And so if a stock breaks deal price, it can sometimes go to places you wouldn't think it would go.

And this means that shorts — if a stock gets close to deal price, they short it because they want to break deal price and then they make a quick 10 or 20%. So it becomes a pile-on because you have this price-insensitive selling that can be triggered. And this is what has happened to Cerebras.

People talk about hate sale — hate selling — but they broke deal price. And so if you're going public and you're listening to this — tell your bankers price this in such a way that we're not going to break deal price in our first nine months as a public company. And that's what I always advise everyone to do and I think it's important. But I also think it takes companies a while to learn how to tell their story and communicate to public markets. It's a very different audience than VCs.

The way I would have respectfully told the Cerebras story — because what happened to Cerebras is they reported a quarter and they're growing fast but relative to the rest of AI they're not growing that fast in the March quarter.

So what I would have said is: we signed this transformational $202-$25 billion (I don't know the exact number) contract with OpenAI in December of 2025. We immediately ordered more wafers from Taiwan Semi — takes 4 months from when we make that order. Taiwan Semi says yes, they start producing — takes 4 months to make the chip. Then it takes us 2 months plus or minus to turn that chip into a server. And then if we're lucky and we can find the power, it takes us a month to energize that chip and start making tokens with it.

So the first time you're going to see the impact of this OpenAI deal at the earliest is probably around Labor Day. So you'll see a little bit of it in the third quarter, but then it really starts to build.

And just really simple math — let's just use some rough numbers. Let's take some Nvidia numbers. It takes them $35 billion to bring on a gigawatt and 15 billion that you can generate — $15 billion in token revenue, in cloud revenue out of that gigawatt. And somebody on the call talked about adding 50 megawatts a month. If they could add 50 megawatts a month in 2027 — forget '26 — that means they exit the year at roughly a $9 billion cloud computing run rate. And we're at less than 40 billion of market cap.

Now, that is going to be really hard to do and they've never done anything like that before. But I would focus, like as an investor, what matters here is not where they sit competitively, not what new demand they can bring on, but just how quickly can they bring on power.

And listen, outside of the hyperscalers, the only companies that have ever brought on more than a gigawatt I think are CoreWeave, Crusoe, and SpaceX AI bringing on 600 megawatts. It's really hard. And that is what I'm focused on as an investor — how many megawatts can they bring on? Because we know what they're going to monetize at. And that is the question. And we'll see.

Yeah. And I think that's well said. Yeah. The other companies that have had this happen of late — I guess Rivian, and famously Chamath — Facebook traded below IPO price in the first year, I think.

And so this isn't necessarily mean a bad company. It just means a lot of hype or maybe going for it in the IPO and pricing it to perfection. And it can go one of two ways. You can either grossly underprice, grossly overprice. And it's really hard to get right. And this is why doing auctions —

Or, you know — yeah, auctions are the way. Auctions are the way. And so it's not hard to price if you just do it the way it's supposed to be done. It's hard to price when you want it to be a certain number.

Yeah. And you have many mouths to feed and that influences the price.

I don't think that happened here. I think there was a good-faith effort to price this thoughtfully, but there was — it was such an in-demand IPO. I think it was a hard IPO to price.

You just auction it, Gavin. You just auction it.

I think that's different than underwriting it. You're underwriting —

But the bankers — like I think they just need to get more in the mode of just doing the auction, you know.

Yeah.

Final reflections：估值规模与 Uber 的"贵宾价"回忆

Final topic was this $3 trillion or actually now it's your $3 trillion call on Anthropic would make it like 6 trillion in offerings. What do we think broadly?

In offerings — because it's they're going to offer a small slice of that.

Sure.

And that small slice is very easy to absorb in the context. I believe we have no precedent. We'll see. I might be wrong, but it's not hard for global capital markets to absorb $50 billion of an offering. It's not like somebody has to come up — it's a 5% float is $150 billion. There's enough —

Guys. Remember when 15 billion used to be a whole lot of money?

Yeah. How weird is what's going on? How weird?

By the way, crazy. Where is all this money coming from?

Oh yeah. Where was it all this time?

Can we just reminisce, man?

We got — my — when I was at Fidelity, my colleagues and I, we got pilloried. I think we priced Uber at $14 billion. And we got pilloried in the press for not knowing what we were doing. And it was seen as — then of course like six months later, I think you guys did a round at, what, like $42 billion or something. But it's like, yeah, $14 billion. That was groundbreaking for a private company back then.

Yeah, for sure. It was 17, but that, you know, no 17. No, you're right. I wanted 14.

And we had like a big negotiation —

And we ran — we just ran an auction.

No, we just ran an auction.

Yeah, you wanted 20 and maybe we landed at 17. What we did was every person who wanted to be involved had to fill out a sheet of how much money they put it at 10, 11, 12, 13, 14 — all the way up to 20. And then we just did the Dutch auction. We said, "We want to clear $1.5 billion." We just did the auction and cleared it. Went back to people and said, "Hey, you're not going to get it. You have another shot." They update their Excel sheet and it just moves the number up a little bit and then you close it down. But yeah, it started — that round started at I think it was 9 or 10 or something like that. Ended up at 17.

Thank you for your service.

Yes, it used to be that was a lot of money and that was unheard of at the time.

And that was — well look, it was 10 years ago when we used to walk to school uphill both ways.

Yes. We used to eat potatoes with no butter, just hot potatoes. Sometimes we were lucky that they were cooked. Sometimes they were raw. No, my dad would tell me that story. It's a famous John the Beard story. He'd say his mom would put four potatoes in the oven. They'd wrap them in tin foil, one in each pocket because they couldn't afford gloves. So, you put your hands in your pocket with the hot potatoes. You get to school, you eat one for breakfast, one for lunch. Him and his sister Johanna — God rest, my aunt who died too young — and they would just go eat these potatoes. That was their life. Walking to school.

Yeah. And our kids are trying both ways.

Yeah. They're trying to get the new iPhone 16 or 17. I don't know.

Remember when it used to be hard to raise $5 billion?

Oh my lord. This is crazy.

Remember when it was hard to raise $1.5 million?

Dude, I remember. Yeah. I remember when it was hard to raise. Anyways, we don't have to do this. It's okay. We already sound like grandpas. It's fine.

We do sound like — it was so hard to raise that first 1.5.

Dude, it's just — we don't need to go there. It's so funny though.

What's with this art piece, Gavin, behind you? Is that like an art piece or you made it?

Yeah.

No, this is —

I'm staying at a rented house in Atherton.

Oh, okay. There you go. It's just Arabian art.

结束语

All right, everybody. For the dictator Jamal Pitia and for David Sacks TKGB. We'll see you next time on the All-In podcast. Bye-bye. Great job everybody.

We'll let your winners ride. Rainman David. We open sourced it to the fans and they've just gone crazy with it. Love you. I want your winners. Besties are gone. That is my dog taking your driveways. Oh man, my appetiter will meet up. We should all just get a room and just have one big huge orgy cuz they're all just useless. It's like this like sexual tension that we just need to release somehow. Your feet. We need to get merch. Going all in. I'm going all in.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反常识的观察：DSA 不是"穷人的革命"，而是"过剩文凭阶层"的政治起义
把 DSA 简单理解为"穷人造反"是被表象骗了。播客里有一个被几位嘉宾从不同角度反复触碰、却没被明说出来的真相——**DSA 的选民基本盘是"向下流动的高学历白人"**，而不是工人阶级、黑人或拉美裔（后者实际上正在流失给共和党）。
这其实是一个被低估的"**学历通胀**"后果：当大学不断扩招、博士产能远超学术岗位时，社会就批量生产出一批"消费习惯像中产、收入和资产像穷人"的过剩文凭人口。他们既无法回到工人阶级身份认同，又被资本市场拒之门外，于是必然在意识形态上寻找出路——而马克思主义恰好给了他们一个"我是先锋队，不是失败者"的叙事容器。
跨领域的连接：这跟历史上所有大规模革命运动的"知识分子—边缘人结盟"结构惊人一致——从1789年的法国律师与巴黎暴民、到1917年的俄国知识分子与士兵水兵、到1960年代美国校园新左派与黑豹党。**革命的火种从来不是最穷的人点燃的，而是"教育水平和经济地位严重错配"的中间层点燃的**。Mamdani 现象只是这条历史规律在 TikTok 时代的最新一次复现。

item.title: [a16z] This is New Media
item.dc:creator: a16z
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的观察：「合法性焦虑」其实是旧媒体最后的护城河

视频里那句话特别值得停下来："legacy media somehow is like where the respectability is." 表面看，这是关于新旧媒体的代际更替；但更深一层，旧媒体真正卖的从来不是内容，而是「被允许存在的许可证」。

当 a16z 说"不再相信合法性来自传统媒体"，他们其实是在做一件比"创办播客"激进得多的事——拆除创作者大脑里那个看不见的审稿编辑。这个内化的编辑，比任何外部审查都更高效地压制表达。所以新媒体革命的真正战场不在 YouTube 或 TikTok，而在每个创作者起床后第一念头里：「这个能发吗？体面吗？」一旦这个念头消失，旧媒体就死了——不是因为没人看，而是因为没人再需要它来盖章。

二、跨领域连接：虚拟制作 × 加密货币 × 米其林餐厅，竟然是同一件事

视频提到"4 周内做虚拟制作，没人做过，但我们喜欢疯狂的想法"——这听起来是制作花絮，其实暴露了一个跨行业的统一模式：

旧范式 新范式 共同结构
好莱坞剧组 6 个月预制 虚拟制作 4 周上线 时间压缩 → 风险前移到决策瞬间
银行 IPO 18 个月路演 加密项目 token 发行 48 小时 同上
米其林 3 年备战评审 Pop-up restaurant 一晚试菜 同上

真正的"新媒体"不是介质的更新，而是「决策—执行—反馈」三段循环被压缩到几乎重合的状态。a16z 不是在做内容公司，他们在做一种金融逻辑的延伸：每一次内容发布等同于一次小型 VC 押注——快速下注、公开验证、立刻迭代。所以他们做媒体的方式，本质和他们做风投是同构的。这才是为什么他们能做、传统媒体不能做。

三、隐藏的系统性真相：「品牌」和「公司」正在反向重合

视频里有一句被很多人会略过的话："the brands were the companies." 在旧媒体里，CNN 这个品牌等于 CNN 这家公司。但在新媒体里，发生了非常诡异的反转——

  • 个人创作者：品牌（人）> 公司（壳）。MrBeast 这个人比 Beast Industries 重要 100 倍。
  • 机构创作者（a16z 是先驱）：公司试图把自己重新人格化，让"a16z"成为一个有声音、有观点、会冒险的"人"。

这意味着 21 世纪后期的组织演化方向是「公司人格化 vs 个人公司化」的双向收敛。中间地带——也就是过去 100 年我们称之为「企业传播」「公关」「企业品牌」的那一整套——正在被掏空。

最讽刺的洞察：当一家 VC 比大多数媒体公司更像媒体公司时，"投资"和"叙事"已经合并成同一个动作。 钱不再追逐故事，钱本身就是故事的一种语法。这是新媒体真正的暗面——不是民主化，而是叙事金融化。

📝 转录文本

旧媒体的局限与遗留焦虑

Old media, you had very restricted channels, very restricted formats, and the brands were the companies.

But there's still this anxiety that people have, which is: legacy media somehow is like where the respectability is, the prestige is.

I I don't believe that anymore, and I think it's very important for people to kind of get that out of their system. The world has changed. It can't be half and half.

全身投入：拥抱疯狂的想法

The whole motion — they pitched to us this idea of working with a virtual production.

And we look at the calendar. This is what — 4 weeks away. No one has ever done this before. It's such a crazy idea.

But we like crazy ideas. So we're like, let's do it.

观看原视频 →

当 a16z 说"不再相信合法性来自传统媒体"，他们其实是在做一件比"创办播客"激进得多的事——**拆除创作者大脑里那个看不见的审稿编辑**。这个内化的编辑，比任何外部审查都更高效地压制表达。所以新媒体革命的真正战场不在 YouTube 或 TikTok，而在每个创作者起床后第一念头里：「这个能发吗？体面吗？」一旦这个念头消失，旧媒体就死了——不是因为没人看，而是因为没人再需要它来盖章。

item.title: [Dwarkesh Clips] Way Catholics Waged War Against the Pope – Ada Palmer
item.dc:creator: Dwarkesh Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、"距离决定神圣性"——抽象与具体的反比定律

这段话揭示了一个反直觉但极具系统性的真相：权威的神圣度，与你和它的物理距离成正比。冰岛人眼中的教皇是金光闪闪的抽象符号，罗马人眼中的教皇是"那个上大学时揍过我弟弟的混蛋"。

这并非历史孤例，而是一条普遍法则。它解释了为什么： - 硅谷工程师最不崇拜乔布斯（共事过），印度的程序员却把他奉为神明； - 梵蒂冈的枢机主教对教皇的"神性"最不敏感，肯尼亚的天主教徒却最虔诚； - 白宫记者最难写出对总统的颂歌，而地方报纸总是溢美之词。

意外的观察：任何制度想要维持神圣性，必须制造距离。一旦你和教皇一起喝过酒，他就再也不是"教皇"了，只是 Giuliano。所以——官僚制不是腐败的产物，而是神圣性的脚手架。中世纪教廷发明的繁文缛节、礼仪、宫廷距离，本质上是一种"抽象化技术"，让近的人也看不见近处的真相。

二、跨领域连接：派系政治与"僵尸意识形态"的物理学

教皇党 vs. 皇帝党最初是关于"谁是罗马合法继承人"的形而上争论。三百年后，意识形态早已掏空，只剩下"那帮混蛋杀了我叔叔 Tibolt"——但派系标签依然驱动着所有人。

这是一个跨领域的深层模式，我称之为 "意识形态的化石化"：

领域 原始内核 化石化后剩下的
中世纪意大利 神权 vs. 皇权 家族仇恨
美国两党 联邦党 vs. 民主共和 文化部落标签
学术派系 方法论之争 引用敌对/师承站队
开源社区 Emacs vs. Vim 身份认同表演
球迷文化 地域忠诚 与城市无关的资本流动

啊哈时刻：当意识形态完全空心化后，它反而最稳定。因为没有内容的标签不会被现实证伪。教皇党之所以能在"教皇本人是反教皇派"的荒谬情境下继续存在，正是因为它早已不关于教皇——这就是为什么佛罗伦萨可以"为捍卫教皇权威而对教皇开战"。

三、隐藏的系统真相：忠诚的对象永远不是它声称的对象

最深的洞察藏在最后一句话里：佛罗伦萨人对"抽象的教皇职位"忠诚，所以可以杀死"具体的那个教皇"。

这揭示了一个所有大型组织都在玩的双层游戏： - 声明层：我们效忠于"国家"/"公司"/"教会"/"科学"； - 运行层：我们效忠于这个声明所象征的、永远不会被任何具体实例所污染的纯粹理念。

所以——最忠诚的下属，往往是最危险的叛逆者。他们捍卫的是"理想中的老板"，而真实的老板永远配不上那个抽象位。这解释了： - 为什么宗教改革来自虔诚者而非异教徒； - 为什么斯大林被最狂热的布尔什维克挑战； - 为什么硅谷"叛徒八人组"恰恰是肖克利最虔诚的门徒； - 为什么家族企业里，最爱父亲的儿子最先反叛父亲。

最反常识的推论：如果你是一个领导者，要警惕的不是反对你的人——而是狂热崇拜你所代表的那个抽象位的人。因为有朝一日，他们会发现你这个具体的人配不上那个神圣的位，然后他们会以"捍卫那个位"为名摧毁你。

教皇国的真正悲剧不在于敌人的攻击，而在于：它最忠诚的盟友，会以"忠于教皇"的名义讨伐教皇。这是所有制度化神圣性的终极悖论。

📝 转录文本

教皇：精神领袖还是世俗权力？

At this time, the Pope is not just a spiritual leader but a temporal power. So he's like — him and his son are literally fighting wars against other Catholics, but the other Catholics are fighting them back.

Q: Like what is it? What does it mean to be a Catholic who is fighting a war against a Pope?

距离决定一切：远方视角下的教皇

So here is where geographic proximity is everything. Because from the perspective of — if you're far from Rome, right, when you're Denmark or Iceland and the Pope is all the way over there — the way you interact with him is that occasionally an incredibly impressive papal legate will visit, and there will be vast pomp and circumstance, and the city will rename a street in honor of the fact that somebody sent by the Pope has visited.

And he has this great power to say yes or no to petitions, and you know different countries have been trying to petition for specific things for ages. And the Pope's legate is here to, you know, interview the emperor, to judge whether the queen can be queen or not. And it feels like a big deal, and the Pope is very abstract.

It's easy to have a lot of respect for that Pope, because what do you see that Pope do? You see that Pope in pomp and circumstance. You see that Pope make judgments about fates of popes and kings. You see that Pope put out papal bulls and edicts that give theological answers to questions. You see that Pope exercise judgment of life or death over people at a distance.

He's very abstract. And the difference between one Pope and the next Pope is kind of small from your perspective. You don't see their policy differences.

近距离视角：教皇只是"那个家伙"

If you're in Italy, the Pope is that guy who went to college with your brother — and beat him up when they were at college, and then, you know, was drunken and irresponsible in middle age, and you've been negotiating with him in these other jobs, and you know this jerk. You know his family. You know the other jerks who are also competitors for this. You're allied with him. You're not allied with him. His ancestors are allies of yours or not allies of yours.

He's a specific dude. And you're much more likely to judge a Pope based on: he's that guy, right? This is not Pope Julius II. This is Giuliano della Rovere. And I judge him based on his uncle who put him in power, and the actions of his friends, and the actions of the city he's from. And you know all of his dirty laundry.

And you are subject to the fact that when he moves into power, everyone who's related to him is going to get promoted within Italy; everybody who's not is going to get removed from Italy.

So it's much easier for an Italian to see this Pope. And it's actually quite hard to see the papacy. And that's how you have these fascinating wars where even the cities that are hereditarily incredibly loyal to the papacy will sometimes be fighting a war against the papacy.

教皇党与皇帝党：派系的起源

So all Italy is divided into these two factions: the Guelphs and Ghibellines.

Theoretically, what these two factions mean is:

  • Guelph powers, Guelph families, Guelph cities — they believe the correct successor to the Roman emperors is the Pope. The Pope is the emperor. He has the right to be the ruler of Italy, and indeed of everything that was once Rome's. He is the ultimate political, the ultimate military power, and he is the rightful and only rightful overlord of Italy.

  • The Ghibellines believe that in 800 AD, when Charlemagne conquered a bunch of stuff and made the empire that we now refer to as the Holy Roman Empire — when the Pope crowned Charlemagne, he delegated the political and military side of his authority to that emperor, and made himself be the spiritual authority but the emperor be the political and temporal authority. Right? And therefore the rightful ruler of Italy is the emperor, the successors of Charlemagne.

These are the two factions for which these parties fought originally 300 years ago.

派系的现实：从理念到家族仇恨

These days, what these factions actually mean is: those jerks murdered Uncle Tibolt and we will never forgive them. So they are the team that is our enemy, and we are this team and they are that team, and we hate them and we want to crush them because they want to crush us.

Which means that sometimes a Pope will be elected who's from a hereditarily Ghibelline family, and the Pope will start promoting people from the anti-papal faction — and the pro-papal faction will unite against the Pope. Which makes no rational sense, until we remember that they are serving the Pope abstractly.

So you get multiple situations where there's a war between Rome and Florence over the fact that Florence wants to defend papal authority in papal land against the Pope itself, because that individual Pope is from the anti-papal faction.

If you enjoyed this clip, you can watch the full episode here and subscribe for more clips. Thanks.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、"距离决定神圣性"——抽象与具体的反比定律
这段话揭示了一个反直觉但极具系统性的真相：**权威的神圣度，与你和它的物理距离成正比**。冰岛人眼中的教皇是金光闪闪的抽象符号，罗马人眼中的教皇是"那个上大学时揍过我弟弟的混蛋"。
这并非历史孤例，而是一条普遍法则。它解释了为什么： - **硅谷工程师**最不崇拜乔布斯（共事过），印度的程序员却把他奉为神明； - **梵蒂冈的枢机主教**对教皇的"神性"最不敏感，肯尼亚的天主教徒却最虔诚； - **白宫记者**最难写出对总统的颂歌，而地方报纸总是溢美之词。
意外的观察：**任何制度想要维持神圣性，必须制造距离**。一旦你和教皇一起喝过酒，他就再也不是"教皇"了，只是 Giuliano。所以——**官僚制不是腐败的产物，而是神圣性的脚手架**。中世纪教廷发明的繁文缛节、礼仪、宫廷距离，本质上是一种"抽象化技术"，让近的人也看不见近处的真相。
## 二、跨领域连接：派系政治与"僵尸意识形态"的物理学
教皇党 vs. 皇帝党最初是关于"谁是罗马合法继承人"的形而上争论。三百年后，意识形态早已掏空，只剩下"那帮混蛋杀了我叔叔 Ti

item.title: [YC Root Access] How Warp Went From YC to a $60M Series B
item.dc:creator: YC Root Access
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的观察：Warp 的"反 SaaS"结构，本质上是一家伪装成软件公司的"税务事务所杀手"

大多数人听到 Warp 的故事，会聚焦在"AI 原生 HR"这个标签上。但真正惊人的数字藏在一句话里——1000+ 客户、覆盖全美 1000+ 税务辖区，却只用 1.5 个税务专员（现在才扩到 2 个）。

这意味着什么？传统 HR 巨头（Workday、ADP、Gusto）30–40% 的人力消耗在合规、税务、客服这些"后台沼泽"里。Warp 不是在做"更好的 HR 软件"——它在做一件更激进的事：用 LLM 把整个行业的"人力中后台"从损益表上抹掉。

换句话说，Warp 真正的护城河不是产品功能，而是人均处理税务辖区的杠杆比（按 1000 jurisdictions / 1.5 人 算，约 666:1，而传统玩家通常是 5:1 到 20:1）。这是一个隐藏的、可被精确测量的"AI 原生度"指标——比任何"我们用了 AI"的宣传词都更硬核。

二、跨领域连接：物理学家的"对称性破缺"思维，正在改写 SaaS 的护城河理论

注意 Aush 的背景——他是 MIT 的物理 + ML 双料，原本想做理论物理学家。他选择创业方向时用了一个非常"物理学家"的判断标准：寻找"对称性破缺"的临界点。

他注意到一个绝大多数 SaaS 投资人都忽略的不对称： - 销售税有"门槛保护"（要达到一定营收/客户量才触发合规） - 薪资税没有任何门槛——雇一个人，整个辖区的合规义务瞬间全开

这是一个组合爆炸的"奇点"：员工数 × 辖区数 × 合规规则 = 指数级复杂度。传统软件在这个奇点前束手无策，所以选择"卖人力（咨询/支持团队）"；LLM 恰好可以处理这种高维、结构化但混乱的语义空间——这是 transformer 架构的甜区。

跨领域类比：这和上世纪 70 年代凝聚态物理学家进入华尔街做 quant 是同一种思维迁移。他们不是在"用物理学搞金融"，而是用物理学家的眼睛识别哪些问题在结构上突然变得可解了。Aush 在 HR 领域做的是同一件事——他识别出 LLM 让薪资合规从"NP-hard 的人力密集型问题"塌缩成了"可被 agent 编排的问题"。

三、最容易被忽略的预言：Workday 暴跌 60–70% 不是"AI 焦虑"，而是"数据库化"的市场定价

Aush 说了一句很值得反复咀嚼的话——Workday 的真正风险不是被 AI 抢生意，而是"沦为一个 dumb data store"。

这是一个非常精妙的二阶洞察。在 agent 时代： - 第一阶恐惧（市场已 priced in 一部分）：AI 会替代 SaaS UI - 第二阶恐惧（市场正在 price in）：当 agent 直接读写底层数据库，所有价值会向"编排层"迁移，原本的系统记录会被降级为"被消费的数据源"

这与 Cloudflare 报告的"agent 流量首次超过人类流量"遥相呼应——互联网的消费者正在从"人"变成"代理"，那么所有为"人"优化的 UI、营销、生态都会贬值，而为"代理"优化的 API/语义层会升值。

更进一步的预言（Aush 没明说但暗含）：未来 SaaS 公司的真正估值锚点，将是"你的 API 文档被多少 agent 阅读"——而不是 DAU/MAU。Mintlify 已经在收集这个数据。这意味着一个新的"SEO"正在诞生，不妨叫它 AEO（Agent Engine Optimization）——而 Warp 这种"从 day one 就为 agent 设计 schema"的公司，可能正在不知不觉中囤积一种全新的、还没被定价的资产。

彩蛋观察：YC 面试的"地狱 10 分钟"暗含一个反向信号

Aush 描述 Gary Tan 第一批面试时"被碾压式拷问"——但他通过了。把这放回上下文：当 YC 在一个看起来不性感的方向上用力拷问你，说明他们已经在反复验证 Schlep Blindness 假说——他们怕的不是你想错了，而是怕你想得不够深。被拷问得越凶的方向，可能反而是 YC 内部正在重点下注的方向。这是创业者读懂 YC 信号的元技巧。

📝 转录文本

开场介绍

I'm excited to be joined here today by Aush, founder and CEO of Warp. Warp is an AI native employee management platform built for high-growth companies. They work with over 1,000 customers and have processed over $600 million in payroll in the past year and are on track to pass $2 billion in the next 12 months.

Warp did YC in winter 2023 and they just announced their $60 million series B led by Battery Ventures with participation from Sapphire, Peak XV, and Shopify CEO Toby Lütke and former Stripe COO Claire Hughes Johnson.

Today we're going to talk about their origin story and what it actually means to be an AI native product and company. Aish, thanks so much for being here.

Pleasure to be here.

一、从印度小镇到 MIT：物理学梦想与现实的拉扯

So are you — I actually want to start by hearing a little bit about your background, how you grew up, because I think you have a very unusual entry into technology and stars, and actually really inspiring story.

Yeah, I grew up in a really small town in India and in a place where I didn't know a single person who had gone to the US. So there's a fun sort of journey and story there, which the short version basically is that growing up I was really into physics. So as early as I can remember, I was trying to borrow textbooks from upper classmen and I remember finding Richard Feynman's lectures on physics. The classic three-volume — the full actual lectures on physics.

Actual lectures — the one he gave on Berkeley which was turned into textbooks later on.

And as I was growing up, I really thought I would go on to become a theoretical physicist and a professor one day. And then sort of life comes at you — you know, the Indian middle-class family expectations — and I was told that the only way to escape the middle-class sort of fate in life in India where I was growing up was you have to become an engineer and go to an IIT, which are these top schools in India.

And I was doing really well and I was on a straight track to go to one of the top three schools in India. And halfway through that prep I just completely pivoted and changed my mind, sort of behind the backs of my parents and family. There's a reason for that I'll get to in a second, but I ended up applying to MIT, which is where I knew Richard Feynman had went. So that's the connection.

And fortunately, the way it worked out, I got into MIT, I got into Columbia — to study computer science, math, physics. So I spent about five years at MIT specializing in machine learning. But overall, I think the fun kind of statistic that I can share is I think I'm the first person out of a state in India that has about 250 million people to get into MIT for undergrad. So it was quite fun. That was about 10 years ago, and now we're here.

二、从消费社交到 B2B 薪资：寻找问题的"低压力练习"

I remember before Warp you were trying out different startup ideas, and maybe — I think I remember some of them in the consumer space. So tell us a little bit about that, like how did you go about trying to come up with the initial startup ideas, and then yeah, why the pivot from consumer to payroll and B2B?

Yeah. So at first, I must have been 24, I think, when I was really looking into what to do first. And like most 24-year-olds do, you try to start a social app.

Yeah, especially a couple years ago. This pre-AI, right?

Yeah. There's like a roommate finder, there's bill splitting, like events —

All the carpet ideas that my kids made five videos, right? Yeah.

But I think I just wanted to build a thing that I found interesting or like I would have used. And so I got started in building this app. It was really fun for a couple months and we had an interesting community and people joining it. It allowed me to get started and just make the leap of like, we're just going to build something and see what happens.

But at the same time, it became very quickly clear that it wouldn't become a really big company. And that's when we sort of had this fork. It was like, okay, me and then the early team members that we had at the time — Adam, who's our CTO — we just sat down and we were like, okay, what are all the problems that we have faced in the past and in doing this that we could come up with?

So kind of really went down and did this very low-pressure exercise of: what are all the problems we've actually run into that we think would want to solve for ourselves? And one of the things that we kept coming back to was: in starting the previous thing, I'd actually had to set up like a company, a C-corp, and try to pay some people.

And I just remember one of the days that I spent — the guy had all these fires going on in the company — and then on top of that, I had to figure out how to make like a New York withholding in Department of Labor's account. And it was one of the most frustrating things I ever spent my time on. And I was like, there's no way nobody has tried to completely automate this.

And I think the early background I had coming from machine learning — and this is right like 2022–23 when LLMs and agents were just about to take off — I think it was sort of interesting to consider: what if we apply AI and LLMs towards this and could we automate this in a way that nobody has before? And that's the start of Warp.

三、为什么选"不性感"的问题：Schlep Blindness 与 Stripe 的启示

How did you think about that as a problem? Like how excited were you to work on that problem? I guess like — I think a big part of the reason why young technical founders start on consumer ideas is because they're things they want to use and they're excited and attention-grabbing. How did you — what motivated you to want to solve this particular problem when on the surface it might not be as obviously interesting?

I think in some ways I was motivated to start it because it is unsexy. And Paul Graham of course has this really foundational essay on schlep blindness. And I remember thinking about that — that so many of these problems hide in plain sight and nobody really tries to attack them because they seem really messy, complex, and unsexy at the start.

And the classic example being the Collisons with Stripe. Nobody was really sort of finding it sexy to figure out all the edge cases with millions of banking APIs that were very janky at the time. But it needed to be solved.

So one motivation was simply that this seems like the kind of problem that is very messy, hairy, and not sexy on the surface. But counterpart to that — the more we learned about it, the more we went a little bit looking into it, we found other people who had run into the same problem. So it's like, okay, we're not the only person that is thinking that this is really bad, the current state of the world here. And two, we saw a path to using that towards building a really big platform for all things employee management. And that was sort of the controversial idea at the time: that we could actually use this multi-state complexity, taxes, deep compliance workflows towards building the entire platform.

四、YC 面试的"地狱十分钟"：薪资税与销售税的隐藏不对称

You're entering a pretty competitive space — like lots of legacy payrolls, there's recent-ish startups that have done well. Making it easy to run payroll in multiple states was the initial wedge. How did you know that that was going to be a sufficiently deep wedge?

So I have a funny story here. When we were thinking of applying to YC, I think I DM'd you actually and you encouraged us to apply.

I didn't remember that at all. Yeah, it's been a while, right?

And you said, "Yeah, definitely apply." So we wrote an application, then we were invited for an interview, and it was the first batch that Gary Tan had come into. And so I hop on this interview, and there's five people on that Zoom call on the other side, including you, Gary, and a couple of other partners.

And I remember the next 10, 12 minutes were just like brutal. Really like — I was — I felt like I was being grilled on the spot. And that's maybe the typical experience, but I really felt that. And one of the big questions exactly was this: like how big can you really use this wedge to grow from here, and is it really a true sort of problem and wedge that you can find enough of a foothold on?

And at the time I think it was a bit of a hypothesis on our part that this is a reasonable wedge into this broader segment. And looking back, I think in some ways we got a little bit lucky. But the calculated part of that hypothesis was:

(1) All the companies were multi-state very early on, as opposed to just a few years ago before COVID, for example, where that was much rarer. So the trend line was just going up — the complexity was exploding.

(2) We realized something kind of a non-obvious thing. YC has funded some sales tax startups, and I think there's a parallel version of this problem in sales tax, but it's actually much worse when you look at payroll taxes. Because unlike sales tax, where there's usually these generous thresholds for when you are beholden to those requirements — like you have to reach a certain amount of sales that's quite high, or you have to like enough sales or enough customers in a jurisdiction — but in payroll you just need one person. You just need to hire one person in one jurisdiction and you have to comply with the entire tax jurisdiction apparatus.

So that kind of thing — it just means like the moment you're scaling a company, you just have one person, and combined with thousands of tax jurisdictions, that complexity really explodes very quickly. So those two things, I think, ended up working in our favor — that it became enough of a wedge at the right time to use that and also apply LLMs and agents to something that traditional software couldn't touch.

五、什么叫"AI 原生"的产品与公司

So as the company's evolved and divisions evolved — you describe yourself now as AI native employee management because you do more than just payroll. But what does that mean exactly? And let's talk about that for just the product — like what does it actually mean to be an AI native product?

I think one big realization we've had is that we have to build the whole platform. And it became very clear to us that our customers — in many cases, early on we had signed up a lot of startups because they tend to be early adopters — they were going through the exact compliance multi-state problems, and they didn't have full HR or legal or accounting teams internally that would try and maybe DIY that stuff.

So those early customers — as they were growing themselves, they just gave us the direction of: okay, as we're going from like 5 to 10 to 50 to 100 to 1,000, they kind of gave us the roadmap of what exactly they would need at those different stages of those companies, and they wanted Warp to do all of it.

So it became clear to us that we needed to do more than just initial payroll, especially if we wanted to serve these scaling high-growth companies throughout their entire life cycle.

And two, I think the bigger realization was: we have to build the product and the platform and the architecture in a very AI-native, agent-native way, such that all the work here that we're doing — starting with this very deep tax compliance workflows — can all be performed by an agent. And that's the kind of efficiency gain that we're really focused on.

If you're a high-growth company — and today we serve tons of really fast-growing AI native startups and customers like Cervil, Bland AI, Grapile, Reductive, and many of which are really cool YC companies of course, but also traditional high-growth companies, mid-market, and now some enterprise customers as well — the pattern that we continue to see is: these customers, they are not interested in linearly scaling their people ops, HR ops, finance teams as their company's scaling. And that's where I think we want to be the entire platform for these customers that allows them to do that.

六、传统 HR 公司 vs Warp：30–40% 人力 vs 1.5 个税务专员

So as you're very involved in product and as you're building the product — just again help people understand: when you're thinking about building these new sort of units or product units, what does it mean to do that in an AI native way? Maybe give us — what's the AI native way to approach building a tax compliance, and here's the regular way to do it, and help us understand the diff.

Great question. So a traditional way of building an HR tech company that has compliance requirements baked in — that you have to solve for all your customers — was purely by headcount.

And so one of the things you notice is, if you look at our legacy HR incumbents — if you look at their headcount and where their headcount is — often, it's very surprising that like 30 to 40% of their headcount goes in functions like support, tax compliance operations, accounting, legal. And this is relatively similar between all the last-generation HR tech companies.

In contrast, Warp — we serve now over a thousand customers, we process over $600 million in payroll already, growing quickly. We touch every single tax jurisdiction that exists in America, and there's over a thousand — without going into the super-locality municipalities — all 50 states. And up until recently we had only one and a half tax person, one full-time, one part-time. And now we've hired the second full-time tax person more because we're anticipating the future growth.

But I think that sort of tells you and points the picture of how I think we're able to build a different kind of company, not just a different product. Because I think it's a very different structure of the company itself if you can use AI to automate these previously really messy things that software couldn't touch. And that's why we get really excited about it.

七、新进入者 vs 巨头：AI 时代为何偏爱技术型创始人

For someone starting an AI native company, the advantage you have against incumbents is it's sort of like green field — you can sort of build a new set of primitives and however you want for the world that we live in today. On the other hand, incumbents in any space — they build up institutional knowledge, they've been working with customers for a long time, a bunch of those people there do have sort of experience and expertise. How do you think about your advantages and disadvantages maybe as a new entrant in a space using AI native as the angle?

I think that net-net, I believe that with AI it's in favor of technical founders. And what I mean by that is, if you looked at the last era — like pre-AI, sort of the latest stage SaaS where it felt like the terminal state of software was this SaaS and mega-SaaS software — it had gotten quite boring in some ways. I don't think I would have started a company in that era necessarily in this direction.

And in that time, I think the advantages were really towards mega-distribution, sales-oriented founders. And you kind of see that in the backgrounds of those companies that got started.

And I think today, really with AI, it really shifted the power of the balance, where younger, more technical founders with deep technical knowledge of the frontier — they can sort of see where this is headed, and they don't have these ideas of how things are supposed to be. They can shed those older norms and build something net new. And I do think that is net-net where this is headed right now.

But at the same time, to your point, the incumbents with distribution — and look, lots of these channels and partners that exist — I think it is one of those situations where it's a classic: will the incumbent adopt this new technology in this platform shift before the new entrants figure out how to become the next-generation entrenched and big software companies.

And I think that as a startup founder, my belief in this space is that it is very hard to retrofit AI on top and just layer these sort of thin chatbots on top of existing architecture and existing customer bases and install bases. And in some ways, I think the market's recognizing that. If you look at something like Workday, which is the last-generation mega-enterprise company in our space — it's down 60–70% from their recent highs. And I think that to me is underappreciated right now.

八、系统记录（System of Record）：AI 时代的真正护城河？

Something else that in general talking about AI and its impact on startups that gets mentioned a lot is this concept of systems of record. So this idea that — you mentioned sort of Workday as an example late-stage SaaS company — it feels like the belief right now is that at least consensus conventional wisdom is a lot of SaaS is not defensible against AI disruption, that there just won't be any need for it. That systems of record will be defensible, that those have real moats against agents doing all the work in this AI future. I think Warp would be classified as a system of record. Maybe just explain to us, like, what does that actually mean when people talk about a system of record? What is that, and why is that sort of theoretically defensible against a future where AI agents are removing the need for software?

So a system of record is basically a database where there's some amount of semantic mapping towards a process that the business uses. And so in very simple terms, storing values, knowing for sure that the values are true, being able to see those histories and logs — all these things that everybody in a company can agree on. That's a system of record.

Shared truth.

Shared truth. That's a great way to put it. And the classic example, of course, is Salesforce — where it is the shared truth for a business for all things that touch sales processes.

So your CRM is in many ways — quick anecdote — we actually started and tried out some AI native platforms for our CRM in the early days. But as soon as we started growing the sales team and went aggressive on that after Series A, the head of sales was like, "We got to get on Salesforce classic."

Because we are missing out all these 20 integrations to all these tools, right? Very classic. I think many companies have gone through that.

I do think that different system of records have different degrees of how quickly, how defensible they are. And so far I found that actually Salesforce CRM is actually quite defensible. But in general, what's interesting is there's a bit of a race where the AI native upstarts — can they become the next-generation system of records faster than the current incumbent system of records can layer on AI on top of their current systems?

九、从"系统记录"到"系统智能"：Agent 时代的下一层

That makes sense. And then — do you have any sense of what's a next-generation system of record? Like why is that going to be different? Why won't it be sort of, you know, essentially just a database with some semantic mapping on top of it?

The reason I think a company like Workday is really down and suffering is because the worry is that they are at risk of becoming a database-only company. And not in a good sense — where like, yes, ClickHouse, for example, is a lovely database company growing really quickly and so on — but more so that it's a dumb data store that agents from external parties just access to do the work. And then if that's the case, all the value moves to the layer that's orchestrating the work with agents.

So I think the next layer that we are excited about building is effectively what is — systems of intelligence, not just systems of record — where agents can natively act on the underlying database with that shared truth in mind, with proper guardrails, workflows, permissioning, controls. But it all has to sort of map out in a way that I think the underlying system of record combined with the agents is where I think the interesting stuff is going to happen and what will be defensible.

Because there's an interesting chart I think Mintlify, for example, was talking about — this cool company — that most of the API docs are now being read by agents. And I fully believe that. I think most of the internal systems and system records will be swarmed by these agents.

Cloudflare — I think it was a couple of weeks ago — announced that total agent traffic to total web traffic from agents surpassed human web traffic for the first time. And I think we've yet to see true enterprises and true enterprise software become like that — that flipping is yet to happen, I think. But it will happen.

And I think what Warp wants to do is build that version — that super powerful, capable system of record platform that is built day by day, from day one, with intelligence in mind.

Make something agents want. It's basically the mantra around here.

十、6000 万美元 B 轮：为何 Battery 如此快速下注

Okay, so let's talk a little bit about the Series B. You just raised a big round, $60 million. It happened really quickly actually, right? So tell us, what did Battery see that made the move with such high conviction and make such a big bet on you?

Well, ultimately you'd have to ask Battery, but from where I see it, we ended up getting pre-empted. This was just less than 10 months after Series A.

I think an interesting shift happened in the last couple of months, where we went from not thinking about what is the AI native future here look like to sort of appreciating this: that if you look at the true enterprise software categories — like for example CRM, there's a number of AI native challengers, some are growing really quickly. If you look at something like ITSM — Servo, who's now a customer — is doing really well and growing quickly. And a couple of other companies. Then ERP, there's related Campfire, which is a YC company as well.

But I think ultimately one of the last categories remaining there where there isn't a clear sense of what comes after — or like a true AI native challenger — is AI native HCM. And I think that's where Warp comes in.

And I do think that the reason the round happened really quickly — yes, we've been shipping a lot, we've built out a really cool platform, and our customer growth has been amazing. We've signed in some awesome customers. But I think when it comes to investors, I do think there's a little bit of that narrative flip that happens in certain times where it goes from very far-fetched to "it's like you can see that maybe this is the future." And I think that people start feeling that a little bit more. That's my guess of why it happened very quickly.

十一、产品路线图：全平台 + Agent 双轮驱动
