You've got a bunch of money now. So what do you plan to do with it? What can you do now that you couldn't do before, and then what are some of the things you're personally most excited about on the product roadmap for Warp?

So now that we have $60 million — and more actually from most of the funds we had from our Series A as well — we've decided to raise a little bit of a bigger round, because I think this next phase of where we're about to head, there's two really big and important things we have to accomplish.

Number one is we have to build an incredibly engineered, really powerful, and still very usable, delightful platform for all things employee management. Which means there's multiple product lines — there's IT, there's benefits brokerage (we became fully licensed countrywide to broker healthcare, medical, dental, vision, 401Ks, life insurance, etc.). We have built out our own device management software — we're besides Rippling one of the only companies that has done that in-house ourselves. We have built out global contractor products, offer letters, traditional HRIS things, and so on. So there's quite a bit of breadth in the platform, and we have to keep building those with all the integrations to other softwares and ERPs and things like that.

But in parallel, we also have to build the agents and the infrastructure for AI to natively act on all of it — on all employee data that's in Warp. And so that means the most capable agent harness that can reliably and with true user trust act on their very sensitive tax, payroll, employee data.

And so those two things — we don't see them as being able to pick one or the other. I think we just have to do both. And that's where I think this bigger round helps us to catapult into building the engineering team, the platform, the product for that next phase.

十二、即将发布：Warp Customer Agent

Any sort of specific things within all of that that you're personally really excited about?

We're about to launch in the next couple of weeks the full GA release of the Warp customer agent.

And what that means is — historically we tended to use AI as background agents a lot to automate workflows on taxes and compliance and filings and registrations a lot in the background. But we've built enough infrastructure now to give all of that to the customer's hand as well.

And so what I mean by that is: anything that you can do in Warp by clicking buttons, period, you can now do with the agent. So the advantage of that is going to be to be able to very easily and very efficiently create these more complex workflows — such as, like, when an employee joins in New York, pro-add them to this healthcare plan, create — add them to this sick policy that is New York compliant, and provision their devices and apps, and make sure that they complete the New York security training, and so on and so forth.

Right? So all of that you can just script that, be a natural language — not some really complicated code-low builder, which historically is how this was done — and then be able to do that very, very efficiently without having to know how to code. And I think that is what I'm personally very excited about. I think we're seeing some interesting use cases emerge from our top customers already. So very excited to do that for all our customers soon.

收尾

Great. Okay. Well, Aish, I think that's all we have time for today. Congratulations again on raising the round, and I'm really excited to see where Warp goes from here. It's been great to have you as our partner and YC on this journey.

So, thank you so much for having me.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的观察：Warp 的"反 SaaS"结构，本质上是一家伪装成软件公司的"税务事务所杀手"
大多数人听到 Warp 的故事，会聚焦在"AI 原生 HR"这个标签上。但真正惊人的数字藏在一句话里——**1000+ 客户、覆盖全美 1000+ 税务辖区，却只用 1.5 个税务专员（现在才扩到 2 个）**。
这意味着什么？传统 HR 巨头（Workday、ADP、Gusto）30–40% 的人力消耗在合规、税务、客服这些"后台沼泽"里。Warp 不是在做"更好的 HR 软件"——它在做一件更激进的事：**用 LLM 把整个行业的"人力中后台"从损益表上抹掉**。
换句话说，Warp 真正的护城河不是产品功能，而是**人均处理税务辖区的杠杆比**（按 1000 jurisdictions / 1.5 人 算，约 666:1，而传统玩家通常是 5:1 到 20:1）。这是一个隐藏的、可被精确测量的"AI 原生度"指标——比任何"我们用了 AI"的宣传词都更硬核。
## 二、跨领域连接：物理学家的"对称性破缺"思维，正在改写 SaaS 的护城河理论
注意 Aush 的背

item.title: [邦妮區塊鏈 Bonnie Blockchain] 億萬富翁：AI泡沫即將爆破3大原因！川普恐為選舉，讓股市崩跌？但為什麼？【邦妮區塊鏈】
item.dc:creator: 邦妮區塊鏈 Bonnie Blockchain
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的观察：这不是"AI 泡沫"，而是一场资本物理学的相变

大多数人把 AI 热潮看作"估值过高"——这是用上一轮泡沫的语言描述这一轮。但 Hayes 在访谈里漏出了一个真正令人震撼的细节："几十年来第一次，股权净发行量大于股权回购"。

这句话被快速带过，却是整段对话里最重要的一句。过去 20 年，美股的底层物理是"股票在消失"——回购把流通股压缩，所以指数自然上行。而现在，资本流动方向反转了：股票在被创造，被创造出来吸收世界的现金。Google 850 亿、SpaceX 200 亿债券、Anthropic / OpenAI 排队 IPO——这是一台全球性的资本吸尘器，把每一块边际美元、每一个台湾家庭的储蓄、每一家中央银行的外汇储备，全部吸向硅片与电力。

这就是为什么 Bitcoin 在印钞中横盘——它不是在抗通胀失败，它是在被一个更大的吸力场暂时压扁。

二、跨领域连接：AI 的折旧表是一颗会计学定时炸弹，而它的引信是摩尔定律

Hayes 提到一个被市场系统性忽略的细节：GPU 实际使用寿命约 2 年，但被按 6 年折旧表入账。

把这件事翻译到其他领域你就懂它的恐怖： - 这相当于用 30 年房贷买一辆只能开 10 年的车； - 相当于金融业的"次贷"——但抵押品是会随时间内在贬值的硅； - 更精确的类比是 1840 年代英国铁路狂热：当时所有人也都在"用永久资产的会计逻辑"为一项加速折旧的技术融资，结果不是技术错了，而是会计与物理之间的错配在 1847 年集体爆破。

中央银行能印钱解决信贷问题，但没有任何央行行长能修改摩尔定律。这就是 Hayes 真正想说的：2028 年的危机不会是"信心崩溃"，而是会计现实终于追上了硅的物理现实。这是一场技术与会计的相对论碰撞，而印钞机救不了它。

三、被忽视的政治学反转：Trump 的"反 AI"可能比"挺 AI"更具选举效率

主流叙事认为 Trump 必然挺 AI，因为他和科技大佬绑定。Hayes 给出了一个让我倒吸一口气的反向推演："美国 90% 的选民持有的股票不足以让他们在意股市崩盘"。

这是民主制度里最少被讨论的隐藏机制——资本市场的政治权重远小于其经济权重。一个候选人可以让市场跌 30%，只要他能让相信"AI 抢了我的工作"的选民相信"我在替你出气"，他就能赢。

这里出现一个跨领域连接：这与 2016 年英国脱欧、1980 年代日本对美贸易战、2010 年代法国黄背心运动是同构事件——资本与劳动的不对称政治权重导致的反向修正。AI 现在所处的位置，像极了 1990 年代美国制造业外移到墨西哥的瞬间——只是这次速度快了 100 倍，而且白领第一次站到了"被自动化"的位置上。

所以真正的"AI 黑天鹅"不是技术失败，不是中国 DeepSeek，不是估值崩盘——而是某个秋天早上 Trump 发一条 Truth Social 说"我要冻结数据中心建设三个月"。他不需要真的做，他只需要让市场相信他可能做。

四、隐藏的系统性真相：Hayes 真正在押注的不是"crypto 牛市"，而是央行的反应函数

最后这点最少被听众捕捉到：Hayes 全程没有说"AI 崩盘 → Bitcoin 涨"。他的逻辑链是三段式：

AI 崩盘 → 信贷黑洞规模超过 2008 → 央行被迫印出史无前例规模的钱 → 这笔钱无处可去（不能回流到刚崩盘的 AI），最终溢出到 Bitcoin、黄金、能源股。

这意味着他在做的不是"crypto 投资"，而是在为一次特定的政策反射弧建仓。这是一种极少有人理解的元层投资：不是赌资产，而是赌权力机构在危机中的可预测行为。

这和 Soros 1992 年做空英镑是同一种思维结构——不是看汇率，而是看英格兰银行的反应函数有上限。Hayes 看的不是 Bitcoin 的基本面，是美联储的"印钞反射弧"的下限。

而这就解释了他全程那个看似矛盾的状态：bearish 一切，但持有 Bitcoin 等着大崩盘。这不是矛盾，这是一种期权式的耐心——他在等一个"政策卖权"被触发的时刻。

📝 转录文本

开场：抛售、印钞与资本吸力场

"I sold most of my AI stuff when I sold all my crypto stuff."

Google raised 85 billion. Google look at the IPO pipeline — Anthropic, OpenAI coming up — and they're like, let's raise before people are still optimistic.

For the first time in maybe a few decades, the net issuance of equity is larger than sort of buybacks and all these other ways that equity has been retired for the last 20 years. And so I think we're in this scenario where there is a global sucking of capital to AI.

Pick somebody in the United States that Trump supported in the past and then turned on them on a dime when it made political sense. I cannot imagine that one day someone says, "Oh, let's crash the market," and people are happy about that.

I've seen all this and I know it's going to end badly at some point.

There are traders that are trying to front-run this passive index buying SpaceX — the date is July 7th.

Index rebal is a tough thing because you're playing this reflexive game of "okay, everyone knows the inclusion math." How soon — you can make more money on a rebound in certain assets that'll do well when the authorities panic than you can trying to time, you know, the AI bubble. Like, I don't like buying these charts that are just completely vertical, because everyone knows that everyone knows that AI is the thing. So obviously that's not the trade that I want to be in.

The real story was: who sells a pick-and-shovel to these guys in Taiwan, Korea, Japan?

Why would you invest in Bitcoin? The marginal fiat dollar is not going to Bitcoin. Cardano's a piece of trash. Cardano is the definition of a shitcoin — does absolutely nothing, promised the world, and has delivered on zero of it.

"Is it easier for Cardano to 10x or AI stock to 10x?" — An AI stock.

The amount of capital that will be destroyed will be so big that the money printing that we saw in 2008 will be like a rounding error compared to what's going to happen with AI. This is the big print. This is what we've been waiting for since COVID — a massive impulse of credit that's going to come and have nowhere to go but crypto, and maybe gold, maybe some energy stocks. And that is going to be the moment where we shine again.

Stop worrying about like, you know, "did I get this little transition in the market right?" You got to get the big ones right. I think this is a big one. This is going to be the opportunity of a generation.

嘉宾介绍

Okay. Arthur Hayes is back, ladies and gentlemen. I'm super excited. He is the co-founder of BitMEX, CIO of Maelstrom, and the only person who can turn a market thesis into a 10,000-word article that the crypto community argues about for months and months.

Welcome back, Arthur. — Thanks for having me.

一、AI 泡沫的三大引爆点

在你最近 6 月发表的文章 The Reality Test 中，你谈到三件可能戳破 AI 泡沫的事。是什么？

1. 油价与美伊战争

The first thing is oil prices due to the US and Iran war. Obviously now we're in this sort of ceasefire — memorandum of understanding, peace deal, who knows. Every day I open the news and something else has happened. At the end of the day, what are we looking for? What will be the price of oil in sort of a 3- to 6-month time frame? Does a falling price of oil mean that Trump has less pressure to accept a deal?

Now, obviously, there was a deal announced — was it last week or the week before last — this 14-point. And we've already seen Israel, which is supposedly America's ally, vassal state, whatever you want to call them — isn't happy about the deal. They're restarting their genocide in Lebanon and in Gaza to sort of force the Iranians to say, "No, this isn't what we agreed to, let's back out of the deal." Because they don't want this deal. They don't want Iran to be reintegrated into the global economy because they see that as a threat to their regional hegemony.

So what happens? I don't know. But at the end of the day, obviously, oil has fallen a lot. It's down something like 70-ish dollars — the WTI and Brent crude. And people are like, "Oh yeah, oil inflation's over, whatnot, and so we can all go back and yip-yipay back to the AI thing."

But I think longer term, there's a very real possibility that as nations sort of rebuild their inventories and people move from a just in time to a just in case sort of hoarding of industrial commodities, that oil could be markedly higher in 3–6 months' time than it is today. And then the question is, for your portfolio, do the things that you own — can they withstand a much higher oil price? Especially if we go into the AI field, which AI is literally just converting energy into compute into intelligence and doing something useful for human civilization.

So I think that's the number one thing. What is the price of oil? What is your view on that?

2. AI 的政治化

And then it comes down to the politics of AI. We've already started to see some inklings of what could happen when you have a very politicized build-out of AI, especially in the United States.

The ban of the Mythos and Fable model by President Trump and his Commerce Department — unilaterally decided that this model was too dangerous and therefore it should only be used by Americans, even inside of Anthropic. So a foreign person cannot work on this model even if they work for the company that released it.

— Kind of crazy. — Yeah.

And so that's why they pulled the model almost immediately once the US government launched this concern. And so now you have this issue where essentially you could be a paying member of Anthropic and using their models, and all of a sudden — something you have no control over — Donald Trump and his lieutenants decide that they don't want you using that model, and all of a sudden Anthropic has to comply because they're a US company, and boom, you're out of luck.

So I think this goes to the open-source versus closed-source debate. And obviously China is a champion of open source for AI. It's much cheaper. It's pretty much just as good — or is slightly worse — than most of the frontier models. And so if you're a non-American user of AI, you have to think: well, what's the point of actually using a US company's frontier models if I don't even know if I can actually ever use them day-to-day? Maybe I should just use the open-source model from China, host it on my own machine, have control over all my data, and pay like 1/5, 1/6, 1/10th the cost of what the US models charge.

And obviously, this is a problem because the valuation for a lot of these companies is based on them charging lots of money and increasing profit margins for these best-in-class models. So again, that's the AI bubble story.

3. 资本被全部吸走

When is the AI bubble going to pop? If it does pop — depending on your view on that and how that relates to crypto — at the end of the day, I think that for the last, call it, 18 months, since October 2025 when sort of the AI capex spend and debt that's been issued has really ramped up to the hundreds of billions and now trillions of dollars, it's taken all the available marginal capital away from anything else.

And so this is why Bitcoin and all the other crypto complexes suffered — because the investor says, "Okay, well I have a unit of fiat. I know it's being debased. The AI thing is going up gangbusters. I can trade Cosby and TYX and all these stocks are going nuts. Why would it be in Bitcoin or Solana or Ethereum when it goes up one or 10%? SK Hynix is up 10x in one year, right?"

So, I think this is the whole zeitgeist of the time. And this is what I was theorizing — well, what could actually pop this AI bubble? Could the politics bring down AI? Then capital is destroyed, and then you sort of have this period where, at least initially, crypto does very, very badly until the authorities print some money and then we get back to up only.

But who knows when the AI bubble is going to pop. I don't know.

二、Trump、选举与 AI 政治

你提到 11 月。

I think there's definitely — you have an election coming up, and around election times people make a lot of choices. So you could see this Trump political antagonism towards certain types of AI usage morph into maybe he says a moratorium on data centers, or at least talks about it, right? Puts the rhetoric out there to gain some votes to help the Republicans win the House and the Senate in the US.

And regardless of whether or not he actually does it is irrelevant. If the investors have enough belief that he might do it, then you might say, "Okay, maybe it's time to take some profit." And once that starts happening, then the whole trade sort of collapses upon itself, and then people start asking lots of other uncomfortable questions about the assumptions that underpin their bullish AI thesis.

你现在做多 AI 吗？

No, I sold most of my AI stuff a few weeks ago when I sold all my crypto stuff. So right now I'm just watching, see what happens.

Obviously the SpaceX IPO is doing really, really well. Elon continues to become richer and richer and richer. We'll see what happens with Anthropic and OpenAI. Or maybe the US government decides that they're going to even get more involved in it by doing what China does — taking equity stakes in these companies, funneling even more debt capital into these things. And then the rally can really continue, right? SpaceX had 100x revenue. Could it be 200x? Of course — if Donald Trump is willing to lend them some more money or buy their equity at the market price, then of course it could go higher.

三、Google 850 亿融资与资本黑洞

Google 融资 850 亿——历史上上市公司最大的股权融资——就在 SpaceX IPO 之前。人们猜 Google 看到 IPO 管道里有 Anthropic、OpenAI，决定趁着大家还乐观先融钱。你觉得这种说法成立吗？

Absolutely. The amount of capital that's needed to build this capex is only going to increase. So you see Google and all these companies are tapping the debt markets, billions of dollars. Even SpaceX did a $20 billion bond issue, I think last week. So now they have the equity option as well. Their free cash flow is declining. Obviously at some point you don't want to keep piling on debt. So if the equity markets are very favorable, why not issue equity?

And so I think for the first time in maybe a few decades, the net issuance of equity is larger than sort of buybacks and all these other ways that equity has been retired for the last 20 years. We're in this scenario where there is a global sucking of capital to AI — and they'll do it via debt, they'll do it via equity. And as long as people are willing to buy this stuff, then it's going to continue.

这是你现在偏 risk-off 的原因吗？

I'm seeing all this and I know it's going to end badly at some point. I don't know when that is, but at the end of the day, I think you can make more money on a rebound in certain assets that'll do well when the authorities panic than you can trying to time the AI bubble.

I don't like buying these charts that are just completely vertical, because everyone knows that everyone knows that AI is the thing. So obviously that's not the trade that I want to be in.

这是动量交易。

I'm not the best momentum trader. So it just doesn't fit with my style.

你可以这么说——在 MU 5x 之后，但接下来又有 2x，对吧？

Of course. Yeah. Exactly. I think we're still in the bull market. So I think there's still money to be made on the upside.

四、被忽视的两个致命假设

But I think one of the big things that we don't appreciate is:

假设一：定价权

Do these AI companies have pricing power? If there's a model from DeepSeek or Kimi or one of these Chinese models, and it's just as good, costs one-tenth the price of what it costs to produce a token in the West — does the marginal consumer say, "Oh, okay, if I'm just building some travel app or I've got some agents combing my email, do I really need the best top-tier model that costs so much more money from a US lab and probably is harvesting all my data to sell it back to me in some way, shape, or form? Why don't I just run a much smaller model on my own device or my own network securely and pay way less, and do this the Chinese way?"

And as long as you don't badmouth the Chinese Communist Party, like, they don't care what you do on these apps. So I don't think many people care about the politics in China — and the price is right.

We've seen with every other vertical that China has entered: initially people think, "Oh, the brand of the Western company matters." And then you present the customer with something that's just as good for a much cheaper price, and the customer says, "Okay, I don't really care about that brand." We can look at automobiles as a prime example — would you rather have the BMW or the BYD?

We've seen BYD and Geely and Xpeng and all these different auto brands completely dominate in countries where they're allowed to sell both types of Western versus Chinese EVs. The price is right and the quality is there. I think the same thing is going to happen with AI — and that would severely undercut the thesis of why I should pay a ridiculous multiple for all these US companies.

假设二：折旧表

The other is: you're underwriting a lot of debt on a depreciation schedule that doesn't match the reality of silicon hardware. If I underwrite a GPU chip at 6 years and the useful life is 2, we're going to see a reckoning come in sort of 2028-ish time frame on these assumptions on depreciation — unless demand really, really kicks into gear for tokens for these frontier Western AI models.

There's a lot of assumptions in there, and I think over time these will be challenged. I take a bearish view on that.

五、AI 支出 = 美国 GDP

AI 公司在芯片、数据中心、员工身上花钱，那些钱支撑了经济与其他企业。如果支出放缓会怎样？

Well, at least from the United States, that's literally the whole economy. If you take a look at GDP growth in the US, it's majority AI tech spend and the derivatives of that. And so that's why Trump and the politicians are very embedded with all of these AI companies, and they want them to succeed because the entire economy is based on this.

But I think there's some very dubious assumptions that this demand is based on for why you need to build, you know, the next trillion dollars' worth of data centers.

但在那篇文章里，你也说 Trump 可能出于政治原因反过来对付 AI 科技大佬——至少短期内。

I think people say, "Oh, no, Trump could never do that because he supported them in the past." Pick somebody in the United States that Trump supported in the past and then turned on them on a dime when it made political sense. So politics is very fickle, and people would be wise to just think about the possibility that Trump could be anti-AI, at least for a few months, to generate some votes.

反 AI 真能赢得选票吗？

If you read anecdotal stories, there's a lot of people who are upset about data centers and the AI job displacement, right? They're noisy. They pollute the water. They cause inflation. And so certain states are evaluating moratoriums on data center construction. Planning permission is becoming harder — not impossible, harder.

On the general zeitgeist of AI — basically, you have all these tech bros saying AI is such a transformative technology, it's literally going to eliminate all these types of drudgery work. But in the future, we're all going to be doing whatever, something better than we were doing today. But that's a future. That's many years from now.

The here and now is: AI is taking people's jobs. People are scared that they did this job for however long, and all of a sudden this AI tool that was launched yesterday now takes their job. And they're not in the SpaceX IPO at the employee price. They're not in Samsung before it ripped 10x. They don't have that much money to invest in the stock market. So they're not really benefiting from all this paper wealth that's being created in this AI stock market boom.

So they're like, "Well, politician — do something about this. Either A, tax these companies, or B, put some sort of regulation so that they can't take my job."

You're starting to see certain politicians on the left start to espouse some of these views — like Bernie Sanders in the United States says that the US should take a 50% stake in some of these AI companies. Even Trump himself thinks that the US government should have a stake in these companies. How it's funded, that's the question. But I think on both sides, people recognize that there has to be some compensation for the average person. Otherwise you're going to have massive social unrest.

When you start getting into those discussions between capital and labor — when you start from the standpoint of capital having everything — then of course your market valuation for that company has to decline.

六、Trump 的双面话术

这不会让 Trump 处境很尴尬吗？他想成为 AI 和加密的首都，又要反过来打 AI？

Politics — the most successful politicians speak out of both sides of their mouths. That is the skill. If Trump can do that and thread the needle — he sits down all the AI bros: "Hey, look, I'm going to come after you guys for four or five months. Don't sweat it. It's all good. Once Team Red wins in November, it's back to business as usual."

If I were Trump, that's what I would do. Not to say that he would do that or not do that — I'm just trying to think about, in his position, what are the things that he could do to win the election.

假设那真的发生，市场崩了，那对他选举怎么有利？

The average person says, "Oh, these tech guys — Trump cares about me." The average worker. In the United States it's one man, one vote, one woman, one vote. So yes, the stock market goes down, but 90% of the American public don't own enough stocks for it to matter. You just have very vocal wealthy people who would be impacted by this — but at the end of the day, they aren't the ones who are going to carry you in the election.

我可能有偏见，因为我来自台湾，大家都在谈 AI 股票，好像大家都赚翻了。

Exactly. Taiwan, Korea, Japan. Exactly.

我无法想象一天有人说"让市场崩盘吧"，然后人们很高兴。

Yeah, exactly. Some people will be — at least in America.

文化差异吗？

I think the people that are in our orbit who are all investing in AI stocks are not the median voter who is going to win an election for a politician. And the good politicians recognize that. They take money from the rich people and then sort of kind of have to wing him a bit to get reelected, and then they continue doing the thing they were going to do anyways. That's politics, at least in the United States as I have observed.

So I think that this is a winning strategy for Trump or any particular politician.

七、Fed、Kevin Warsh 与"工作组"

这怎么影响利率？他想要更低的利率。

I particularly think — you know, Kevin Warsh, new chairman of the Fed — he gave his first press conference, and I think that his tell was this whole task force business.

So if you go back, he was a Fed governor up until I think 2010 or 2011. He left the Fed because he was upset about this balance sheet expansion for quantitative easing to rebuild the banking system in the United States after the global financial crisis in 2008. And so he left the Fed — and when someone leaves the Fed, then all of a sudden they get their conscience back and they start criticizing all these things: the inequality, the money printing, the inflation, yada yada yada — all these ways in which the Fed should be doing a better job at price stability.

He's been saying this stuff for 16 years. So now he's the big head dog at the Fed. Now, I'm not saying he has the votes to get through a lot of these things, because you need 7 out of 12 to pass anything. But if he's about breaking with the past tradition of money printing towards a new Fed — a new independent Fed that cares about the average person and protecting their currency, their hard work — then at least go out there and state, "Here's my position on all these issues: balance sheet, this and that." He could just repeat what he's been saying for the last 16 years. Whether or not it actually happens immediately, that's not the point. At least you put everyone on notice.

Instead, he's launching a task force. We know what happens with a task force in politics, right? Everyone has to do a task force. They're going to spend months getting a bipartisan panel of economists and market participants — they're going to sit around for 6 or 12 months, produce some report, then Warsh is going to say, "Oh, I have the task force report," and then they're going to discuss it for two years. There'll be nothing accomplished, but people say, "Oh yeah, Warsh cares about inflation and changing the way the Fed is operating."

If he actually cared about it, then state your position clearly at that press conference last week and then do something about it over time. Instead — you're going to do a task force, which we all know means nothing is going to change.

So I think the Fed is boxed in a corner. They have to become dovish because of the financial situation of the United States government and this AI boom. Think about AI — the amount of tax dollars generated by OpenAI, SpaceX, and Anthropic in terms of insider selling: they have to pay capital gains, and some states are earning 10–15% taxes on top as well. It's in the government's interest for these IPOs to go very, very well. Do you really want the Federal Reserve tanking the market by jacking up rates to tighten monetary conditions and reduce inflation — if it's going to cause literally something that could be 10, 15, 20% of the federal tax bill, which is just people selling their shares on these big AI companies and paying taxes? I don't think so.

So I think the Fed always does what's in the best interest of the government, and the best interest of the government is easy monetary policy and supporting this AI bubble.

所以你预期更宽松的货币政策？

I'm expecting the status quo. Right now the Fed is printing money — called "reserve management purchases." Another fancy word for quantitative easing, another fancy word for money printing. That is what they're doing. Their balance sheet is growing, and he didn't stop that. He just held rates.

If you follow the economic dogma, the policy rate — they target 2% inflation, and CPI is at what, 4% or something like that? Anyways, by their numbers, inflation is too high. So why are you holding rates? You should be raising rates aggressively to reduce inflation. The 10-year yield has risen markedly over the past 18 months — the 10-year is telling you, "Hey, we want higher rates." If you're not going to give higher rates, Fed, then we'll do it for you, market.

Again, if he really cared about the average American and inflation and price stability — all these things he said for 16 years when he wasn't the man in charge — you don't do a task force. You state what your position is and how you're going to change the institution. And anyone who's not on board, you're going to find a way to get them out of the picture. That's what you do. But again, he launched a task force.

So I think it's this status quo. The same policies of Jay Powell, Janet Yellen, Ben Bernanke, Alan Greenspan — all these guys, they're all the same. Money printing, support the market, support the financial position of the government.

你认为印钞会更激进吗？毕竟 Trump 任命他的。

Right now the policy is perfect. AI is pumping. SpaceX is — what is it, fifth, fourth largest company in the world? — with losing however many billions of dollars a year, trading at some ridiculous price-to-revenue multiple. That just tells you that monetary policy is too easy. If you're able to believe all the things that Elon is selling you in that S-1, monetary policy is too easy, money is too loose, the Fed should do something about that. And they're not. Just by holding it steady, they're continuing the Ponzi scheme — which is fine, that's what they're there to do.

So I think he doesn't have to do anything. Just leaving the dials where they are is perfectly enough juice for the AI bubble to continue.

八、SpaceX IPO：低流通、高 FDV

SpaceX IPO，低流通、高 FDV——我们在加密货币里很熟悉。你怎么看？

He's engineered everything perfectly to make this thing go up. You only float 4% of the stock. You change the index rules — or get them to change the index rules — so that this is included in all the major indices, so you have passive money flowing into this. Maybe the US government's going to invest in this thing to give the American people an opportunity to own this highly overvalued company.

Okay, cool — Reddit. He's doing all the things, ticking all the boxes, just like any crypto DJ muppet would do with their piece-of-shit coin that they want to flog off to the public. Elon is doing it.

And we know — once the insiders start selling, which they will — because you have some people who really had generational wealth created on paper, of course you're going to take some chips off the table when your shares unlock. Can the stock continue to rally when you go from 4% float to 30% float by October of this year? Crypto tells you usually that marks the top of a particular market. We'll see.

I don't think it really particularly matters for the general AI consensus, because as long as SpaceX is around these valuation levels, that just gives signals that, okay, the game is still long — we can still sell all this future revenue and book it today, and people are willing to pay that multiple. So even if the stock stalls, it's still bullish for the market — because it's a two-plus-trillion-dollar valuation on no profitability.

有人想抢跑指数被动买入 SpaceX——7 月 7 日。聪明吗？

Index rebal is a tough thing because you're playing this reflexive game of "okay, everyone knows the inclusion math." How soon do you start prepositioning? Or do you take the opposite view that there's too much money going into the index inclusion, and then the amount of money disappoints and then people swing the other way?

I've never been a rebal trader. There's lots of philosophies on how you sort of position yourselves. So I can't really speak to that.

你会买 SpaceX 吗？

No, not at this valuation. Maybe if the thing crashed 99% and it looked like —

99? 你要 Amazon 那种情形？

I want, you know — basically I want the thing to go down 99%, 95%, and then "Oh, okay, Starlink — great business, profitable." XAI could be profitable, absolutely. Mining in space does not make sense today because it's probably like four times more expensive than a terrestrial data center, but it will make sense at some point in the future. Maybe 5–10 years from now. But do I want to pay a multiple at this level for that future? No, I don't want to pay that multiple.

I'm not mentally primed to participate in this kind of stock price mania.

九、Bitcoin 为何没涨

大量资金涌入 AI 相关股票与债券，规模大致匹配 M2 的扩张。你说这就是 Bitcoin 没涨的原因。

Yeah, that's what I put in my essay.

Because the money is going on — if you have a unit of fiat, what are you doing in Taiwan? You're trading TSMC, Compeq, all the names. You're going nuts. It's the supply chain. It's the same trade that we used to do with Apple — every time Apple would come up with shipments, we'd be all in all these names. They're all the same names, because they sell similar components. Now they're into the AI trade and we're all treating these Taiwanese names up and down.

I was talking to my friends in San Francisco who live very close to these AI companies, and they're saying a house would list for $2 million and sell for $4.5 million because there's just so much cash in that area and industry.

那 AI 的财富会不会在某个时刻轮动到 Bitcoin？

I don't think so. Because Bitcoin has not performed, and these people are going to buy hard assets — they're going to buy the house, the car, the expensive watch, fly first class or buy the plane. All these sorts of things that folks do when they've generated a massive amount of wealth.

I don't think they're going to go into Bitcoin just because, "Oh well, it hasn't really performed. I thought it was supposed to be this inflation-protecting store of wealth — you know, all the narratives we talk about with why you should own Bitcoin. I should have just bought NASDAQ. Maybe I just buy some more NASDAQ. Okay, maybe I'll sell my stake in this one company. Let me diversify by owning a basket of other up-and-coming tech companies." Because you intrinsically believe in the story of AI — otherwise you wouldn't have been doing this for so long when it wasn't the hottest trade on the street.

So I don't see this money rotating into crypto until the entire AI story collapses on itself.

那 AI 跌的时候，Bitcoin 也会跌？

Yes. Everything goes down together — correlation one, at least in the beginning. Because these assets are all connected. Investors sell what they can, not what they want. Bitcoin is a liquid asset, trades 24/7. If you've got to generate cash for margin calls and all sorts of other spending, you just sell everything as quickly as you can. That's why things move in a correlation-one moment in the initial part of the downdraft, and then you start to sort out the relative winners and how you think the rebound's going to happen.

十、AI 泡沫破裂时点：2028

大胆猜一下，AI 泡沫什么时候破？

I think the prime date would be sometime in 2028. The majority of this new wave of debt was originated starting in late 2024, 2025, 2026. And essentially we're underwriting, number one, a GPU with a useful life of two years, but we're giving it a six-year depreciation schedule.

The chickens come home to roost in about two and a half years — roughly 2028. You start to see: okay, is that demand there? Did that H100 chip or that Blackwell chip that I underwrote in 2024–2025 — is that still relevant for me to generate enough revenue? And is that revenue even more depressed by the fact that there's a Chinese model that's just as good, maybe that's on a Huawei chip, and it's one-tenth the price?

The entire economics of why I should build a Western data center at this price evaporate. That math is going to collide with demand. So either demand is so ridiculous and we're all wrong — agents and companies and productivity and whatever, there's so much demand it dwarfs all these negative things that could happen — or it's as we expect, and people prefer the cheaper, just-as-good product, and this chip that you bought two years ago is now obsolete because you need to buy the new chip to get the best inference per kilowatt-hour.

That crucible happens in 2028.

你会关注什么指标来验证你的判断？

The acceleration or deceleration of AI capex spend. If we want this bull market to continue, we want the amount of capex spend to accelerate. 2026 estimate over 2025 is like double, something like that. We needed at least a doubling to meet our forecasts.

Even if it goes up in 2027, that's not the point. You can't invest in a chart that goes up like that if you're not accelerating the amount of spend on capex. So a deceleration in AI capex is market-negative for the trend — even if their earnings are going to be positive for a lot of these companies, you paid some ridiculous multiple, so you need accelerating earnings to justify an even higher price for what you paid for in the past.

十一、四年周期已死？

你提到 2028，那是下一次比特币减半。故事就这么延续？

No, I don't care about Bitcoin halvings. I think it's coincidence. I don't like to use "oh, it has to be a four-year cycle" — because if you bet, one time, two times you're going to be wrong on that. You just bet your entire farm on "it has to be the four-year cycle, I picked out the date, that's the date."

You can't be so exact on things. Things change. It's fluid. It's a cycle. I just don't like to be so exact on anything. This is why I don't like the four-year cycle. It might be correct in your backtested past, but the future does not necessarily have to conform to that. You don't want to be pigeonholing your investment strategy based on what's happened in the past. You want to inform yourself with what has happened in the past and use your judgment to see how things might change in the future based on the current circumstances.

但回顾历史——第一次减半周期我们涨了 92x，然后 30x、8x，这一轮大约 2x。如果 Micron 能涨 7x，我为什么还要持有比特币？

Correct. And that is the AI trade. This is the largest build-out of capex ever in human history — larger than the railroads as a percentage of global GDP. Of course the people who sell the picks and shovels — the Microns, the DRAM memory providers, basically Samsung, SK Hynix — this is why these stocks have just mooned recently. I sell the picks and shovels, I sell the energy, I sell the transformers, I sell whatever I sell to these data centers.

That's the trade. It's not going long the hyperscalers — yeah, you've made some money, but the real story was: who sells a pick and shovel to these guys in Taiwan, Korea, Japan? These are the real stories of this AI bull market. Which is why everyone in these markets — KOSPI was the best-performing market last year. KOSPI has higher realized volatility than it did during the global financial crisis — and this is upside volatility on KOSPI. It's absolutely insane.

So that's the story. "Yeah, why the f*** would I invest in Bitcoin or Ethereum or — I should just find some no-name AI component manufacturer in Taiwan, Korea or Japan, and my stock in 3 months I'll be up 10x." That's how people are thinking — and they're not wrong, based on the past.

那未来呢？

You're assuming, number one, that these guys continue to spend at an aggressive pace, and that the demand for their tokens continues to increase such that it negates all the negative ways in which they underwrote the economics of a chip. That is the question.

现在是不是买比特币的好时机？毕竟超卖。

I don't know about that. I own Bitcoin, I'm long, I've been long for a very long time. It goes up, it goes down. If you had a unit of fiat capital and you are an avid trader looking at your screen all day, I couldn't in good conscience say, "Oh yeah, you need to own Bitcoin." Go trade the AI thing — if you live and breathe AI, live and breathe the capex numbers, the news cycle, understand these trends, and you believe that you're going to be able to get off the train before everyone else, before the thing collapses on itself — then why would you invest in Bitcoin?

The marginal fiat dollar is not going to Bitcoin. It's going to the debt issuance of the hyperscaler. It's going to the equity issuance of the hyperscaler. At the government level, they're going to be pushing their central banks and their treasury departments to fund AI for national security, to build more drones — all the things why a politician is pro-AI is only going to intensify.

十二、卖掉山寨币与新发现

你最近卖了不少山寨币——Zcash、NEAR、Worldcoin、Hyperliquid。改变什么了？

I became more bearish on this AI story. I had a good run — I was up a lot in terms of profitability. So I said, okay, let me take some chips off the table. These trades have become almost consensus. If people are picking up my Holy Trinity and saying, "Oh yeah, that's the only thing that I need to own" — that doesn't mean these things won't continue to go up, but the asymmetry in the trade has diminished.

I'm not staring at my screen all day. I'm not monitoring every particular news cycle and can time the top and pick the bottom. I did well. I created a narrative. I pumped my narrative. People believed it. They made some money. I made some money. I'm going to step back and try to reevaluate and find asymmetry somewhere else in the investing landscape.

找到了吗？

I have. There's a coin called CARDS — the collectible trading card ecosphere, if you want to call it that. It's a very small market cap coin at this point, but they generate a lot of revenue. The problem has been previously — and this is something I harp on a lot about — protocol-generated revenue: there's no path to that revenue going to token holders. But we've done some research and we believe that the team behind the CARDS coin has started to do buybacks with their revenue, and we believe that the trading card narrative is just in the beginnings of doing a run, and this could do very, very well.

十三、Ethereum、Solana、Cardano

之前你提到 Ethereum、Solana，我想加上 Cardano，因为还有很多人持有这些大盘币。你怎么看？

I mean, Cardano should not be included in those three. ADA is a piece of trash. They've done nothing. Yes, the best thing they did was good tokenomics in the beginning and giving away a lot of tokens — and it's been around for a long time, so it's gone up a lot in price, so people like it. But smart contracts — never going to happen. What actual development has happened on this chain? It's a zero.

Ethereum and Solana — different stories. There's actually things going on on these networks. The question for Solana is: do they have another memecoin-like narrative that's going to generate a lot of transaction fees or transactions on chain, or the perception that those transactions will increase in the future? That's what Solana needs to get out of its funk. Maybe the trading cards narrative will do that. I'm not sure.

And then Ethereum — obviously has not performed very well since the 2021 peak. They have some interesting economics between the L2s and the L1 that needs to be sorted out. Why do you need to own the main chain versus the L2s? I think that's the question for Ethereum, and we'll see if they sort it out.

如果还持有这些，要现在卖吗？

Depends on what your opportunity cost of capital is. If you're down a lot — yeah, but everything is relative. If you don't need the money, do you care if the thing is down 50% if you believe that in some particular period of time it's going to go up another 10x? But if you say, "Oh well, I bought this trade to do this thing and it didn't happen" — okay, then you exit the trade and take that capital and redeploy it in something else. Or maybe it's AI, or whatever you think will generate a better return for your capital.

Cardano 10x 容易还是 AI 股票 10x 容易？

An AI stock. Absolutely. An AI stock actually has some fundamentals. Cardano has had years and chances to provide fundamentals. It has done nothing. At least SK Hynix generates revenue and profitability. Now whether or not you want to pay the multiple for that — that is the question. But these are very profitable companies who've been around for a very long time.

Cardano is the definition of a shitcoin that does absolutely nothing. Promised the world and has delivered on zero of it.

你会卖 ETH 去赌 AI 股？

Again, depends on your goal. Would I? No, I have Ethereum, I still have a bag of Ethereum. Am I selling it to buy ASX? Nope. But I have spare capital. If I wanted to buy an AI stock, I don't need to fund it by selling Ethereum. So everyone is in a different situation. That is why people need to do their own research and consider their own situation before they follow anyone's musings on social media or these podcasts.

那为什么 Cardano 这么受欢迎？

I think a lot of people invested early. It's still — what is it, top 10? Top 20. From zero to top 20, that's a lot of wealth creation. Of course people are going to be loyal to Cardano: "Oh, it did something for me. It went up in price."

This is what I tell founders: you can have the best, baddest-ass tech and all the pedigree credentialed developers, build a really cool thing, and not give your token holders a way to make money — and you go nowhere even if you have real usability. Or you could be like Cardano or Ripple — do absolutely nothing, lie to your people that you're going to do something about it — however, people got this thing really, really cheap. You allowed them to get rich with you. Garlinghouse and Hoskinson — the people got rich with you, and so they're going to support you.

This is why the price is so important. It's more important than the fundamentals of what you actually build. Because if you give the majority of the community a way to participate in what you are building and they get wealthy alongside the founders of the project, then they will be with you forever regardless of what you do.

So Cardano will be, you know, in 15 years probably still be in the top 50 coin, continue to do nothing — because people made money along the way and they're going to continue to support it.

现在是第 19 名。

Still top 20, but it dropped a lot.

十四、什么会让你重新买回 crypto？

你怎么样才会重新开始买这些 coin？

Which coins? Crypto? Now, since we have 24-hour tokenized stocks trading on various exchanges, people are like, "I can trade TSMC like an altcoin." Right. Why do I need altcoins?

I think that crypto is going to find its groove again when the AI bubble collapses. And I think that the AI bubble — given the amount of capital and what I consider misallocation that is happening — obviously the market thinks I'm wrong at this point — will be bigger than subprime.

There are credit time bombs ticking in the global financial system with the way in which this credit is being underwritten on the assumption that:

  1. I can take a chip that depreciates within two years but give it a six-year depreciation, underwrite it for a six-year loan.
  2. People care about the brand of the AI company — i.e. the US brand — more than they care about cost. Which has never been the case for any particular vertical that China has entered.

These are the two assumptions that underpin, at least in my opinion, the AI bull market. I think these will be proven wrong by 2028. The amount of capital that will be destroyed will be so big that the money printing that we saw in 2008 will be like a rounding error compared to what's going to happen with AI.

Because, unfortunately, central banks cannot fix Moore's Law, and the depreciation schedule of silicon chips. They're going to print, print, print. These companies are not going to respond. The banks that underwrite these — they're done. And Bitcoin and crypto — "Oh, there's all this capital. It can't go back into AI, because everyone's like, 'Well, why would I buy a Western AI company when the Chinese one is just as good and one-tenth the price? Why would I buy a loan when the underlying economics are — I have a 2-year chip that goes to zero versus six years on this loan, doesn't make any sense. No, I don't want anything to do with that.'"

Well, I got this free money — they just kept printing it — what do I do? I buy Bitcoin.

十五、大印钞与世代级机会

This is the big print. This is what we've been waiting for since COVID — that massive impulse of credit that's going to come and have nowhere to go but crypto and maybe gold, maybe some energy stocks. And that is going to be the moment where we shine again. Bitcoin could go up 10–20x in value, because the printed money cannot go back to where it was before — because people have learned and they need to adjust.

That is when we get our next massive bull market again. I don't know when that's going to happen, but I want to be mentally prepared. That's my mental model. I need to get that transition: from AI → to money printing → back to crypto. If you can hit that right, you can get everything wrong between now and then, and afterwards. It doesn't matter if you got "COVID Bitcoin" right, or Ethereum — does it matter that you got everything else wrong? Doesn't matter. You made so much money.

So stop worrying about, "Did I get this little transition in the market right?" You got to get the big ones right. I think this is a big one. AI collapse, money printing, crypto boom. That's my mental model. When it happens, I don't know. But I want to be ready, and have dry capital ready to allocate to crypto when that happens, because I think this is going to be the opportunity of a generation.

In terms of — you wanted to go long the S&P in March 2009 at triple-6, right? You're up 10x on the biggest stock market in the world. You wanted to go long Citibank at a dollar. These are the things you wanted to do out of the crisis. Because are they going to let these things fail? Think about all the debt — the companies that are underwriting all this — are they going to let JP Morgan and Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley and BofA — all these banks that were underwriting — are they going to let them fail? Of course not.

But you're going to throw the baby out with the bathwater: "Oh, look at the crypto exposure, the AI exposure of this bank or this financial institution. I don't want to own any of that." Sure, okay, I get it. But guess what the government's going to do? They're going to bail them out.

There's going to be a generational money-making opportunity at the turn, and that's what we have to get right. Nothing else matters if you do that right. That's what I'm focused on and trying to update my mental model as we go — to think about that. And hopefully that is the case of what happens. And if I'm wrong, well, let me make sure that I understand what has changed to alter that particular worldview.

十六、AI 失业、被裁与押注自己

根据 King's College 的研究，约 70% 的人担心 AI 造成失业。你自己也亲身经历过职业上的重大颠覆——在 BitMEX 之前，你被花旗银行裁员。

Wasn't that the greatest thing that happened to you? Absolutely, it was the greatest thing that happened to me.

But again, I didn't have any debt. I didn't have a family. There's a lot of things that were going for me. So like, okay, I lost my job — who gives a f***. I can be poor. I don't really have any other — at the end of the day, I think a lot of people need to believe in themselves.

I see a lot of people on social media: "Oh, I didn't invest in SpaceX at this valuation. I should have done this. I did something else." But at the end of the day, whatever you do, you should always bet on yourself, because you have complete control over what you can do with your own mind and your own effort and make your own way. You can't control what someone else does.

If you have the opportunity to bet on yourself with a lot of these new tools that allow us to express ourselves economically in much more impactful ways — then I think there's a massive opportunity for those who want to take it.

未来五年应该专注做什么？

I have no idea. Whatever you enjoy doing. I couldn't give you — if you're going to be an entrepreneur and you don't enjoy what you're doing, then what's the point? So I think everyone's different.

Yes — that's a bit trite, because obviously you have bills to pay and whatnot. But if you're able to solve the basic needs of human existence, and now you have these tools that allow you to have a much more impactful economic footprint using cheap AI — you can do so many more things that you couldn't do 10 years ago, even with the internet, that you can do now as an entrepreneur with an idea and some grit and determination.

That's not to say that this job displacement should be dismissed. This is a real thing. Entrepreneurship is not for everyone. Some people just want to work a job, which is fine. There's nothing wrong with that. So understand your own psychological makeup of what makes sense for you. But there is a massive amount of opportunity for a few people.

结语

Arthur, really, thank you. This is amazing, and it's always fun to have you on. Let's do this in a couple of months.

Let's do a couple months. Yeah, exactly. Is this reality, or is this a test?

Yeah. Yeah. Right. Yeah. Thank you.

Thanks for having me.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的观察：这不是"AI 泡沫"，而是一场**资本物理学的相变**
大多数人把 AI 热潮看作"估值过高"——这是用上一轮泡沫的语言描述这一轮。但 Hayes 在访谈里漏出了一个真正令人震撼的细节：**"几十年来第一次，股权净发行量大于股权回购"**。
这句话被快速带过，却是整段对话里最重要的一句。过去 20 年，美股的底层物理是"股票在消失"——回购把流通股压缩，所以指数自然上行。而现在，资本流动方向**反转**了：股票在被创造，被创造出来吸收世界的现金。Google 850 亿、SpaceX 200 亿债券、Anthropic / OpenAI 排队 IPO——这是一台**全球性的资本吸尘器**，把每一块边际美元、每一个台湾家庭的储蓄、每一家中央银行的外汇储备，全部吸向硅片与电力。
这就是为什么 Bitcoin 在印钞中横盘——**它不是在抗通胀失败，它是在被一个更大的吸力场暂时压扁**。
## 二、跨领域连接：AI 的折旧表是一颗**会计学定时炸弹**，而它的引信是摩尔定律
Hayes 提到一个被市场系统性忽略的细节：**GPU 实际使用寿命约 2 年，但

item.title: [No Priors: AI, Machine Learning, Tech, & Startups] Why Traditional Benchmarks Fail Modern AI Models with OpenAI Research Scientist Noam Brown
item.dc:creator: No Priors: AI, Machine Learning, Tech, & Startups
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、基准测试的"度量单位错乱"：一场AI界的"米与英尺"事件

Noam 指出的问题表面是"benchmark grid 不带 x 轴"，但本质上是整个行业在用错误的物理量描述模型。这就像物理学家在比较两辆车时只报告"最高速度"，却忽略了"达到那个速度需要多少燃油"。当模型能力变成一个关于推理预算（token/美元/时间）的连续函数时，单点数值（scalar）注定会误导——这是从标量时代进入函数时代的范式转换。

一个让人意外的观察：GPT-5.5 比 5.4 好，却在 benchmark 上"看起来差不多"，原因竟然是5.5 太节俭了——它用更少的思考达到相近分数。这意味着我们正在用"考试时长一样"的规则去比较两个不同效率的学生，结果误判了真正聪明的那一个。模型变强的一个隐藏维度，是"省着想"的能力，而当前的评测框架反而惩罚这种能力。

二、跨领域连接：扑克河牌解算器、Erdős 单位距离猜想、人类文明史——其实是同一件事

注意 Noam 在访谈中跳跃的三个场景： - 用 5.5 写扑克 solver（他的博士论文） - 用内部模型反证 Erdős 猜想 - 谈人类 5 万年来认知积累

这三件事在他的潜意识里是同构的：智力 = 单体能力 × 协调与累积的脚手架（scaffold）。

扑克 solver 的关键不是"会算牌"而是"会优化代码十倍"；Erdős 反证的关键不是模型一次想出来，而是"列出策略 → 逐条深挖"的脚手架；人类文明的关键不是脑容量变大，而是亿万人代代相传。

令人意外的推论：Noam 实际上在暗示一件激进的事——AGI 的最后一公里可能不是模型变聪明，而是模型学会"做人类做了五万年的事"：互相阅读、互相校验、累积知识。当前 LLM 像"出生即死亡的天才"——每次对话都是一次性的生命。多智能体不是工程优化，而是给 AI 装上文明的脚手架。这把"recursive self-improvement"从"模型递归改自己"重新定义成了"模型递归构建文明"。

三、最反直觉的洞察：时间，意外地成了对齐与安全的最后护城河

整篇访谈最深的一句话被埋在中段："时间本身成了瓶颈"。

主流叙事里，"快速起飞（fast takeoff）"是危险来源——模型一夜之间自我提升、人类失控。但 Noam 给出了一个逆向论证：正因为最强能力需要海量 test-time compute，而 compute 必须在真实时间里跑，所以"一夜超人"在物理上不可能。模型越依赖推理时间，takeoff 就越平缓。

这里藏着一个跨领域的隐喻——这和金融市场的"流动性"很像：高频交易越极端，市场反而越脆弱；而当系统的输出必须经过缓慢的物理过程（货物、燃料、电力），危险就被"稀释"在时间里。AI 的安全性，意外地由 GPU 时钟频率而非对齐研究兜底。

而真正令人后背发凉的观察：Noam 顺口提到"评估一个能跑一年的 agent，唯一可靠的方法是真跑一年"——这意味着没有任何实验室真正知道自己已发布模型的能力上限。GPT-5.5 已经在你手上，它能做什么，连 OpenAI 都不知道。我们正生活在一个自己造出来却没读完说明书的世界。Erdős 猜想被反证，其实早在 5.5 发布时就已经可能，只是没人花十万美元的 token 去问它。

这指向一个反共识结论：与其等下一代模型，不如"挖"现有模型——当前最大的认知红利，可能不在前沿，而在已发布但未被推到极限的旧模型里。这是一种 AI 时代的"考古学"。

📝 转录文本

开场：测试时计算（test-time compute）的盲点

With GPT-3, you couldn't scale test time compute. If you gave it a budget of $10 million and said, "OK, let's see what GPT-3 can do," it really can't do that much.

The preparedness frameworks and responsible scaling policies, they don't really account for the amount of test-time compute. They just say, "OK, what's the capability of the model?"

The problem is we're in a world now where the capability of the model is a function of how much money you put into it, basically. If you give it a budget of $10,000, it can do a lot more than what it can do with a budget of $10. If you give it a budget of $10 million, it can do even more. At what budget should you evaluate these models? The policies that exist today don't really address that question.

主持人开场

Hi listeners, I'm Sarah Goa, and welcome back to No Priors. Today I'm here with Noam Brown, one of our godfathers of AI reasoning. We talk about the broken state of evaluations, very large-scale test-time compute, how he thinks about recursive self-improvement, and what's next on the horizon for competition at the frontier.

Welcome, Noam, I'm so excited to have you back.

— That's great to be back.

You are our first guest. I'm very proud of my taste in friends and researchers for the pod. Given how important inference-time scaling has become to the industry, you should be proud, too, having actually pioneered it.

— Played a part, yeah, among many others.

一、为什么写那篇关于 test-time compute 的 essay？

You just wrote this essay that really resonated about large-scale test-time compute and why the industry is not evaluating these models as robustly as it should be. What was the motivation for it?

The motivation was: we released 5.5, and the initial reaction was kind of skepticism that it was a substantially better model. To be fair, that only lasted for a few hours before people had some time to play around with it and tried it out themselves and saw that it was actually substantially better.

But I think a lot of the skepticism came from the benchmark grid that was published. Basically whenever a new model is released there is this benchmark grid where they show all these different benchmarks on the x-axis and then the performance of different models on the y-axis. You can just compare different models — it's a single number for a model on a single benchmark.

If you look on paper at the difference between 5.5 and 5.4 or other models, it was an improvement, but it wasn't a huge improvement. It was only a few percentage points in some benchmarks. So people looked at that and they were skeptical that it was actually a better model. Once they played around with it, the story changed.

I think the reason why it doesn't show up as so much better on the benchmarks is because the benchmarks are being presented in the wrong way. They're not controlling for the amount of test-time compute that is being used on that benchmark question. It turned out that 5.5 is just much more efficient with its thinking. If you run it at max settings, 5.4 is thinking for a lot longer — it takes longer to get back a response than 5.5. Once you control for the amount of thinking time, actually you can see that 5.5 is a substantial jump over 5.4. That is, I think, people's day-to-day experience with it.

When I mention this to people, the typical question I get is: "OK, well why not just have 5.5 think for as long as 5.4?" And the question is: how long should they think for? Typically the response is: "Well, until the performance plateaus." There's some point where the performance on the benchmark is going to plateau and you just evaluate to that point.

The thing is, the point at which it plateaus is actually really far out these days. It was true in GPT-3 land back in 2022 — the models couldn't really think productively for that long and so you could just run them until they plateau, it's not that far away. But what we're seeing today with the modern models is that 5.5 and other models, if you scaffold them reasonably well, can think for weeks before having performance plateau on some of these benchmarks. So the point at which they plateau is simply too far out to reasonably test.

— We all need to actually enforce either a patience limit or a budget limit from a token perspective now, and that wasn't true a few years ago.

Exactly. So my claim is the proper way to evaluate the models now is: you either have some kind of budget for the benchmark — whether it's tokens or cost or time or whatever — or you plot the performance as a function of the amount of test-time compute that's going into the model. Then it becomes much more clear how to compare the performance between these different models.

二、当 eval 超出模型发布周期

Given the model evaluation cycle and the fact that performance does not asymptote for many tasks over quite a long period of time, what do you do about the issue that some of the evals you would want to run are both beyond the scope of budget or time that's reasonable given the current model release cycle?

I mean, for things like cyber — and actually the AISI in their evaluations has shown — the models continue to improve at 100 million tokens. If you run them for 100 million tokens, they're still improving beyond that point. That can take a very long time to run. But you also do see that the performance is not just a discontinuous jump — you can see the slope of improvement over those 100 million tokens.

So you could probably do some kind of evaluation up to a certain budget and then just say, "OK, this is what we project the performance to look like." There hasn't been a lot of research on this yet. I actually think this would be a great paper to publish — if there's any academics out there looking for something to research: can you predict what the performance looks like at an inference budget of, say, $10,000, only using inference budgets up to $10 or $100?

三、用户是否在"想得不够久"？

So maybe an orthogonal question for you: do you think users are systematically not thinking long enough with their models about problems?

— What do you mean by not thinking long enough?

If you can build an agent or control the amount of test-time compute being used — there's what is done by the model itself and there's what the user can do — do you think that the industry is using test-time compute at an optimal amount, or way undershooting it? Or is it a problem in the models where they just need to be able to do that thinking faster?

I think it depends on the problem. This idea that you just let the models think for a week or whatever and then they respond — it sounds nice, and yes, the benchmarks look great, but it's not very practical when working. Because, OK, you ask the model a question and then you sit there for a week waiting for it to come back to you.

I think what people have found most effective is to iterate quickly with the models. The thinking time needs to be flexible: when it makes sense to respond quickly to the user, it should respond quickly; when it makes sense to think for a long time, then it makes sense to think for a long time. I think people have been striking the right balance given what they have to deal with right now.

四、Benchmark maxing 与脚手架陷阱

How would you characterize the landscape of benchmarks today? Do you have favorites that you think are more indicative of capability than others?

The benchmark-maxing thing is also motivation for writing the essay. It's really easy to show you can do much better than previous benchmarks or previous models on benchmarks by, for example, scaffolding a bunch of models together. If you say, "We're going to instead of just running this model once, run it five times and take the best of the five responses, or ask a judge which one it thinks is best" — you can get much higher scores than that model. So it's really easy to make something that looks a lot better on paper but is actually not better once you control for the amount of test-time compute. That is one thing I'm worried about when it comes to benchmark maxing — it's a little misleading.

As far as the benchmarks themselves, there is always a risk of just optimizing for the benchmark. I've certainly encouraged my team and I think at OpenAI we're pretty good about not trying to optimize for specific benchmarks. But once you put out a benchmark, it's always at risk of just being optimized for. One way to address that is to keep held-out private sets that aren't publicly available.

五、Noam 的私房 eval：用模型写扑克 bot

The most popular fallback advice — figure out if a model is significantly better or not — is to just play with it for a while. Do you have anything more sophisticated than that?

I think everybody has their own set of questions that they like to ask the model whenever it comes out. For me lately, it's been: I use them to make poker bots and see how good they can make a poker bot. It's a nice eval because there is very little open-source code for making poker bots, and there's a lot of published papers on it, but you really have to reason through everything. It requires a lot of reasoning and iteration and a lot of small gotchas that I've already worked through myself, so I can see where the models fail along the way. They've gotten really good at it now.

推理能力的迭代曲线

When the early models were really bad at it, they could not basically do anything. Then 5.2 — I was able to work with it to make a river solver (the final stage of poker). That itself was, I thought, really impressive. I was actually really impressed because I was able to make the river solver probably about five times faster than I would have alone. There were a couple things it got tripped up on — blockers was always a big issue — but overall, with a bit of gentle steering, it kind of felt like a grad student. They would run into issues, but at least I would know what those issues were, know how to fix it, and I could just make suggestions and it would go off and do it, and pretty quickly come back with something really good.

The optimization, especially, I thought was very impressive — it was able to make it like 10 times faster than what I was able to do because it could optimize the code so well.

The downside with 5.2: I felt like it was gaslighting me a lot. I always had to be very careful checking it. There was one point — for one of the models I was playing around with, not 5.2 — as a unit test I told it: "OK, let's say I have $100 in the pot and I fold. How much am I losing?" The model said $92. I was like, "That's crazy. I have $100 in the pot and I just folded. How do I not lose $100?" It said, "Oh, you know, it's 92. It's close to 100. It's fine, it's no big deal." I was like, "Clearly this is a problem."

But once we got to 5.5, I actually thought it was way better. It was able to basically do it zero-shot. In fact, I've been working on doing a full-scale poker solver, and it's basically able to do the whole thing with some gentle steering from me. I wouldn't be surprised if six months or a year from now the model is able to do, zero-shot, an entire poker solver — basically my entire PhD thesis in one go.

六、安全评估的"不便之真相"

Let's talk about the larger implications of needing to evaluate these models relative to speed of reasoning, efficiency, token volume, dollar budget — whatever your scaler is. Can you describe some of the larger implications in your essay, including around safety evaluations?

The safety evaluations thing is a bit of an inconvenient truth.

For background: all the labs have these things called either responsible scaling policies or preparedness frameworks. They go by various names, but the idea is that whenever a model is released, they go through a series of evaluations to measure: are there dangerous capabilities? Could these models do things that we wouldn't want a bad actor to do? If the model isn't very capable, then it's no big deal. But if it is very capable — if it could be used, for example, to make bioweapons — then you want to put in mitigations against that.

The question is: how do you evaluate whether the model is capable of that? They have various protocols, but a lot of these frameworks were developed around the era of ChatGPT — either before or after — when test-time compute scaling was not really as much of a thing. It made sense: with GPT-3, you couldn't scale test-time compute. If you gave it a budget of $10 million, it really can't do that much more than what you could do with $10 or $1.

The preparedness frameworks and responsible scaling policies don't really account for the amount of test-time compute. They just say, "What's the capability of the model?" The problem is, the capability of the model is a function of how much money you put into it. If you give it a budget of $10,000, it can do a lot more than with $10. Give it $10 million, it can do even more.

So at what budget should you evaluate these models? The policies that exist today don't really address that question. Some do better than others, but for the most part this is not really a factor being heavily considered. Now whether it should be released anyway — I don't want to wade into that question; there are arguments on both sides. But the important thing to recognize is that this is a question that we're just kind of pretending doesn't exist.

— It's the mirror image of the capability question. If models can continue to do more and more without asymptoting on some tasks at very large budgets, then they should also be able to do so for tasks we don't want them to do as a society.

七、模型发布周期 vs. 长周期 eval

And testing for that — what budget is allocated — also seems out of sync from the model release cycle itself. You get a new model every sometimes few days and weeks at this point versus six months. You have a line in the essay where you say the only way to truly evaluate an agent on some very long-running task might be to run it for a year. That's going to be true of both useful and negative tasks.

This is also an interesting dynamic: as the models have become stronger, they're better able to operate over longer horizons. With GPT-3, if you wanted to run it for a week, there's really not much you could do to scaffold it into something useful. But with the most recent models, you can actually scaffold 5.5 into doing a series of experiments that can run for weeks, for months.

— Have you given your poker [solver such a long horizon]?

I haven't really scaffolded something where I just tell it, "OK, just run this for weeks." I think I could probably give it /goal and just tell it to go nuts. At this point it could 100% do the river solver if I just give it /goal. I don't think it's at the level yet where it could do the full poker solver if I gave it /goal and told it to run for a month. But we're going to pretty soon be at that point where I probably could just tell it, "Go work on this for a month and come back to me with a full complete poker solver that's state of the art."

The problem is: if you want to evaluate the capabilities of a model — what it can do after running for a month — the only way to be fully sure is to actually run it for a month. If you want to know after six months, the only way to know fully is to run it for six months.

I'll get to things we could do to address that later. But the model release cycle is: we're releasing new models every two or three months at this point. A model comes out, it takes two or three months to push it to its limits, and then another model comes out. Nobody actually knows what the ceiling of capabilities are for these models because nobody's actually run them for long enough to really tell.

When /goal came out, for example, people started running things that took over a week to finish. So people actually didn't realize this was a big deal until a week after it was released. I think that's going to be more and more true.

The implications: what do the labs do to fully evaluate their models before release? It's actually very difficult. The only way to really do the evaluations is to delay the model release cycle, and there's a lot of competitive pressure right now to not do that.

八、已发布模型里未被挖出的潜力：Erdős 单位距离猜想

Do you think there's exciting latent capability in the models that are already released that people have not fully explored given timeline?

Absolutely. A great example is the Erdős unit distance problem. We used an internal model at OpenAI a few weeks ago to disprove the Erdős unit distance conjecture. I'm not a mathematician, but this seems like it was a pretty big deal in the math community. It was the first problem that a lot of mathematicians had really spent a lot of time on, and the model was able to do something that they weren't able to do — and do it in a way that was actually interesting and useful for mathematicians.

Honestly, it did it at a budget that was dirt cheap. We didn't put a lot of effort into this. We tried a new model, were curious what it could do, ran it through some problems, and at a pretty low budget it was like, "Oh yeah, I think I have a disproof." We verified that yes, the disproof is correct.

After we announced the results, a bunch of people found that you could get the answer out of 5.5 as well. It's not as simple as just asking 5.5 "here's the Erdős unit distance conjecture, what's the disproof?" You had to scaffold it a bit, steer it a bit. Somebody found: ask 5.5 to list a bunch of ways you could tackle this problem, then for it lists one of the paths that are actually promising to get to the disproof, then tell it, "Explore this some more," and if you do this enough times it actually ends up arriving at the disproof.

In principle you could ask 5.5, as a general-purpose scaffold, to list a bunch of different strategies, and then for each strategy tell it to investigate that strategy, and it would probably arrive at the disproof. That scaffold would be very expensive — probably $1,000 to $10,000 to $100,000 — but it would be possible. Somebody could have disproved the Erdős unit distance conjecture before we did using a general-purpose model, and nobody had explored sufficiently what happens if I put $100,000 worth of compute into 5.5. The answer is: yeah, you probably could get stuff like that out of it.

— So people should be experimenting more with the current generation?

It's an interesting question of whether it's worth it to experiment with, because again, the model release cycle is every couple months we put out a new model that's even more powerful. The cost of disproving the Erdős unit distance conjecture drops by 10x or 100x with every model release cycle — probably in some cases more.

— You've seen the meme: "Why bother doing any engineering work when I should just wait for the next model?"

— Yeah, just go on vacation, come back two months later, and then it's a thousand times cheaper. Do you agree with that? Is that what you're doing right now at OpenAI?

I will say we're in a period where progress is very fast and the models are becoming more capable. At OpenAI, one of the things we're actively not doing — and look, we have a lot of mathematicians, a lot of physicists, people are very excited about what these models can do right now, especially the internal models — we are trying to encourage people to not spend all their time going through all the mathematical open problems, physics problems, pushing the models to their limits to see what they can prove or disprove. Because we really think the focus should be on: how do we make even more capable models? How can we get them out safely to the world as quickly as possible so that all the scientists in the world can use these models to solve the problems themselves?

So yes, it's really tempting to put all our efforts into scaling up these models and see what they can do at their limits right now. But really, the focus should be on how do we use these models to make even more powerful, more capable models that can do everything much more cost-effectively.

九、Recursive self-improvement（RSI）与"瞬间起飞"

What is changing about the direction or allocation of resources for research in your mind, given your beliefs about very large-scale test-time compute? How does this interact with the idea of recursive self-improvement?

One thing I should clarify: I don't think we're at the point where you just give it an arbitrary, extremely high inference budget and it's just super-intelligent across the board.

— /goal, make GPT-7 or whatever, just go nuts.

What's between us and there?

There are some benchmarks where the models will just not improve if they have more inference budget. A lot of factual retrieval questions fall into this category. If you ask a person, "When was Abraham Lincoln born?" and they don't know the date, they could sit there and think about it for a week — if they don't have access to Wikipedia, they're not going to be able to do better. Same with the model. (Interestingly, if you give the model these kinds of factual retrieval questions and you give them a little bit of time to think, they do actually do better. But if you give them a week, they're not suddenly going to do better at remembering dates.)

On the other extreme: there are benchmarks where they obviously will keep improving without limit with more test-time compute. Sudoku — there's a really simple strategy: try a bunch of different random numbers, see if it fits the constraints; if it doesn't, try a different random combination. With enough time, you will solve any Sudoku. Any model could keep doing better and better if given more test-time compute.

All the benchmarks exist somewhere between these two extremes. The models are not at the level where if you just give them enough test-time compute, they will be able to do all of our jobs.

研究品味（research taste）

One thing I see for research in particular: they don't have very good research taste right now. They're actually very good complements to researchers — I've found I'm much more effective using these models — but they're not able to fully replace the whole research cycle.

Does that change with time? Probably. The models are getting better across the board, some things faster than others. But they're not at the point where they're fully replacing researchers with just enough test-time compute.

— Can you give an example of asking the model to do a research task that's a terrible idea?

Going back to my poker solver: I was really impressed with the model's ability to optimize the algorithms I had developed in my PhD. It was shocking to see how inefficient I was in retrospect — they were able to make it 10x, 100x faster. Then I was like, "Can you come up with an algorithm that is better than the algorithms I came up with or that anybody else came up with? Look at all the published work, synthesize that, and try to come up with something novel." It's not able to do it. I can give it a lot of time and it's still not able to do it.

It's possible that if I scaffolded something, kind of constrained it a bit more, maybe it could eventually come up with something better. But it's not as simple as saying, "Please come up with a better algorithm."

What I've seen is that with every model release cycle, it does get better at this sort of thing. It's still bad, in my opinion, but not as bad as it used to be. I wouldn't be surprised if at some point — same with coding, same with math — there's an inflection point where suddenly it's actually good enough to be useful. I wouldn't be surprised if we encounter that point for research taste as well.

当下的 RSI 是"加速"，不是"替代"

What is your framing of RSI today?

The models are definitely accelerating what researchers can do inside the labs. But they're accelerating some things and not other things. Currently, if something goes 100x faster, you get bottlenecked by the things that don't go 100x faster. Over time, the things we're getting bottlenecked on are going to shrink, and there will be a kind of gradual takeoff. But it's more about transforming what researchers do rather than fully replacing the researchers.

— That actually implies you don't think we're close to a very fast takeoff right now?

Fast takeoff is relative. Things are moving very fast, but there is this hypothesis that you could have basically an overnight intelligence explosion — where the models discover some kind of breakthrough to make themselves smarter, and that leads to more breakthroughs that make them even smarter immediately, and in an instant the models become very superhuman across the board in moments.

I don't think we're headed to that world, largely because the models rely so much on large-scale test-time compute in order to achieve their greatest intelligence. If it requires so much test-time compute to unlock the full capabilities of the model, you're bottlenecked by time. Things can only go so fast because the models need to run for long enough to actually do something really powerful. Time itself becomes a bottleneck.

That is the case right now for a lot of the labs. Ultimately, I think the biggest bottleneck for all of us is time, and that's why all the researchers are working so intensely right now.
